Three real-time tasks are to be scheduled on an embedded controller:

| Task | Period $T_i$ | WCET $C_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 50 ms | 10 ms |
| $\tau_2$ | 80 ms | 15 ms |
| $\tau_3$ | 100 ms | 20 ms |

**Step 1 — Order tasks by increasing period (RMS rule: shorter period = higher priority)**

Priorities: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$.

**Step 2 — Compute individual utilizations**

$$
u_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{10}{50} = 0.200
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{15}{80} = 0.1875
$$

$$
u_3 = \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.200
$$

**Step 3 — Total CPU utilization**

$$
U = u_1 + u_2 + u_3 = 0.200 + 0.1875 + 0.200 = \mathbf{0.5875}
$$

**Step 4 — Liu & Layland sufficient bound for $n = 3$ tasks**

$$
U_{\text{bound}}(n) = n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

$$
U_{\text{bound}}(3) = 3\left(2^{1/3} - 1\right)
$$

$$
U_{\text{bound}}(3) = 3\left(1.2599 - 1\right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = \mathbf{0.7797}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**

$$
U = 0.5875 \;\le\; U_{\text{bound}}(3) = 0.7797
$$

*Since the utilization is below the sufficient bound, the task set is **schedulable under RMS**. The embedded system can guarantee that no deadlines will be missed — fulfilling the **hard real-time purpose** of the design.*

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Selecting an MCU Based on MIPS

A digital stethoscope samples audio at $f_s = 44.1\;\text{kHz}$ and runs a 1024-point FFT per sample. Each FFT requires $N \log_2 N = 1024 \times 10 = 10240$ cycles. MCU clock is $f_{\text{clk}} = 50\;\text{MHz}$.

**Step 1 — Compute required cycles per second**

$$
\text{Cycles/sec} = f_s \times C_{\text{FFT}} = 44100 \times 10240
$$

$$
\text{Cycles/sec} = 451{,}584{,}000\;\text{cycles/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MIPS needed**

$$
\text{MIPS}_{\text{req}} = \frac{451.584 \times 10^{6}}{10^6} = \mathbf{451.58\;\text{MIPS}}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision**

A general-purpose MCU at **50 MHz / 50 MIPS** is wholly insufficient. The embedded system design must either:
- Upgrade to a **DSP** (e.g., 600+ MIPS), or
- Reduce FFT size, or
- Lower sample rate.

*This illustrates the **purpose of MIPS-budget analysis** during embedded system hardware selection.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Embedded System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Environment] -->|Stimulus| B[Sensors]
    B -->|Analog Signal| C[ADC]
    C -->|Digital Samples| D[Embedded Processor with Firmware]
    H[Memory ROM and RAM] --- D
    I[Timer and Interrupt Controller] --- D
    D -->|Control Decision| E[DAC or PWM or GPIO]
    E -->|Analog or Digital Drive| F[Actuators]
    F -->|Physical Action| A
    D -->|UART SPI I2C CAN USB| G[Communication Interface]
    G -->|Wired or Wireless| J[Other Embedded Nodes or Cloud]
    K[Power Supply Unit] --- D
    K --- C
    K --- E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram (left to right):** The physical world stimulates a sensor → the signal is digitized → the embedded processor (running firmware out of ROM, using RAM for variables) computes a response → the response is converted back to a physical action via an actuator → optionally, the node communicates with peers. The Power Supply Unit (PSU) feeds all active blocks.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — SENSE → THINK → ACT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["SENSE STAGE"] --> S2["Sensors, ADC, Signal Conditioning"]
    S2 --> T1["THINK STAGE"]
    T1 --> T2["Embedded Processor, Firmware, RTOS, Decision Logic"]
    T2 --> A1["ACT STAGE"]
    A1 --> A2["Actuators, DAC, PWM, GPIO, Communication"]
    A2 -.Feedback Loop.-> S1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **closed feedback loop** between ACT and SENSE is what makes an embedded system a *cyber-physical* (CPS) device rather than a passive data logger. The KTU syllabus emphasizes this closed-loop control property.

## 4.3 Taxonomy Diagram — Categories of Embedded Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Embedded Systems"]
    R --> P1["Standalone - e.g. Digital Camera"]
    R --> P2["Real-Time - e.g. Anti-lock Braking System"]
    R --> P3["Networked - e.g. IoT Sensor Node"]
    R --> P4["Mobile - e.g. Smartphone SoC"]
    R --> P5["Safety-Critical - e.g. Flight Controller"]
    P2 --> P2a["Hard Real-Time - Deadline miss causes failure"]
    P2 --> P2b["Soft Real-Time - Deadline miss degrades quality"]
    P5 --> P5a["Mission-Critical - Aerospace, Nuclear"]
    P5 --> P5b["Life-Critical - Pacemaker, Insulin Pump"]
```

## 4.4 Design-Constraint Radar — Purpose-Driven Trade-offs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["Real-Time"] --> X["Design Trade-off Space"]
    C2["Low Power"] --> X
    C3["Low Cost"] --> X
    C4["Small Size"] --> X
    C5["High Reliability"] --> X
    C6["Security"] --> X
    X --> Y["Optimized Embedded Solution"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define an embedded system. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from a general-purpose computer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An embedded system is a microprocessor-/microcontroller-based computing system designed to perform a **single dedicated function** as part of a larger mechanical or electrical product.

**Four distinguishing characteristics (½ mark each):**
1. **Single-functioned** — built to do one job; software is "frozen" in ROM.
2. **Tight resource constraints** — limited RAM/ROM, low power (< 1 W typical).
3. **Real-time operation** — bounded response time to external events.
4. **Highly reliable** — must operate for years without maintenance, often in harsh environments.

*(0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 2.5 → round to 3. A clean 3-mark answer: definition 1 mark + 4 × 0.5 = 2 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between hard real-time and soft real-time embedded systems. Give one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|---|---|---|
| Deadline | **Must** be met | Occasionally missed OK |
| Consequence of miss | Catastrophic / system failure | Degraded QoS |
| Verification | Formal schedulability test | Statistical / best-effort |
| Example | **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** | **Video streaming set-top box** |

*(1 mark for differentiation + 1 mark per example = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each 14-mark question carries **two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each**, and you are offered an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the fundamental purpose of embedded systems in modern engineering. With a neat block diagram, describe the SENSE → THINK → ACT paradigm and illustrate it with the example of an **anti-lock braking system (ABS)** in an automobile.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A real-time embedded controller runs three periodic tasks: $\tau_1$ with $T_1 = 30\;\text{ms}, C_1 = 6\;\text{ms}$; $\tau_2$ with $T_2 = 60\;\text{ms}, C_2 = 10\;\text{ms}$; $\tau_3$ with $T_3 = 90\;\text{ms}, C_3 = 12\;\text{ms}$. Apply the **Liu & Layland Rate Monotonic test** to determine if the task set is schedulable. Comment on the design implications.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Purpose of Embedded Systems (3 marks):**
The purpose of an embedded system is to deliver *dedicated, deterministic, energy-efficient, and reliable computation* embedded within a physical product so that the product can autonomously **sense, decide, and act** on its environment. They bridge the cyber (digital) and physical (analog) worlds, enabling automation, intelligence, and connectivity in domains ranging from consumer electronics to mission-critical aerospace.

**2. SENSE → THINK → ACT Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Wheel Speed Sensor] -->|Pulses| S2[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    S2 -->|Digital RPM| T1[Embedded MCU Firmware]
    T1 -->|PWM Command| A1[Brake Hydraulic Modulator]
    A1 -->|Reduced Brake Pressure| S1
```

**3. ABS Example Walk-through (2 marks):**
- **SENSE:** Wheel-speed sensors (Hall-effect) detect rapid deceleration of a wheel — impending lock.
- **THINK:** Embedded ECU runs a slip-ratio control loop; computes required brake-pressure adjustment within **< 5 ms**.
- **ACT:** PWM-driven hydraulic modulator releases and re-applies pressure up to **15–20 times/second** to prevent wheel lock and maintain steering control.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Sort by period (RMS priority):** $T_1 = 30 < T_2 = 60 < T_3 = 90$ ⇒ Priorities: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$.

**Step 2 — Compute utilizations:**

$$
u_1 = \frac{6}{30} = 0.200
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{10}{60} \approx 0.1667
$$

$$
u_3 = \frac{12}{90} \approx 0.1333
$$

**Step 3 — Total utilization:**

$$
U = 0.200 + 0.1667 + 0.1333 = \mathbf{0.5000}
$$

**Step 4 — Liu & Layland bound for $n = 3$:**

$$
U_{\text{bound}} = 3\left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = \mathbf{0.7797}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):**

$$
U = 0.5000 \;\le\; U_{\text{bound}} = 0.7797
$$

The task set is **guaranteed schedulable under RMS**. Design implication: the embedded firmware can confidently run these three hard real-time tasks on a single-core MCU, leaving **27.97% CPU headroom** for additional low-priority or best-effort work such as diagnostics or CAN-bus communication.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **five major design constraints** that govern every embedded system, and explain how each one shapes the system's hardware-software architecture. Use a **smart insulin pump** to illustrate.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* An IoT environmental sensor node runs on a 3.0 V coin-cell battery rated 240 mAh. The node's active current is 18 mA and the duty cycle is 1% (1% active, 99% sleep at 5 µA). Compute: (i) average current, (ii) total energy consumed in 30 days, and (iii) the expected battery lifetime in days.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Design Constraints of the Insulin Pump (7 marks)

| # | Constraint | Pump-Specific Implication |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Real-Time** | Must deliver a precisely timed bolus (e.g., 0.5 U over 5 s); deadline miss → hypo/hyperglycemia. |
| 2 | **Low Power** | Battery life > 14 days; aggressive duty cycling; sub-µA sleep modes. |
| 3 | **Low Cost** | Disposable pump form → BOM < \$20; uses low-cost Cortex-M0+ MCU. |
| 4 | **Small Size** | Wearable on belt-clip → SoC + integrated ADC, no discrete components. |
| 5 | **Safety & Reliability** | Redundant watchdog, single-fault tolerance; certified to **IEC 60601-1**. |

Each constraint → 1 mark = **5 marks**; concluding remark on the closed coupling of HW/SW = **2 marks**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — IoT Battery Math (7 marks)

**Given:** $V = 3.0\;\text{V}$, $C_{\text{bat}} = 240\;\text{mAh}$, $I_{\text{active}} = 18\;\text{mA}$, $I_{\text{sleep}} = 5\;\mu\text{A} = 0.005\;\text{mA}$, duty cycle $\delta = 0.01$, $T = 30\;\text{days} = 720\;\text{h}$.

**(i) Average current (2 marks):**

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = \delta \cdot I_{\text{active}} + (1 - \delta) \cdot I_{\text{sleep}}
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = 0.01 \times 18 + 0.99 \times 0.005
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = 0.180 + 0.00495 = \mathbf{0.18495\;\text{mA}}
$$

**(ii) Energy consumed in 30 days (3 marks):**

First, energy in mAh:

$$
Q_{30d} = I_{\text{avg}} \times T = 0.18495 \times 720 = \mathbf{133.16\;\text{mAh}}
$$

Convert to Joules using $E = V \cdot Q \cdot 3600$:

$$
E = 3.0 \times 133.16 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600
$$

$$
E = \mathbf{1438.2\;\text{J}}
$$

**(iii) Expected battery lifetime in days (2 marks):**

$$
t_{\text{life}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{240}{0.18495}
$$

$$
t_{\text{life}} = 1297.6\;\text{hours} = \mathbf{54.07\;\text{days}}
$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** The duty-cycling strategy extends lifetime from a few hours of continuous operation to **> 54 days**, fulfilling the **purpose of low-power embedded design** for IoT deployments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write**
> 1. **Never write "general-purpose PC" in your definition of an embedded system** — it is the *opposite*. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to sort tasks by period** before applying Liu & Layland — RMS *requires* increasing period order. Common 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Confusing $C_{\text{bat}}$ (mAh) with $E$ (Joules)** — they are not interchangeable; you must multiply by $V \times 3600$ to convert. 1–2 mark trap.
> 4. **Stating only $U \le 1$ as the schedulability test** — that is *necessary but not sufficient* for hard real-time; always apply Liu & Layland bound.
> 5. **Leaving the SENSE → THINK → ACT diagram unlabeled** — unlabeled blocks ⇒ 0 marks for diagram portion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Purpose of Embedded Systems"**

- [ ] **Definition:** Computing system dedicated to a *single function*, embedded within a larger product, with real-time, power, cost, and reliability constraints.
- [ ] **Slogan to memorize:** *"SENSE → THINK → ACT"* — the universal purpose loop.
- [ ] **Five Design Constraints:** Real-time · Low power · Low cost · Small size · High reliability.
- [ ] **Real-time classes:** Hard (miss = catastrophe) vs Soft (miss = degraded QoS).
- [ ] **Power formula:** $P = V \cdot I$ · **Energy:** $E = P \cdot t$.
- [ ] **Liu & Layland RMS bound:** $U_{\text{bound}}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ · converges to **0.693**.
- [ ] **CPU utilization test:** Compute $U = \sum C_i / T_i$; compare to bound.
- [ ] **Battery-life math:** $t_{\text{life}} = C_{\text{bat}} / I_{\text{avg}}$; $E = V \cdot C_{\text{bat}} \cdot 3600$ (J).
- [ ] **MIPS selection:** $\text{MIPS}_{\text{req}} = (f_s \cdot C_{\text{per-sample}}) / 10^6$.
- [ ] **Generic architecture blocks:** Sensor → ADC → MCU + Memory + Timer → DAC/PWM/GPIO → Actuator → optional Communication.
- [ ] **Examples to remember:** ABS (hard real-time, safety), Smart Insulin Pump (life-critical), IoT node (low power), Set-top box (soft real-time).
- [ ] **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS):** the modern framing — embedded system as the bridge between *cyber* (computation) and *physical* (world).
- [ ] **Avoid** confusing embedded with general-purpose; always mention *dedicated function*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Embedded System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Typical Embedded System ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Embedded System (KTU 2024 Definition):** > An **Embedded System** is a **purpose-built**, **dedicated computer system** designed to perform a **specific function** as part of a larger mechanical, electrical, or computational system. It is *embedded* as a subsystem within the host product and operates under strict constraints of **real-time performance**, **power consumption**, **size**, **cost**, and **reliability**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, a *typical* embedded system is characterized by three inseparable layers: 1. **Hardware Layer** – Sensor → Processor + Memory + Actuator 2. **Software Layer** – Application Firmware / RTOS 3. **Real-Time Operating Environment (RTOE)** – Strict timing deadlines ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Dedicated Chef vs. The Multi-Tool Waiter** > Think of a **desktop computer** as a *versatile restaurant waiter* — he can take orders, explain menus, serve water, cutlery, and the bill (he does *everything*, but is slower at each task). > An **embedded system** is like a *dedicated chef in a sushi kiosk* — he does **only one job** (makes sushi), does it **extremely fast**, **repeatedly**, and **predictably**, with **minimal wastage** (low power, low cost). > Just as the sushi chef has specialized knives (peripherals), a fixed recipe (firmware), and a single stove (processor), an embedded system is *engineered for a single repetitive task* under tight constraints. ### 1.3 Common Everyday Examples > [!TIP] > **Typical Embedded System Examples You Already Use:** > - **Microwave Oven** → Controls magnetron timing, display, and keypad > - **Washing Machine** → Manages motor cycles, water level, and timers > - **Automotive ABS / Airbag ECU** → Crash detection in **< 10 ms** > - **Smart Watch** → Heart-rate sensing, step counting, BLE communication > - **Drone Flight Controller** – Real-time PID loops at **> 1 kHz** > - **IoT Smart Meter** – Periodic power data logging over Wi-Fi ### 1.4 Why "Typical"? The phrase *"typical embedded system"* refers to the **canonical architecture** — a **single-MCU-based** system with **on-chip memory**, **limited I/O**, and **single-application firmware**. It excludes complex distributed or networked embedded systems (like full ADAS) but includes everything from a digital thermometer to a smart printer controller. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of a Typical Embedded System #### **Pillar 1: Hardware Building Blocks** | # | Block | Function | Typical Component | |---|-------|----------|-------------------| | 1 | **Sensor / Input** | Converts physical → electrical signal | LDR, Thermistor, Accelerometer (MPU6050) | | 2 | **A-D Converter** | Analog → Digital | 10-/12-bit SAR ADC | | 3 | **Processor (Core)** | Executes instructions | 8051, ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4, AVR, PIC | | 4 | **Memory** | Stores code & data | Flash (program), SRAM (runtime), EEPROM (config) | | 5 | **D-A Converter / PWM** | Digital → Analog / pulsed | PWM-driven motor driver | | 6 | **Actuator / Output** | Electrical → physical action | Relay, DC motor, LED, Buzzer, LCD | | 7 | **Communication** | External interfacing | UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB, BLE | | 8 | **Power Supply** | Energy delivery | 3.3 V / 5 V LDO, Battery, Buck converter | #### **Pillar 2: Software Layer** > [!IMPORTANT] > The software of a typical embedded system follows a **3-tier firmware stack**: > 1. **BSP (Board Support Package)** – Startup code, linker scripts, vector tables > 2. **HAL / Drivers** – Peripheral libraries (GPIO, UART, ADC) > 3. **Application Logic** – The "main loop" or RTOS tasks #### **Pillar 3: Real-Time Constraints** A typical embedded system is **hard real-time** if missing a deadline = **system failure** (e.g., airbag). It is **soft real-time** if missing a deadline = **degraded quality** (e.g., video frame skip). ### 2.2 Core Characteristics (KTU Frequently Asked) - **Single-Functioned** → runs one dedicated program repeatedly - **Tightly Constrained** → limited CPU, memory, power - **Real-Time Responsive** → deterministic response within deadline $T_d$ - **Reactive & Concurrent** → multiple events handled via ISRs / RTOS - **Reliability / Safety Critical** → MTBF often > **100,000 hours** - **Low Power** → often battery-operated, deep-sleep currents in **μA** ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Save this table for the exam — it covers ~70% of numerical questions in this module.** | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|--------------------|------------|----------------| | 1 | **CPU Clock Period** | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | seconds (s) | | 2 | **Instruction Execution Time** | $T_{inst} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clk}$ | N = cycles per instruction | | 3 | **Throughput / MIPS** | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions / s | | 4 | **Deadline Slack** | $S_d = T_{deadline} - T_{exec}$ | Positive ⇒ feasible task | | 5 | **CPU Utilization (single task)** | $U = \dfrac{T_{exec}}{T_{period}}$ | $0 \le U \le 1$ | | 6 | **Power Dissipation (CMOS)** | $P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | α = switching activity, C = load cap | | 7 | **Battery Life (Hours)** | $L_h = \dfrac{\text{Capacity (mAh)}}{I_{avg}\text{ (mA)}}$ | Ideal linear discharge | | 8 | **ADC Resolution** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | n = ADC bits | | 9 | **ADC Digital Output** | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n}-1) \right\rfloor$ | Integer code 0 … 2ⁿ−1 | | 10 | **PWM Duty Cycle** | $D_{pwm} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\,\%$ | Average output voltage $V_{avg} = D_{pwm} \cdot V_{s}$ | | 11 | **UART Baud Period** | $T_{baud} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | e.g., 9600 baud ⇒ 104.17 μs/bit | | 12 | **Response Time Lower Bound (Liu & Layland)** | $R_n \ge C_n + \sum_{j \in hp(n)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_n}{T_j}\right\rceil C_j$ | Used for Rate Monotonic Analysis | | 13 | **Schedulability (RM bound)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \le n(\sqrt[n]{2}-1)$ | Sufficient (not necessary) | | 14 | **Von Neumann / Harvard Memory Access** | $T_{mem} = N \times T_{acc}$ | Harvard: parallel fetch, faster | > [!NOTE] > **Important exam note:** For *typical embedded system* questions, KTU usually tests **ADC resolution, PWM duty cycle, MIPS, and battery life** numerically. Memorize rows **1, 2, 6, 8, 9, 10, and 14**. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are the **backbone** of every embedded design interview and product spec: - **Formula 6** (CMOS power) is why your phone gets hot during gaming but stays cool in standby — engineers lower $V_{dd}$ and $f$ dynamically. - **Formula 7** (battery life) drives the design of every IoT node — choosing sleep current over active current is the #1 design lever. - **Formula 8–9** (ADC) determines whether your medical glucometer can read 100 mg/dL or only 200 mg/dL — bits matter in health tech. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example #1 — ADC Quantization of a Temperature Sensor **Problem:** A typical embedded weather node uses an **NTC thermistor** feeding a **10-bit ADC** with $V_{ref} = 3.3\text{ V}$. At an instant, $V_{in} = 1.25\text{ V}$. Determine: 1. ADC resolution (LSB size) 2. Digital output code 3. Quantization error in volts 4. Equivalent temperature, given a linear calibration $T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = (D - 100)/2$ **Step 1 — Resolution (LSB size):** Apply Formula 8 from the cheat sheet: $$ \Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{1023} $$ $$ \Delta V = 3.2258 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \approx 3.226 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 2 — Digital output code:** Apply Formula 9: $$ D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n} - 1) \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1.25}{3.3} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor $$ $$ D = \left\lfloor 0.3788 \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 387.51 \right\rfloor = 387 $$ **Step 3 — Quantization error:** The maximum quantization error is $\pm \frac{1}{2} \text{ LSB}$: $$ V_{err} = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{3.226 \text{ mV}}{2} = \pm 1.613 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 4 — Equivalent temperature:** Using the calibration $T = (D - 100)/2$: $$ T = \frac{387 - 100}{2} = \frac{287}{2} = 143.5^{\circ}\text{C} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** This temperature is unusually high for weather monitoring. A real system would use a thermistor curve and signal conditioning (e.g., a Wheatstone bridge + op-amp) to map a 0–50 °C range into 0.1–3.0 V. Always design the **input range** to fully utilize the ADC's dynamic range — KTU often tests this. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example #2 — CPU Utilization for a Hard Real-Time Task **Problem:** An automotive wiper controller runs on an **ARM Cortex-M0 @ 48 MHz**. The wiper task has: - Execution time $C = 1.2 \text{ ms}$ - Period $T = 5 \text{ ms}$ - Deadline $D = 5 \text{ ms}$ Find: CPU utilization, deadline slack, and MIPS budget. **Step 1 — CPU Utilization (Formula 5):** $$ U = \frac{T_{exec}}{T_{period}} = \frac{1.2 \text{ ms}}{5 \text{ ms}} = 0.24 = 24\% $$ **Step 2 — Deadline Slack (Formula 4):** $$ S_d = T_{deadline} - T_{exec} = 5 - 1.2 = 3.8 \text{ ms} $$ Positive slack ⇒ the task is **feasible** and the scheduler has **3.8 ms of headroom** for other tasks (sensor reads, CAN communication, diagnostics). **Step 3 — MIPS Budget:** First compute instruction count, assuming CPI = 1 (Cortex-M0 is single-cycle for most instructions): $$ N_{inst} = \frac{T_{exec}}{T_{clk}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1 / (48 \times 10^{6})} $$ $$ N_{inst} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 48 \times 10^{6} = 57{,}600 \text{ instructions} $$ Therefore, achievable throughput (Formula 3): $$ \text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^6} = \frac{48 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^6} = 48 \text{ MIPS} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Even though the MCU can deliver 48 MIPS, the application only uses ~0.058 MIPS for the wiper task. This is *why* typical embedded systems can use cheap low-MHz MCUs instead of GHz-class processors — the workload is **bounded and predictable**. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Topic to KTU Learning Outcomes | KTU 2024 CO | Learning Outcome | Coverage in This Topic | Bloom Level | |--------------|------------------|-------------------------|--------------| | CO1 | Identify components of a typical embedded system | Hardware block table (§2.1) | Remember / Understand | | CO1 | Differentiate embedded vs. general-purpose systems | §1.2 analogy + §2.2 characteristics | Analyze | | CO2 | Compute ADC, PWM, MIPS, power, battery metrics | §3.1, §3.2 worked examples | Apply / Compute | | CO2 | Evaluate real-time feasibility | §3.2 slack & utilization | Evaluate | | CO3 | Map real-world products to system architecture | §1.3 examples | Apply | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Embedded System The following Mermaid diagram shows the **end-to-end signal and data flow** of a canonical embedded system (e.g., a smart temperature-controlled fan). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical World
Temperature Humidity Motion] --> B[Sensors
NTC Thermistor LM35 MPU6050] B --> C[Signal Conditioning
Op-Amp Filter] C --> D[ADC
10 or 12 bit SAR] D --> E[Processor Core
ARM Cortex M0 M3] E --> F[Memory
Flash SRAM EEPROM] E --> G[Communication
UART SPI I2C CAN BLE] E --> H[PWM Generator
16 bit Timer] H --> I[Actuator Driver
MOSFET H-Bridge L293D] I --> J[Actuator
DC Motor Fan Relay] E --> K[User Interface
LCD LED Buzzer Buttons] E --> L[RTOS Scheduler
FreeRTOS Tasks] L --> E M[Power Unit
3V3 LDO Battery 7805] --> E M --> C M --> I ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the diagram for exams:** Trace the data path *left → right*. Whenever the examiner asks *"draw a block diagram of a typical embedded system"*, ensure these **8 boxes are present**: Sensor, ADC, Processor, Memory, Actuator, Communication, UI, and Power. Missing **Power** is the most common deduction (see §5 warning). ### 4.2 Architectural Classification of Typical Embedded Systems ```mermaid flowchart TB P[Typical Embedded System Classification] P --> Q1[By Complexity] P --> Q2[By Performance] P --> Q3[By Determinism] P --> Q4[By Triggering] Q1 --> R1[Small Scale
8 or 16 bit MCU
8051 PIC16] Q1 --> R2[Medium Scale
32 bit MCU
ARM Cortex M] Q1 --> R3[Complex Scale
MPU SoC DSP
ARM Cortex A] Q2 --> S1[Low Performance
less than 100 MIPS] Q2 --> S2[Medium Performance
100 to 1000 MIPS] Q2 --> S3[High Performance
more than 1000 MIPS] Q3 --> T1[Hard Real Time
Airbag Antilock Brake] Q3 --> T2[Soft Real Time
Set Top Box Streaming] Q3 --> T3[Non Real Time
Smart Washing Machine] Q4 --> U1[Event Triggered
Doorbell Sensor Alert] Q4 --> U2[Time Triggered
Periodic Sampling 10 ms] ``` ### 4.3 Data-Flow Inside the Software Stack ```mermaid flowchart TD APP[Application Layer
main function task logic] --> HAL[Hardware Abstraction Layer
GPIO UART ADC PWM drivers] HAL --> BSP[Board Support Package
startup linker script clock init] BSP --> HW[Hardware Registers
Memory Mapped IO] HW --> APP RTOS[RTOS Kernel
FreeRTOS scheduler queues] --> APP RTOS --> HAL ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a typical embedded system. List any FOUR distinguishing characteristics.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** A typical embedded system is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based, dedicated computer system designed to perform a specific task as part of a larger system, operating under tight constraints of cost, power, and real-time response. **Four distinguishing characteristics (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Single-functioned** – runs a fixed, dedicated application 2. **Tight constraints** – low power, limited memory, small form factor 3. **Real-time behavior** – deterministic response within deadlines 4. **Reactive and concurrent** – handles multiple asynchronous events via ISRs/RTOS *(Also acceptable: reliability, low cost, user interface, networked connectivity.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Compare Harvard architecture and Von Neumann architecture. Why is Harvard preferred for typical embedded systems?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Von Neumann | Harvard | |--------|-------------|----------| | Memory | Single bus for data & instructions | Separate buses for data & instructions | | Fetch–Execute | Sequential (bottleneck) | Parallel (simultaneous) | | Speed | Slower | Faster (typical 2× for same clock) | | Complexity | Simpler hardware | More complex | | Cost | Lower | Slightly higher | > **Why preferred in embedded systems (1 mark):** Harvard allows **simultaneous instruction fetch + data access**, giving deterministic, higher throughput for real-time tasks (e.g., DSP in audio codecs). Most modern MCUs (8051, ARM Cortex-M, AVR) use **modified Harvard** to retain speed with a unified programming model. --- ### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Path 1** **Q3 (a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (7 marks)** **Draw the block diagram of a typical embedded system and explain the function of EACH block. Illustrate with a real-world example of a digital weighing scale.** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (3 marks)** — Refer to the diagram in §4.1. Mandatory blocks: *Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Processor → Memory → Display/Actuator → Communication → Power Supply*. **Function of each block (3 marks — 0.5 each for 6 blocks):** 1. **Sensor (Load Cell / Strain Gauge):** Converts weight (force) into a small mV-level differential voltage. 2. **Signal Conditioning (Instrumentation Amplifier – INA125):** Amplifies μV to ~3 V range and filters 50 Hz mains noise. 3. **ADC (24-bit Sigma-Delta, e.g., HX711):** High-resolution conversion for sub-gram accuracy. 4. **Processor (ARM Cortex-M0 / MSP430):** Runs calibration, tare, unit conversion, and UI logic. 5. **Memory (Flash + EEPROM):** Stores calibration constants; Flash holds firmware. 6. **Actuator / Display (16×2 LCD + Buzzer):** Shows weight; buzzer beeps on stable reading. 7. **Communication (UART / I²C):** Optional PC link for data logging. 8. **Power (9 V battery → 3.3 V LDO):** Long battery life via low Iq regulator. **Real-world example summary (1 mark):** A digital weighing scale uses a load cell → INA125 → HX711 ADC → STM32 MCU → LCD display; the entire system fits in < ₹500 BOM and runs for **> 1 year on 4×AA cells** due to ultra-low sleep current. --- **Q3 (b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (7 marks)** **A temperature monitoring embedded node uses a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$. The sensor output spans 0.5 V to 4.5 V. Calculate (i) LSB resolution, (ii) Digital output at $V_{in} = 3.0\text{ V}$, and (iii) the maximum quantization error. Comment on whether the design is optimal.** **Model Solution:** **(i) LSB Resolution (2 marks):** $$ \Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{5}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{5}{4095} = 1.2205 \text{ mV/LSB} $$ **(ii) Digital Output at 3.0 V (3 marks):** $$ D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n} - 1) \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{3.0}{5} \cdot 4095 \right\rfloor $$ $$ D = \left\lfloor 0.6 \times 4095 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 2457 \right\rfloor = 2457 $$ **(iii) Maximum Quantization Error (2 marks):** $$ V_{err,max} = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{1.2205 \text{ mV}}{2} = \pm 0.6103 \text{ mV} $$ **Comment on Optimality (1 mark):** > The design is **NOT optimal** because the sensor's actual swing (0.5–4.5 V) does not use the full ADC range (0–5 V). The lower 0.5 V and upper 0.5 V of input swing are *wasted* — only **80% of dynamic range is utilized**, reducing effective resolution to $\log_2(4095 \times 0.8) \approx 11.68$ bits. An **optimal design** would either (a) add an op-amp with 0.5 V offset and 1.1× gain to map 0.5–4.5 V → 0–4.4 V, or (b) use a lower $V_{ref}$ such that the sensor swing fills most of the input range. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Path 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** **Q4 (a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (7 marks)** **Explain the classification of typical embedded systems based on (i) complexity & performance, and (ii) determinism. Give one example for each category.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Classification by Complexity & Performance (4 marks):** | Class | CPU Class | Bits | Clock | Example | |-------|-----------|------|-------|----------| | **Small-Scale** | 8051, PIC16 | 8-bit | < 50 MHz | Remote control, toy, calculator | | **Medium-Scale** | ARM Cortex-M, AVR | 16/32-bit | 50–200 MHz | Washing machine, smart meter, wearables | | **Large/Complex** | ARM Cortex-A, SoC with DSP | 32/64-bit | > 200 MHz | Smart camera, ADAS, infotainment | **(ii) Classification by Determinism (3 marks):** - **Hard Real-Time:** Missing a deadline = system failure. *Example:* Airbag ECU must fire within 10 ms of crash detection. - **Soft Real-Time:** Missing a deadline = degraded performance. *Example:* Set-top box drops one video frame. - **Non-Real-Time / Best Effort:** No strict timing. *Example:* Smart refrigerator's inventory app. --- **Q4 (b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (7 marks)** **An embedded battery-powered sensor node uses CMOS logic at $V_{dd} = 3.3\text{ V}$, clock $f = 50\text{ MHz}$, average switching activity $\alpha = 0.1$, and load capacitance $C = 10\text{ pF}$. Compute (i) dynamic power dissipation, (ii) the node's battery life if powered by a 1000 mAh Li-ion cell with average current 12 mA, and (iii) suggest TWO engineering techniques to reduce power.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Dynamic Power Dissipation (3 marks):** $$ P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f $$ $$ P = 0.1 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times (3.3)^{2} \times 50 \times 10^{6} $$ $$ P = 0.1 \times 10^{-11} \times 10.89 \times 50 \times 10^{6} $$ $$ P = 0.1 \times 10^{-11} \times 5.445 \times 10^{8} = 5.445 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} = 544.5 \text{ μW} $$ **(ii) Battery Life (2 marks):** $$ L_h = \frac{\text{Capacity (mAh)}}{I_{avg} \text{ (mA)}} = \frac{1000 \text{ mAh}}{12 \text{ mA}} = 83.33 \text{ h} \approx 3.47 \text{ days} $$ **(iii) Power Reduction Techniques (2 marks — 1 each):** 1. **Dynamic Voltage & Frequency Scaling (DVFS):** Lower $V_{dd}$ to 1.8 V during idle; $P$ scales with $V_{dd}^{2}$, so reducing $V_{dd}$ to 1.8 V gives a power saving factor of $(1.8/3.3)^2 \approx 0.30$ — i.e., **70% power reduction**. 2. **Sleep Modes with Wake-on-Event:** Put the MCU in deep sleep (current ~1 μA) for 99% of the cycle and wake only for a 10 ms measurement window every 1 s. Average current drops from 12 mA to ~0.12 mA, extending battery life to **~347 days**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - In Q4(b) part (i), students **often forget to convert pF to F (×10⁻¹²)** and lose 1 mark. > - In Q3(b), students **ignore dynamic range utilization** when commenting on optimality and lose 1 mark. > - In Q1 & Q2, students list more than 4 characteristics but skip the **definition line** — KTU mandates definition + list, and skipping the definition costs 1 mark even if 4 characteristics are correct. > - In Q3(a) block diagrams, omitting the **Power Supply block** is the most common 0.5–1 mark deduction. Always include it. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Typical Embedded System" (Module 1)** > - ✅ Definition: *purpose-built, dedicated, constrained computer system* — must include **three keywords**: dedicated, constrained, real-time. > - ✅ 8 mandatory diagram blocks: **Sensor → ADC → Processor → Memory → Actuator → Communication → UI → Power**. > - ✅ Three software tiers: **BSP → HAL → Application** (+ optional RTOS). > - ✅ Two architecture types: **Von Neumann** (single bus) vs **Harvard** (separate buses). Modified Harvard is dominant in modern MCUs. > - ✅ Classification axes: **complexity** (small/medium/large), **determinism** (hard/soft/non), **triggering** (event/time). > - ✅ Hard real-time deadline = system failure; Soft = degraded quality. > - ✅ ADC output formula: $D = \lfloor V_{in}/V_{ref} \cdot (2^n - 1) \rfloor$ — round DOWN, not nearest. > - ✅ CMOS Power: $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$ — voltage dominates; halving $V_{dd}$ → quarter the power. > - ✅ Battery life is driven by **average current**, not peak; sleep modes are the biggest lever. > - ✅ CPU utilization $U = C/T$; for hard real-time, $U \le 1$ is necessary, $U \le n(\sqrt[n]{2}-1)$ is the Rate-Monotonic sufficient bound. > - ✅ Typical exam traps: missing **Power block** in diagrams, forgetting unit conversions (pF→F, μs→s), using $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$ in ADC formulas. > - ✅ Real-world examples to mention: **weighing scale, washing machine, airbag ECU, smart watch, IoT sensor node**. ---

topics.title: Core of the Embedded System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Core of the Embedded System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Core of an Embedded System** refers to the central processing unit along with the supporting hardware components (memory, buses, I/O interfaces, and clock circuitry) that together form the computational heart of any embedded device. Formally, the core is the **integrated hardware subsystem** responsible for fetching, decoding, and executing instructions while coordinating all peripheral interactions. In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the core consists of the following fundamental building blocks: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition – Core Components** > The core of an embedded system comprises: > 1. **Processor (Microprocessor / Microcontroller / SoC)** > 2. **Memory Subsystem (Program Memory + Data Memory)** > 3. **System Interconnects (Buses – Data, Address, Control)** > 4. **I/O Peripherals (Timers, ADC/DAC, UART, GPIO, SPI, I2C)** > 5. **Clock and Reset Circuitry** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Think of an embedded system core as the **human nervous system inside a digital watch**: - The **processor** is the **brain** — it makes all decisions. - The **memory** is the **long-term and short-term memory** — storing the program (long-term) and temporary variables (short-term). - The **buses** are the **nerves** — carrying signals (instructions, data, control) between brain and body parts. - The **I/O peripherals** are the **senses and limbs** — interacting with the outside world (display, buttons, sensors). - The **clock** is the **heartbeat** — synchronizing every operation to a precise rhythm. > [!NOTE] > Just as a human cannot think without a brain, an embedded system cannot operate without its core. Removing any one component (memory, clock, or bus) causes complete system failure — a classic KTU viva question! ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Standard Value | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Logic Voltage (TTL) | **5 V** | Older embedded boards | | Logic Voltage (CMOS) | **3.3 V / 1.8 V / 1.2 V** | Modern SoCs, ARM cores | | Clock Frequency Range | **1 MHz – 2 GHz** | Varies with application | | Bus Width | **8 / 16 / 32 / 64 bit** | Determines data throughput | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Major Core Architectures ### A. Microprocessor-Based Core - Contains only the **CPU** on-chip. - Requires **external memory, I/O, and peripherals** to function. - Used in **PCs, high-end industrial controllers**. - Example: Intel 8086, Motorola 68000. ### B. Microcontroller-Based Core - A **single IC** containing CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O + Timers + ADC. - Designed for **control-oriented** applications. - Example: 8051, PIC, AVR, MSP430. ### C. System-on-Chip (SoC) - Integrates **CPU + GPU + Memory Controller + I/O + DSP + Wireless** on a single die. - Used in **smartphones, IoT, automotive infotainment**. - Example: ARM-based SoCs (Qualcomm Snapdragon, NXP i.MX, STM32). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Trend**: Modern KTU questions increasingly focus on **ARM Cortex-M based SoCs**, not legacy 8086/8051 architectures. Always relate examples to ARM cores in answers. ## 2.2 Memory Subsystem – Hierarchical Organization Embedded cores use a strict memory hierarchy based on the **speed–cost–capacity trade-off**: $$\text{Register} \rightarrow \text{Cache (L1, L2)} \rightarrow \text{Primary Memory (RAM/ROM)} \rightarrow \text{Secondary Storage (Flash, SD)}$$ | Memory Type | Volatility | Speed | Use in Embedded Core | |---|---|---|---| | Register | Volatile | Fastest (sub-ns) | ALU operands, PC, SP | | Cache (SRAM) | Volatile | Very Fast | Temporary CPU data | | Primary RAM (SRAM/DRAM) | Volatile | Fast | Run-time variables, stack, heap | | ROM (Mask/EPROM) | Non-volatile | Medium | Boot code, firmware | | Flash / EEPROM | Non-volatile | Slower | Program storage, user data | | External Storage (SD/SSD) | Non-volatile | Slowest | Logs, multimedia | ## 2.3 System Buses – The Communication Backbone The three classical buses in any embedded core are: 1. **Data Bus** – Bidirectional; carries actual data (8/16/32/64 bits wide). 2. **Address Bus** – Unidirectional; carries memory/I/O addresses (e.g., 32-bit → $2^{32} = \textbf{4 GB}$ addressable space). 3. **Control Bus** – Carries timing and command signals (Read/Write, Interrupt, Clock, Reset). ### AMBA Bus Architecture (ARM Standard) ARM's **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)** is the de-facto standard for SoC interconnects: | AMBA Bus | Full Form | Speed | Used For | |---|---|---|---| | **AHB** | Advanced High-performance Bus | High | CPU ↔ RAM, DMA, high-speed peripherals | | **ASB** | Advanced System Bus | Medium | Legacy ARM systems | | **APB** | Advanced Peripheral Bus | Low | UART, GPIO, Timers, I2C, SPI | | **AXI** | Advanced eXtensible Interface | Very High | High-bandwidth, multi-master SoCs | > [!NOTE] > **AMBA Bridge Concept**: A typical SoC uses an **AHB-to-APB bridge** so that the high-speed CPU talks to slow peripherals without bottlenecking the main bus — a frequent 14-mark KTU question! ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | Addressable Memory | $M = 2^{n}$ bytes | $n$ = address bus width (bits) | | Data Throughput | $T = W \times f$ | $W$ = bus width, $f$ = clock | | Instruction Cycle Time | $T_{cyc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | $f_{clk}$ in Hz | | Power Dissipation (dynamic) | $P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$ | CMOS switching power | | MIPS Rating | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | $CPI$ = cycles per instruction | | AMBA Bus Width (AHB) | 32 / 64 / 128 bits | Standard for ARM Cortex-A | | AMBA Bus Width (APB) | 8 / 16 / 32 bits | Standard for peripherals | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world utility**: These formulas are used in **SoC power budgeting** (battery-powered IoT), **real-time system scheduling** (automotive ECU timing), and **memory-mapped I/O design** in industrial controllers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Addressable Memory from Address Bus Width **Problem:** An ARM Cortex-M3 embedded core has a **32-bit address bus**. Calculate the maximum directly addressable memory. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: $$\text{Number of unique addresses} = 2^{n}$$ where $n$ = number of address lines. Substituting $n = 32$: $$M = 2^{32}$$ $$M = 4,294,967,296 \text{ bytes}$$ $$M \approx \textbf{4 GB}$$ **Conversion logic:** Each address line doubles the addressable space. Starting from 1 byte (with 0 address lines), $2^{32}$ gives the maximum. This is why 32-bit processors top out at **4 GB** of address space — a key reason 64-bit ARM Cortex-A exists. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Data Throughput of a System Bus **Problem:** An AHB bus on an ARM SoC runs at **100 MHz** with a **32-bit data width**. Calculate peak throughput. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: $$T = W \times f$$ $$T = 32 \text{ bits} \times 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ transfers/sec}$$ $$T = 3,200 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/sec}$$ $$T = \textbf{3.2 Gbps}$$ Converting to bytes: $$T = \dfrac{3.2 \text{ Gbps}}{8} = \textbf{400 MB/s}$$ **Conversion logic:** Bus width tells us how many bits move per cycle. Frequency tells us cycles per second. Their product is the data rate — a fundamental parameter for **DMA, video streaming, and ADC sampling calculations** in KTU lab exams. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MIPS Performance Rating **Problem:** A microcontroller core runs at **48 MHz** with an average **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) = 1.5**. Calculate its MIPS rating. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: $$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$$ $$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{48 \times 10^{6}}{1.5 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$\text{MIPS} = \textbf{32 MIPS}$$ **Conversion logic:** MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second) is a legacy metric — modern ARM cores quote **DMIPS/MHz** (Dhrystone MIPS) since CPI varies by instruction mix. This is often asked as a **2-mark Part A** question. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Microprocessor Core | Microcontroller Core | SoC Core | |---|---|---|---| | Integration Level | Low (CPU only) | Medium (CPU + Memory + I/O) | Very High (Everything on one die) | | External Components | Many (RAM, ROM, I/O) | Minimal (just power & clock) | None — fully self-contained | | Power Consumption | High (watts) | Low (mW) | Very Low (μW in sleep mode) | | Cost | High | Low | Medium-High | | Clock Speed | 100 MHz – 3 GHz | 1 – 200 MHz | 10 MHz – 2.5 GHz | | Example | Intel i7 | ATmega328 (Arduino) | NXP i.MX8, Snapdragon 845 | | Application | Desktop, Server | Washing machine, ECU | Smartphone, Smartwatch, IoT gateway | | Bus Architecture | PCI, DDR | Internal single bus | AMBA (AHB, APB, AXI) | | KTU Weightage | Low | Medium | **High (favourite topic)** | ## 3.5 Why Modern Cores Use AMBA – The Bottleneck Problem A single shared bus creates a **contention bottleneck** as the number of peripherals grows. AMBA solves this through **layered buses**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CPU} &\leftrightarrow \text{AHB (high-speed backbone)} \\ \text{AHB} &\leftrightarrow \text{APB Bridge} \leftrightarrow \text{APB (slow peripherals)} \end{aligned} $$ The bridge acts as a **protocol translator + speed buffer**, so the high-speed CPU is never stalled by a slow UART or GPIO operation. This hierarchical design is the **cornerstone of all modern ARM-based SoCs** and is a guaranteed 14-mark question in KTU ESE. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Embedded System Core – Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU["CPU CORE
ALU + CU + Registers"] CLK["CLOCK
and RESET"] PROG["PROGRAM MEMORY
ROM / Flash"] DATA["DATA MEMORY
RAM / SRAM"] DBUS["DATA BUS
32 bit"] ABUS["ADDRESS BUS
32 bit"] CBUS["CONTROL BUS
R W INT"] PERI["PERIPHERALS
GPIO UART ADC Timer"] IO["I/O INTERFACE
Sensors and Actuators"] EXT["EXTERNAL
Storage SD eMMC"] CLK --> CPU CPU --> DBUS CPU --> ABUS CPU --> CBUS DBUS --> PROG DBUS --> DATA DBUS --> PERI ABUS --> PROG ABUS --> DATA ABUS --> PERI CBUS --> PROG CBUS --> DATA CBUS --> PERI PERI --> IO CPU --> EXT ``` ## 4.2 AMBA-Based SoC Core Architecture (Detailed) ```mermaid flowchart LR ARM["ARM CORTEX CPU"] AHB["AHB HIGH SPEED BUS"] BRIDGE["AHB to APB BRIDGE"] APB["APB LOW SPEED BUS"] SRAM["SRAM
Tightly Coupled Memory"] FLASH["FLASH
Program Memory"] DMA["DMA CONTROLLER"] TIMER["TIMER and PWM"] UART1["UART"] GPIO1["GPIO"] I2C1["I2C"] SPI1["SPI"] ADC1["ADC and DAC"] EXTMEM["EXTERNAL MEMORY
SDRAM Controller"] ARM --> AHB AHB --> SRAM AHB --> FLASH AHB --> DMA AHB --> EXTMEM AHB --> BRIDGE BRIDGE --> APB APB --> TIMER APB --> UART1 APB --> GPIO1 APB --> I2C1 APB --> SPI1 APB --> ADC1 DMA --> AHB ``` ## 4.3 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TB L0["REGISTERS
Few Bytes
Sub Nanosecond"] L1["L1 CACHE
8 to 64 KB
1 to 2 ns"] L2["L2 CACHE
128 KB to 1 MB
5 to 10 ns"] L3["PRIMARY RAM
SRAM or DRAM
50 to 100 ns"] L4["FLASH PROGRAM MEMORY
128 KB to 2 MB
100 to 200 ns"] L5["EXTERNAL STORAGE
SD or eMMC
Milliseconds"] L0 --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Diagram Tip**: Always label bus widths, clock frequencies, and data flow direction in your exam diagrams. A neat AMBA diagram with **CPU → AHB → Bridge → APB → Peripherals** fetches full marks almost every time. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between Harvard and Von-Neumann architecture in the context of embedded cores.** **Model Answer:** - **Von-Neumann Architecture**: A *single* memory and *single* bus hold both program instructions and data. This causes the **Von-Neumann bottleneck** because instruction fetch and data access cannot occur simultaneously. Example: 8086 microprocessor core. - **Harvard Architecture**: *Separate* program memory and data memory with *separate* buses. Allows **simultaneous** instruction fetch and data access, improving throughput. Example: ARM Cortex-M, 8051, AVR cores. | Parameter | Von-Neumann | Harvard | |---|---|---| | Memory | Unified | Separate | | Buses | Single | Dual (code + data) | | Speed | Slower | Faster | | Complexity | Low | High | | Example | 8086 | ARM Cortex-M3 | --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **List any three differences between a microprocessor and a microcontroller as the core of an embedded system.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Microprocessor | Microcontroller | |---|---|---| | Integration | CPU only | CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O | | External Components | Required | Not required | | Cost | High | Low | | Power | High (W) | Low (mW) | | Application | PC, Server | Washing machine, ECU | | Example | Intel i5 | ATmega328P | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the internal architecture of a **System-on-Chip (SoC)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss how AMBA buses interconnect the components. **(b) [7 Marks]** An embedded core has a **16-bit address bus** and runs at a **20 MHz clock** with an **average CPI of 2.0**. Calculate: (i) Maximum addressable memory. (ii) MIPS rating. (iii) Data throughput assuming a 16-bit data bus. #### Model Solution to Question A: **(a) SoC Internal Architecture & AMBA Interconnect** A System-on-Chip integrates the **entire computer** on a single silicon die. The ARM SoC consists of: 1. **ARM Cortex Core** – The main processor (CPU) with ALU, register bank, and control unit. 2. **Memory Sub-system** – Tightly-Coupled Memory (TCM), internal SRAM, Flash, and external memory controller. 3. **High-Speed Interconnect (AHB / AXI)** – Connects CPU to high-bandwidth peripherals like DMA, external memory. 4. **Bridge (AHB-to-APB)** – Decouples fast and slow buses to avoid bottlenecks. 5. **Low-Speed Peripheral Bus (APB)** – Hosts UART, GPIO, Timers, I2C, SPI, ADC/DAC. 6. **Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** – Manages prioritized interrupts. 7. **Clock & Power Management Unit** – Controls dynamic voltage/frequency scaling. ``` ARM CPU ↔ AHB ↔ SRAM / Flash / DMA ↔ AHB-APB Bridge ↔ APB ↔ Peripherals ``` The **AMBA standard** ensures that the high-speed CPU is not blocked by slow peripherals, leading to deterministic, low-latency operation — a key requirement in **real-time embedded systems**. **(b) Numerical Solutions:** **(i) Maximum Addressable Memory:** $$ \begin{aligned} M &= 2^{n} \\ M &= 2^{16} \\ M &= 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} \\ M &= \textbf{64 KB} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) MIPS Rating:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MIPS} &= \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}} \\ &= \dfrac{20 \times 10^{6}}{2.0 \times 10^{6}} \\ &= \textbf{10 MIPS} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Data Throughput:** $$ \begin{aligned} T &= W \times f \\ T &= 16 \text{ bits} \times 20 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} \\ T &= 320 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/sec} \\ T &= 320 \text{ Mbps} = \textbf{40 MB/s} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **memory hierarchy** in an embedded system core. Justify why registers are the fastest and external storage the slowest. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **AHB and APB** buses of the AMBA protocol. List at least four differences. Why is a bridge required between them? #### Model Solution to Question B: **(a) Memory Hierarchy in an Embedded Core** The hierarchy (top to bottom in speed, bottom to top in capacity): 1. **Registers** (inside CPU) – Smallest, fastest, accessed in 1 cycle. 2. **Cache (L1, L2)** – SRAM-based, holds recently used data/instructions. 3. **Primary Memory (SRAM/DRAM)** – Holds running program and variables. 4. **Secondary Memory (Flash, EEPROM)** – Non-volatile program storage. 5. **External Storage (SD, eMMC)** – Bulk data, logs, multimedia. *Justification:* Speed inversely correlates with capacity. Registers are physically close to the ALU (in nanometer distance), reducing signal propagation delay. External storage is accessed via I/O controllers and slow buses, hence milliseconds-level latency. This is the **fundamental trade-off** in core design. **(b) AHB vs APB Comparison:** | Feature | AHB | APB | |---|---|---| | Full Form | Advanced High-performance Bus | Advanced Peripheral Bus | | Speed | High (100+ MHz) | Low (typically <50 MHz) | | Bus Width | 32 / 64 / 128 bits | 8 / 16 / 32 bits | | Pipelining | Yes | No | | Burst Transfers | Supported | Not supported | | Masters | Multi-master (CPU, DMA) | Single master (bridge) | | Peripherals | DMA, RAM, External Memory | UART, GPIO, Timers, I2C | | Power | Higher | Lower | **Why a Bridge is Required:** - **Protocol Conversion:** AHB is pipelined and burst-capable; APB is simple and un-pipelined. - **Speed Decoupling:** The bridge isolates the slow APB peripherals from the fast AHB, preventing the CPU from waiting for slow I/O. - **Address Mapping:** The bridge maps APB peripherals into a fixed address region of the AHB address space. - **Power Gating:** APB peripherals can be clock-gated independently to save power — critical in battery-operated IoT devices. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > - **Do NOT** confuse **address bus width** with **data bus width**. Many students write $2^{32}$ for data — losing 2 marks. > - **Do NOT** state that all embedded cores use Von-Neumann architecture. Modern ARM Cortex-M uses **modified Harvard** — note this in answers. > - In MIPS calculations, students forget to **divide by $10^6$** — losing 1 mark. > - In AMBA diagrams, always show the **AHB-to-APB bridge explicitly** — it's the most-skipped component in answer sheets. > - For SoC vs Microcontroller, **never** write that SoC = Microcontroller. SoC includes GPU, DSP, wireless — be precise. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ⭐ The **core** = Processor + Memory + Buses + I/O + Clock/Reset. - ⭐ **Microprocessor** = CPU only; needs external memory. **Microcontroller** = CPU + Memory + I/O on-chip. **SoC** = entire system on one die. - ⭐ ARM Cortex-M cores use **modified Harvard architecture** with separate code/data buses. - ⭐ The **three classic buses** are: Data (bi-directional), Address (uni-directional), Control (timing/command). - ⭐ **AMBA standard** has **AHB (fast)** and **APB (slow)** — connected via a **bridge**. - ⭐ Addressable memory formula: $M = 2^n$ bytes, where $n$ = address bus width. - ⭐ Data throughput: $T = W \times f$ (bits/sec). - ⭐ MIPS rating: $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^6}$. - ⭐ **Memory hierarchy** flows: Register → Cache → RAM → Flash → External Storage. - ⭐ **Volatile memory (RAM)** loses data on power-off; **Non-volatile (Flash/ROM)** retains it. - ⭐ Modern KTU questions focus on **ARM Cortex-M3/M4 SoCs**, not 8085/8086. - ⭐ Always draw the **AHB-to-APB bridge** explicitly in SoC diagrams. - ⭐ A **32-bit address bus** → max **4 GB** addressable memory. Memorize this. - ⭐ For real-time embedded systems, **deterministic bus latency** (via AMBA) is more important than raw throughput. - ⭐ KTU loves asking: *"Why is Harvard faster than Von-Neumann?"* — answer: **simultaneous instruction + data fetch**.

topics.title: Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Memory # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Embedded Systems**, **Memory** refers to the physical and logical storage subsystem used by the microcontroller or microprocessor to store executable code, runtime data, configuration parameters, and intermediate computational results. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framing, embedded memory is characterized by **deterministic access behaviour**, **non-volatility** properties (where required), **predictable timing**, and **constrained physical footprint**. The embedded memory subsystem is broadly classified into: - **Program Memory (Code Memory)** — stores the firmware/instructions; typically non-volatile. - **Data Memory (Working Memory)** — stores runtime variables, stack, and heap; typically volatile. - **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — mapped control/status registers. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Embedded memory study must cover memory types, memory hierarchy, memory-mapped I/O versus I/O-mapped I/O, address decoding, cache considerations in embedded processors, and endianness. These form the foundation for later modules on interfacing and RTOS. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine your **study desk at home**: - The **open notebook on your desk** (the one you are actively writing into) is like **Registers** — the fastest, smallest, and directly accessed by your "processor" (your brain). - The **stack of textbooks kept within arm's reach** is like **Cache (L1/L2)** — slightly slower, but still quick to fetch. - The **bookshelf in your room** is like **Main Memory (RAM/ROM)** — large, but you must stand up to retrieve data. - The **library downstairs or your godown (storage)** is like **Secondary Storage (Flash/HDD/SD Card)** — very large but takes significant time. Just as you strategically arrange your desk for *speed* vs *capacity*, an embedded system architect picks memory based on **speed, volatility, cost, and power** — all critical in resource-constrained embedded platforms. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following **standard metrics** govern any embedded memory analysis: - **Access Time ($t_A$):** Time taken to read/write a single memory location (in **nanoseconds, ns**). - **Cycle Time ($t_C$):** Minimum time between successive memory operations. - **Bandwidth ($BW$):** Maximum data transfer rate (in **MB/s** or **GB/s**). - **Latency ($L$):** Delay between request initiation and data availability. - **Hit Ratio ($H$):** Probability that requested data is found in the relevant memory level ($0 \le H \le 1$). - **Miss Penalty:** Time penalty incurred on a cache miss. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Memory Performance Equation:** > $$t_{avg} = H \cdot t_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot t_{main}$$ > This **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** is a recurring KTU examination question pattern. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Embedded Memory Embedded memory is organized along two primary axes: **Volatility** and **Mutability**. ### A. Volatile Memory (loses data on power-off) - **SRAM (Static RAM):** Uses 6 transistors per bit. Fast ($\sim$ **1–10 ns**), expensive, low density. Used for **Cache, Registers**. - **DRAM (Dynamic RAM):** Uses 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit. Requires periodic **refresh**. Higher density, cheaper. Used for main RAM. - **SDRAM / DDR SDRAM:** Synchronous DRAM, used in high-performance embedded SoCs (e.g., Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone). ### B. Non-Volatile Memory (retains data without power) - **ROM (Read-Only Memory):** Mask-programmed at factory; cannot be modified. - **PROM (Programmable ROM):** One-time programmable by user. - **EPROM (Erasable PROM):** Erasable via **UV light** through quartz window. - **EEPROM (Electrically EPROM):** Byte-level erase/write, $\sim$ **1 million cycles**, used for configuration storage. - **Flash Memory:** Block-level erase, two subtypes: - **NOR Flash** — random access, execute-in-place (XIP), used for code storage. - **NAND Flash** — sequential access, high density, used for data storage (SD cards, eMMC). > [!IMPORTANT] > **NOR vs NAND Flash:** NOR allows random byte access and **Execute-In-Place (XIP)** — meaning the CPU can fetch instructions directly from flash without copying to RAM. NAND is block-oriented and used as a data store. KTU frequently tests this distinction. ## 2.2 Memory Hierarchy Embedded systems use a strict memory hierarchy based on the trade-off between **speed, cost, and capacity**: | Level | Type | Access Time | Capacity | Cost / bit | |-------|------|------------|----------|------------| | L0 | CPU Registers | $\sim$ **1 ns** | Bytes | Highest | | L1 | L1 Cache (SRAM) | $\sim$ **2–5 ns** | 16–64 KB | High | | L2 | L2 Cache (SRAM) | $\sim$ **5–20 ns** | 256 KB – few MB | Medium-High | | L3 | Main Memory (DRAM/SDRAM) | $\sim$ **50–100 ns** | MB – GB | Medium | | L4 | Storage (Flash/SSD) | $\sim$ **µs–ms** | GB – TB | Lowest | ## 2.3 Memory-Mapped I/O vs I/O-Mapped I/O | Feature | Memory-Mapped I/O | I/O-Mapped I/O (Port-Mapped) | |---------|-------------------|-------------------------------| | Address Space | Shared with memory | Separate I/O address space | | Instructions | Any memory instruction (LOAD/STORE) | Special IN/OUT instructions | | Address Space Size | Reduced | Full memory space preserved | | Speed | Faster (single instruction) | Slower (special bus cycles) | | Example | ARM, 8051 (mostly) | x86 (IN, OUT) | For **8051 microcontroller** (a KTU-favoured architecture): - **DATA memory:** 128 bytes internal RAM (00H–7FH). - **SFR area:** 128 bytes (80H–FFH) accessed via direct addressing. - **CODE memory:** Up to 64 KB (0000H–FFFFH) for program. - **XDATA memory:** Up to 64 KB external data memory. ## 2.4 Cache Memory Mapping Techniques | Mapping Type | Description | Conflict Misses | |--------------|-------------|-----------------| | **Direct Mapping** | Each main memory block maps to exactly one cache line | High | | **Fully Associative** | Any block can map to any cache line | None (only capacity) | | **Set-Associative (k-way)** | Block maps to one of *k* lines in a specific set | Reduced | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **board-tested and high-yield**. | # | Formula | Description | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | $t_{avg} = H \cdot t_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot t_{main}$ | Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) | | 2 | $t_{avg} = t_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot t_{miss\,penalty}$ | AMAT with explicit miss penalty | | 3 | $\text{Number of cache lines} = \dfrac{\text{Cache size}}{\text{Line size}}$ | Cache line count | | 4 | $\text{Number of sets} = \dfrac{\text{Cache size}}{\text{Line size} \times \text{Associativity}}$ | Set count for k-way set associative | | 5 | $\text{Tag bits} = \text{Address bits} - \text{Index bits} - \text{Block offset bits}$ | Address breakdown | | 6 | $\text{Block offset bits} = \log_2(\text{Line size in bytes})$ | Offset calculation | | 7 | $\text{Index bits} = \log_2(\text{Number of sets})$ | Index calculation | | 8 | $BW = \dfrac{\text{Data transferred (bytes)}}{t_{transfer}\,(\text{s})}$ | Memory bandwidth | | 9 | $\text{Hit Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Number of hits}}{\text{Total accesses}}$ | Hit ratio | | 10 | $\text{Address pins required} = \log_2(\text{Memory size in bytes})$ | Address bus width | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) **Problem Context:** A system has an L1 cache with access time $t_{L1} = 5$ ns and main memory with access time $t_{M} = 100$ ns. The cache hit ratio is $H = 0.9$. Compute AMAT. ### Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters $$t_{L1} = 5 \text{ ns}, \quad t_M = 100 \text{ ns}, \quad H = 0.9$$ ### Step 2 — State the AMAT Formula The average memory access time combines hit time and miss penalty: $$t_{avg} = H \cdot t_{L1} + (1 - H) \cdot t_M$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Values $$ \begin{aligned} t_{avg} &= 0.9 \times 5 \text{ ns} + (1 - 0.9) \times 100 \text{ ns} \\ &= 0.9 \times 5 + 0.1 \times 100 \\ &= 4.5 + 10 \\ &= 14.5 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Interpret Result > The AMAT is **14.5 ns**, dominated by the miss penalty. This demonstrates why **increasing hit ratio** has a much larger impact than reducing $t_{L1}$ marginally. --- ## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Cache Address Breakdown (Set-Associative) **Problem:** A 32 KB cache is **4-way set associative** with a line size of **64 bytes**. The main memory is **4 GB** and byte-addressable. Determine the tag, index, and block-offset bit fields. ### Step 1 — Compute Total Address Bits Main memory = $4 \text{ GB} = 2^{32}$ bytes. So, total physical address bits = **32 bits**. ### Step 2 — Compute Block Offset Bits $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Line size} &= 64 \text{ bytes} = 2^6 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Block offset bits} &= \log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Number of Sets $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total lines} &= \frac{\text{Cache size}}{\text{Line size}} = \frac{32 \text{ KB}}{64 \text{ B}} = \frac{32768}{64} = 512 \text{ lines} \\ \text{Number of sets} &= \frac{\text{Total lines}}{\text{Associativity}} = \frac{512}{4} = 128 \text{ sets} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Index Bits $$ \text{Index bits} = \log_2(128) = 7 \text{ bits} $$ ### Step 5 — Compute Tag Bits $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Tag bits} &= \text{Total address bits} - \text{Index bits} - \text{Block offset bits} \\ &= 32 - 7 - 6 = 19 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Final Address Decomposition | Field | Tag | Index | Block Offset | |-------|-----|-------|---------------| | **Bit width** | 19 | 7 | 6 | | **Bits used** | [31:13] | [12:6] | [5:0] | --- ## 3.3 Worked Derivation: Memory Decoding using 74LS138 **Problem:** Design address decoding for a **16 KB ROM** and **16 KB RAM** in an 8085 system with address lines A15–A0. ### Step 1 — Memory Map Requirements ROM: 16 KB $\Rightarrow$ $2^{14}$ locations $\Rightarrow$ A15 = 0, A14 = 0. RAM: 16 KB $\Rightarrow$ $2^{14}$ locations $\Rightarrow$ A15 = 0, A14 = 1. ### Step 2 — Range Computation $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ROM range} &= 0000\text{H} \text{ to } 3\text{FFF}\text{H} \\ \text{RAM range} &= 4000\text{H} \text{ to } 7\text{FFF}\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Address Pin Count for Decoder $$ \text{Number of address pins needed} = \log_2(\text{Total memory}) = \log_2(64 \text{ KB}) = 16 $$ A **3-to-8 decoder (74LS138)** can decode A15, A14, A13 to generate 8 chip-select lines, each covering 8 KB. For 16 KB chips, use **Y0̄ + Y1̄** combined for ROM, and **Y2̄ + Y3̄** combined for RAM. --- ## 3.4 Endianness — A Numerical Example Consider storing the **32-bit value 0x12345678** at address 0x1000: | Address | Big-Endian | Little-Endian | |---------|-----------|---------------| | 0x1000 | 0x12 (MSB) | 0x78 (LSB) | | 0x1001 | 0x34 | 0x56 | | 0x1002 | 0x56 | 0x34 | | 0x1003 | 0x78 (LSB) | 0x12 (MSB) | **Embedded examples:** ARM cores are **bi-endian** (configurable); 8051 is **big-endian** for some variants; x86 is **little-endian**. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid (Block Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CPU REGISTERS
Access ~1 ns
Bytes"] --> B["L1 CACHE SRAM
Access 2 to 5 ns
16 to 64 KB"] B --> C["L2 CACHE SRAM
Access 5 to 20 ns
256 KB to 4 MB"] C --> D["MAIN MEMORY DRAM
Access 50 to 100 ns
MB to GB"] D --> E["SECONDARY FLASH
Access microseconds
GB to TB"] A -.->|Slow path on miss| E ``` > [!NOTE] > This pyramid illustrates the **principle of locality** — programs access a small subset of memory frequently. Designers exploit this by placing hot data in faster (smaller) tiers. ## 4.2 Memory-Mapped vs Isolated I/O Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU["CPU Core"] REG["General Purpose Registers"] ALU["ALU"] end REG --> BUS["System Address and Data Bus"] BUS --> MEM["Memory Space
0000 to FFFF"] BUS --> MMIO["Peripherals
Mapped in same address space"] BUS -.->|Isolated I_O only| PORT["Separate I_O Bus
IN and OUT instructions"] ``` ## 4.3 Direct-Mapped Cache Lookup Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CPU Issues Address"] --> B["Split Address into Tag Index Offset"] B --> C["Index selects one cache line"] C --> D{"Valid bit set?"} D -->|No| E["CACHE MISS
Fetch from main memory"] D -->|Yes| F{"Stored Tag matches?"} F -->|No| E F -->|Yes| G["CACHE HIT
Return data using offset"] E --> H["Update cache line"] H --> G ``` ## 4.4 8085 Memory Map with Decoded Regions ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MEMMAP["8085 Address Space 64 KB"] ROM["ROM 16 KB
0000H to 3FFFH"] RAM["RAM 16 KB
4000H to 7FFFH"] IO1["Peripheral 1
8 KB 8000H to 9FFFH"] IO2["Peripheral 2
8 KB A000H to BFFFH"] FREE["Unused 32 KB
C000H to FFFFH"] end ``` ## 4.5 Flash Memory Read/Write Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Issue READ command 0x03 plus address"] --> B["Flash drives data onto bus"] C["Issue WRITE ENABLE 0x06"] --> D["Issue PAGE PROGRAM 0x02 plus data"] D --> E["Flash enters programming state"] E --> F["Internal write takes 1 to 5 ms"] F --> G["Status register polled for BUSY bit clear"] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Differentiate between **NOR Flash** and **NAND Flash** in terms of access mechanism, density, and typical embedded application. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | NOR Flash | NAND Flash | |-----------|-----------|------------| | Access | Random byte access | Sequential/page-based | | Execute-In-Place (XIP) | Supported | Not supported | | Density | Lower | Higher | | Cost/bit | Higher | Lower | | Typical use | Code storage (firmware boot) | Data storage (files, multimedia) | | Interface | SRAM-like bus | I/O-like, command-based | **Conclusion:** NOR is preferred for **boot code and XIP**, NAND for **mass data storage**. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** Define **Memory-Mapped I/O**. State one advantage and one disadvantage. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition:** Memory-Mapped I/O is a scheme in which I/O devices are addressed using the **same address bus** as memory; peripheral registers appear as memory locations. **Advantage:** Any memory-access instruction (LOAD/STORE) can be used to access I/O → **simpler programming**. **Disadvantage:** I/O devices consume part of the memory address space, **reducing available memory range**. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **memory hierarchy** in an embedded system. Why is hierarchy necessary? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A system has an **L1 cache** with access time **2 ns** and a hit ratio of **0.95**. Main memory access time is **80 ns**. Calculate the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**. If the hit ratio drops to 0.85, recompute AMAT and comment. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): The memory hierarchy consists of **multiple levels** with decreasing speed and increasing capacity: **Level 0 – CPU Registers:** Fastest ($\sim$ 1 ns), smallest (bytes), directly manipulated by the ALU. **Level 1 – L1 Cache (SRAM):** Small ($\sim$ 16–64 KB), very fast ($\sim$ 2–5 ns). Split into I-cache and D-cache in modern cores. **Level 2 – L2 Cache (SRAM):** Larger (256 KB – few MB), slightly slower ($\sim$ 5–20 ns). May be unified (instruction + data). **Level 3 – Main Memory (DRAM/SDRAM):** Large (MB–GB), access time $\sim$ 50–100 ns. Requires periodic refresh. **Level 4 – Secondary Storage (Flash/SSD):** Largest (GB–TB), slowest (µs–ms). Non-volatile. **Why Hierarchy is Necessary:** 1. **Principle of Locality** — temporal (recently accessed items likely reused) and spatial (nearby items likely accessed). 2. **Cost-Performance Trade-off** — registers and SRAM are fast but expensive per bit; DRAM and Flash are cheap but slow. 3. **Determinism** — embedded real-time systems require predictable timing; careful memory placement guarantees bounds. 4. **Power Efficiency** — accessing smaller memories consumes less energy — critical for battery-powered IoT devices. A hierarchical arrangement **balances speed, capacity, cost, and power**. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Given:** $t_{L1} = 2 \text{ ns}$, $t_M = 80 \text{ ns}$, $H = 0.95$. **AMAT Formula:** $$t_{avg} = H \cdot t_{L1} + (1 - H) \cdot t_M$$ **Case 1: H = 0.95** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{avg} &= 0.95 \times 2 + (1 - 0.95) \times 80 \\ &= 1.9 + 0.05 \times 80 \\ &= 1.9 + 4.0 \\ &= 5.9 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Case 2: H = 0.85** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{avg} &= 0.85 \times 2 + (1 - 0.85) \times 80 \\ &= 1.7 + 0.15 \times 80 \\ &= 1.7 + 12.0 \\ &= 13.7 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Comment:** A 10% drop in hit ratio (from 95% to 85%) caused AMAT to more than **double** (from 5.9 ns to 13.7 ns — a $\sim$**132% increase**). This demonstrates that **hit ratio dominates** performance more than cache access time itself. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **(a)** Compare **Von Neumann** and **Harvard architectures**. Which is preferred in modern DSP/embedded systems and why? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **32 KB**, **4-way set-associative** cache has a **line size of 32 bytes**. The main memory size is **16 MB** and is byte-addressable. Compute: (i) Number of sets, (ii) Tag bits, (iii) Index bits, (iv) Offset bits. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): | Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard | |---------|-------------|---------| | Bus structure | Single address + data bus (shared) | Separate buses for instructions and data | | Fetch & Execute | Cannot overlap (fetch and data share bus) | Can overlap (simultaneous instruction fetch + data access) | | Bottleneck | **Von Neumann bottleneck** | No such bottleneck | | Hardware complexity | Simpler, cheaper | More complex, costlier | | Determinism | Less deterministic | More deterministic | | Power | Lower | Slightly higher due to dual buses | | Examples | 8086, ARM7 (von Neumann mode) | 8051, DSPs, ARM Cortex-M (modified Harvard) | **Preference in modern embedded/DSP systems:** Harvard (or Modified Harvard) is preferred because: 1. **Deterministic instruction fetch timing** — vital for real-time systems. 2. **Parallel fetch and data access** — improves throughput (single-cycle MAC operations in DSPs). 3. **Separate code protection** — code space can be marked read-only in hardware. 4. **Higher bandwidth** — concurrent access to two memories doubles effective bus bandwidth. **Examples:** TMS320C55x DSPs, ARM Cortex-M3/M4, AVR, PIC, 8051 — all use Harvard or Modified Harvard. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Given:** - Cache size = 32 KB - Associativity = 4-way - Line size = 32 bytes - Main memory = 16 MB = $2^{24}$ bytes **Total address bits** = $\log_2(16 \text{ MB}) = \log_2(2^{24}) = 24 \text{ bits}$ **(i) Number of sets:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total cache lines} &= \frac{32 \text{ KB}}{32 \text{ B}} = \frac{32768}{32} = 1024 \text{ lines} \\ \text{Number of sets} &= \frac{1024}{4} = 256 \text{ sets} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Index bits:** $$\text{Index bits} = \log_2(256) = 8 \text{ bits}$$ **(iii) Offset bits:** $$\text{Offset bits} = \log_2(32) = 5 \text{ bits}$$ **(iv) Tag bits:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Tag bits} &= \text{Total bits} - \text{Index bits} - \text{Offset bits} \\ &= 24 - 8 - 5 \\ &= 11 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Summary Table:** | Field | Tag | Index | Offset | |-------|-----|-------|--------| | **Bits** | 11 | 8 | 5 | | **Position** | [23:13] | [12:5] | [4:0] | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Many students confuse **cache size with line size** when computing sets. Always verify: *Total cache size ÷ line size ÷ associativity = number of sets*. > - For **byte-addressable** memory, **offset = $\log_2$(line size in bytes)**, not bits. > - **Endianness** questions are commonly missed — always clarify whether data is stored MSB-first or LSB-first. > - In **AMAT problems**, do not add $t_{L1}$ and $t_M$ directly — you must **weight by hit and miss probabilities**. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST - **Memory types:** SRAM (fast, expensive, no refresh), DRAM (refresh needed, dense), NOR Flash (XIP, code), NAND Flash (data, sequential), EEPROM (byte-erase, config). - **Hierarchy rule:** *Smaller & faster as you go up; larger & cheaper as you go down.* - **AMAT formula is mandatory** — memorize $t_{avg} = H \cdot t_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot t_{miss}$. - **Cache address breakdown:** Total address = **Tag + Index + Offset**. - **Set count** = Cache size ÷ Line size ÷ Associativity. - **Memory-mapped I/O** shares the address bus; **I/O-mapped** uses special IN/OUT instructions (x86 style). - **Big-endian** stores MSB at lowest address; **little-endian** stores LSB at lowest address. - **Von Neumann** = single bus (bottleneck); **Harvard** = dual bus (parallel fetch + data). - **NOR Flash supports XIP**, NAND does not. - **Hit ratio improvements** yield far greater AMAT gains than marginal $t_{cache}$ reductions. - **Address pins** required = $\log_2(\text{memory size})$. - **Refresh** is required only for DRAM — not SRAM, NOR, or NAND. - **EEPROM** byte-erasable, slower write cycles; **Flash** block-erasable, faster bulk writes. - **KTU-favourite MCQ topics:** SRAM transistor count (6T), DRAM (1T+1C), refresh cycle, endianness, XIP. ---

topics.title: Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensors and Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Sensor?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A **sensor** is a physical device or transducer that detects, measures, and converts a physical, chemical, or biological quantity (such as temperature, pressure, light, humidity, motion, or chemical composition) from the real world into an equivalent electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or digital word) that can be read, processed, and interpreted by an embedded system, microcontroller, or processor.

**Intuitive Analogy (The "Five Senses" of an Embedded System):**
