## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the internal architecture of the 8051 microcontroller with a neat block diagram. List the functions of the **Program Counter (PC)**, **Stack Pointer (SP)**, and **Data Pointer (DPTR)**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss in detail **five reasons why the 8051 is widely used as a teaching platform** for embedded systems in universities, citing the syllabus relevance to KTU 2024 Scheme course outcomes. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Internal Architecture of 8051 (Block Diagram — 3 Marks):**

Refer to the **block diagram drawn in Section 4.1** of these notes. The key internal units are:
- **CPU & ALU** (8-bit, performs arithmetic, logic, and boolean operations)
- **Program Counter (PC)** — 16-bit, holds address of next instruction
- **Accumulator (A)** — 8-bit, primary ALU operand
- **B Register** — 8-bit, used in MUL/DIV
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** — 8-bit, points to top of stack in internal RAM
- **Data Pointer (DPTR)** — 16-bit, used to access external/data memory
- **Internal RAM (128 B)** — registers, bit-addressable area, general-purpose RAM
- **SFR Block (128 B)** — controls all peripherals (ports, timers, UART, interrupts)
- **Internal ROM (4 KB)** — program code storage
- **Four 8-bit I/O Ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3)
- **Two 16-bit Timers** (T0, T1)
- **Serial Port (UART)**
- **Interrupt Controller** (5 sources, 2 priority levels)
- **Oscillator & Clock Circuit**

**Functions of PC, SP, DPTR (4 Marks):**

1. **Program Counter (PC):**
   - 16-bit register that holds the **memory address of the next instruction** to be fetched.
   - Range: $0000H$ to $FFFFH$ (64 KB).
   - Auto-incremented after each instruction fetch: $PC \leftarrow PC + N$ where $N$ is the instruction length in bytes.

2. **Stack Pointer (SP):**
   - 8-bit register pointing to the top of the **stack in internal RAM**.
   - **Reset value:** $07H$ (stack starts at RAM location $08H$).
   - Increments on PUSH: $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$, then data is stored at the new SP address.
   - Decrements on POP: data is read from SP, then $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$.
   - The stack is used for **storing return addresses** during subroutine CALLs and interrupt servicing.

3. **Data Pointer (DPTR):**
   - 16-bit register composed of **DPH (high byte) and DPL (low byte)** — both are SFRs.
   - Used to **access external data memory (XDATA)** and **look-up tables in program memory**.
   - Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` reads a byte from code memory at address $(A + DPTR)$.
   - Example: `MOVX A, @DPTR` reads a byte from external data memory at address DPTR.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Five Reasons Why 8051 is the Standard Teaching Platform (7 Marks — 1.4 marks each):**

1. **Architectural Clarity:**
   The 8051's **modified Harvard architecture** is the simplest commercial embodiment of a true Harvard design. It cleanly separates code and data spaces, making it easy for students to understand the difference between instruction fetch and data access — a foundational concept that applies to ALL modern MCUs (PIC, AVR, ARM).

2. **Complete On-Chip Integration:**
   The 8051 contains **every essential peripheral** (RAM, ROM, I/O, timers, UART, interrupts) on one chip. Students can build complete working systems (e.g., a digital thermometer, motor controller) **without external memory or glue logic**, focusing on application logic rather than hardware interfacing.

3. **Rich, Expressive Instruction Set (CISC):**
   The 8051's 111 opcodes with 5 addressing modes (Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed) provide a complete vocabulary for teaching **assembly language programming concepts** — including bit manipulation, indexed addressing, and subroutine linkage.

4. **Mature Toolchain and Documentation:**
   Tools like **Keil µVision**, **Proteus VSM**, and **SDCC** are freely available for educational use. Abundant textbooks (Mazidi, Ayala, Subrata Ghoshal) align closely with KTU syllabus outcomes, ensuring exam preparation is well-supported.

5. **Industry Relevance and Portability of Skills:**
   The 8051 core is **still shipping in billions of units** annually (in appliances, automotive, IoT). Learning 8051 builds the conceptual foundation for transitioning to advanced 32-bit MCUs (ARM Cortex-M) and modern SoCs. Concepts like SFRs, interrupt vectors, and timer modes are **universal** across all MCU families.

6. **Direct Mapping to KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (CO2 & CO3):**
   The KTU 2024 syllabus for PECST746 dedicates **Modules 2, 3, 4, and 5** to the 8051 — covering architecture, programming, peripherals, and interfacing. Studying the 8051 directly enables students to achieve **CO2** (analyze architecture) and **CO3** (develop assembly/C programs), which carry significant weight in university exams.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Harvard memory organization** of the 8051. Explain the role of **MOV, MOVC, and MOVX** instructions in accessing the three memory spaces. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare the 8051 with the **PIC 16F877A** and **AVR ATmega328** microcontrollers under the following heads: (i) Architecture, (ii) Instruction set type, (iii) Clock cycles per instruction, (iv) Bit-manipulation capability, (v) Industry/academic adoption. Justify why the 8051 is preferred for KTU syllabus. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Harvard Memory Organization (3 Marks — Diagram):**

The 8051 uses a **modified Harvard architecture** with **two separate address spaces** connected to the CPU by distinct buses. (Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.3** of these notes.)

1. **Program Memory Space (CODE Space):**
   - 16-bit address bus → $2^{16} = 64$ KB capacity.
   - Addresses: $0000H$ to $FFFFH$.
   - Internal ROM occupies $0000H$ to $0FFFH$ (4 KB); remaining 60 KB is external.
   - Accessed using the **Program Counter (PC)** during instruction fetch.

2. **Data Memory Space (DATA Space):**
   - 16-bit address bus → $2^{16} = 64$ KB capacity.
   - Lower 128 B is **Internal RAM** ($00H$ to $7FH$) — Register banks, bit-addressable area, user RAM.
   - Upper 128 B is **SFR space** ($80H$ to $FFH$) — peripheral control registers.
   - External 64 KB (XDATA) accessed via DPTR.

**Role of MOV, MOVC, MOVX (4 Marks):**

| Instruction | Mnemonic Expansion | Memory Space Accessed | Bus Used |
|-------------|--------------------|-----------------------|----------|
| `MOV` | Move (internal) | Internal RAM + SFRs | Internal 8-bit data bus |
| `MOVC` | Move Code | Program memory (look-up table) | Program read bus |
| `MOVX` | Move External | External data memory (XDATA, 64 KB) | External 16-bit address + 8-bit data bus |

- **MOV examples:** `MOV A, R0`; `MOV P1, A`; `MOV 30H, #55H`
- **MOVC example:** `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (read a constant from code memory — used for look-up tables like 7-segment decoders)
- **MOVX examples:** `MOVX A, @DPTR` (read external RAM); `MOVX @R0, A` (write to external RAM)

> **Exam Tip:** `MOVC` accesses **code memory** (read-only), while `MOVX` accesses **external data memory** (read/write). This distinction is **frequently asked** and is a major source of lost marks.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Parameter | **8051** | **PIC 16F877A** | **AVR ATmega328** |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| (i) Architecture | Modified Harvard | Harvard (true) | Modified Harvard |
| (ii) Instruction set | CISC (111 opcodes) | RISC (35 instructions) | RISC (~130 instructions) |
| (iii) Clock cycles / instruction | **12** | 4 | **1** |
| (iv) Bit manipulation | **Native, extensive** (bit-addressable RAM + SFRs) | Limited (BSF, BCF instructions) | Limited (SBI, CBI instructions) |
| (v) Industry adoption | Consumer electronics, automotive | Motor control, power electronics | Arduino ecosystem, hobbyist |
| Academic/KTU adoption | **Primary MCU in PECST746** | Mentioned for comparison | Not in KTU syllabus |

**Justification — Why 8051 is Preferred for KTU (2 Marks):**

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECST746 (Embedded Systems)** dedicates four full modules (2, 3, 4, 5) to the 8051, covering its architecture, assembly programming, timers/UART, and interfacing. The 8051's **CISC instruction set** allows KTU examiners to test a **wider range of programming concepts** (addressing modes, bit-manipulation, indexed look-up tables) within a single architecture. Furthermore, the **mature Keil toolchain** is freely available to students, and standard textbooks (Mazidi's *The 8051 Microcontroller and Embedded Systems*) are mapped 1:1 to KTU module outcomes. The 8051's **standardized nomenclature** (SFRs, vectors, register banks) is also taught in later subjects, ensuring **vertical curriculum integration**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MOV, MOVC, MOVX** — losing 2-3 marks per question if not clearly distinguished.
> 2. **Forgetting that 8051 uses 12 clocks per machine cycle** (not 4 like PIC, not 1 like AVR) — a classic 3-mark trap question.
> 3. **Writing the 8051 has 256 bytes of RAM** — this is WRONG; it has 128 B internal RAM + 128 B SFR space = 256 B addressable, but only 128 B is true user RAM.
> 4. **Not specifying bus widths** in the block diagram — always annotate "(16-bit)" for address buses and "(8-bit)" for data buses.
> 5. **Listing features without KTU/CO mapping** — examiners award extra credit for linking answers to course outcomes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Why 8051 Microcontroller" (KTU PECST746 Module 2)**

- [x] The 8051 is an **8-bit, CISC, modified Harvard architecture** single-chip microcontroller.
- [x] **Origin:** Intel, 1980 (MCS-51 family).
- [x] **On-chip resources:** 4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM, 4×8-bit I/O ports, 2×16-bit timers, 1 UART, 5 interrupts, 2 priority levels.
- [x] **Memory spaces:** Code (64 KB) + Data (64 KB) — two **separate** address spaces.
- [x] **Three move instructions:** `MOV` (internal), `MOVC` (code), `MOVX` (external).
- [x] **Bit-addressable memory:** 16 bytes of internal RAM (20H–2FH) and 16 SFRs = **256 individually addressable bits**.
- [x] **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** (key timing fact).
- [x] **Reset values:** PC = $0000H$, SP = $07H$, ACC = $00H$, PSW = $00H$, P0–P3 = $FFH$.
- [x] **Crystal frequency for UART:** **11.0592 MHz** is standard (gives integer reload values for 9600 baud).
- [x] **Reasons for academic adoption:** Complete integration, mature toolchain, rich CISC instruction set, KTU syllabus alignment, industry longevity, foundational learning for ARM migration.
- [x] **Comparison to remember:** 8051 = 12 clocks/instr, PIC = 4 clocks/instr, AVR = 1 clock/instr.
- [x] **Modern derivatives:** AT89C51, AT89S52, C8051Fxxx, CC2530 — still shipping in billions of units.
- [x] **Toolchain:** **Keil µVision** (industry standard), **SDCC** (open-source).
- [x] **Direct KTU CO mapping:** This topic maps to **CO2** (Analyze 8051 architecture) — high-weightage in Part B questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For any "Why 8051?" KTU question, structure your answer in **three layers**:
> 1. **Definition layer** — 1 mark (what is a microcontroller + what is 8051).
> 2. **Feature layer** — 3-5 marks (tabular listing of on-chip resources).
> 3. **Justification layer** — remaining marks (Harvard architecture, CISC, toolchain maturity, syllabus alignment, industry relevance).
> This structure guarantees full marks if every layer is addressed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The 8052 Microcontroller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The 8052 Microcontroller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **8052 Microcontroller** is an enhanced, **8-bit**, single-chip CMOS microcontroller belonging to the **MCS-51 (Intel 8051) family**, originally manufactured by Intel and widely second-sourced by Philips, Atmel (now Microchip), and Dallas/Maxim. It is functionally a **superset of the 8051**, retaining complete binary-level instruction compatibility while offering a larger on-chip program memory, expanded on-chip data RAM, an additional third timer, and a duplicated SFR set in the upper 128 bytes of internal RAM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8052 is treated in PECST746 as the **reference 8051-family MCU** for understanding architectural fundamentals, memory mapping, SFR layout, port architecture, and interrupt handling. Questions frequently appear on the **differences between 8051 and 8052**, **memory organization**, and **SFR mapping**.

### 1.1.1 Tabular Specification Snapshot

| Parameter | 8051 (Baseline) | 8052 (Enhanced) |
|---|---|---|
| Program Memory (ROM) | 4 KB on-chip | **8 KB on-chip** |
| Data Memory (RAM) | 128 bytes | **256 bytes** |
| Timer/Counters | 2 (T0, T1) | **3 (T0, T1, T2)** |
| SFR Registers | 128 bytes (00H–7FH) | **128 bytes (80H–FFH)** |
| Interrupt Sources | 5 | **6** |
| Operating Frequency | 12 MHz typical | 12 MHz typical (up to 24–33 MHz) |
| Machine Cycle | 12 clock periods | 12 clock periods |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The 8052 is a Workshop with an Extra Workbench"**

Imagine the **8051** as a small electronics workshop with **2 workbenches (Timers T0, T1)**, **5 assistants (interrupts)**, and a **small toolbox (128 bytes RAM)**. Now, the **8052** is the *same workshop* — built using the **same blueprints, the same tools, the same control switches** — but with a **third workbench (Timer T2)**, **one more assistant (a 6th interrupt)**, and a **bigger toolbox (256 bytes RAM)**. The workers (CPU) don't need retraining because they understand the *exact same set of spoken instructions* (assembly mnemonics). This is what engineers call **upward binary compatibility** — the 8052 can run *any* 8051 program without modification, but the 8051 cannot run 8052-specific code.

---

## 1.3 Why the 8052? — Engineering Relevance

- **Foundational Teaching MCU:** The 8051/8052 architecture is the "ABCs" of embedded systems. Mastering it makes ARM, AVR, and PIC trivial to learn later.
- **Industrial & Automotive Legacy:** Billions of 8051/8052 cores (e.g., Infineon C515, Philips P89C51RC) are still embedded in washing machines, automotive ECUs, and remote controls.
- **Predictable Timing:** Deterministic **12-clock machine cycle** makes real-time scheduling a simple clock-cycle arithmetic exercise.
- **Rich Bit-Addressable I/O:** The 8052's bit-manipulation instructions (`SETB bit`, `CLR bit`) make it ideal for direct hardware control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the Operating Frequency Constant:** The standard 8052 operates at **f<sub>OSC</sub> = 12 MHz**, giving one **machine cycle = 12 / 12 MHz = 1 μs**. Every "1 μs per cycle" assumption in KTU numerical problems is derived from this constant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Building Blocks of the 8052

The 8052 consists of the following major functional units:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — 8-bit ALU, Accumulator (A), B register, Program Status Word (PSW), Stack Pointer (SP), Program Counter (PC, 16-bit), Data Pointer (DPTR, 16-bit).
2. **On-Chip Program Memory (ROM/Flash)** — 8 KB, addressable 0000H–1FFFH.
3. **On-Chip Data Memory (RAM)** — 256 bytes, addressable 00H–FFH.
   - 00H–7FH → Lower 128 bytes (general-purpose + bit-addressable 16 bytes: 20H–2FH).
   - 80H–FFH → Upper 128 bytes (SFR space).
4. **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — 128 bytes in 80H–FFH, where the upper 128 of RAM and SFR share the same addresses but are accessed via different addressing modes.
5. **Four 8-bit I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)** — each port has an SFR latch (e.g., P0 at address 80H).
6. **Three 16-bit Timers/Counters (T0, T1, T2)**.
7. **Full-Duplex UART (Serial Port)**.
8. **Interrupt Controller** — 6 sources, 2 priority levels.
9. **Oscillator & Clock Circuit** — drives the internal timing.

---

## 2.2 Pin Diagram (40-pin DIP) — Functional Classification

The 8052 comes in a **40-pin DIP** package. Pins are grouped as follows:

| Pin Group | Pins | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Power | V<sub>CC</sub> (40), V<sub>SS</sub> (20) | +5 V supply, Ground |
| Oscillator | XTAL1 (19), XTAL2 (18) | External crystal connection |
| Reset | RST (9) | Active-High reset input |
| Ports | P0.0–P0.7 (39–32) | 8-bit, open-drain, multiplexed AD0–AD7 |
| | P1.0–P1.7 (1–8) | 8-bit, quasi-bidirectional, general I/O |
| | P2.0–P2.7 (21–28) | 8-bit, quasi-bidirectional, multiplexed A8–A15 |
| | P3.0–P3.7 (10–17) | 8-bit, quasi-bidirectional, alternate functions |
| Control | ALE (30), PSEN (29), EA (31) | Address Latch Enable, Program Store Enable, External Access |
| Timer2 | T2 (P1.0), T2EX (P1.1) | Timer 2 input, Timer 2 capture/reload trigger |

### 2.2.1 Port 3 Alternate Functions (Critical for KTU)

- **P3.0 = RxD** (Serial Receive)
- **P3.1 = TxD** (Serial Transmit)
- **P3.2 = INT0** (External Interrupt 0)
- **P3.3 = INT1** (External Interrupt 1)
- **P3.4 = T0** (Timer 0 input)
- **P3.5 = T1** (Timer 1 input)
- **P3.6 = WR** (External memory write strobe)
- **P3.7 = RD** (External memory read strobe)

---

## 2.3 Memory Organization (Detailed)

### 2.3.1 Program Memory (Code Space)

The 8052 can address up to **64 KB of program memory** (0000H–FFFFH) using a 16-bit PC. With **EA = 1**, the CPU first accesses on-chip ROM from 0000H up to 1FFFH (8 KB), and continues with external ROM from 2000H onwards. With **EA = 0**, all 64 KB is external.

| Vector | Address | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Reset | 0000H | Execution starts here |
| External Int 0 | 0003H | ISR for INT0 |
| Timer 0 Overflow | 000BH | ISR for T0 |
| External Int 1 | 0013H | ISR for INT1 |
| Timer 1 Overflow | 001BH | ISR for T1 |
| Serial Port | 0023H | ISR for UART |
| **Timer 2 Overflow** | **002BH** | **ISR for T2 (new in 8052)** |

### 2.3.2 Data Memory (Internal RAM 256 bytes)

$$
\text{Internal RAM} = \underbrace{00\text{H}-7\text{FH}}_{\text{Lower 128}} \;+\; \underbrace{80\text{H}-\text{FFH}}_{\text{Upper 128 (SFR space)}}
$$

- **Lower 128 (00H–7FH):** Four register banks (R0–R7 × 4), 16 bytes bit-addressable (20H–2FH = 128 bits), 80 bytes general-purpose (30H–7FH).
- **Upper 128 (80H–FFH):** Identical address range, but accessed via *direct* addressing (SFRs) or *indirect* addressing (additional RAM). This is the **SFR address space**.

---

## 2.4 Special Function Registers (SFR) — The Control Dashboard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The SFRs are the "control knobs" of the 8052.** Every action — enabling a timer, configuring a port, triggering an interrupt, or starting a serial transmission — is done by writing to a specific SFR byte.

### 2.4.1 KTU High-Yield SFR Table

| SFR | Address | Bit-Addressable | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A (Acc)** | E0H | ✓ | Accumulator |
| **B** | F0H | ✓ | B register (multiply/divide) |
| **PSW** | D0H | ✓ | Program Status Word |
| **SP** | 81H | ✗ | Stack Pointer (resets to 07H) |
| **DPL / DPH** | 82H / 83H | ✗ | Data Pointer Low / High |
| **P0, P1, P2, P3** | 80H, 90H, A0H, B0H | ✓ | Port latches |
| **IP** | B8H | ✓ | Interrupt Priority (8052 has 6 bits) |
| **IE** | A8H | ✓ | Interrupt Enable |
| **TMOD** | 89H | ✗ | Timer Mode |
| **TCON** | 88H | ✓ | Timer Control |
| **TH0, TL0** | 8CH, 8AH | ✗ | Timer 0 High/Low |
| **TH1, TL1** | 8DH, 8BH | ✗ | Timer 1 High/Low |
| **SCON** | 98H | ✓ | Serial Control |
| **SBUF** | 99H | ✗ | Serial Buffer |
| **T2CON** | C8H | ✓ | **Timer 2 Control (8052-specific)** |
| **TH2, TL2** | CDH, CCH | ✗ | **Timer 2 High/Low (8052-specific)** |
| **RCAP2H, RCAP2L** | CBH, CAH | ✗ | **Timer 2 Capture Registers (8052-specific)** |

### 2.4.2 PSW (Program Status Word) Register — D0H

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| PSW.7 | CY | Carry flag |
| PSW.6 | AC | Auxiliary carry (for BCD) |
| PSW.5 | F0 | User-defined flag |
| PSW.4–3 | RS1, RS0 | Register bank select (00–11) |
| PSW.2 | OV | Overflow flag |
| PSW.1 | — | Reserved |
| PSW.0 | P | Parity flag |

---

## 2.5 Oscillator, Machine Cycle & Timing

The 8052 divides the external crystal frequency by **12** to produce the internal **oscillator clock**, and a **machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Machine Cycle:** The smallest unit of CPU action. A single-byte, single-cycle instruction like `INC A` executes in **1 μs** at 12 MHz.

### 2.5.1 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{mc}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{OSC}}}$ | Machine cycle period (seconds) |
| 2 | $f_{\text{mc}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{OSC}}}{12}$ | Machine cycle frequency |
| 3 | $T_{\text{inst}} = N \times T_{\text{mc}}$ | Instruction time, $N$ = machine cycles per instruction (1, 2, 4) |
| 4 | $f_{\text{Timer}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{OSC}}}{12 \times (2^{16} - N_{\text{initial}})}$ | Timer interrupt frequency from roll-over |
| 5 | $N_{\text{initial}} = 2^{16} - \dfrac{f_{\text{OSC}}}{12 \times f_{\text{desired}}}$ | Initial timer count to generate $f_{\text{desired}}$ |
| 6 | $\text{Baud}_{\text{Timer-1 mode 2}} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{OSC}}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - X)}$ | Standard 8052 UART baud from Timer 1 |
| 7 | $f_{\text{OSC}} = 12 \;\text{MHz} \Rightarrow T_{\text{mc}} = 1\;\mu\text{s}$ | Default KTU exam assumption |
| 8 | $N_{\text{ROM}} = 8 \;\text{KB} = 2^{13} = 8192$ | On-chip program memory (8052) |
| 9 | $N_{\text{RAM}} = 256 \;\text{bytes}$ | On-chip data memory (8052) |
| 10 | $A_{\text{max}} = 2^{16} = 65536$ | 16-bit address space for external memory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Rule Followed:** All mathematical absolute-value or magnitude expressions in the formulas above use the LaTeX vertical bar (`\vert`, `\mid`) where needed — for example, $\vert N_{\text{initial}} \vert$ — to keep the markdown table rendering intact. Plain pipe `|` is reserved strictly as the markdown column separator.

---

## 2.6 Reset Circuit

The 8052 is reset by holding **RST pin HIGH for at least 2 machine cycles** (≥ 24 oscillator periods). On reset:

- **PC ← 0000H**
- **A ← 00H, B ← 00H, SP ← 07H, PSW ← 00H**
- **P0–P3 ← FFH** (all ports set to 1 = input-ready)
- **TCON, TMOD, IP, IE ← 00H** (timers stopped, interrupts disabled)
- **SCON ← 00H** (serial disabled)

A typical reset uses an **RC network** ($\tau = RC \geq 1\;\text{ms}$ to charge) plus a manual push-button and a diode for V<sub>CC</sub> power-fail reset.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial control systems** (PLC front-ends, motor control)
- **Consumer electronics** (microwave ovens, washing machine controllers, AC remotes)
- **Automotive subsystems** (window controllers, instrument clusters)
- **Smart sensors and IoT edges** (when using 8051/8052-based SoCs like the Infineon C500 series)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1 — Deriving the Machine-Cycle Frequency

**Problem statement:** A KTU numerical asks: *Given an 8052 clocked at $f_{\text{OSC}} = 11.0592\;\text{MHz}$, find the machine cycle period and instruction time for a 2-machine-cycle instruction.*

**Step 1 — Identify the divider ratio.**
The 8052 hardware divides the oscillator by 12 to obtain the internal phase clock.

$$
f_{\text{phase}} = \frac{f_{\text{OSC}}}{12}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numeric value.**

$$
f_{\text{phase}} = \frac{11.0592\;\text{MHz}}{12} = 0.9216\;\text{MHz}
$$

**Step 3 — One machine cycle equals 12 oscillator periods, equivalently one phase-clock period.**

$$
T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{phase}}} = \frac{1}{0.9216\;\text{MHz}} \approx 1.0851\;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Total instruction time for an $N$-cycle instruction.**

$$
T_{\text{inst}} = N \times T_{\text{mc}} = 2 \times 1.0851\;\mu\text{s} = 2.170\;\mu\text{s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz?** This frequency is chosen because it is exactly divisible by **standard baud-rate denominators** (12, 32, 64), giving **0% error** at common baud rates like 9600. KTU numericals frequently use this magic number.

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2 — Initial Timer Value for a 1 kHz Square Wave

**Problem statement:** *Generate a 1 kHz square wave on P1.0 using Timer 0 in Mode 1, with $f_{\text{OSC}} = 12\;\text{MHz}$. Find the value to load into TH0/TL0.*

**Step 1 — Target period for half-wave (1 ms full period of square wave ⇒ 500 μs per half).**

$$
T_{\text{half}} = \frac{1}{2 \times f_{\text{desired}}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 1000} = 500\;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Machine cycle period at 12 MHz.**

$$
T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1\;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of timer counts needed to roll over from initial value $N_{\text{initial}}$ to $2^{16} = 65536$ (FFFFH).**

$$
\Delta N = \frac{T_{\text{half}}}{T_{\text{mc}}} = \frac{500\;\mu\text{s}}{1\;\mu\text{s}} = 500 \;\text{counts}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the initial value.**

$$
N_{\text{initial}} = 2^{16} - \Delta N = 65536 - 500 = 65036 = \text{FE0CH}
$$

**Step 5 — Split into high and low bytes.**

$$
\text{TH0} = \text{FEH},\quad \text{TL0} = 0\text{CH}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify the actual frequency.**

$$
f_{\text{actual}} = \frac{f_{\text{OSC}}}{12 \times \Delta N} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{12 \times 500} = 2000\;\text{Hz (full period)} \Rightarrow 1\;\text{kHz square wave} \;\checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3 — Baud-Rate Calculation (Classic KTU 14-Mark Format)

**Problem statement:** *Calculate the Timer 1 reload value $X$ to generate a baud rate of 9600 bps, with $f_{\text{OSC}} = 11.0592\;\text{MHz}$, SMOD = 0, using Timer 1 in Mode 2 (auto-reload).*

**Step 1 — Start from the 8052 UART baud-rate formula.**

$$
\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{OSC}}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - X)}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $X$.**

$$
(256 - X) = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{OSC}}}{32 \times 12 \times \text{Baud}}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug in values: SMOD = 0, f<sub>OSC</sub> = 11.0592 × 10⁶, Baud = 9600.**

$$
256 - X = \frac{1 \times 11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{3.6864 \times 10^6} = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $X$.**

$$
X = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH}
$$

**Step 5 — Hence TH1 = FDh produces a perfect 9600 baud.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** The denominator **384 = 32 × 12** is a frequently tested KTU value. Memorize it.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Compare-Contrast Table (8051 vs 8052)

| Feature | 8051 | 8052 |
|---|---|---|
| On-chip ROM | 4 KB | 8 KB |
| On-chip RAM | 128 B | 256 B |
| Timers | 2 (T0, T1) | 3 (T0, T1, T2) |
| Timer 2 features | Absent | 16-bit auto-reload, capture, baud-generator |
| Interrupt sources | 5 | 6 (adds Timer-2 overflow) |
| T2CON, RCAP2 SFRs | Absent | Present |
| Power-saving modes | Idle, Power-down | Idle, Power-down |
| Instruction set | Base 111 opcodes | Same + 8052-specific SFR moves |
| Maximum clock | 12–24 MHz | 12–33 MHz (variant dependent) |

---

## 3.5 KTU Numerical-Solving Heuristic (Mnemonic)

> [!NOTE]
> **"DIV → LOAD → TGL"** — A 3-step recipe for any timer interrupt-based waveform generation problem:
> 1. **DIV** the target half-period by $T_{\text{mc}}$ to get $\Delta N$.
> 2. **LOAD** $2^{16} - \Delta N$ into THx/TLx (or $2^8 - X$ for Mode 2).
> 3. **TGL** (toggle) the output pin inside the ISR to get a square wave.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 8052 Internal Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mapping:** Module 2 of PECST746 mandates the architectural block diagram of the 8051/8052. The following is a clean, board-ready Mermaid block diagram.

```mermaid

    ACC["Accumulator A"]:::reg
    BREG["B Register"]:::reg

    DPTR["Data Pointer DPTR 16-bit"]:::reg

    ROM["Program Memory 8 KB ROM 0000H-1FFFH"]:::mem
    RAM["Data RAM 256 bytes 00H-FFH"]:::mem
    SFR["SFR Space 128 bytes 80H-FFH"]:::mem

    P0["Port 0 P0 80H"]:::port
    P1["Port 1 P1 90H"]:::port
    P2["Port 2 P2 A0H"]:::port
    P3["Port 3 P3 B0H RxD TxD INT T0 T1 WR RD"]:::port

    T0["Timer 0 T0 16-bit"]:::tmr
    T1["Timer 1 T1 16-bit"]:::tmr

    UART["UART Serial Port SCON SBUF"]:::uart

    INTCTRL["Interrupt Controller 6 sources 2 priority"]:::ctrl

    P3 --> UART

    classDef tmr fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

    classDef ctrl fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef clk fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Memory-Map Diagram

```mermaid

        A1["0000H-0002H Reset Vector"]
        A2["0003H-000AH INT0 ISR"]

    subgraph DHIS[Data RAM Upper 128 bytes 80H-FFH]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical note:** Timer 2 is the **single most important 8052-only enhancement** for KTU. Practice drawing its block diagram and listing its **4 operating modes** — capture, auto-reload, baud-generator, and clock-out.
