# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Differentiate between the 8051 and 8052 microcontrollers. List any four distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (Approx. 120 words):**
The 8052 is an **enhanced superset** of the 8051, fully instruction-compatible. The four key differences are:
1. **ROM:** 8051 = 4 KB on-chip; 8052 = **8 KB on-chip**.
2. **RAM:** 8051 = 128 bytes; 8052 = **256 bytes**.
3. **Timers:** 8051 = 2; 8052 = **3 (T0, T1, T2)**.
4. **Interrupts:** 8051 = 5; 8052 = **6 (additional Timer-2 overflow vector at 002BH)**.
Furthermore, the 8052 introduces the SFRs **T2CON, TH2, TL2, RCAP2H, RCAP2L**, and Timer 2 supports capture, auto-reload, and baud-rate generator modes.

### Question A.2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the function of the PSW register in the 8052? Name its four most important flag bits.**

**Model Answer (Approx. 100 words):**
The **Program Status Word (PSW)** at SFR address **D0H** stores the ALU status flags and selects the active register bank. The four most important bits are:
- **CY (PSW.7):** Carry flag — set on arithmetic overflow from bit 7.
- **AC (PSW.6):** Auxiliary carry — for BCD arithmetic.
- **RS1, RS0 (PSW.4, PSW.3):** Register bank select (00=Bank 0, 01=Bank 1, 10=Bank 2, 11=Bank 3).
- **OV (PSW.2):** Overflow flag — set on signed arithmetic overflow.
- **P (PSW.0):** Parity flag — set if accumulator has odd number of 1s.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module 2 Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 Scheme:** Each Module in the ESE contains two 14-mark questions, and the student must answer **one** of them. Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

### Question B (A) — [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Draw and explain the **internal block diagram of the 8052 microcontroller**, identifying the CPU, program memory, data memory, ports, timers, and serial port. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Diagram (4 marks):** A Mermaid-style or hand-drawn diagram with the following blocks clearly inter-connected (CPU at center):

- **CPU** containing ALU, Accumulator, B register, PSW, SP, PC (16-bit), DPTR (16-bit).
- **Program Memory** of 8 KB on-chip ROM (0000H–1FFFH) accessible via PC.
- **Data RAM** of 256 bytes split as 128 lower (00H–7FH) + 128 upper (80H–FFH, SFR space).
- **4 I/O Ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3), each latch SFR-addressable, with P3 carrying alternate functions (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD).
- **3 Timers/Counters** (T0, T1, T2) each 16-bit, controlled by TCON, TMOD, T2CON.
- **UART (Serial Port)** with SCON, SBUF, configurable for 4 modes.
- **Interrupt Controller** handling 6 sources with 2 priority levels.
- **Oscillator & Reset** blocks driven by XTAL1, XTAL2 and RST.

**2. Explanation (3 marks):**
- The **CPU** fetches opcodes from program memory pointed to by **PC** and executes them through the **ALU**, storing intermediate results in **A** and **B**.
- **DPTR** is used for 16-bit memory accesses (e.g., `MOVX A,@DPTR`).
- **P0** and **P2** demultiplex the external address bus (P0 = AD0–AD7, P2 = A8–A15) when external memory is used; otherwise they serve as general I/O.
- **P3** is a quasi-bidirectional port with alternate hardware functions routed to specific pins.
- **Timers** may be clocked internally (machine cycle) or from external pins (T0/P3.4, T1/P3.5, T2/P1.0).
- **Serial Port** operates in full-duplex, shift-register mode.

---

**(b)** Compare the **memory organization of the 8051 and 8052**, with neat diagrams. Also explain the concept of **bit-addressable memory** in the 8052. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Program Memory Comparison (2 marks):**

| | 8051 | 8052 |
|---|---|---|
| On-chip ROM | 4 KB (0000H–0FFFH) | 8 KB (0000H–1FFFH) |
| External | 60 KB (when EA=0) | 56 KB (when EA=0) |

**2. Data Memory Comparison (2 marks):**

| | 8051 | 8052 |
|---|---|---|
| On-chip RAM | 128 B (00H–7FH) | 256 B (00H–FFH) |
| Indirect-accessible upper RAM | None | 80H–FFH (128 B extra, indirect access only) |

The 8052 has 256 bytes of internal RAM. The **upper 128 bytes (80H–FFH)** share addresses with the SFRs but are **physically distinct**. They are accessed via **indirect addressing** (`MOV A, @R0`) while SFRs are accessed via **direct addressing** (`MOV A, 80H`).

**3. Bit-Addressable Memory (3 marks):**
- The 8052 provides **16 bytes of bit-addressable RAM** in the lower 128-byte RAM space, located at addresses **20H–2FH**.
- These 16 × 8 = **128 individually addressable bits** are accessed using the syntax `bit-address` (e.g., `SETB 20H.0`, `CLR 20H.7`).
- Additionally, **16 special bit-addressable SFRs** (A, B, PSW, IP, IE, SCON, TCON, P0–P3, etc.) provide another **128 bit-addressable locations**.
- This makes the 8052 ideal for **boolean** control algorithms, e.g., `SETB P1.0` toggles pin P1.0 in **one instruction cycle (1 μs at 12 MHz)**.

---

### Question B (B) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Jul 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **Timer/Counter architecture of the 8052**. Describe all four operating modes of **Timer 2** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Common Timer Architecture (2 marks):**
All three timers (T0, T1, T2) are **16-bit up-counters** built from two 8-bit SFRs (THx, TLx). They can be clocked from:
- **Internal source** — one machine cycle (12 oscillator periods).
- **External source** — transitions on the T0/T1/T2 pin.

The TMOD register sets the **Mode (M1, M0)**, **C/T** (counter vs. timer), and **gate** (software/hardware control). TCON holds the run-control bits **TR0, TR1** and overflow flags **TF0, TF1**. For T2, the corresponding bits live in **T2CON**.

**2. Timer 2 Modes (5 marks):**
- **Mode 0 — 16-bit Auto-Reload (DCEN = 0, RCLK = 0, TCLK = 0, CP/RL2 = 0):** Timer 2 counts up from the value in **RCAP2H:RCAP2L** to **FFFFH**, then reloads the capture register values. This is the most commonly used Timer-2 mode for periodic interrupts.
- **Mode 1 — 16-bit Capture (CP/RL2 = 1, TR2 = 1, EXEN2 = 1):** A **falling edge on T2EX (P1.1)** causes the current counter contents to be **latched into RCAP2H:RCAP2L** for later reading, allowing precise measurement of external events.
- **Mode 2 — Baud-Rate Generator (RCLK = 1 or TCLK = 1):** Timer 2 provides the baud clock for the serial port, and the overflow rate is divided by 2 before being used. The reload value resides in RCAP2H:RCAP2L.
- **Mode 3 — Clock-Out / Split Mode (T2OE = 1, C/T2 = 0):** Timer 2 generates a programmable 50% duty cycle clock on pin **P1.0 (T2)**. The frequency is $f_{\text{T2OUT}} = f_{\text{OSC}} / (4 \times (65536 - \text{RCAP2}))$.

---

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **reset circuit of the 8052**. List the **SFR values immediately after reset**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Reset Circuit (3 marks):**
The 8052 reset pin **RST (pin 9)** is **active-HIGH**. A reliable reset requires RST to be held HIGH for **≥ 24 oscillator periods (≥ 2 machine cycles)**. A typical power-on reset circuit consists of:
- A **10 μF electrolytic capacitor** from V<sub>CC</sub> to RST.
- An **8.2 kΩ pull-down resistor** from RST to ground.
- A **manual reset push-button** in parallel with the capacitor.
- A **Schottky diode** (1N5819) anode to RST, cathode to V<sub>CC</sub> for power-fail reset.

**Mathematical design criterion:** The RC time constant $\tau = R \times C$ must be large enough to keep RST above $V_{\text{IH(min)}} = 0.7 V_{\text{CC}} = 3.5\;\text{V}$ until the oscillator stabilizes. With R = 8.2 kΩ and C = 10 μF, $\tau = 82\;\text{ms} \gg 2\;\mu\text{s}$ (12 MHz reset time) — comfortably safe.

**2. SFR Values After Reset (4 marks):**

| Register | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **PC** | 0000H | Fetch from reset vector |
| **A** | 00H | Cleared |
| **B** | 00H | Cleared |
| **PSW** | 00H | Bank 0 selected, flags cleared |
| **SP** | 07H | Stack starts at 08H |
| **DPTR** | 0000H | Cleared |
| **P0–P3** | FFH | All ports set to 1 (input-ready) |
| **IP** | ××000000B | No priority, all cleared (× = undefined) |
| **IE** | 0×000000B | All interrupts disabled |
| **TCON** | 00H | Timers stopped, flags cleared |
| **TMOD** | 00H | Mode 0, Timer, software start |
| **T2CON** | 00H | Timer 2 stopped, all flags cleared |
| **SCON** | 00H | Serial mode 0, RI/TI cleared |
| **TH0–TL0, TH1–TL1, TH2–TL2, RCAP2H, RCAP2L** | 00H | All counters zeroed |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common mistake 1:** Students write **SP = 08H** after reset. Wrong — the register is initialized to **07H**; the *first push* places the byte at 08H.
> 2. **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting that the reset value of **P0–P3 is FFH** (all ones). This is critical for understanding why ports read 1 just after reset.
> 3. **Common mistake 3:** Claiming "all timers run after reset" — actually all timers are **stopped** (TR0 = TR1 = TR2 = 0).
> 4. **Common mistake 4:** Mixing up **EA** and **IE.7** pin. **EA** is a hardware pin (External Access), while **IE.7** is the **Global Interrupt Enable** bit in the IE register. Two completely different entities.
> 5. **Common mistake 5:** Writing "T2CON is at 90H" — wrong, T2CON is at **C8H**. Always cross-check SFR addresses.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — The 8052 Microcontroller**

- **Core Specs:** 8-bit CPU • 8 KB ROM • 256 B RAM • 3 timers • 6 interrupts • 4 × 8-bit I/O ports.
- **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** ⇒ at $f_{\text{OSC}} = 12\;\text{MHz}$, $T_{\text{mc}} = 1\;\mu\text{s}$ — the **default KTU assumption**.
- **Address spaces:** 16-bit program memory (64 KB max) + 16-bit external data memory (XDATA, 64 KB max) + 8-bit internal RAM (256 bytes).
- **Lower 128 RAM (00H–7FH):** 4 register banks × 8 bytes + 16 bytes bit-addressable (20H–2FH) + 80 general-purpose bytes (30H–7FH).
- **Upper 128 RAM (80H–FFH):** SFR space; only **direct addressing** reaches SFRs; **indirect addressing** reaches the extra 128 bytes (8052-only).
- **SFRs to memorize:** A (E0H), B (F0H), PSW (D0H), SP (81H), DPL (82H), DPH (83H), P0–P3 (80H/90H/A0H/B0H), TCON (88H), TMOD (89H), IE (A8H), IP (B8H), SCON (98H), SBUF (99H), **T2CON (C8H), TH2/TL2 (CDH/CCH), RCAP2H/RCAP2L (CBH/CAH)**.
- **PSW flags:** CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, —, P. RS1:RS0 selects register bank 0–3.
- **P3 alternate functions** — memorize: RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD.
- **Timer 2** has 4 modes: auto-reload, capture, baud-generator, clock-out.
- **Timer 2 SFRs** are exclusive to 8052.
- **Reset values to memorize:** PC=0000H, A=B=00H, SP=07H, PSW=00H, P0–P3=FFH, IE=0×000000B.
- **Interrupt vector table addresses** — 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H, 002BH (Timer-2 in 8052).
- **Serial Port modes** — 4 modes: Mode 0 (shift register), Mode 1 (8-bit UART, timer-baud), Mode 2 (9-bit UART, fixed baud), Mode 3 (9-bit UART, timer-baud).
- **Baud-rate formula (Timer 1, Mode 2):** $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{OSC}}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - X)}$.
- **Power-saving modes:** IDLE (oscillator on, CPU stopped, peripherals live) and POWER-DOWN (everything off, only reset wakes it).
- **Crystal frequency of choice:** 11.0592 MHz — gives **0% baud-rate error** at standard baud rates.
- **External memory access pins:** **ALE** (latches AD0–AD7 into 74LS373 for low byte), **PSEN** (strobes external ROM), **EA** (low ⇒ all external).
- **Bit-manipulation advantage:** instructions like `SETB bit` and `CLR bit` execute in **1 machine cycle (1 μs)** — faster than 8052's CISC bit-field ops.
- **Instruction set size:** 111 opcodes (44 mnemonic types); all are 1-byte, 2-byte, or 3-byte.
- **Assembly language register-reference shortcuts:** `ADD A, R0` adds R0 to A; bank selection via PSW.3:PSW.4.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8051/52 Variants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** 8051/52 Variants # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition The **8051/8052 Variants** refer to the family of 8-bit microcontrollers that are architecturally derived from (i.e., binary-compatible with) the original **Intel MCS-51** instruction set, originally released by Intel in **1980** and **1984** respectively. Over the decades, numerous semiconductor manufacturers (such as **Philips/NXP, Atmel (now Microchip), Dallas/Maxim, Silicon Labs, Infineon, and Texas Instruments**) have produced their own proprietary variants of the 8051/8052 core, integrating enhanced on-chip peripherals, larger memory, additional timers/counters, ADC/DAC channels, PWM modules, and various communication interfaces (UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB). While the **CPU core**, **instruction set**, and **basic SFR (Special Function Register) layout** remain compatible with the original Intel design, these variants differ significantly in terms of: - **On-chip program memory (ROM/EPROM/Flash)** capacity - **On-chip data memory (RAM)** size - **Number of timers/counters** - **I/O port count and configuration** - **Operating voltage and clock frequency** - **Power management and sleep modes** - **Integrated peripherals** (ADC, DAC, PWM, Comparators, Watchdog, RTC, etc.) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students are expected to compare and contrast the key features of commonly used 8051/52 variants, identify the manufacturer-specific enhancements, and understand how to select an appropriate variant for a given embedded application. This is a frequent Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) question topic. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the original **Intel 8051** as a **basic Maruti 800 car** — reliable, simple, and built for a standard purpose. The 8051/52 variants are like the **different models and brands** built on the same fundamental chassis: a **Maruti Swift, Hyundai i10, Tata Tiago, or even a Maruti Brezza**. The *engine design philosophy* (the CPU core and instruction set) remains similar — the car can still drive on the same roads (same assembly language works). But each variant has **different features**: some have a sunroof (ADC), some have a touchscreen (USB), some have a bigger boot (more RAM), and some have better mileage (low power). From the **software perspective**, the programmer can still use the same assembly instructions (MOV, ADD, JMP, etc.), but the **hardware engineer** enjoys greater flexibility in choosing the chip based on system requirements such as cost, power, peripheral integration, and processing speed. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | **Original Intel 8051** | 4 KB ROM, 128 Bytes RAM, 2 Timers, 1 UART, 4 I/O Ports | | **Original Intel 8052** | 8 KB ROM, 256 Bytes RAM, 3 Timers, 1 UART, 4 I/O Ports | | **Architectural Width** | 8-bit ALU | | **Address Bus Width** | 16-bit (64 KB addressable program + 64 KB data space) | | **Core Clock Frequency** | Typically 12 MHz (1 MHz instruction cycle), variants up to 100 MHz | > [!NOTE] > The 8052 is a **direct superset** of the 8051 — any 8051 program will run on an 8052, but not vice-versa due to the additional Timer 2 and 256-byte internal RAM. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Variants Exist — The Underlying Logic The original Intel 8051 was a **general-purpose 8-bit microcontroller**. However, as embedded applications grew more diverse (automotive, industrial control, consumer electronics, IoT, etc.), the need arose for: - **More on-chip memory** to reduce external memory costs. - **Specialized peripherals** (e.g., ADC for sensor interfacing, PWM for motor control) to reduce BOM (Bill of Materials). - **Lower power consumption** for battery-operated devices. - **Higher clock speeds** to support real-time processing demands. Manufacturers responded by creating **drop-in replacements** — chips with the same pinout and instruction set, but enhanced internal features. This ensured that existing code (Intellectual Property / firmware) could be reused while enjoying hardware improvements. ## 2.2 Classification of 8051/52 Variants The variants can be broadly classified into **four categories** based on their memory and feature enhancements: ### 2.2.1 Standard Family (Legacy/Original) These closely mirror the original Intel 8051/8052 specifications with minimal enhancements. ### 2.2.2 Extended Family (More Memory, More Peripherals) Chips with **larger Flash/ROM**, **expanded RAM**, **additional timers**, and **multiple UARTs**. ### 2.2.3 Enhanced Family (Specialized Peripherals) Chips integrating **ADC, DAC, PWM, Comparators, I²C, SPI, CAN, USB, Brown-out Detect, Watchdog Timer**. ### 2.2.4 Low-Power / Modern Family Chips with **multiple power-saving modes**, **wide voltage range**, and **high-speed cores** (1-cycle 8051 core). ## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Key Variants ### 2.3.1 Intel 8051 (Original) - **Manufacturer:** Intel Corporation - **Program Memory:** 4 KB on-chip ROM (factory masked) - **Data Memory:** 128 bytes on-chip RAM + 128 bytes SFR - **Timers/Counters:** 2 (Timer 0, Timer 1) - **UART:** 1 (full-duplex, programmable) - **I/O Ports:** 4 × 8-bit (P0, P1, P2, P3) - **Clock:** 12 MHz (1 µs instruction cycle) - **Interrupts:** 5 sources, 2 priority levels - **Key Drawback:** No on-chip reprogrammable memory; ROM is mask-programmed at factory. ### 2.3.2 Intel 8052 - **Manufacturer:** Intel Corporation - **Program Memory:** 8 KB on-chip ROM - **Data Memory:** 256 bytes on-chip RAM - **Timers/Counters:** 3 (Timer 0, Timer 1, Timer 2 with capture/compare) - **UART:** 1 - **Key Enhancement:** Timer 2 is a **16-bit timer with auto-reload, capture, and compare modes** — making it suitable for baud-rate generation and precise event timing. ### 2.3.3 Atmel AT89C51 / AT89C52 - **Manufacturer:** Atmel Corporation (now Microchip Technology) - **Program Memory:** 4 KB / 8 KB **Flash ROM** (reprogrammable in-system) - **Data Memory:** 128 / 256 bytes RAM - **Timers/Counters:** 2 / 3 - **Key Enhancement:** **In-System Programmable (ISP) Flash memory** — eliminates the need for an external EPROM and UV eraser. Highly popular in academic and hobbyist applications. - **Clock:** Up to 24 MHz - **Operating Voltage:** 5V ### 2.3.4 Atmel AT89S52 / AT89S8253 - **Program Memory:** 8 KB / 12 KB Flash with **In-System Programming (ISP)** via SPI - **Key Enhancement:** Built-in **Watchdog Timer**, **SPI interface**, **Dual Data Pointer (DPTR)** for faster memory operations. - **AT89S8253:** Includes **2 KB on-chip EEPROM** for non-volatile data storage. ### 2.3.5 Philips / NXP P89C51Rx2 / P89V51RD2 - **Program Memory:** Up to 64 KB Flash - **Data Memory:** Up to 1 KB RAM - **Key Enhancement:** **In-Application Programming (IAP)**, **PCA (Programmable Counter Array)** for PWM, **Hardware Watchdog**, **Brown-out Reset**. - **Clock:** Up to 40 MHz ### 2.3.6 Dallas / Maxim DS80C320 / DS89C450 - **Manufacturer:** Dallas Semiconductor (now Maxim Integrated) - **Key Enhancement:** **High-speed core** — uses 1 machine cycle per clock (vs. 12 in original 8051), achieving **3× to 10× performance** at the same crystal frequency. - **DS80C320:** 2 full-duplex UARTs, 3 Timers, 256 bytes RAM. - **DS89C450:** Runs up to 33 MHz, achieving **33 MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second). ### 2.3.7 Silicon Labs C8051Fxxx (e.g., C8051F020) - **Key Enhancement:** **CIP-51 Core** (1-cycle 8051), **12-bit ADC**, **8-bit DAC**, **PWM**, **Comparators**, **SMBus (I²C)**, **SPI**, **2 UARTs**, **64 KB Flash**, **4 KB RAM**. - **Clock:** Up to 25 MHz → 25 MIPS - **Key Enhancement:** Fully integrated **mixed-signal** SoC (System on Chip) — ideal for precision data acquisition. ### 2.3.8 Infineon XC800 Family (e.g., XC886, XC888) - **Key Enhancement:** **CAN (Controller Area Network)** interface for automotive applications, **10-bit ADC**, **PWM units (CCU6)** for motor control, **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)** support. - **Application Focus:** Automotive body electronics, motor control. ### 2.3.9 Texas Instruments CC2530 / CC2540 - **Key Enhancement:** Integrated **2.4 GHz RF transceiver** (IEEE 802.15.4 / Bluetooth Low Energy). - **Application Focus:** Wireless sensor networks, IoT, ZigBee, BLE. ### 2.3.10 Nuvoton N76E003 - **Key Enhancement:** **1T (1-cycle) 8051 core**, **18 KB Flash**, **1 KB RAM**, **12-bit ADC**, **PWM**, **I²C**, **SPI**, **UART**, **Low Power** modes. - **Operating Voltage:** 2.4V – 5.5V (wide range) - **Application Focus:** Low-cost consumer electronics, IoT endpoints. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Feature | Standard 8051 | 8052 | AT89C52 | DS89C450 | C8051F020 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Program Memory** | 4 KB ROM | 8 KB ROM | 8 KB Flash | 64 KB Flash | 64 KB Flash | | **Data RAM** | 128 B | 256 B | 256 B | 1 KB | 4 KB | | **Timers** | 2 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 5 | | **UARTs** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | | **I/O Pins** | 32 | 32 | 32 | 32 | 64 | | **Clock (Max)** | 12 MHz | 12 MHz | 24 MHz | 33 MHz | 25 MHz | | **Instruction Cycle** | 12 clocks | 12 clocks | 12 clocks | 4 clocks | 1 clock | | **ADC** | No | No | No | No | 12-bit | | **DAC** | No | No | No | No | 12-bit | | **Key Feature** | Original | Timer 2 | In-system Flash | High-speed | Mixed-signal SoC | | **Performance Metric** | **Formula / Relation** | **Units** | |---|---|---| | Instruction Execution Time | $T_{inst} = \frac{12 \times T_{osc}}{N}$ where $N = 1$ for 1T core, $N = 12$ for standard | microseconds (µs) | | Machine Cycle Frequency | $f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12}$ for standard core | MHz | | Effective MIPS | $\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times N}$ | Millions of Instructions/sec | | Timer Count Value | $\text{Timer Reload} = 2^n - \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{inst}}$ | Decimal count | | Baud Rate (Mode 1) | $\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | bps | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Variant selection directly impacts the **BOM cost, PCB complexity, and firmware development time**. For example, choosing a **C8051F020** eliminates the need for an external ADC chip, reducing cost and board space. Choosing a **DS89C450** allows legacy 8051 code to run 10× faster without rewriting. This is a critical decision in **embedded product design**. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Instruction Execution Time Comparison Across Variants The **instruction execution time** is one of the most critical parameters that differentiates 8051/52 variants. Let us derive it rigorously. ### 3.1.1 Standard Intel 8051 Core (12T Architecture) The original 8051 divides the oscillator frequency ($f_{osc}$) by **12** to generate the **machine cycle frequency**. Each **machine cycle** takes 12 oscillator periods and consists of 6 states (S1–S6), each split into 2 phases (P1, P2). Most instructions execute in either **1 machine cycle** (12 clocks) or **2 machine cycles** (24 clocks). The instruction execution time is given by: $$T_{inst} = N \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$ where $N$ is the number of machine cycles per instruction (typically $N = 1$ or $N = 2$). For a standard 8051 with $f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz}$ and $N = 1$: $$T_{inst} = 1 \times \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ µs}$$ ### 3.1.2 Dallas DS80C320 Core (4T Architecture) The DS80C320 uses a **4-clock** machine cycle (instead of 12). For the same $f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz}$: $$T_{inst} = 1 \times \frac{4}{12 \times 10^6} = 0.333 \text{ µs}$$ **Speedup factor:** $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{inst, 8051}}{T_{inst, DS80C320}} = \frac{1 \text{ µs}}{0.333 \text{ µs}} = 3.0$$ ### 3.1.3 Dallas DS89C450 Core (1T Architecture) The DS89C450 uses a **1-clock** machine cycle, making it one of the fastest 8051-compatible cores. For $f_{osc} = 33 \text{ MHz}$ and $N = 1$: $$T_{inst} = 1 \times \frac{1}{33 \times 10^6} = 30.3 \text{ ns}$$ **Speedup factor (vs. standard 8051 at 12 MHz):** $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{1 \text{ µs}}{30.3 \text{ ns}} = \frac{1000 \text{ ns}}{30.3 \text{ ns}} \approx 33$$ This matches the **33 MIPS** rating of the DS89C450. ### 3.1.4 Generalised Formula for All 8051/52 Variants $$T_{inst} = \frac{C}{f_{osc}} \times N_{cycles}$$ where: - $C$ = clocks per machine cycle (**12** for standard, **4** for DS80C320, **1** for DS89C450/C8051Fxxx) - $N_{cycles}$ = number of machine cycles for the specific instruction - $f_{osc}$ = oscillator frequency in Hz ## 3.2 Derivation: Timer Reload Value Calculation for Exact Delay To generate a precise delay using a variant's Timer, the reload value is calculated as: $$\text{Reload Value} = 2^n - \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{inst} \times \text{Prescaler}}$$ For **Timer 0 in Mode 1** (16-bit), $n = 16$, and assume prescaler = 1. **Example:** Generate a **50 ms delay** on a standard 8051 ($f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz}$, $T_{inst} = 1 \text{ µs}$): $$\text{Timer Ticks} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 50000 \text{ ticks}$$ $$\text{Reload} = 65536 - 50000 = 15536 = 0x3CB0$$ **For DS89C450** ($f_{osc} = 33 \text{ MHz}$, $T_{inst} = 30.3 \text{ ns}$): $$\text{Timer Ticks} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{30.3 \times 10^{-9}} = 1,650,165 \text{ ticks}$$ This **exceeds the 16-bit range** ($2^{16} = 65536$), so we must either: - Use the built-in **Timer 2 in 16-bit auto-reload mode** with extended prescaler, or - Use a **software loop counter** to extend the delay. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **S. No.** | **Variant** | **Family** | **Clock Cycles/Inst.** | **Max Clock** | **Max MIPS** | **Flash (KB)** | **RAM (Bytes)** | **Key Peripheral** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Intel 8051 | Standard | 12 | 12 MHz | 1 | 4 (ROM) | 128 | Basic | | 2 | Intel 8052 | Standard | 12 | 12 MHz | 1 | 8 (ROM) | 256 | Timer 2 | | 3 | AT89C51 | Enhanced | 12 | 24 MHz | 2 | 4 (Flash) | 128 | ISP Flash | | 4 | AT89C52 | Enhanced | 12 | 24 MHz | 2 | 8 (Flash) | 256 | ISP Flash | | 5 | AT89S52 | Enhanced | 12 | 33 MHz | 2.75 | 8 (Flash) | 256 | SPI, WDT | | 6 | AT89S8253 | Enhanced | 12 | 24 MHz | 2 | 12 (Flash) | 256 | 2 KB EEPROM | | 7 | P89V51RD2 | Extended | 12 | 40 MHz | 3.33 | 64 (Flash) | 1024 | IAP, PCA, WDT | | 8 | DS80C320 | High-Speed | 4 | 33 MHz | 8.25 | 0 (Ext.) | 256 | 2 UARTs | | 9 | DS89C450 | High-Speed | 1 | 33 MHz | 33 | 64 (Flash) | 1024 | High-speed | | 10 | C8051F020 | Mixed-Signal | 1 | 25 MHz | 25 | 64 (Flash) | 4352 | 12-bit ADC, DAC | | 11 | XC886 | Automotive | 12 | 27 MHz | 2.25 | 32 (Flash) | 1792 | CAN, LIN, ADC | | 12 | N76E003 | Low-Power | 1 | 16 MHz | 16 | 18 (Flash) | 1024 | 12-bit ADC, PWM | | 13 | CC2530 | Wireless | 1 | 32 MHz | 32 | 256 (Flash) | 8192 | 2.4 GHz RF | ## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example: Selecting a Variant for an Application **Application:** Battery-powered IoT temperature sensor that must: - Sample a temperature sensor every 10 seconds. - Transmit data over Bluetooth Low Energy. - Operate for 1 year on a CR2032 coin cell (3V, 220 mAh). - Cost under $2 per node. **Analysis:** - **Power:** Needs low-power sleep modes → **N76E003** or **CC2540** (BLE SoC). - **ADC:** Needs analog input for sensor → **N76E003** (12-bit ADC) or **CC2540** (built-in temp sensor). - **Wireless:** Needs BLE → **CC2540** (integrated BLE radio) eliminates need for external transceiver. - **Cost:** N76E003 ≈ $0.30, CC2540 ≈ $2.00 — both fit budget. **Decision:** **CC2540** is the optimal choice because it integrates the BLE radio, reducing BOM and PCB area, which is critical for a coin-cell-powered IoT node. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Compare 8051 variants," always structure your answer with a **comparison table** and highlight **3–4 key differentiators** (clock, memory, peripherals, application). This is the fastest way to score full marks. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: 8051/52 Variant Family Tree ```mermaid graph TD A[Intel 8051 1980
Original 8-bit MCU] --> B[Intel 8052 1984
Added Timer 2 256B RAM] A --> C[Atmel AT89C51
4KB Flash ISP] A --> D[Philips P89C51Rx2
64KB Flash IAP] A --> E[Dallas DS80C320
4T High-Speed Core] A --> F[SiLabs C8051F020
1T Mixed-Signal SoC] C --> G[Atmel AT89S52
SPI WDT Dual DPTR] C --> H[Atmel AT89S8253
2KB EEPROM] D --> I[Nuvoton N76E003
1T Low-Power 12-bit ADC] E --> J[Dallas DS89C450
1T 33 MIPS] F --> K[Infineon XC886
CAN LIN Automotive] F --> L[TI CC2530
2.4GHz RF ZigBee] L --> M[TI CC2540
BLE SoC IoT] ``` ## 4.2 Mapped Feature-Enhancement Flow ```mermaid graph LR P0[Program Memory] -->|4KB ROM| P1[8051] P1 -->|8KB ROM| P2[8052] P2 -->|4KB Flash| P3[AT89C51] P3 -->|8KB Flash| P4[AT89C52] P4 -->|12KB Flash + EEPROM| P5[AT89S8253] P4 -->|64KB Flash IAP| P6[P89V51RD2] P4 -->|64KB Flash 1T| P7[DS89C450] P7 -->|64KB Flash + ADC/DAC| P8[C8051F020] P8 -->|256KB Flash + RF| P9[CC2530] ``` ## 4.3 On-Chip Peripheral Integration Map ```mermaid graph TD CPU[8051/52 CPU Core] --> T[Timers/Counters] CPU --> U[UART] CPU --> IO[I/O Ports] CPU --> INT[Interrupt Controller] CPU --> MEM[Memory Controller] T -->|Variant Specific| T2[Timer 2 Capture Compare] U -->|Variant Specific| U2[Dual UART] IO -->|Variant Specific| IO2[Extended I/O 64-pin] MEM -->|Variant Specific| MEM2[Flash ISP IAP] MEM -->|Variant Specific| MEM3[EEPROM] CPU -.->|Enhanced Variants| PER[Integrated Peripherals] PER --> ADC[12-bit ADC] PER --> DAC[8-bit DAC] PER --> PWM[PWM Channels] PER --> SPI[SPI Interface] PER --> I2C[I2C SMBus] PER --> CAN[CAN Controller] PER --> RF[2.4 GHz RF Transceiver] PER --> WDT[Watchdog Timer] PER --> BOR[Brown-out Reset] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Block Diagram: Typical 8051/52 Variant Internal Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph External_Interface P0_PORT[Port 0 D0-D7] P1_PORT[Port 1 D8-D15] P2_PORT[Port 2 A8-A15] P3_PORT[Port 3 Alt Functions] end subgraph Core_Processing_Unit ALU[8-bit ALU] ACC[Accumulator A] B_REG[B Register] PSW[Program Status Word] end subgraph Memory_Subsystem PROG_MEM[Program Memory
Flash ROM 4-256 KB] DATA_MEM[Internal RAM
128-8192 Bytes] SFR[SFR Region
128 Bytes] DPTR[Data Pointer DPTR] end subgraph Control_Unit PC[16-bit Program Counter] SP[Stack Pointer] OSC[Oscillator Circuit] CLK_DIV[Clock Divider 12T/4T/1T] end subgraph On_Chip_Peripherals T0[Timer 0] T1[Timer 1] T2[Timer 2 Variant] UART1[UART] UART2[UART2 Variant] WDT[Watchdog Variant] ADC[ADC Variant] PWM[PWM Variant] end OSC --> CLK_DIV CLK_DIV --> CPU_BUS PC --> CPU_BUS ALU --> ACC ALU --> B_REG ALU --> PSW PROG_MEM --> CPU_BUS DATA_MEM --> CPU_BUS SFR --> CPU_BUS DPTR --> CPU_BUS CPU_BUS --> P0_PORT CPU_BUS --> P1_PORT CPU_BUS --> P2_PORT CPU_BUS --> P3_PORT T0 --> CPU_BUS T1 --> CPU_BUS T2 --> CPU_BUS UART1 --> CPU_BUS UART2 --> CPU_BUS WDT --> CPU_BUS ADC --> CPU_BUS PWM --> CPU_BUS ``` ## 4.5 Variant Selection Decision Flowchart ```mermaid graph TD START[Start: Application Requirements] --> Q1{Need Wireless?} Q1 -->|Yes BLE| BLE[CC2540 / CC2640] Q1 -->|Yes ZigBee| ZB[CC2530] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need ADC DAC?} Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{High Performance Needed?} Q2 -->|No| Q4{Low Power Critical?} Q3 -->|Yes| HS[C8051F020 / DS89C450] Q3 -->|No| ADCMCU[AT89S52 + External ADC] Q4 -->|Yes| LP[N76E003] Q4 -->|No| Q5{Automotive CAN?} Q5 -->|Yes| AUTO[Infineon XC886] Q5 -->|No| STD[AT89C52 / P89V51RD2] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: List any three enhanced features of AT89C52 compared to the standard Intel 8051.** **Model Answer:** The AT89C52, manufactured by Atmel, offers the following enhanced features over the standard Intel 8051: 1. **In-System Programmable (ISP) Flash Memory** — 8 KB of reprogrammable Flash replaces the factory-masked 4 KB ROM, enabling field updates and reducing development cost. 2. **256 bytes of on-chip RAM** (vs. 128 bytes in 8051), providing more space for stack and data variables. 3. **Three 16-bit Timers/Counters** (vs. 2 in 8051), with the additional **Timer 2** supporting auto-reload, capture, and compare modes for precise event timing and baud-rate generation. 4. **Higher clock speed** support up to 24 MHz, allowing faster instruction execution. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: What is the significance of the DS89C450 being a "1T" 8051 core?** **Model Answer:** The **DS89C450** uses a **1-clock-per-machine-cycle architecture** (1T), meaning each instruction executes in just **1 oscillator clock cycle**, compared to the **12 clocks per machine cycle** in the original Intel 8051. At its maximum clock of **33 MHz**, the DS89C450 achieves a throughput of **33 MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second), which is approximately **33 times faster** than a standard 8051 running at 12 MHz. This allows the DS89C450 to execute legacy 8051 code at vastly improved speed without any firmware modifications, making it ideal for **performance upgrades** in existing 8051-based products. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: Compare and contrast the features of the following 8051/52 variants: (a) Intel 8051, (b) AT89C52, (c) DS89C450, and (d) Silicon Labs C8051F020. Highlight the key architectural and peripheral differences.** #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **Describe the features of Intel 8051 and AT89C52.** **Model Solution:** **Intel 8051 (1980):** - **Program Memory:** 4 KB on-chip ROM (mask-programmed, not reprogrammable) - **Data Memory:** 128 bytes on-chip RAM + 128 bytes SFR region - **Timers/Counters:** 2 (Timer 0, Timer 1), both 16-bit - **UART:** 1 full-duplex serial port - **I/O Ports:** 4 × 8-bit bidirectional ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) = 32 I/O lines - **Clock:** Up to 12 MHz, 12 clocks per machine cycle → 1 µs per instruction - **Interrupts:** 5 sources (2 external, 2 timer, 1 serial) with 2 priority levels - **Drawbacks:** No reprogrammable memory, no on-chip ADC, no high-speed option. **Atmel AT89C52:** - **Program Memory:** 8 KB **Flash ROM** (reprogrammable in-system via ISP) - **Data Memory:** 256 bytes on-chip RAM - **Timers/Counters:** 3 (including Timer 2 with capture/compare) - **UART:** 1 - **Clock:** Up to 24 MHz - **Key Enhancements:** Flash memory eliminates need for external EPROM/UV eraser; Timer 2 enables advanced timing applications; 256-byte RAM allows larger stack. - **Application:** Widely used in academic labs, hobby projects, and cost-sensitive industrial controls. **Key Differences:** - AT89C52 has **reprogrammable Flash** (vs. masked ROM in 8051). - AT89C52 has **double the RAM** and **double the program memory**. - AT89C52 has an **extra Timer 2** with capture/compare capability. - AT89C52 supports **2× higher clock speed** (24 MHz vs. 12 MHz). #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **Describe the features of DS89C450 and C8051F020, and explain their application domains.** **Model Solution:** **Dallas DS89C450:** - **Core:** 1T (1-clock) 8051-compatible core - **Program Memory:** 64 KB Flash - **Data Memory:** 1 KB on-chip RAM - **Clock:** Up to 33 MHz → **33 MIPS** throughput - **Timers:** 3 (16-bit) - **UARTs:** 2 (full-duplex) - **I/O:** 32 pins - **Special Features:** Dual data pointers, hardware watchdog, power-on reset, brown-out detector. - **Application:** High-speed data acquisition, industrial control, and as a **drop-in upgrade** for legacy 8051 systems requiring 10×–30× speedup. **Silicon Labs C8051F020:** - **Core:** CIP-51 (1T 8051 core) - **Program Memory:** 64 KB Flash - **Data Memory:** 4 KB RAM (4352 bytes total) - **Clock:** Up to 25 MHz → 25 MIPS - **Timers:** 5 (16-bit) - **UARTs:** 2 - **I/O:** Up to 64 pins (8-bit ports) - **On-Chip Peripherals:** **12-bit 8-channel ADC** (100 ksps), **12-bit 2-channel DAC**, **PWM**, **SMBus (I²C)**, **SPI**, **5 comparators**, **voltage reference**, **temperature sensor**. - **Application:** Precision data acquisition systems, mixed-signal instrumentation, industrial sensors, portable medical devices. **Comparative Summary:** | Feature | DS89C450 | C8051F020 | |---|---|---| | Architecture | 1T, high-speed | 1T, mixed-signal | | ADC | None | 12-bit, 8-ch | | DAC | None | 12-bit, 2-ch | | Application Focus | Speed upgrade | Mixed-signal integration | **Conclusion:** The **DS89C450** is chosen for **computational speed upgrades**; the **C8051F020** is chosen for **mixed-signal SoC** applications requiring on-chip ADC/DAC. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: (a) Explain the key architectural differences between the standard 8051 core and the enhanced 1T/4T cores used in modern 8051 variants. How does this affect performance?** **(b) Discuss the role of the Philips/NXP P89V51RD2 and Infineon XC886 in embedded applications, with focus on their unique peripherals.** #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **Explain 12T vs. 4T vs. 1T 8051 cores and their performance impact.** **Model Solution:** **Standard 12T Core (Intel 8051, AT89C52):** - Each **machine cycle = 12 oscillator clocks**. - An instruction takes **1 or 2 machine cycles** (12 or 24 clocks). - At $f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz}$: instruction time = **1 µs**. - The bus is internally divided into 6 states × 2 phases, creating a complex timing structure inherited from Intel's early 1980s design. **4T Core (Dallas DS80C320):** - Each **machine cycle = 4 oscillator clocks**. - At $f_{osc} = 33 \text{ MHz}$: instruction time = 4/33 MHz ≈ **121 ns**. - Speedup over 12T at 12 MHz: 1 µs / 0.333 µs = **3× faster**. - Achieved by simplifying the internal state machine and using a pipelined fetch-execute unit. **1T Core (Dallas DS89C450, Silicon Labs C8051F020, Nuvoton N76E003):** - Each **machine cycle = 1 oscillator clock**. - At $f_{osc} = 33 \text{ MHz}$: instruction time = 1/33 MHz ≈ **30 ns**. - Speedup over 12T at 12 MHz: 1 µs / 30 ns ≈ **33× faster** (33 MIPS). - Uses a **pipelined RISC-like architecture** internally while maintaining binary compatibility with the 8051 instruction set. **Performance Impact:** $$\text{Speedup Factor} = \frac{12 \times f_{osc, 1T}}{f_{osc, 12T}}$$ For a **DS89C450 at 33 MHz** vs. **standard 8051 at 12 MHz**: $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{12 \times 33}{12} = 33 \times$$ **Trade-offs:** - 1T cores consume **more dynamic power** ($P \propto f \times C \times V^2$). - 1T cores may have **higher EMI** due to faster edge rates. - 12T cores are more **predictable** in real-time interrupt latency. #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **Discuss P89V51RD2 and Infineon XC886 with their unique peripherals.** **Model Solution:** **Philips/NXP P89V51RD2:** - **Program Memory:** 64 KB Flash with **In-Application Programming (IAP)** — allows the firmware to update itself at runtime, useful for remote firmware upgrades in deployed systems. - **Data Memory:** 1 KB RAM - **Timers:** 4 (including **PCA — Programmable Counter Array** for PWM, capture, compare, and watchdog functions). - **Communication:** UART, I²C - **Special Features:** **Dual data pointers**, **hardware watchdog timer**, **brown-out reset**, **keyboard interrupt** on P1. - **Applications:** Smart energy meters, industrial PLCs, consumer appliances requiring **field-upgradeable firmware**. **Infineon XC886/XC888:** - **Core:** Standard 8051 (12T) - **Program Memory:** 32 KB Flash - **Data Memory:** 1.75 KB RAM - **Key Peripherals:** - **CAN (Controller Area Network) 2.0B** interface — for automotive body networks (e.g., door modules, dashboard ECUs). - **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)** interface — for low-speed automotive sub-buses (e.g., window regulators, mirror controls). - **CCU6 (Capture Compare Unit 6)** — generates complementary PWM signals with **dead-time insertion** for 3-phase motor control (BLDC, PMSM). - **10-bit ADC** with 8 channels. - **On-chip temperature sensor** and **voltage regulator**. - **Applications:** **Automotive body electronics**, HVAC controls, electric window modules, wiper controllers, small motor drives. - **Why XC886 for automotive:** Automotive designs require **robust communication (CAN/LIN)**, **precise motor control (PWM)**, and **wide operating temperature range (-40°C to +125°C)** — all provided by the XC886. **Comparative Insight:** The P89V51RD2 targets **field-upgradeable industrial applications**, while the XC886 targets **automotive communication and motor control** — both are excellent examples of how the 8051 core has been specialized through on-chip peripherals to serve diverse embedded domains. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where Marks Are Lost):** > 1. **Confusing ROM with Flash:** Many students write "AT89C51 has 4 KB RAM" — this is wrong. The **4 KB is Flash (program memory)**, and the **RAM is 128 bytes**. This error alone costs **1–2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting Timer 2 in 8052:** The 8052 has **3 timers**, not 2. Timer 2 is a critical differentiator. Missing this loses marks in comparison questions. > 3. **Stating "8052 has 8 KB RAM":** Wrong. The 8052 has **8 KB ROM and 256 bytes RAM**. RAM and ROM are different things! > 4. **Ignoring the 1T/4T architecture distinction:** When asked about DS89C450 or C8051F020, students must mention the **1-clock machine cycle** as the key differentiator. Simply listing the clock frequency is insufficient. > 5. **Not drawing a comparison table:** KTU examiners reward structured answers. Always present variant comparisons in a **table format** with at least 5–6 parameters. > 6. **Missing application domains:** Each question on variants typically expects a brief mention of the **target application** (e.g., C8051F020 for data acquisition, CC2530 for wireless). Omitting this costs 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — 8051/52 Variants:** - ✅ The **Intel 8051** is the original 8-bit MCU (1980) with 4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM, 2 timers, 1 UART, 4 I/O ports. - ✅ The **Intel 8052** (1984) is a superset: 8 KB ROM, 256 B RAM, **3 timers (including Timer 2)**, 1 UART. - ✅ The **Atmel AT89C51/52** replaced ROM with **in-system programmable Flash** (4 KB / 8 KB), making it the most popular variant for academic use. - ✅ The **AT89S52** adds **SPI interface, hardware Watchdog Timer, and dual data pointers**. - ✅ The **P89V51RD2 (Philips/NXP)** offers **64 KB Flash, IAP, PCA for PWM**, and brown-out reset — suitable for industrial applications. - ✅ The **DS80C320/DS89C450 (Dallas/Maxim)** use **4T and 1T cores** respectively, achieving **8.25 MIPS and 33 MIPS** — ideal for high-speed upgrades. - ✅ The **C8051F020 (Silicon Labs)** is a **mixed-signal SoC** with **12-bit ADC, 12-bit DAC, PWM, I²C, SPI, and 2 UARTs** — ideal for data acquisition. - ✅ The **Infineon XC886** integrates **CAN and LIN** interfaces — designed for **automotive body electronics and motor control**. - ✅ The **TI CC2530/CC2540** integrate **2.4 GHz RF transceivers** (ZigBee/BLE) — used in **wireless sensor networks and IoT**. - ✅ The **Nuvoton N76E003** is a **low-cost, low-power 1T core** with 12-bit ADC — popular in modern consumer IoT. - ✅ Performance comparison formula: $T_{inst} = \frac{C}{f_{osc}} \times N_{cycles}$ where $C = 12, 4, \text{ or } 1$. - ✅ For KTU exams, always present comparisons in a **structured table** with **clock, memory, timers, peripherals, and application** as columns. - ✅ Remember the **three categories of enhancement**: (1) Memory expansion, (2) Peripheral integration, (3) Architectural speed (1T/4T cores). ---

topics.title: Addressing Modes Supported by 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Addressing Modes Supported by 8051 # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Addressing Mode** is the method by which an instruction in the 8051 microcontroller specifies the **location of its operand(s)** — i.e., where the CPU should fetch the data required to execute the instruction, or where the result should be stored. The **Intel 8051** family supports **five primary addressing modes** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECST746 (Embedded Systems, Module 2): 1. **Immediate Addressing Mode** 2. **Register Addressing Mode** 3. **Direct Addressing Mode** 4. **Register Indirect Addressing Mode** 5. **Indexed Addressing Mode** A sixth, derived mode (the **Implied/Inherent Addressing Mode** seen in instructions like `SWAP A`, `CPL A`) is universally acknowledged but is not the focus of KTU Part B questions. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Examination Mapping (Dec 2023 / June 2024 patterns):** > - Part A (3 marks): *"List the addressing modes of 8051"* or *"Differentiate between Direct and Register Indirect addressing"*. > - Part B (14 marks): Full descriptive question with sub-parts (a) 7 marks, (b) 7 marks. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Delivery Address" Metaphor Imagine you order a product from a courier service. You can communicate *where* the product should come from (or go to) in several different ways: - **"Send me a 25-rupee coin"** → The **value itself** is embedded in the instruction itself. *(This is **Immediate** addressing.)* - **"Get the coin from my pocket"** → You already have a fixed **pocket (register)** where the coin is stored. *(This is **Register** addressing.)* - **"Get the coin from drawer number 5 of my desk"** → A specific **drawer label (direct memory address)** is given. *(This is **Direct** addressing.)* - **"Go to the address written on this slip in your hand"** → A **register holds a pointer**, and the courier follows it. *(This is **Register Indirect** addressing.)* - **"Go to the address 'base + 3'"** → A **base offset** is added to an index. *(This is **Indexed** addressing.)* > [!TIP] > **The Golden Rule:** Every 8051 instruction has an **opcode** (operation) and an **operand** (location/value). The addressing mode describes *how* the operand is interpreted. --- ## 1.3 Why Addressing Modes Matter — Physical Constants & Metrics The 8051 operates with these fundamental storage metrics that govern which modes are usable: - **Internal RAM size: 128 bytes** (00H–7FH), expandable to 256 bytes in the 8052 variant. - **Special Function Registers (SFRs): 128 bytes** (80H–FFH). - **General-purpose registers: R0–R7** (32 bytes in 4 banks, occupying 00H–1FH). - **Data pointer: DPTR** — a 16-bit register for external memory access. - **Stack pointer: SP** — an 8-bit register pointing to the top of stack. - **Program counter: PC** — a 16-bit register holding the address of the next instruction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Board Note:** The addressing mode is **not chosen by the programmer alone** — it is **dictated by the instruction opcode**. For example, `MOV A, R0` uses *Register* addressing, but `MOV A, @R0` uses *Register Indirect* addressing. The mnemonic looks similar but the CPU behavior is completely different. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Addressing Modes — Structured Analytical Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Immediate Addressing Mode **How it works:** The operand (data) is a **constant** that is literally written as part of the instruction itself. The `#` symbol prefix indicates immediate data. **General Form:** $$ \texttt{INSTRUCTION DEST, \#data} $$ **Examples:** - `MOV A, #25H` → Load accumulator with hex value 25 (decimal 37). - `MOV R3, #0F0H` - `MOV DPTR, #2000H` (16-bit immediate) **Why it matters:** Used to load **constants** into registers or SFRs. Saves one memory cycle because no operand fetch is required from RAM. > [!NOTE] > **Opcode size:** 2 bytes for `MOV A, #data` (1 byte opcode + 1 byte immediate). For 16-bit immediate (`MOV DPTR`), the size is **3 bytes**. --- ### 2.1.2 Register Addressing Mode **How it works:** The operand resides in one of the eight **general-purpose registers R0–R7** of the currently selected register bank. The instruction opcode itself contains a 3-bit field identifying R0–R7. **General Form:** $$ \texttt{INSTRUCTION DEST, Rn} \quad \text{where } n \in \{0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7\} $$ **Examples:** - `MOV A, R0` - `ADD A, R1` - `DEC R3` **Register Bank Selection:** Controlled by bits **RS0 and RS1** in the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register. > [!IMPORTANT] > **PSW Register Layout:** > | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | > |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| > | CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | — | P | > > Bank selection: **RS1=0, RS0=0 → Bank 0 (00H–07H)**; **RS1=0, RS0=1 → Bank 1 (08H–0FH)**; etc. --- ### 2.1.3 Direct Addressing Mode **How it works:** The instruction specifies the **8-bit internal RAM address** (00H–7FH) or **SFR address** (80H–FFH) directly in the instruction. **General Form:** $$ \texttt{INSTRUCTION DEST, address\_8bit} $$ **Examples:** - `MOV A, 30H` → Copy contents of RAM[30H] into accumulator. - `MOV 30H, A` → Copy accumulator contents into RAM[30H]. - `MOV P1, #0FFH` → Direct addressing of Port 1 SFR. - `SETB PSW.3` → Direct bit addressing of an SFR bit. **Why it matters:** Provides direct access to **any internal RAM location** and **all SFRs**. Very commonly tested in KTU exams. --- ### 2.1.4 Register Indirect Addressing Mode **How it works:** A register is used as a **pointer** to the memory location. The `@` symbol denotes indirection. The 8051 supports indirection using: - **R0 or R1** (8-bit pointer, accesses internal RAM 00H–FFH). - **SP** (stack pointer, used implicitly). - **DPTR** (16-bit pointer, used for external data memory via `MOVX`). **General Form:** $$ \texttt{INSTRUCTION DEST, @Rn} \quad \text{or} \quad \texttt{INSTRUCTION DEST, @DPTR} $$ **Examples:** - `MOV A, @R0` → A ← RAM[address stored in R0]. - `ADD A, @R1` - `MOVX A, @DPTR` → Read from external data memory at the 16-bit address in DPTR. **Pointer Arithmetic:** In indirect mode, incrementing `Rn` (`INC Rn`) effectively moves the pointer through a block of memory — the foundation of **block data transfer**. --- ### 2.1.5 Indexed Addressing Mode **How it works:** Used exclusively for **read-only lookup table access** in program memory (code ROM). The effective address is the sum of a **base address** (from DPTR or PC) and the **accumulator** (used as an index offset). **General Form:** $$ \text{Effective Address} = \text{Base} + \text{Offset (from A)} $$ **Two Variants:** 1. **MOVC A, @A + DPTR** → Base = DPTR (programmer-controlled). 2. **MOVC A, @A + PC** → Base = PC (current program counter, auto-advances). **Examples:** - `MOV DPTR, #0400H` → `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` reads code ROM[0400H + A]. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table for last-day revision.** Every row is a **verified KTU 2024 exam-relevant fact**. ### Master Table — 8051 Addressing Modes Summary | **Mode** | **Syntax Pattern** | **Operand Source** | **Opcode Size** | **Cycles** | **Example Instruction** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Immediate | `DEST, #data` | Constant in instruction | 2 or 3 bytes | 1 | `MOV A, #25H` | | Register | `DEST, Rn` | R0–R7 of current bank | 1 byte | 1 | `MOV A, R3` | | Direct | `DEST, addr8` | Internal RAM 00H–7FH / SFR 80H–FFH | 2 bytes | 1 | `MOV A, 30H` | | Register Indirect | `DEST, @Rn` or `@DPTR` | Pointer-held address | 1 byte | 1 (internal), 2 (`MOVX`) | `MOV A, @R0` | | Indexed | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` | Code ROM (table lookup) | 1 byte | 2 | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | ### Memory Map Constants for Addressing | **Memory Region** | **Address Range** | **Pointer Used (Indirect)** | **Access Instruction** | |---|---|---|---| | Internal RAM (Lower) | 00H – 7FH | R0 / R1 (8-bit) | `MOV` family | | Internal RAM (Upper / SFR) | 80H – FFH | R0 / R1 only (with PSW.3 set or direct) | `MOV` family | | External Data Memory | 0000H – FFFFH | DPTR (16-bit) | `MOVX` family | | Code ROM (Program Memory) | 0000H – FFFFH | DPTR or PC + A | `MOVC` family | ### KTU Critical Mnemonics | **Keyword in Opcode** | **Memory Type Accessed** | **Address Width** | |---|---|---| | `MOV` | Internal RAM / SFR | 8-bit | | `MOVX` | External Data Memory | 16-bit | | `MOVC` | Code ROM (Program Memory) | 16-bit | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** In modern ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers (the de-facto successors to 8051), the same five addressing paradigms are preserved but with 32-bit extensions, multiple load/store instructions, and PC-relative indexing. Understanding 8051 modes is the **gateway to ARMv7-M addressing architecture**. --- ## 2.3 Engineering Significance The choice of addressing mode directly affects: - **Code size (bytes consumed)** - **Execution time (machine cycles)** - **Energy consumption (critical for battery-powered IoT nodes)** - **Programmer flexibility** in writing compact, efficient firmware. For example, `INC R0` (Register mode, 1 byte) vs. `INC 30H` (Direct mode, 2 bytes) — both increment the same byte if R0 contains 30H, but register mode saves 1 byte. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Address Calculation for Indexed Mode **Problem Statement:** Suppose `DPTR = 0400H` and `A = 05H`. Find the address in program memory accessed by `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`. **Step 1:** Identify the base address. $$ \text{Base} = \text{DPTR} = 0400\text{H} $$ **Step 2:** Identify the offset (contents of accumulator). $$ \text{Offset} = A = 05\text{H} = 0000\,0101_{\text{b}} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the **Effective Address** by adding base + offset. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Effective Address} &= \text{DPTR} + A \\ &= 0400\text{H} + 0005\text{H} \\ &= 0405\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** The CPU fetches the byte at program memory location `0405H` and loads it into the accumulator. **Step 5 (Alternate — PC-Relative):** For `MOVC A, @A+PC`: - Before execution, PC is already at the address of the *next* instruction (PC has auto-incremented during fetch). - Let `PC_current = 0200H` (after fetch), then `Effective Address = 0200H + A`. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Block Copy Using Register Indirect Mode **Problem Statement:** Copy 10 bytes from internal RAM starting at source address `30H` to destination `50H` using R0 and R1 as pointers. **Initialization:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{R0} &\leftarrow 30\text{H} \quad (\text{Source pointer}) \\ \text{R1} &\leftarrow 50\text{H} \quad (\text{Destination pointer}) \\ \text{R2} &\leftarrow 0\text{AH} \quad (\text{Counter} = 10) \end{aligned} $$ **Loop Execution (one iteration):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & MOV \,\, A, @R0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow \text{RAM}[R0] = \text{RAM}[30\text{H}] \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & MOV \,\, @R1, A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{RAM}[R1] \leftarrow A = \text{RAM}[50\text{H}] \leftarrow \text{RAM}[30\text{H}] \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & INC \,\, R0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{R0} \leftarrow 31\text{H} \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & INC \,\, R1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{R1} \leftarrow 51\text{H} \\ \text{Step 5:} \quad & DJNZ \,\, R2, \text{LOOP} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{R2} \leftarrow \text{R2}-1; \text{jump if not zero} \end{aligned} $$ **Loop termination:** After 10 iterations, R2 = 0, loop exits. The block from `30H–39H` has been mirrored to `50H–59H`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key takeaway:** Register Indirect mode is the *only* mode (besides DPTR-based `MOVX`) that allows **runtime-variable addressing** — i.e., the address itself is computed at execution time. Direct addressing always uses a **fixed** address hard-coded into the instruction. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Addressing Mode Decision Tree Use this matrix to identify the addressing mode from any 8051 instruction: | **Instruction Has `#` ?** | **Instruction Has `@` ?** | **Has `Rn` operand?** | **Special Mnemonic?** | **Mode Identified** | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | — | — | **Immediate** | | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ R0–R7 | — | **Register** | | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ | Address is 8-bit literal | **Direct** | | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `@R0` or `@R1` | — | **Register Indirect** | | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `@DPTR` | `MOVX` | **Register Indirect (External)** | | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `@A+...` | `MOVC` | **Indexed** | | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ | e.g. `SWAP A`, `NOP` | **Implied** | --- ## 3.4 Numerical Example — SFR Access **Instruction:** `MOV P1, #0FH` **Step 1:** Decode opcode. Opcode identifies `MOV direct, #immediate`. **Step 2:** Destination operand is `P1`. From the SFR map, `P1 = 90H`. **Step 3:** Effective destination address = **90H** (Direct addressing). **Step 4:** Source = `0FH` (Immediate). **Step 5:** After execution, pins P1.0–P1.3 are LOW, P1.4–P1.7 are HIGH. --- ## 3.5 Numerical Example — Indirect with DPTR (External Memory) **Instruction sequence:** $$ \begin{aligned} & \text{MOV DPTR, \#2000H} \\ & \text{MOVX A, @DPTR} \\ & \text{MOV R0, A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step evaluation:** | **Step** | **Action** | **Register State After** | |---|---|---| | 1 | Load DPTR with immediate 2000H | DPTR = 2000H | | 2 | Read external data memory at 2000H into A | A = content at external[2000H] | | 3 | Copy A to R0 | R0 = A | **Machine cycles:** Step 2 takes 2 cycles (read from external bus); Steps 1 and 3 take 1 cycle each. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — How Addressing Modes Route Data in 8051 ```mermaid flowchart TD PC["Program Counter PC"] --> IF["Instruction Fetch Unit"] IF --> DEC["Instruction Decoder"] DEC --> MODE{"Identify Addressing Mode"} MODE -->|Immediate| IMM["Operand = constant in instruction

No.** | **Addressing Mode** | **Example Instruction** | **Explanation** | |:---:|---|---|---| | 1 | Immediate Addressing | `MOV A, #25H` | Operand `25H` is a constant embedded in the instruction | | 2 | Register Addressing | `MOV A, R0` | Operand is the content of register R0 | | 3 | Direct Addressing | `MOV A, 30H` | Operand is in internal RAM at direct address `30H` | | 4 | Register Indirect Addressing | `MOV A, @R0` | Address of operand is held in R0 | | 5 | Indexed Addressing | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Effective address = A + DPTR (used for ROM tables) | **(3 marks — full credit if all 5 listed with correct example each.)** --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Q: Differentiate between Direct Addressing and Register Indirect Addressing modes of 8051.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Parameter** | **Direct Addressing** | **Register Indirect Addressing** | |---|---|---| | Symbol used | Address literal (e.g., 30H) | `@` followed by register (e.g., `@R0`) | | Address source | Hard-coded in instruction | Stored in a register at runtime | | Address range | 8-bit (00H–FFH) | R0/R1 → 8-bit; DPTR → 16-bit | | Pointer variable? | No | Yes | | Example | `MOV A, 30H` | `MOV A, @R0` | | Used for | Accessing fixed RAM/SFR locations | Block transfer, array traversal | --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **Q: Explain the different addressing modes supported by the 8051 microcontroller with suitable examples. Also explain how indexed addressing is useful in implementing lookup tables.** #### **Part (a) — Addressing Modes Explanation (7 marks)** The 8051 supports **five addressing modes**: **1. Immediate Addressing (1.5 marks):** The data operand is a constant that forms part of the instruction itself. It is indicated by the `#` prefix. - `MOV A, #0FH` → Load A with 0FH. - `MOV DPTR, #2000H` → 16-bit immediate load. **2. Register Addressing (1 mark):** The operand is one of the eight general-purpose registers R0–R7 in the currently selected bank (selected via PSW bits RS0, RS1). - `ADD A, R3` → Add R3 to A. **3. Direct Addressing (1.5 marks):** The instruction contains the 8-bit address of the operand. Allows access to internal RAM (00H–7FH) and SFRs (80H–FFH). - `MOV A, 50H` → A ← RAM[50H]. - `SETB P1.0` → Set bit 0 of Port 1 SFR. **4. Register Indirect Addressing (1.5 marks):** A register holds the address of the operand. R0, R1 (8-bit), and DPTR (16-bit) may act as pointer registers. Indicated by `@`. - `MOV A, @R0` → A ← RAM[R0]. - `MOVX A, @DPTR` → A ← External RAM[DPTR]. **5. Indexed Addressing (1.5 marks):** Used for code memory lookup tables. Effective address = Base + Accumulator. - `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (programmer-controlled base). - `MOVC A, @A+PC` (PC-relative, auto-advancing base). #### **Part (b) — Indexed Addressing for Lookup Tables (7 marks)** **Concept (2 marks):** Indexed addressing enables reading constant data (tables such as 7-segment codes, sine wave samples, ASCII conversion) from code ROM. The base address (DPTR) is set once, and the accumulator is loaded with an index value to retrieve the corresponding entry. **Example Implementation (5 marks):** Suppose we have a lookup table of square numbers from 0² to 4² stored in code ROM starting at `0300H`. ``` ORG 0300H TABLE: DB 00H, 01H, 04H, 09H, 10H ; 0², 1², 2², 3², 4² ... MOV DPTR, #TABLE ; DPTR = 0300H MOV A, #03 ; A = 3 (we want 3²) MOVC A, @A+DPTR ; A ← code[0300H + 03] = 09H ``` **Effective Address calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} EA &= DPTR + A \\ &= 0300H + 03H \\ &= 0303H \end{aligned} $$ After execution, `A = 09H = 9`, the square of 3. **Advantages (for remaining marks):** - Saves internal RAM (data stays in ROM). - Faster execution than computation. - Cleaner, more maintainable firmware. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Q: With neat examples, explain the Register and Register Indirect addressing modes of 8051. Write an 8051 assembly program to copy a block of 10 bytes from internal RAM location 30H to location 60H using register indirect addressing.** #### **Part (a) — Register & Register Indirect Modes (7 marks)** **Register Addressing Mode (3.5 marks):** The operand resides in one of the eight registers R0–R7. There are four banks of registers (each 8 bytes), occupying RAM locations 00H–1FH. The active bank is selected by the RS0 and RS1 bits of the PSW register. **Instruction format:** `OPCODE DEST, Rn` **Examples:** - `MOV A, R5` → Copy R5 into accumulator. - `INC R0` → Increment R0. - `ADD A, R2` → Add R2 to accumulator. - `XCH A, R1` → Exchange A with R1. **Advantages:** - Compact (1-byte opcode, e.g., `08H–0FH` for INC R0–R7). - Fastest mode (no operand fetch). - Used in loops with `DJNZ Rn, label`. **Register Indirect Addressing Mode (3.5 marks):** The address of the operand is contained in a register. R0, R1 (8-bit) can access internal RAM (00H–FFH); DPTR (16-bit) accesses external data memory. **Instruction format:** `OPCODE DEST, @Rn` or `OPCODE DEST, @DPTR` **Examples:** - `MOV A, @R0` → Load A from RAM address pointed by R0. - `MOV @R1, A` → Store A at RAM address pointed by R1. - `MOVX A, @DPTR` → Read external RAM at DPTR into A. - `MOVX @DPTR, A` → Write A to external RAM at DPTR. **Pointer Increment/Decrement:** - `INC R0` / `DEC R0` modifies the pointer without affecting the actual operand. #### **Part (b) — Block Copy Program (7 marks)** ```asm ORG 0000H MOV R0, #30H ; R0 = source pointer (1 mark) MOV R1, #60H ; R1 = destination pointer (1 mark) MOV R2, #0AH ; R2 = counter (10 decimal) (1 mark) LOOP: MOV A, @R0 ; A = RAM[R0] (1 mark) MOV @R1, A ; RAM[R1] = A (1 mark) INC R0 ; R0++ (0.5 mark) INC R1 ; R1++ (0.5 mark) DJNZ R2, LOOP ; Decrement R2; if not zero, jump to LOOP (1 mark) END ; End of program ``` **Trace (for one iteration):** | **Cycle** | **R0** | **R1** | **R2** | **Action** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Initial | 30H | 60H | 0AH | Setup | | Iter 1 | 31H | 61H | 09H | Copied RAM[30H] → RAM[60H] | | Iter 2 | 32H | 62H | 08H | Copied RAM[31H] → RAM[61H] | | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | | Iter 10 | 3AH | 6AH | 00H | Loop exits | After 10 iterations, RAM block `30H–39H` is mirrored at `60H–69H`. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly LOSE marks in KTU exams on this topic:** > > 1. **Confusing Register vs. Register Indirect mode** — writing `MOV A, R0` instead of `MOV A, @R0` (or vice versa) costs **1 full mark**. > 2. **Forgetting the `#` symbol** — writing `MOV A, 25H` instead of `MOV A, #25H` completely changes the mode (Direct vs. Immediate). This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation keys. > 3. **Mixing up `MOV`, `MOVX`, `MOVC`** — these three mnemonics access *different* memory spaces. Using `MOV` instead of `MOVX` for external memory access is **wrong** and flagged. > 4. **Omitting the Effective Address calculation** in Indexed mode answers. KTU 2024 scheme explicitly asks for the EA computation — **don't skip it**. > 5. **Not specifying register bank** when answering Register mode questions. Always mention PSW bits RS0/RS1 for full marks. > 6. **Writing `MOVC` for internal/external data memory** — `MOVC` is exclusively for code ROM. Valued strictly. > 7. **Spelling errors:** "addressing mode" vs. "addressing mod" — examiners are instructed to deduct **0.5 mark** for repeated spelling errors in 14-mark answers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this section or screenshot it before walking into the exam hall.** ### ✅ Must-Know List (Every Item = Guaranteed KTU Question) - [ ] The 8051 supports **exactly 5 addressing modes** (Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed). - [ ] **`#` prefix = Immediate**; **`@` prefix = Indirect or Indexed**. - [ ] Only **R0 and R1** can be used as 8-bit pointer registers for Register Indirect mode. - [ ] **DPTR** is a 16-bit pointer used exclusively with **`MOVX`** (external RAM) and **`MOVC`** (code ROM). - [ ] `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` and `MOVC A, @A+PC` are the **only two** Indexed mode instructions in 8051. - [ ] **Effective Address in Indexed mode** = Base + Accumulator (DPTR or PC as base). - [ ] Register bank is selected by **PSW bits RS0 and RS1**. - [ ] **Direct addressing** can access SFRs (80H–FFH); **Register Indirect** with R0/R1 can also reach SFRs. - [ ] For 14-mark questions, **always include opcode size, cycle count, and a numerical example**. - [ ] `MOV` = internal data; `MOVX` = external data; `MOVC` = code memory. ### 🔁 Quick Memory Aid > **"**#** Comes Early, @ Comes Indirectly"** > - `#` (hash) → **immediate data** baked into the instruction. > - `@` (at) → **pointer dereference** for indirect/indexed addressing. ### 🎯 High-Probability KTU Question Topics for 2025 1. Block copy program using Register Indirect mode (likely 14-mark question). 2. Indexed mode for lookup table implementation. 3. Comparison table between Direct and Register Indirect mode. 4. PSW bank selection with Register mode. 5. Identifying the addressing mode from a given instruction (reverse problem). --- **End of Module 2 Notes — Addressing Modes Supported by 8051** *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) | PECST746 — Embedded Systems*

topics.title: 8051 Instruction Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** 8051 Instruction Set # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **8051 Instruction Set** is the complete collection of machine-readable binary commands (opcodes) recognized by the Intel MCS-51 (8051) microcontroller's Central Processing Unit (CPU). Each instruction is an 8-bit, 16-bit, or 24-bit binary pattern that the CPU's instruction decoder interprets to perform a specific operation such as data movement, arithmetic, logical manipulation, boolean bit-level processing, or control flow branching. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for PECST746, the 8051 instruction set is classified based on: - **Functionality** (Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean, Branching) - **Operand Count** (1-byte, 2-byte, 3-byte instructions) - **Addressing Mode** (Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 has **111 distinct instructions** (some sources say 255 opcodes with 111 mnemonics) executing across **255 opcodes**, supporting **7 distinct addressing modes**, and operating on **8-bit ALU data** with selected 16-bit operations (DPTR, PC, timers). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine the **8051 Instruction Set** as a **vocabulary dictionary** that the microcontroller "understands": - **The CPU** is like a chef 👨‍🍳 in a kitchen. - **The Instruction Set** is the chef's recipe book 📖 containing exactly 111 unique recipes (instructions). - **Each instruction** is a single recipe step (e.g., "add 5 to the batter", "stir for 30 seconds", "check if it's done"). - **The opcode** is the recipe's ID number (a binary code like `0110 0100`). - **The operand(s)** are the ingredients (the data on which the chef operates). - **The addressing mode** is *how* the chef gets the ingredients — from a labeled shelf (direct), from a numbered bin (register), or by following a note that points to a shelf (indirect). Just as a chef cannot perform actions not listed in the recipe book, the 8051 cannot execute instructions outside its defined set. The 8051 uses **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** architecture — meaning each instruction can be of variable length (1, 2, or 3 bytes), which is more compact but slightly slower to decode than RISC. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Every C program written for the 8051 is ultimately translated (compiled) into a sequence of these 111 instructions. Writing in assembly gives direct control; using C relies on the compiler to choose the right instructions. ## 1.3 Instruction Format — The Anatomy of an 8051 Instruction An 8051 instruction consists of two parts: 1. **Opcode (Operation Code):** The first byte (or first few bits) tells the CPU *what* to do. 2. **Operand(s):** Zero, one, or two bytes specifying *where* the data is or *where* to put the result. ### Structure of a 3-Byte Instruction (Most Common) | Byte 1 (Opcode) | Byte 2 (Operand 1) | Byte 3 (Operand 2) | |-----------------|--------------------|--------------------| | `ADD A, #45H` | `45H` (immediate) | — | ### Structure of a 1-Byte Instruction (No Operand) Examples: `INC A`, `CPL A`, `RL A` — opcode *implicitly* includes the register. ### Structure of a 2-Byte Instruction Example: `ADD A, #0FH` → Opcode + immediate value. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of 8051 Instructions (KTU High-Yield) The 8051 instruction set is broadly divided into **5 functional groups** for KTU exam purposes: ### 2.1.1 Data Transfer Instructions (29 Instructions) Move data between internal RAM, SFRs, and registers. **Do not modify any flags** (except `MOVX` indirectly). | Mnemonic | Operation | Bytes | Cycles | |----------|-----------|-------|--------| | `MOV A, Rn` | Copy register Rn to A | 1 | 12 | | `MOV A, direct` | Copy direct byte to A | 2 | 12 | | `MOV A, @Ri` | Copy RAM[Ri] to A | 1 | 12 | | `MOV A, #data` | Load immediate to A | 2 | 12 | | `MOV Rn, A` | Copy A to Rn | 1 | 12 | | `MOV Rn, direct` | Copy direct to Rn | 2 | 24 | | `MOV Rn, #data` | Load immediate to Rn | 2 | 12 | | `MOV direct, A` | Copy A to direct | 2 | 12 | | `MOV direct, Rn` | Copy Rn to direct | 2 | 24 | | `MOV direct, direct` | Copy direct to direct | 3 | 24 | | `MOV direct, @Ri` | Copy RAM[Ri] to direct | 2 | 24 | | `MOV direct, #data` | Load immediate to direct | 3 | 24 | | `MOV @Ri, A` | Copy A to RAM[Ri] | 1 | 12 | | `MOV @Ri, direct` | Copy direct to RAM[Ri] | 2 | 24 | | `MOV @Ri, #data` | Load immediate to RAM[Ri] | 2 | 12 | | `MOV DPTR, #data16` | Load 16-bit immediate to DPTR | 3 | 24 | | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Code memory read (DPTR-based) | 1 | 24 | | `MOVC A, @A+PC` | Code memory read (PC-based) | 1 | 24 | | `MOVX A, @Ri` | External RAM read (8-bit addr) | 1 | 24 | | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | External RAM read (16-bit addr) | 1 | 24 | | `MOVX @Ri, A` | External RAM write (8-bit addr) | 1 | 24 | | `MOVX @DPTR, A` | External RAM write (16-bit addr) | 1 | 24 | | `PUSH direct` | Push to stack | 2 | 24 | | `POP direct` | Pop from stack | 2 | 24 | | `XCH A, Rn` | Exchange A with Rn | 1 | 12 | | `XCH A, direct` | Exchange A with direct | 2 | 12 | | `XCH A, @Ri` | Exchange A with RAM[Ri] | 1 | 12 | | `XCHD A, @Ri` | Exchange digit (low nibble) | 1 | 12 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Mnemonic: "MOV" is King.** All data transfer uses MOV, MOVC (code memory), or MOVX (external memory). The 'C' stands for "Code" and 'X' for "eXternal." ### 2.1.2 Arithmetic Instructions (24 Instructions) Operate on the **Accumulator (A)** and update **PSW (Program Status Word)** flags: **CY (Carry), AC (Auxiliary Carry), OV (Overflow), P (Parity)**. #### A. Addition Instructions | Mnemonic | Operation | |----------|-----------| | `ADD A, Rn` | A = A + Rn | | `ADD A, direct` | A = A + (direct) | | `ADD A, @Ri` | A = A + RAM[Ri] | | `ADD A, #data` | A = A + data | | `ADDC A, Rn` | A = A + Rn + CY | | `ADDC A, direct` | A = A + (direct) + CY | | `ADDC A, @Ri` | A = A + RAM[Ri] + CY | | `ADDC A, #data` | A = A + data + CY | #### B. Subtraction Instructions (Use 2's Complement + CY) | Mnemonic | Operation | |----------|-----------| | `SUBB A, Rn` | A = A - Rn - CY | | `SUBB A, direct` | A = A - (direct) - CY | | `SUBB A, @Ri` | A = A - RAM[Ri] - CY | | `SUBB A, #data` | A = A - data - CY | #### C. Increment / Decrement | Mnemonic | Operation | |----------|-----------| | `INC A` | A = A + 1 (no flag change) | | `INC Rn` | Rn = Rn + 1 | | `INC direct` | (direct) = (direct) + 1 | | `INC @Ri` | RAM[Ri] = RAM[Ri] + 1 | | `INC DPTR` | DPTR = DPTR + 1 | | `DEC A` | A = A - 1 (no flag change) | | `DEC Rn` | Rn = Rn - 1 | | `DEC direct` | (direct) = (direct) - 1 | | `DEC @Ri` | RAM[Ri] = RAM[Ri] - 1 | #### D. Multiplication, Division, Decimal Adjust | Mnemonic | Operation | |----------|-----------| | `MUL AB` | B:A = A × B (8-bit × 8-bit = 16-bit) | | `DIV AB` | A = A / B; B = A mod B | | `DA A` | Decimal Adjust A (for BCD) | > [!NOTE] > **Multiplication & Division Details:** > - `MUL AB`: Result in B (high byte) and A (low byte). If product > 255, OV = 1; else OV = 0. CY = 0. > - `DIV AB`: Quotient in A, remainder in B. If B = 0, OV = 1. ### 2.1.3 Logical Instructions (25 Instructions) Perform bitwise AND, OR, XOR, NOT, rotate, clear, swap. | Mnemonic | Operation | |----------|-----------| | `ANL A, Rn` / `ANL A, direct` / `ANL A, @Ri` / `ANL A, #data` | A = A AND operand | | `ANL direct, A` | (direct) = (direct) AND A | | `ANL direct, #data` | (direct) = (direct) AND data | | `ORL A, Rn` / `ORL A, direct` / `ORL A, @Ri` / `ORL A, #data` | A = A OR operand | | `ORL direct, A` | (direct) = (direct) OR A | | `ORL direct, #data` | (direct) = (direct) OR data | | `XRL A, Rn` / `XRL A, direct` / `XRL A, @Ri` / `XRL A, #data` | A = A XOR operand | | `XRL direct, A` | (direct) = (direct) XOR A | | `XRL direct, #data` | (direct) = (direct) XOR data | | `CLR A` | A = 00H | | `CPL A` | A = NOT A (1's complement) | | `RL A` | Rotate A left (no CY) | | `RLC A` | Rotate A left through CY (9-bit rotate) | | `RR A` | Rotate A right (no CY) | | `RRC A` | Rotate A right through CY (9-bit rotate) | | `SWAP A` | Swap low/high nibbles of A | ### 2.1.4 Boolean (Bit) Instructions — (17 Instructions) The 8051 has a powerful **bit-addressable** capability. The SFR space from addresses **20H–2FH** (16 bytes = 128 bits) and bit-addressable SFRs (P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, SCON, etc., addresses ending in 0H or 8H) allow bit-level operations. | Mnemonic | Operation | |----------|-----------| | `CLR C` | CY = 0 | | `CLR bit` | bit = 0 | | `SETB C` | CY = 1 | | `SETB bit` | bit = 1 | | `CPL C` | CY = NOT CY | | `CPL bit` | bit = NOT bit | | `ANL C, bit` | CY = CY AND bit | | `ANL C, /bit` | CY = CY AND (NOT bit) | | `ORL C, bit` | CY = CY OR bit | | `ORL C, /bit` | CY = CY OR (NOT bit) | | `MOV C, bit` | CY = bit | | `MOV bit, C` | bit = CY | | `JC rel` | Jump if CY = 1 | | `JNC rel` | Jump if CY = 0 | | `JB bit, rel` | Jump if bit = 1 | | `JNB bit, rel` | Jump if bit = 0 | | `JBC bit, rel` | Jump if bit = 1 and clear bit | ### 2.1.5 Program Branching Instructions (16 Instructions) | Mnemonic | Operation | |----------|-----------| | `ACALL addr11` | Absolute call (2KB page) | | `LCALL addr16` | Long call (64KB) | | `RET` | Return from subroutine | | `RETI` | Return from interrupt | | `AJMP addr11` | Absolute jump (2KB) | | `LJMP addr16` | Long jump (64KB) | | `SJMP rel` | Short jump (relative, -128 to +127) | | `JMP @A+DPTR` | Jump indirect (computed) | | `JZ rel` | Jump if A = 0 | | `JNZ rel` | Jump if A ≠ 0 | | `CJNE A, direct, rel` | Compare and jump if not equal | | `CJNE A, #data, rel` | Compare and jump if not equal | | `CJNE Rn, #data, rel` | Compare and jump if not equal | | `CJNE @Ri, #data, rel` | Compare and jump if not equal | | `DJNZ Rn, rel` | Decrement and jump if not zero | | `DJNZ direct, rel` | Decrement and jump if not zero | | `NOP` | No operation (1 byte, 1 cycle) | ## 2.2 Addressing Modes (KTU High-Yield — 7 Modes) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Focus:** Addressing modes are a high-weightage topic. Memorize the 7 modes with examples. | # | Mode | Syntax | Example | Description | |---|------|--------|---------|-------------| | 1 | **Immediate** | `#data` | `MOV A, #25H` | Constant embedded in instruction | | 2 | **Register** | `Rn` (R0–R7) | `MOV A, R3` | Operands in selected register bank | | 3 | **Direct** | `direct` (0–255) | `MOV A, 30H` | Internal RAM or SFR address | | 4 | **Register Indirect** | `@Ri` (R0, R1) | `MOV A, @R0` | Address in R0 or R1 (internal RAM only) | | 5 | **Indexed** | `@A+DPTR` / `@A+PC` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | For look-up tables (code ROM) | | 6 | **Relative** | `rel` (signed offset) | `SJMP HERE` | For branch instructions | | 7 | **Absolute** | `addr11` / `addr16` | `ACALL / LCALL` | For AJMP/ACALL/LJMP/LCALL | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** `@Ri` (R2–R7) is **NOT allowed** for register indirect. Only **R0 and R1** can be used. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Instruction Count Summary | Category | Instruction Count (Approx.) | |----------|-----------------------------| | Data Transfer | 29 | | Arithmetic | 24 | | Logical | 25 | | Boolean (Bit) | 17 | | Branching | 16 | | **Total** | **111** | ## 2.4 PSW (Program Status Word) Flag Summary | Flag | Bit | Set When | |------|-----|----------| | **CY** | PSW.7 | Carry out from bit 7 (add) or borrow (sub) | | **AC** | PSW.6 | Carry out from bit 3 (auxiliary, for BCD) | | **F0** | PSW.5 | User-defined flag | | **RS1** | PSW.4 | Register bank select high | | **RS0** | PSW.3 | Register bank select low | | **OV** | PSW.2 | Signed arithmetic overflow | | **—** | PSW.1 | Reserved (user bit addressable) | | **P** | PSW.0 | Parity of A (1 if odd # of 1's) | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded Firmware Development:** Every embedded C program ultimately compiles to these 111 instructions. - **Assembly Optimization:** Time-critical routines (interrupt service routines, signal processing) are hand-written in 8051 assembly. - **Reverse Engineering & Security:** Firmware analysts use 8051 instruction set knowledge to decompile binary code for bug fixing or malware analysis in IoT devices. - **Automotive & Industrial:** Legacy 8051 cores are still used in automotive ECUs, washing machines, and remote controls. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: ADD Instruction Flag Behavior > This is a high-frequency KTU exam question.
