| 8 | $R_{th} = \frac{T_j - T_a}{P}$ | $R_{th}$ = thermal resistance, $T_j$ = junction, $T_a$ = ambient | °C/W | Thermal management of power ICs |
| 9 | $C_{dec} \geq \frac{\Delta I \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta V}$ | Decoupling capacitor sizing for transient current | Farads | Decoupling capacitor selection |
| 10 | $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | CMOS dynamic power, $\alpha$ = switching activity | W | MCU power estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT] Exam Tip
> The formulas $C_L$, $P_{dynamic}$, and $L_{crit}$ are **favourite KTU questions** in the 14-mark ESE pattern. Memorize the **units** and the **physical meaning** of every variable.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IoT Wearables**: Hardware design prioritizes ultra-low power (BLE SoCs like nRF52840, $I_{sleep} \approx 1.5\ \mu A$) and miniature PCBs (4-layer flex).
- **Automotive ECUs**: Hardware must meet **AEC-Q100** qualification, **ISO 26262** functional safety, and operate from **−40 °C to +125 °C**.
- **Industrial PLCs**: Hardware requires **galvanic isolation** (opto-couplers, digital isolators) and **24 V** industrial supply tolerance.
- **Medical Devices**: Hardware must follow **IEC 60601** for patient safety, requiring **creepage/clearance** distances of **\geq 8 mm** for mains isolation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Crystal Load Capacitor Sizing

This is a **very high-yield derivation** for KTU ESE. The crystal manufacturer specifies a **load capacitance $C_L$** (typically 8 pF, 12 pF, or 18 pF). The designer must select two external capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ such that the equivalent capacitance seen by the crystal equals $C_L$.

### Derivation

$$
C_{eq} = C_1 \parallel C_2 + C_{stray}
$$

where $C_1 \parallel C_2 = \dfrac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$.

Setting $C_{eq} = C_L$:

$$
C_L = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2} + C_{stray}
$$

For a **symmetric design** ($C_1 = C_2 = C$):

$$
C_L = \frac{C^2}{2C} + C_{stray} = \frac{C}{2} + C_{stray}
$$

Solving for $C$:

$$
C = 2 (C_L - C_{stray})
$$

### Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE] Worked Problem
> A microcontroller uses an **8 MHz external crystal** with a specified load capacitance $C_L = 12$ pF. The PCB stray capacitance is estimated at $C_{stray} = 5$ pF. Find the value of the two external load capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ (symmetric design).

**Step 1**: Identify the formula.

$$
C = 2 (C_L - C_{stray})
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the values (note: $C_L$ and $C_{stray}$ must be in the same units).

$$
C = 2 \times (12\ \text{pF} - 5\ \text{pF})
$$

$$
C = 2 \times 7\ \text{pF} = 14\ \text{pF}
$$

**Step 3**: Choose the **nearest standard E12 series value** (because exact values are unavailable).

$$
C_1 = C_2 = 15\ \text{pF}\ (\text{nearest standard})
$$

**Result**: Use two **15 pF** NP0/C0G ceramic capacitors in the design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Decoupling Capacitor Minimum Sizing

When a digital IC switches states, it draws a transient current $\Delta I$ over a time $\Delta t$, causing a voltage droop $\Delta V$ on the supply rail. The minimum local decoupling capacitance to suppress this droop is:

$$
C_{dec} \geq \frac{\Delta I \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta V}
$$

### Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE] Worked Problem
> An ARM Cortex-M4 MCU core switches states, drawing a transient current of $\Delta I = 200$ mA over $\Delta t = 5$ ns. The supply rail may droop by at most $\Delta V = 50$ mV. Calculate the minimum decoupling capacitance.

**Step 1**: Write the equation.

$$
C_{dec} \geq \frac{\Delta I \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta V}
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute. Convert all to SI base units.

$$
\Delta I = 200 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A},\quad \Delta t = 5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s},\quad \Delta V = 50 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute the numerator.

$$
\Delta I \cdot \Delta t = 200 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-9} = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C}
$$

**Step 4**: Divide by $\Delta V$.

$$
C_{dec} \geq \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-9}}{50 \times 10^{-3}} = 20 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{F} = 20\ \text{nF}
$$

**Step 5**: Add a **safety factor of \times 10** (standard design practice to account for capacitor DC bias derating, temperature, and aging).

$$
C_{dec,\,chosen} = 10 \times 20\ \text{nF} = 200\ \text{nF}
$$

**Result**: Use a **220 nF** (nearest E12 value) **X7R ceramic capacitor** placed within **3 mm** of each V\_DD pin.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Trace Length (High-Speed PCB)

For a digital signal, the **critical length** $L_{crit}$ is the maximum length of a trace that can be driven **without termination**. Above this length, transmission line effects (reflections, ringing) dominate.

$$
L_{crit} = \frac{t_{rise}}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}
$$

where $t_{pd} \approx 150\ \text{ps/inch}$ for a typical FR-4 PCB microstrip.

### Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE] Worked Problem
> An SPI bus runs at $f = 50$ MHz with a signal rise time $t_{rise} = 2$ ns. Determine whether a **4-inch** trace requires termination. Use $t_{pd} = 150$ ps/inch.

**Step 1**: Compute $L_{crit}$.

$$
L_{crit} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 150 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^{-10}} = 6.67\ \text{inches}
$$

**Step 2**: Compare to the actual trace length.

$$
L_{actual} = 4\ \text{inches} < L_{crit} = 6.67\ \text{inches}
$$

**Result**: The 4-inch trace is **electrically short** — no series termination is required. If the trace length were **> 6.67 inches**, a **series resistor $R_s \approx 33\ \Omega$** would be required to match the typical $50\ \Omega$ microstrip impedance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: MCU Selection

This is a common KTU ESE question format. The student is asked to **justify an MCU choice** for a given application. The matrix below is the evaluator's reference:

| Selection Criterion | Weight (Typical) | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| CPU Core (Cortex-M0/M3/M4/M7) | High | Determines DSP capability, FPU availability |
| Clock Speed | High | Determines real-time responsiveness |
| On-chip Flash | High | Stores firmware code |
| On-chip RAM | High | Stores variables, stack, heap |
| Peripheral Set (ADC, PWM, I2C, SPI, CAN) | Critical | Must match I/O requirements |
| Power Consumption (active/sleep) | Critical | For battery-powered designs |
| Package Size & Type | Medium | Affects PCB area and assembly cost |
| Operating Temperature Range | Medium | Industrial vs. consumer grade |
| Toolchain Support (GCC, Keil, IAR) | Medium | Affects development cost |
| Unit Cost (BOM) | High | For mass production |
| Community & Documentation | Low–Medium | Speeds up development |

### Sample Application Mapping

| Application | Recommended MCU | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Wearable heart-rate monitor** | Nordic nRF52840 (Cortex-M4, BLE) | Low power, integrated BLE radio |
| **Motor controller (BLDC)** | STM32F4 (Cortex-M4, FPU) | High-speed ADC, advanced PWM timers |
| **Smart agriculture sensor** | ESP32-S3 (Cortex-M4, Wi-Fi/BLE) | Wireless + dual-core |
| **Automotive dashboard** | NXP S32K1 (Cortex-M4, CAN-FD) | AEC-Q100 qualified, hardware security |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Typical Embedded Hardware Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture** of a generic embedded system hardware platform. It is the visual equivalent of what a senior hardware engineer would draw during the "system block diagram" phase of the HRS.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POWER[POWER MANAGEMENT SUBSYSTEM]
        VIN[DC Input 9V to 24V] --> DCDC1[Buck Converter 5V 2A]
        DCDC1 --> LDO1[LDO Regulator 3V3 500mA]
        DCDC1 --> LDO2[LDO Regulator 1V8 200mA]
    end

    subgraph CORE[CORE PROCESSING UNIT]
        MCU[ARM Cortex M4 MCU]
        XTAL[8MHz HSE Crystal]
        LDO1 --> MCU
        XTAL --> MCU
        RST[Reset Button and POR Circuit] --> MCU
    end

    subgraph MEM[MEMORY SUBSYSTEM]
        INTFLASH[Internal Flash 1MB]
        INTRAM[Internal SRAM 256KB]
        SPIFLASH[External SPI Flash 16MB]
        MCU --- INTFLASH
        MCU --- INTRAM
        SPIFLASH <-->|SPI Bus| MCU
    end

    subgraph SENSOR[SENSOR SUBSYSTEM]
        TEMP[Digital Temperature Sensor]
        IMU[6 Axis IMU Accelerometer Gyro]
        LDR[Ambient Light Sensor LDR]
        TEMP <-->|I2C Bus| MCU
        IMU <-->|I2C Bus| MCU
        LDR -->|Analog Input| MCU
    end

    subgraph ACT[ACTUATOR SUBSYSTEM]
        DRV1[MOSFET Driver]
        MOT[DC Motor or Relay]
        BUZ[Piezo Buzzer]
        LED[Status LED RGB]
        MCU -->|PWM| DRV1
        DRV1 --> MOT
        MCU --> BUZ
        MCU --> LED
    end

    subgraph COM[COMMUNICATION SUBSYSTEM]
        UART1[USB to UART Bridge CP2102]
        CAN1[CAN Transceiver TJA1050]
        BLE1[BLE Module or Onchip Radio]
        MCU <-->|UART| UART1
        MCU <-->|CAN Bus| CAN1
        MCU <-->|SPI or UART| BLE1
    end

    subgraph DBG[DEBUG AND TEST SUBSYSTEM]
        SWD[SWD JTAG Header 10 pin]
        MCU --- SWD
    end
```

## 4.2 Hardware Design and Development Workflow (Process Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 Requirement Analysis HRS] --> B[Step 2 MCU and Component Selection]
    B --> C[Step 3 Schematic Capture EDA Tool]
    C --> D[Step 4 Power Tree Design]
    D --> E[Step 5 Clock and Reset Circuitry]
    E --> F[Step 6 Memory and Interface Routing]
    F --> G[Step 7 PCB Layout 4 Layer]
    G --> H[Step 8 Design Rule Check DRC]
    H --> I[Step 9 Gerber Generation and Fabrication]
    I --> J[Step 10 Board Bring Up and Test]
    J --> K{Tests Pass?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Step 11 Firmware Integration]
    K -->|No| M[Step 12 Debug and Iterate]
    M --> C
    L --> N[Step 13 EMC EMI Compliance Test]
    N --> O[Step 14 Production Release]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Symbol Diagram — Power Supply Decoupling (Textual Schematic)

For KTU viva and theory questions, students must be able to **read and draw basic schematics**. The following textual representation is the canonical "**MCU with proper decoupling**" pattern:

```
            +-------------------+
  VDD_5V ---| VIN     VOUT 3V3 |--- VDD_3V3 (Rail)
            |  LDO  AMS1117     |
  GND   ---| GND              |--- GND
            +-------------------+

  VDD_3V3 ---+---[ 100nF ]---+--- GND     (High-freq decoupling, near VDD pins)
              |
              +---[ 10uF  ]---+--- GND     (Bulk decoupling, near LDO output)
              |
              +---[ 100uF ]---+--- GND     (Bulk capacitor, board entry)
              |
   +---------------------+
   |     MCU VDD pins    |
   |  [100nF] x N pins   |  (One 100nF per VDD pin, within 3mm)
   +---------------------+
```

## 4.4 Pin Multiplexing Decision Flow

One of the most common hardware design tasks is deciding **which physical MCU pin to assign to which peripheral function**. The flowchart below captures the canonical decision process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Identify Required Peripherals] --> B[Consult Datasheet Pinout Table]
    B --> C{Peripheral Function Available on Multiple Pins?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Evaluate Pin Remap Options in MCU]
    C -->|No| E[Assign Function to Only Available Pin]
    D --> F{Check Pin Voltage Domain 3V3 or 5V Tolerant?}
    F -->|No| G[Reject Pin Risk of Damage]
    F -->|Yes| H[Check Adjacent Pin Usage No Crosstalk]
    H --> I{Trace Length Acceptable for Bus Speed?}
    I -->|No| J[Choose Closer Pin to Connector]
    I -->|Yes| K[Finalise Pin Assignment in Schematic]
    E --> K
    G --> L[Iterate to Next Candidate Pin]
    L --> F
    J --> K
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the term **Hardware Design Flow** as applied to embedded systems. List any **four major steps** in the flow.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Hardware Design Flow is the **structured, sequential engineering process** used to convert an abstract embedded system specification into a **fully functional physical electronic circuit board (PCB)**, satisfying constraints of cost, power, performance, and reliability.

**Four Major Steps (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Requirement Analysis** — Drafting the Hardware Requirements Specification (HRS).
2. **Component Selection** — Choosing the MCU, sensors, actuators, and passives.
3. **Schematic Capture** — Drawing the circuit using EDA tools (KiCad, Altium).
4. **PCB Layout & Fabrication** — Translating the schematic into a physical board (Gerber files), followed by assembly and bring-up.

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Why is a **decoupling capacitor** placed near every IC power pin? State the typical value and the maximum recommended physical distance from the pin.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Why (2 marks):** A decoupling capacitor acts as a **local energy reservoir** that supplies the instantaneous current surge demanded by the IC during logic state transitions. Without it, the supply rail would experience **voltage droop (noise)**, leading to logic errors, ADC inaccuracy, and brown-out resets. It also forms a **low-impedance loop** that returns high-frequency noise to ground.

**Typical value (0.5 marks):** **100 nF (0.1 µF) X7R ceramic** (one per V\_DD pin).

**Maximum distance (0.5 marks):** **Within 3 mm** of the V\_DD pin (the "**0.1 µF / 3 mm rule**").

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE INSTRUCTION:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Model Paper PECST746]** With respect to the design of an **embedded hardware platform**:

**(a)** Explain the **schematic design process** for a microcontroller-based system, including the role of **EDA tools**, **pin multiplexing**, and **buses**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **crystal load capacitor formula** and compute the value of $C_1$ and $C_2$ for an **STM32** system using a **16 MHz HSE crystal** with $C_L = 18$ pF and $C_{stray} = 5$ pF. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Schematic Design Process (7 marks):**

1. **EDA Tool Selection (1 mark):** The designer uses an Electronic Design Automation tool such as **KiCad (open-source)**, **Altium Designer**, or **OrCAD Capture**. These tools provide a unified library of schematic symbols, footprints, and SPICE models.

2. **Library and Component Creation (1 mark):** Each component is either selected from a vendor library (e.g., SnapEDA, Ultra Librarian) or custom-created with its **schematic symbol**, **PCB footprint**, and **3D model**.

3. **MCU Pin Multiplexing (2 marks):** Modern MCUs (e.g., STM32, NXP LPC) have **multiplexed pins** — a single physical pin can serve multiple peripheral functions (UART TX, SPI MOSI, GPIO, PWM, etc.). The designer consults the **datasheet pinout table** to map the required peripheral to a specific pin, considering:
   - Peripheral availability on the pin
   - Voltage domain compatibility (e.g., 5 V tolerance)
   - Adjacent pin usage (avoiding crosstalk on high-speed buses)
   - Remap/alternate-function options for routing flexibility

4. **Bus Design (1 mark):** Inter-MCU communication buses — **I²C (2-wire, open-drain, requires pull-ups)**, **SPI (4-wire, push-pull, point-to-point)**, **UART (asynchronous, 2-wire)** — are routed with their required support components (pull-up resistors for I²C, series termination for high-speed SPI).

5. **Annotation and Netlisting (1 mark):** Once the schematic is complete, the designer runs an **Electrical Rules Check (ERC)** to detect unconnected pins, power conflicts, and un-driven outputs. A **netlist** is then exported for PCB layout.

6. **Documentation (1 mark):** A **schematic PDF** with reference designators, net labels, and comments is generated for the design review and assembly team.

**(b) Crystal Load Capacitor Derivation and Calculation (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The crystal oscillates correctly only when the **total capacitance** presented to its terminals equals the manufacturer's specified **load capacitance $C_L$**. This total capacitance is the **series combination of $C_1$ and $C_2$**, summed with the **stray capacitance** of the PCB traces and MCU oscillator pins.

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2} + C_{stray}
$$

For **symmetric design** ($C_1 = C_2 = C$):

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C}{2} + C_{stray}
$$

Setting $C_{eq} = C_L$:

$$
C_L = \frac{C}{2} + C_{stray} \implies C = 2 (C_L - C_{stray})
$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $C_L = 18$ pF, $C_{stray} = 5$ pF.

$$
C = 2 \times (18\ \text{pF} - 5\ \text{pF}) = 2 \times 13\ \text{pF} = 26\ \text{pF}
$$

Since **26 pF is not a standard E12 value**, the designer selects the **nearest standard capacitor value** (typically up-rounded to provide margin for PCB variations):

$$
C_1 = C_2 = 27\ \text{pF}\ (\text{nearest E12})
$$

**Result:** Use two **27 pF NP0/C0G** ceramic capacitors. The use of **NP0 dielectric** is critical to ensure capacitance stability over temperature.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Model Paper PECST746]** For a **battery-powered IoT sensor node**:

**(a)** Explain the **MCU selection criteria** for a low-power embedded design, with a comparative justification of **Cortex-M0+** vs **Cortex-M4** for this use case. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **battery life formula** and estimate the operating lifetime of a sensor node powered by a **CR2032 coin cell (3 V, 220 mAh)**. The node spends **99 % of the time in sleep mode (5 µA)** and **1 % in active mode (15 mA)**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) MCU Selection Criteria for Low-Power Design (7 marks):**

**Key Selection Criteria (4 marks — 1 mark each major criterion):**

1. **Active and Sleep Current Consumption:** The MCU must have an **ultra-low sleep current** (sub-µA range) to maximise battery life. Cortex-M0+ cores (e.g., STM32L0, SAMD21) typically offer **\sim 0.5 µA in deep sleep** with RTC running.

2. **Wake-up Sources and Speed:** The MCU must wake from sleep via **RTC alarm**, **external interrupt**, or **low-power timer**, and execute the sensor read + transmission task within milliseconds before re-entering sleep.

3. **Integrated Peripherals:** The MCU should integrate the **radio (BLE/LoRa)**, **ADC**, and **temperature sensor** to minimise external component count and quiescent current.

4. **Operating Voltage Range:** A wide V\_DD range (e.g., **1.65 V to 3.6 V**) allows the battery to be used until near-end-of-life (EOL voltage).

**Comparative Justification (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cortex-M0+ (e.g., STM32L0) | Cortex-M4 (e.g., STM32L4) |
|---|---|---|
| Sleep Current | **\sim 0.5 µA** (lower) | \sim 1 µA |
| Active Power | Lower (no FPU) | Higher (with FPU + DSP) |
| DSP Capability | None | Yes (FPU + MAC) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Verdict for IoT Node | **PREFERRED — most battery-efficient** | Overkill unless on-sensor DSP needed |

For a simple **temperature/humidity sensor node** transmitting via BLE every 10 minutes, the **Cortex-M0+** is the optimal choice due to its lower sleep current and lower BOM cost.

**(b) Battery Life Derivation and Estimation (7 marks):**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The **average current consumption** of a duty-cycled system is the time-weighted sum of currents in each mode:

$$
I_{avg} = I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D) + I_{active} \cdot D
$$

where $D$ is the **duty cycle** (fraction of time in active mode).

The **battery life** in hours is:

$$
L_{hours} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}}
$$

and in years:

$$
L_{years} = \frac{L_{hours}}{8760}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $C_{battery} = 220$ mAh, $I_{sleep} = 5$ µA, $I_{active} = 15$ mA, $D = 0.01$.

**Step 1:** Convert $I_{sleep}$ to mA for unit consistency.

$$
I_{sleep} = 5\ \mu\text{A} = 0.005\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $I_{avg}$.

$$
I_{avg} = (0.005 \times 0.99) + (15 \times 0.01)
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 0.00495 + 0.15 = 0.15495\ \text{mA} \approx 0.155\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute battery life in hours.

$$
L_{hours} = \frac{220\ \text{mAh}}{0.155\ \text{mA}} = 1419\ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to years.

$$
L_{years} = \frac{1419}{8760} \approx 0.162\ \text{years} \approx 59\ \text{days}
$$

**Result:** The sensor node will operate for approximately **59 days** on a single CR2032 cell. The active-mode current dominates the budget — a clear signal that **reducing the active time (e.g., faster wake-sleep cycles, faster radio transmission)** is the most effective design lever.

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
> **Common mistakes students make in Hardware Design questions (and how marks are deducted):**
> - **Confusing $C_{stray}$ units**: Forgetting to subtract $C_{stray}$ from $C_L$ **before** multiplying by 2 — leads to a capacitor value **twice as large** as required. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> - **Skipping the safety factor** in decoupling capacitor sizing: The KTU rubric expects the **\times 10 safety multiplier** to be mentioned. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Using wrong dielectric type**: Recommending X7R for the crystal load capacitor instead of **NP0/C0G**. X7R's capacitance varies with temperature and DC bias, causing oscillator frequency drift. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Omitting units in intermediate steps** in battery life derivation: KTU's strict valuation penalises dimensionless quantities. Always carry units explicitly. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - **Forgetting to mention the EDA tool** in the schematic design answer: The question asks for a *process*, and the tool is integral to the process. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly)

### Core Concepts
- **Hardware Design Flow** = 10-step iterative process from HRS to EMC compliance.
- **MCU** is the **heart** of the design; its choice dictates the schematic, layout, and firmware architecture.
- **Schematic design** is a **logical representation**; **PCB layout** is the **physical realisation** — both are equally critical.
- **Hardware–software co-design** is mandatory in modern embedded systems.

### Non-Negotiable Design Rules
- **100 nF decoupling capacitor** on every IC V\_DD pin, within **3 mm**.
- **Continuous ground plane** under high-speed signals.
- **NP0/C0G dielectric** for crystal load capacitors.
- **4-layer stack-up** (Sig / GND / PWR / Sig) for mixed-signal designs.
- **Series termination** ($R_s \approx Z_0$) for traces longer than $L_{crit}$.
- **ERC + DRC** must be clean before fabrication.

### High-Yield Formulas (must memorise)
- Crystal: $\;C = 2 (C_L - C_{stray})$
- Decoupling: $\;C_{dec} \geq \dfrac{\Delta I \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta V}$ (then **\times 10** for safety)
- Critical length: $\;L_{crit} = \dfrac{t_{rise}}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}$ (with $t_{pd} \approx 150$ ps/inch)
- Battery life: $\;L_{years} = \dfrac{C_{battery}}{8760 \cdot [I_{sleep}(1-D) + I_{active} D]}$
- CMOS power: $\;P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$

### Component Selection Mnemonics
- **"P-S-M-R-C"** = Package, Speed, Memory, peripherals, **Cost** — the 5-pillar MCU selection check.
- **"A-B-C-D"** PCB = **A**llocate ground plane, **B**ulk capacitors on power entry, **C**oupling (100 nF) per pin, **D**ifferential pairs length-matched.

### ESE Question Patterns to Expect
- 3-mark Q: "Define … and list four …" — pure recall.
- 7-mark Q (within 14): Derive a formula with numerical substitution.
- 7-mark Q (within 14): Compare/justify two components or approaches using a table.
- Common modules: crystal oscillator, decoupling, power budget, MCU selection, PCB stack-up.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VLSI and Integrated Circuit Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** VLSI and Integrated Circuit Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration)** is the process of creating an **Integrated Circuit (IC)** by combining **millions to billions of MOS transistors** onto a single semiconductor die (chip) to implement complex digital, analog, or mixed-signal systems on a single substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Moore's Law (1965):** The number of transistors on an IC doubles approximately every **18 to 24 months**, while the cost per transistor decreases. Current process nodes: **3 nm, 5 nm, 7 nm, 14 nm, 22 nm, 28 nm, 45 nm, 65 nm, 90 nm, 130 nm, 180 nm, 250 nm, 350 nm, 500 nm, 1 µm**.

### 1.2 Classification of Integration Scales

| Integration Level | Transistor Count (approx.) | Abbreviation |
|---|---|---|
| Small Scale Integration | < 10 | SSI |
| Medium Scale Integration | 10 – 100 | MSI |
| Large Scale Integration | 100 – 10,000 | LSI |
| Very Large Scale Integration | 10,000 – 1,000,000 | VLSI |
| Ultra Large Scale Integration | > 1,000,000 | ULSI |
| System on Chip | Billions + IP Cores | SoC |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The City Inside a Chip":**
> Imagine a **massive city** built on a single **1 cm² plot of silicon**.
> - **Transistors** = Buildings (switch ON/OFF)
> - **Wires (interconnects)** = Roads connecting them
> - **Power rails (VDD/GND)** = Highway supply lines
> - **Clock signal** = The city's synchronized heartbeat
> - **Fabrication masks** = The blue-prints of the city
> - **VLSI Design** = The master urban planning discipline that fits millions of "buildings" without collisions, traffic jams (delays), or power blackouts.
>
> Just as a city planner needs zoning rules (λ-design rules), traffic management (routing), and standardized building blocks (standard cells), VLSI design requires **layout rules, routing algorithms, and cell libraries**.

### 1.4 Why VLSI Matters for Embedded Systems

- **Miniaturization**: Entire CPU, memory, and peripherals fit on a fingernail-sized die.
- **Low Power**: Critical for battery-operated IoT, mobile, and wearable embedded devices.
- **High Speed**: Short on-chip interconnects → propagation delay in **picoseconds (ps)**.
- **Low Cost per Function**: Mass production reduces cost.
- **Reliability**: Fewer off-chip connections → fewer failure points.

### 1.5 Two Pillars of IC Realization

1. **ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)** — designed for one specific task; non-reprogrammable (mask-programmed).
2. **FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array)** — generic array of logic blocks; reprogrammable by the end user in the field.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 VLSI Design Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> VLSI design follows a strict top-down hierarchical decomposition to manage complexity. **"Divide, Conquer, and Compose"** is the governing philosophy.

| Design Level | Abstraction | Representation |
|---|---|---|
| System Level | Behavioural | Algorithm, C/C++/SystemC |
| RTL (Register Transfer Level) | Data flow | Verilog / VHDL |
| Logic / Gate Level | Boolean | Gates, Flip-Flops |
| Circuit Level | Transistor | SPICE netlist |
| Layout / Physical | Geometric | Mask polygons, GDSII |

### 2.2 The Y-Chart (Gajski-Kuhn Chart)

The **Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart** captures three design domains intersecting at concentric abstraction levels:

- **Behavioural Domain** — *what* the system does (algorithms, FSMs).
- **Structural Domain** — *how* it is composed (blocks, gates, transistors).
- **Geometric (Layout) Domain** — *where* it is placed (physical mask layout).

Movement **outward** = higher abstraction; **inward** = more physical detail.

### 2.3 Three Guiding Principles of VLSI Design

1. **Hierarchy** — Decompose system into sub-modules recursively.
2. **Regularity** — Reuse identical cells (e.g., array of identical SRAM bit-cells) to simplify design and verification.
3. **Modularity** — Each module has well-defined function and interface; performance of one module is decoupled from the others.
4. **Locality** — Local interconnections dominate; global wires cause delay, power, and noise problems.

> [!NOTE]
> These four principles are **high-yield KTU short-answer material** (frequently asked for 3–4 marks).

### 2.4 CMOS Inverter — The Heart of Digital VLSI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CMOS (Complementary MOS) Inverter** consists of a **PMOS pull-up network** and an **NMOS pull-down network** in series between **V_DD** and **GND**. It is the fundamental VLSI cell.

| Input (V_in) | PMOS State | NMOS State | Output (V_out) |
|---|---|---|---|
| LOW (0) | ON (conducting) | OFF | HIGH → V_DD |
| HIGH (1) | OFF | ON (conducting) | LOW → 0 V |

**Key Property:** In steady state, **no direct path** exists between V_DD and GND → **near-zero static power dissipation** (only leakage flows).

### 2.5 Stick Diagram Notation

> [!NOTE]
> **Stick Diagrams** are an *intermediate representation* between schematic and physical layout. They use coloured lines to indicate different layers:

| Layer | Colour / Symbol | Material |
|---|---|---|
| n-diffusion | Green | n-type Si |
| p-diffusion | Yellow / Brown | p-type Si |
| Polysilicon (poly / gate) | Red | Doped poly-Si |
| Metal-1 | Blue | Aluminium interconnect |
| Metal-2 | Violet / Purple | Higher metal layer |
| Contact / Cut | Black X | Via connecting layers |
| Well | Dashed line | n-well or p-well boundary |

### 2.6 Lambda (λ) Design Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lambda Rules** — invented by **Calvin Mead and Lynn Conway** — express all geometric constraints in terms of a single parameter **λ**, which equals **half the minimum feature size (F)** of the fabrication process: **λ = F / 2**.

**Rationale:** λ-rules are *scalable* and *technology-independent*; the same stick diagram works for 90 nm, 45 nm, etc., by simply changing λ.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lambda relation | $\lambda = \dfrac{F_{\min}}{2}$ | µm or nm | $F_{\min}$ = minimum feature size |
| 2 | Channel length (L) | $L = 2\lambda$ | µm | Minimum drawn gate length |
| 3 | MOS transistor width | $W = n \cdot 2\lambda$ | µm | n = integer for current drive |
| 4 | NMOS aspect ratio | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | dimensionless | n ≥ 1 |
| 5 | PMOS aspect ratio (ratioed inverter) | $\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p = 2\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | dimensionless | Compensates for lower hole mobility |
| 6 | Power dissipation (static) | $P_{\text{static}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak}}$ | Watt | ≈ 0 in ideal CMOS |
| 7 | Dynamic power (switching) | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f$ | Watt | α = activity factor, $C_L$ = load cap |
| 8 | Short-circuit power | $P_{\text{sc}} = \dfrac{1}{12}\beta (V_{DD}-2V_T)^3 \cdot \tau \cdot f$ | Watt | During transition |
| 9 | Propagation delay (inverter) | $t_p = \dfrac{0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L}{2}$ | seconds | RC-delay form |
| 10 | Gate oxide capacitance / area | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | F/m² | $\varepsilon_{ox}=3.9\varepsilon_0$ |
| 11 | Drain current (saturation) | $I_D = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ | Ampere | Long-channel square-law model |
| 12 | Energy per switching event | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}$ | Joule | Fundamental CV² energy |
| 13 | Gate delay (order of magnitude) | $t_d \approx 10\,ps$ to $100\,ps$ | ps | At modern process nodes |
| 14 | Maximum transistors / die (Moore) | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/T}$ | count | T ≈ 2 years |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Table Rendering Rule:** Within markdown tables, absolute-value bars are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent column break. Example: $\vert V_{GS} - V_T \vert$ **not** $\vert V_{GS} - V_T \vert$.

### 2.8 Comparison: ASIC vs FPGA vs Full-Custom vs Semi-Custom

| Parameter | Full-Custom ASIC | Semi-Custom (Standard Cell) ASIC | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Design effort | Very high | Moderate | Low |
| NRE cost | Very high | High | None (off-the-shelf) |
| Unit cost (volume) | Lowest | Low | High |
| Performance | Best | Good | Lower |
| Power | Lowest | Low | Higher |
| Time-to-market | Longest | Medium | **Shortest** |
| Flexibility | None | None | **Reprogrammable** |
| Embedded use | High-volume products | Mid-volume | Prototyping, low-volume |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded SoCs** (smartphone chips like Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series) = **semi-custom ASIC** with embedded CPU cores, GPUs, DSPs, and analog/RF blocks.
- **Aerospace & Defence** = **radiation-hardened full-custom ASICs**.
- **5G base stations** = **ASIC + FPGA** hybrid.
- **Automotive ECUs** = **ISO 26262 certified ASICs**.
- **R&D / prototyping labs** = **FPGA-based** rapid iteration.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: CMOS Inverter Switching Threshold ($V_M$ / $V_{INV}$)

> [!NOTE]
> The **switching threshold $V_M$** of a CMOS inverter is the point where $V_{in} = V_{out}$. Below it, we derive the standard textbook expression used in every KTU solution.

**Step 1 — Write KCL at the output node at threshold:**

At $V_{in} = V_M$, both transistors are in **saturation** (for the classic derivation). The NMOS drain current equals the PMOS drain current (no current flows out of the high-impedance output node, but the two transistors must balance):

$$I_{D,n} = I_{D,p}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the square-law saturation equation for each device:**

For NMOS (long-channel, saturation):

$$I_{D,n} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W_n}{L_n} (V_{GS,n} - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{k_n}{2} (V_M - V_{TN})^2$$

For PMOS (source at $V_{DD}$, so $V_{GS,p} = V_{in} - V_{DD}$):

$$I_{D,p} = \frac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{2} \cdot \frac{W_p}{L_p} (V_{GS,p} - V_{TP})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2} (V_M - V_{DD} - V_{TP})^2$$

Since $V_{TP} < 0$, we have $V_{GS,p} - V_{TP} = V_M - V_{DD} - V_{TP} = V_{DD} - V_M + \vert V_{TP}\vert$.

**Step 3 — Equate and simplify:**

$$\frac{k_n}{2} (V_M - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2} (V_{DD} - V_M + \vert V_{TP}\vert)^2$$

**Step 4 — Take the positive square root (voltages are positive-definite relative to the trip point):**

$$\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{TN}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_M + \vert V_{TP}\vert)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_M$ algebraically:**

$$V_M - V_{TN} = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} (V_{DD} - V_M + \vert V_{TP}\vert)$$

$$V_M - V_{TN} = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} (V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert) - \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} \, V_M$$

$$V_M \left(1 + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\right) = V_{TN} + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} (V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)$$

$$\boxed{\,V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Define the symmetry parameter $k_r$:**

$$k_r = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_p W_p}{\mu_n W_n}}$$

For a **symmetric inverter** ($V_M = V_{DD}/2$), we set $k_r = 1$, which gives:

$$\frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx \frac{2 \text{ to } 3}{1}$$

In standard practice for KTU problems, **$W_p = 2 W_n$** is taken as the canonical ratioed inverter.

### 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Power Dissipation

**Step 1 — Energy stored on the load capacitor per cycle:**

When the output transitions from 0 → $V_{DD}$, the load capacitor $C_L$ is charged from the supply:

$$E_{\text{charge}} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}$$

**Step 2 — Energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS during charging:**

$$E_{\text{dissipated, charge}} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}$$

**Step 3 — When output discharges (1 → 0):**

The energy stored in $C_L$ is **dissipated in the NMOS** (not recovered):

$$E_{\text{dissipated, discharge}} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}$$

**Step 4 — Total energy dissipated per switching cycle (one full 0→1→0):**

$$E_{\text{total/cycle}} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2} + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{\,2} = C_L V_{DD}^{\,2}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by activity factor α and switching frequency f:**

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f\,}$$

**Step 6 — Why $V_{DD}^2$ matters for embedded design:**

A **20% reduction in $V_{DD}$** yields **~36% reduction in dynamic power** ($0.8^2 = 0.64$). This is why **dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS)** is a cornerstone of low-power embedded SoC design.

### 3.3 Derivation: Propagation Delay of CMOS Inverter (RC Model)

**Step 1 — Model the ON-transistor as a resistor $R_{eq}$:**

The equivalent resistance of a MOSFET in linear region is approximately:

$$R_{eq} \approx \frac{1}{\mu C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_T)}$$

**Step 2 — Charging a load capacitor $C_L$ through $R_{eq}$ follows:**

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t / R_{eq} C_L}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Propagation delay $t_{pHL}$** is the time to reach 50% of $V_{DD}$ (i.e., $V_{out} = 0.5 V_{DD}$):

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t_{pHL} / R_{eq} C_L}$$

$$e^{-t_{pHL} / R_{eq} C_L} = 0.5$$

$$t_{pHL} = R_{eq} C_L \ln 2 = 0.69\, R_{eq} C_L$$

**Step 4 — Average propagation delay:**

$$\boxed{\,t_p = \frac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2} \approx 0.69\, R_{eq}\, C_L\,}$$

For a chain of $N$ inverters driving a load $C_L$ with equal-sized stages, **minimum delay** occurs when each stage is **e ≈ 2.718** times larger than the previous, but in practice a tapering factor of **3 to 4** is used.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Delay, Power, Area Trade-off (the VLSI "Iron Triangle")

| Design Choice | Effect on Delay | Effect on Power | Effect on Area | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase W/L | ↓ faster | ↑ switching power | ↑ larger | Speed vs Power/Area |
| Decrease $V_{DD}$ | ↑ slower | ↓↓ $V^2$ reduction | — none | Power vs Speed |
| Decrease $t_{ox}$ | ↓ faster | ↑ leakage ↑ | — | Speed vs Leakage |
| Multi-$V_{TH}$ design | Mixed | ↓ leakage | Slightly ↑ | Speed vs Leakage |
| Clock gating | — | ↓ dynamic | Slight ↑ | Power vs Area |
| Pipeline | ↓ throughput latency ↓ | ↑ slightly (more flops) | ↑ | Throughput vs Area |
| Parallelism | ↓ latency | ↑ power (more units) | ↑ | Throughput vs Power/Area |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "optimize" a design, always explicitly mention the **delay-power-area triangle** — this single phrase is worth bonus marks.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A CMOS inverter in a 90 nm process drives a load capacitance of **50 fF** at $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V and clock frequency **500 MHz** with activity factor α = 0.2. Compute the dynamic power.

**Solution:**

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f$$

$$= 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.2)^2 \times 500 \times 10^{6}$$

Step-by-step numerical evaluation:

$$(1.2)^2 = 1.44 \, \text{V}^2$$

$$50 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 = 72 \times 10^{-15} \, \text{C·V}$$

$$72 \times 10^{-15} \times 500 \times 10^{6} = 36 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{W}$$

$$0.2 \times 36 \times 10^{-6} = 7.2 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{dyn}} = 7.2\,\mu\text{W}\,}$$

If the same inverter is used in a chip with **10 million such gates**, the total switching power becomes:

$$P_{\text{total}} = 7.2\,\mu W \times 10^7 = 72\,\text{W}$$

This dramatic scaling is why **low-$\alpha$** and **low-$V_{DD}$** design are non-negotiable in modern embedded VLSI.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    poly1[Red Poly Gate crossing both diffusions] --> ndiff1[Green N Diff NMOS Active]
    poly1 --> pdiff1[Yellow P Diff PMOS Active]
    vddmetal1[Blue Metal VDD top rail] --> pcontact1[Black Contact to P Diff]
    gndmetal1[Blue Metal GND bottom rail] --> ncontact1[Black Contact to N Diff]
    outmetal1[Blue Metal Output Y] --> pcontact2[Black Contact to P Diff]
    outmetal1 --> ncontact2[Black Contact to N Diff]

    nwell2[Dashed N Well boundary around PMOS] --> pdiff1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Stick Diagram:**
> - The **red poly line** crossing the **green n-diff** forms the **NMOS** (transistor is wherever poly crosses diffusion).
> - The same red poly crossing the **yellow p-diff** (inside the n-well) forms the **PMOS**.
> - **VDD** and **GND** rails are in **Metal-1 (blue)**, connected to source diffusions via **black contact cuts (X)**.
> - The **output node** is the blue metal wire connecting the drains of both transistors.

### 4.7 Lambda Design Rule Constraints (Visual)

| Rule Type | Constraint | Minimum Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| Poly width | ≥ 2λ | $2\lambda$ |
| Diff width | ≥ 2λ | $2\lambda$ |
| Poly spacing | ≥ 2λ | $2\lambda$ |
| Diff spacing (same type) | ≥ 3λ (with contact) / 2λ (without) | $3\lambda$ / $2\lambda$ |
| Poly over diff overlap | ≥ 2λ | $2\lambda$ |
| Contact size | 2λ × 2λ | $2\lambda$ |
| Metal width | ≥ 3λ | $3\lambda$ |
| Metal spacing | ≥ 3λ | $3\lambda$ |
| Contact to gate | ≥ 2λ | $2\lambda$ |
| N+ to P+ spacing | ≥ 4λ (with substrate contact) | $4\lambda$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Student Pitfall:** The most violated rule in KTU stick-diagram answers is the **n-diff to p-diff spacing of 4λ** when both are connected to a substrate contact. Memorize the table above.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define VLSI. List the four guiding principles of VLSI design with a one-line explanation of each.*

**Model Answer:**

**VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration):** VLSI is the technology of integrating **tens of thousands to billions of MOS transistors** on a single semiconductor die to implement complete digital/analog/mixed-signal systems.

**Four Guiding Principles:**

| # | Principle | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hierarchy** | Decompose complex system into sub-modules recursively. |
| 2 | **Regularity** | Reuse identical structures (cells, arrays) to reduce design effort. |
| 3 | **Modularity** | Each module has well-defined function and interface. |
| 4 | **Locality** | Most interconnections are local; avoid long global wires. |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for principles)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Compare ASIC and FPGA. State one application where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ASIC | FPGA |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Custom-designed chip for one application | Pre-fabricated array of configurable logic blocks |
| Reprogrammable | No (mask-programmed) | Yes (in the field) |
| NRE cost | High | None |
| Unit cost (volume) | Low | High |
| Performance | Highest | Lower (overhead of programmable switches) |
| Power | Lowest | Higher (leakage from switches) |
| Time-to-market | Long | Short |
| Preferred application | **Smartphone SoC, automotive ECU** (high volume) | **5G base-station prototyping, aerospace reconfigurable systems, low-volume embedded products** |

*(3 marks: 1 for definitions + 2 for comparison + application)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* **Explain the CMOS inverter in detail. Derive the expression for its switching threshold $V_M$. State the condition for a symmetric inverter.**

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* **Explain the CMOS fabrication process with a neat flow diagram. List the key λ-design rules for stick-diagram layout.**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**1. CMOS Inverter — Description (3 marks):**

The CMOS inverter consists of a **PMOS transistor** (pull-up) and an **NMOS transistor** (pull-down) connected in series between **$V_{DD}$** and **GND**. The gates of both transistors are tied together to form the input $V_{in}$, and the common drain terminal forms the output $V_{out}$.

| $V_{in}$ | PMOS | NMOS | $V_{out}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 V | ON | OFF | $V_{DD}$ |
| $V_{DD}$ | OFF | ON | 0 V |

**Key advantages:**
- Near-zero static power dissipation (no direct path from $V_{DD}$ to GND in steady state).
- Full output voltage swing (0 to $V_{DD}$).
- High noise margin.

**2. Switching Threshold Derivation (3 marks):**

(Show the derivation from Section 3.1, leading to the boxed expression.)

At the switching threshold, $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$, and both transistors are in saturation. Equating drain currents and solving:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$

**3. Symmetric Inverter Condition (1 mark):**

For $V_M = V_{DD}/2$:

$$\sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{W_p/L_p}{W_n/L_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3$$

Standard practice: $W_p \approx 2 W_n$.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**1. CMOS Fabrication Process — Step-by-Step (5 marks):**

(Refer to the Mermaid flow in Section 4.5; explain each step with the *why* behind it.)

1. **Wafer preparation:** Start with a polished p-type silicon wafer (substrate).
2. **N-well formation:** Grow SiO₂, deposit Si₃N₄, photolithographically pattern, etch, and implant **phosphorus** to form n-wells (housing for PMOS).
3. **Field oxidation / LOCOS:** Grow thick field oxide (SiO₂) in non-active regions for isolation.
4. **Gate oxidation:** Grow a thin, high-quality gate oxide (~1–2 nm at modern nodes).
5. **Polysilicon deposition & patterning:** Deposit doped polysilicon and pattern it to form gate electrodes.
6. **N⁺ source/drain implant:** **Arsenic** implant self-aligned to the poly gate (NMOS source/drain + poly doping).
7. **P⁺ source/drain implant:** **BF₂** implant masked to n-well regions (PMOS source/drain).
8. **Dielectric deposition (BPSG):** Deposit borophosphosilicate glass for planarization and insulation.
9. **Contact hole etching:** Photolithographically open contacts to silicon and poly.
10. **Metal-1 deposition & patterning:** Sputter aluminium, pattern with photolithography + etch.
11. **Higher metal layers:** Repeat dielectric + metal for Metal-2, Metal-3, etc.
12. **Passivation:** Deposit Si₃N₄ overglass; open bonding-pad windows.
13. **Testing, dicing, packaging.**

**2. Lambda Design Rules (2 marks):**

State the table from Section 4.7 — minimum poly width = 2λ, minimum diff width = 2λ, contact size = 2λ × 2λ, n⁺ to p⁺ spacing with substrate contact = 4λ, metal width and spacing = 3λ, poly-to-contact spacing = 2λ, etc.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* **With neat stick diagrams, explain the layout of:**
**(i) CMOS Inverter**  *(3 marks)*
**(ii) CMOS NAND2 gate**  *(4 marks)*

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* **Explain the various abstraction levels in VLSI design using the Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart. Discuss the Y-chart domains and the design steps involved in converting a specification to a fabricated chip.**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**1. CMOS Inverter Stick Diagram (3 marks):**

```
(Use plain ASCII / verbal description for clarity, since the Mermaid is the visual.)

LAYER         LOCATION
--------------------------------------------------
Red (poly)    Vertical line crossing both diffusions
Yellow (p+diff)  Left block inside n-well — PMOS source/drain
Green (n+diff)   Right block inside p-substrate — NMOS source/drain
Blue (M1)    VDD rail (top) → contact to yellow p+ (PMOS source)
Blue (M1)    GND rail (bottom) → contact to green n+ (NMOS source)
Blue (M1)    Output Y → contact to p+ drain and n+ drain
Black X      Contact cuts at: VDD-p+, GND-n+, Y-p+, Y-n+
Dashed       N-well boundary around p+; P-well/substrate around n+
```

**2. CMOS NAND2 Stick Diagram (4 marks):**

A NAND2 has **two PMOS in parallel** (pull-up) and **two NMOS in series** (pull-down).

```
        VDD ────────────────────────── (M1, top rail)
            │                          │
            ├──── p+ ──┐               │
            │   (PMOS A)│              │
        A ──┤──── Red poly ────────────┤
            │          │               │
            │     ┌──── p+ ──────── ─ ─┤
            │     │   (PMOS B)│         │
            ├─────┤            │        │
        B ──┤──── Red poly ────┘        │
            │                          │
            ├──── n+ ──┐               │   (NMOS A on left)
            │  (NMOS A)│               │
            │          ├──── n+ ──┐    │
            │          │ (NMOS B)  │    │
            │          │          ├──── GND (M1, bottom rail)
```

**Key points to mention in the answer:**
- PMOS transistors (yellow p-diff inside dashed n-well) are placed in **parallel** (both sources tied to $V_{DD}$, both drains tied to output Y).
- NMOS transistors (green n-diff) are placed **in series** (drain of one = source of next).
- Poly gates (red) for inputs A and B are vertical lines.
- Output Y is the central blue metal node.
- Both VDD and GND rails run in Metal-1 horizontally.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**1. Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart Description (3 marks):**

The Y-chart consists of **three domains** arranged radially:
- **Behavioural** (top) — *what* the system does (algorithms, data flow, FSMs).
- **Structural** (bottom-left) — *how* the system is built (interconnected blocks, gates, transistors).
- **Geometric (Layout)** (bottom-right) — *where* components are placed (physical mask polygons).

The chart has **concentric rings** representing abstraction levels: **System → Algorithm → Register-Transfer → Logic → Circuit → Layout** (outer to inner).

**2. Design Steps from Spec to Fab (3 marks):**

1. **System specification** — define function, performance, power, area targets.
2. **Behavioural design** — write C/C++/SystemC model; verify with simulation.
3. **RTL design** — describe in Verilog/VHDL.
4. **Logic synthesis** — convert RTL to gate-level netlist using standard-cell library.
5. **Verification & DFT** — functional simulation, formal verification, scan/BIST insertion.
6. **Floorplanning & Place-and-Route (PnR)** — convert netlist to physical layout.
7. **Sign-off** — Static Timing Analysis (STA), power analysis, DRC/LVS checks.
8. **Tape-out** — send GDSII to foundry for mask generation and fabrication.
9. **Test & Package** — wafer probe, dicing, packaging, final test.

**3. Domains interplay (1 mark):**
- Moving **outward** = higher abstraction (faster simulation, less detail).
- Moving **inward** = more physical detail (more accurate but slower).
- The three domains must remain **consistent** at every level (e.g., RTL code must match the synthesized netlist must match the placed-and-routed layout).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention "no direct current path from VDD to GND in steady-state"** in CMOS inverter — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Writing $|V_{GS} - V_T|$ with vertical pipes** in a markdown table answer — this breaks the table renderer on KTU's online portal. **Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.**
> 3. **Confusing the unit of λ** — λ is in **µm or nm depending on the technology node**, not dimensionless. State "λ = F_min/2" explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping the "why" of each fabrication step** — KTU wants the engineering rationale (e.g., *why* use SiO₂, *why* use ion implantation, *why* deposit BPSG) — not just a recipe list.
> 5. **Forgetting to dimension the stick diagram** — every line should be labelled with its λ-rule constraint (e.g., "poly width = 2λ").
> 6. **Mixing up static and dynamic power** — Static power = leakage; Dynamic power = switching ($\alpha C V^2 f$).
> 7. **Not stating the symmetric-inverter ratio $W_p = 2 W_n$** — high-frequency KTU question.
> 8. **Omitting Moore's Law year and doubling period** — frequently asked as 1-mark sub-part.
> 9. **Confusing SSI/LSI/VLSI thresholds** — write out the transistor counts.
> 10. **No block-diagram for design flow** — always accompany with a flowchart; KTU values figures.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist. Memorize it. You will recover 4–6 marks guaranteed.**

- [ ] **VLSI** = Very Large Scale Integration; > 10,000 transistors on one die.
- [ ] **Moore's Law** = transistor count doubles every **~2 years** (Moore, 1965).
- [ ] **IC Scale** → SSI < MSI < LSI < VLSI < ULSI < SoC.
- [ ] **Four principles**: **Hierarchy, Regularity, Modularity, Locality**.
- [ ] **Y-Chart** = Behavioural / Structural / Geometric domains × abstraction levels.
- [ ] **CMOS inverter** = 1 PMOS (pull-up) + 1 NMOS (pull-down); no static current.
- [ ] **Switching threshold formula**: $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$
- [ ] **Symmetric inverter**: $W_p \approx 2 W_n$ to compensate for $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.5$.
- [ ] **Dynamic power**: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — quadratic in $V_{DD}$.
- [ ] **Propagation delay**: $t_p = 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$.
- [ ] **λ-rule**: $\lambda = F_{min}/2$ — technology-independent layout.
- [ ] **Stick diagram colours**: Red=poly, Green=n-diff, Yellow=p-diff, Blue=Metal1, Black X=contact.
- [ ] **ASIC** = custom, high NRE, low unit cost, high volume. **FPGA** = reprogrammable, fast TTM, mid volume.
- [ ] **Full-custom > Semi-custom (standard cell) > Gate array > FPGA** in performance, density, power; reverse order in design effort & TTM.
- [ ] **Key λ-rules**: poly width ≥ 2λ, metal width ≥ 3λ, contact = 2λ × 2λ, n+ to p+ with substrate contact ≥ 4λ.
- [ ] **Fabrication steps**: Wafer → N-well → Gate oxide → Poly → N+/P+ S/D → Contacts → Metal → Passivation.
- [ ] **Delay-Power-Area triangle** — every VLSI optimization involves trading off these three.
- [ ] **CV² energy** is the fundamental switching cost — drives low-power design philosophy.

---

**End of Module 3 — VLSI and Integrated Circuit Design Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PECST746)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Design Automation (EDA) Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electronic Design Automation (EDA) Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electronic Design Automation (EDA)** refers to the category of software tools, methodologies, and integrated computer-aided engineering workflows used to design, model, simulate, verify, synthesize, and physically realize electronic systems — ranging from digital Integrated Circuits (ICs), Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs), Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs), Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs), to complete embedded hardware-software subsystems.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: PECST746 — Embedded Systems)**, Module 3 situates EDA tools as the *industrial backbone* that transforms an abstract embedded specification into a tangible, manufacturable, testable, and deployable product. EDA tools are formally classified under **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** discipline, but the "Automation" prefix specifically denotes the algorithmic, rule-driven, and AI-assisted transformation of Register-Transfer Level (RTL) descriptions into gate-level netlists and ultimately into physical layout geometries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition (KTU Board Expected):**
> *EDA Tools are a coordinated suite of software applications that automate the design, simulation, synthesis, physical implementation, and verification of electronic and embedded systems, replacing manual circuit drafting with algorithmic, rule-based, and optimization-driven design flows.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Factory" Metaphor

Imagine you want to build a house (your embedded system). You cannot just tell workers "build me a smart home" and expect it to work. You need:

- **An architect** (Specification & Behavioral Modeling) who drafts blueprints.
- **A structural engineer** (Synthesis & Logic Optimization) who decides whether to use steel or concrete columns.
- **A city planner** (Place & Route) who places the kitchen, bedrooms, and electrical wiring optimally.
- **A building inspector** (Verification & DRC) who checks whether the design meets safety codes.
- **A construction crew** (Fabrication / FPGA Programming) who physically builds or programs the structure.

**EDA Tools are precisely this orchestrated, automated workforce — but for silicon chips and PCBs.** A single RTL file written in Verilog or VHDL passes through dozens of automated stages, each handled by a specialized EDA tool, until it emerges as a fabricated chip or a programmed FPGA bitstream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Embedded systems today are **too complex** (billions of transistors in modern SoCs) for manual design. EDA tools are not optional — they are the **only viable engineering methodology**. This is why Module 3 dedicates a dedicated topic to their workflow.

## 1.3 Categories of EDA Tools (High-Level Classification)

EDA tools are broadly partitioned by their position in the design abstraction hierarchy. The KTU syllabus implicitly tests this taxonomy:

| **Abstraction Level** | **EDA Tool Category** | **Example Tools** |
|---|---|---|
| System / Behavioral | System-level modeling, HLS | MATLAB/Simulink, LabVIEW |
| RTL / Logic | HDL simulation, Synthesis | ModelSim, Vivado Simulator, GHDL |
| Gate / Netlist | Logic synthesis, Equivalence checking | Synopsys Design Compiler, Yosys |
| Physical / Layout | Place & Route, Floorplanning | Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2 |
| Verification | DRC, LVS, Static Timing Analysis | Calibre, IC Validator, PrimeTime |
| PCB / Embedded | Schematic capture, PCB layout | Altium Designer, KiCad, OrCAD |
| Firmware / Co-design | IDEs, Compilers, Debuggers | Keil MDK, STM32CubeIDE, GCC |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Industry Standard Metrics

EDA tools evaluate designs against stringent numerical thresholds. Critical metrics engineers constantly monitor:

- **Process Node**: measured in **nanometers (nm)**, e.g., **7 nm, 5 nm, 3 nm FinFET**.
- **Clock Frequency (f<sub>clk</sub>)**: measured in **MHz / GHz**.
- **Propagation Delay (t<sub>pd</sub>)**: measured in **picoseconds (ps)** or **nanoseconds (ns)**.
- **Power Dissipation (P)**: measured in **milliwatts (mW)** or **microwatts (μW)** for low-power MCUs.
- **Area Utilization**: measured in **μm² or mm²** of die area.
- **Design Rule Check (DRC) violations**: dimensionless integer count.
- **Static Timing Analysis slack**: measured in **ns** (positive = setup met, negative = timing failure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse **EDA** with **CAD**. **CAD** is a superset (mechanical, architectural, electrical drafting). **EDA** is the *electrical-electronics-specific subset* of CAD focused on semiconductors, ICs, and PCBs. Marks are routinely lost in Part A for this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical EDA Design Flow (KTU Board Favorite)

Every modern embedded/IC design follows a structured, layered flow. Each transition between layers is handled by a specialized EDA tool. Understanding *which tool does what at which stage* is **the single most important Module 3 takeaway**.

### Stage 1 — Specification & System-Level Modeling
- Engineers write the design intent in natural language + formal specs.
- High-Level Synthesis (HLS) tools (e.g., Xilinx Vitis HLS, Cadence Stratus) accept C/C++/SystemC and synthesize to RTL.
- **Output:** Behavioral model + design constraints (timing, area, power budgets).

### Stage 2 — RTL Design Entry
- Designer writes **Hardware Description Language (HDL)** code: **Verilog** or **VHDL**.
- **EDA Tool Used:** Text editor + linting tools (Verilator, HDL designers).
- **Output:** `.v` or `.vhd` source files.

### Stage 3 — Functional Simulation (Pre-Synthesis)
- HDL is **simulated without synthesis** to validate logic behavior.
- **EDA Tools Used:** ModelSim (Mentor/Siemens EDA), Vivado Simulator (AMD Xilinx), QuestaSim, Icarus Verilog (open-source), GHDL (VHDL).
- Testbenches inject stimulus; waveform viewers dump `.vcd` or `.fst` files.
- **Output:** Verified behavioral correctness.

### Stage 4 — Logic Synthesis
- HDL is translated into a **gate-level netlist** (logical gates + flip-flops) using a **standard cell library**.
- **EDA Tools Used:** Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus, Yosys (open-source), Xilinx Vivado Synthesis.
- Optimizations performed: **area minimization, timing-driven synthesis, power optimization**.
- **Output:** Gate-level netlist + SDF (Standard Delay Format) file.

### Stage 5 — Formal Verification & Equivalence Checking
- Verifies that the synthesized netlist is **functionally equivalent** to the original RTL.
- **EDA Tools Used:** Synopsys Formality, Cadence JasperGold, Yosys + SymbiYosys.
- **Output:** Equivalence proof (pass/fail).

### Stage 6 — Physical Design (Place & Route)
- Netlist gates are **physically placed** on the silicon die and **routed** with metal interconnect.
- Sub-steps: **Floorplanning → Placement → Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS) → Routing**.
- **EDA Tools Used:** Cadence Innovus, Synopsys IC Compiler II, Xilinx Vivado Implementation.
- **Output:** GDSII or OASIS layout database.

### Stage 7 — Physical Verification
- **Design Rule Check (DRC):** Ensures layout obeys foundry fabrication rules (min width, spacing, density).
- **Layout vs. Schematic (LVS):** Ensures physical layout matches the synthesized netlist.
- **Antenna & ERC Checks:** Electrical rule compliance.
- **EDA Tools Used:** Mentor Calibre, Synopsys IC Validator, Cadence Pegasus.
- **Output:** DRC-clean, LVS-clean layout ready for tape-out.

### Stage 8 — Static Timing Analysis (STA)
- Verifies that all timing paths meet setup/hold constraints across **all PVT corners** (Process, Voltage, Temperature).
- **EDA Tools Used:** Synopsys PrimeTime, Cadence Tempus, Vivado Timing Analyzer.
- **Output:** Timing reports with slack values.

### Stage 9 — FPGA-Specific Flow (Bitstream Generation)
- For FPGAs: synthesis → place & route → bitstream generation → device programming.
- **EDA Tools Used:** Xilinx Vivado, Intel Quartus, Lattice Diamond, Yosys + nextpnr (open-source).
- **Output:** `.bit` or `.sof` file loaded onto the FPGA via JTAG.

### Stage 10 — PCB & Embedded System Integration
- For embedded systems, EDA tools also handle **schematic capture + PCB layout**.
- **EDA Tools Used:** Altium Designer, KiCad (open-source), OrCAD, Cadence Allegro.
- Sub-stages: **Schematic → Netlist → Component Placement → Routing → Gerber File Generation → Fabrication**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — EDA Tools Formula & Metrics Reference Table**
> (Use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid markdown pipe conflicts.)

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Setup Time Slack | $t_{slack} \;=\; T_{clk} \;-\; (t_{cq} \;+\; t_{logic} \;+\; t_{setup} \;-\; t_{skew})$ | Positive slack = setup met | ns |
| Hold Time Slack | $t_{hold\_slack} \;=\; (t_{cq} \;+\; t_{logic}) \;-\; t_{hold}$ | Must be $\geq 0$ | ns |
| Maximum Clock Frequency | $f_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{clk(min)}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{t_{cq} \;+\; t_{comb\_max} \;+\; t_{setup}}$ | Highest sustainable clock | Hz / MHz / GHz |
| Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} \;=\; \alpha \cdot C_{load} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f$ | Switching power consumption | W |
| Static Power | $P_{static} \;=\; I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Leakage current loss | W |
| Total Power | $P_{total} \;=\; P_{dyn} \;+\; P_{static}$ | Sum of both regimes | W |
| Power Delay Product | $PDP \;=\; P_{avg} \cdot t_{delay}$ | Energy-efficiency metric | J |
| Propagation Delay | $t_{pd} \;=\; \dfrac{t_{pLH} \;+\; t_{pHL}}{2}$ | Average gate delay | s (ns/ps) |
| Fan-Out Loading | $C_{L} \;=\; n \cdot C_{input}$ | Total load capacitance | F |
| EDA Tool Runtime Complexity | $O(n \cdot \log n)$ typical for synthesis; $O(n^{k})$ for full P&R | Algorithmic scaling | dimensionless |
| Design Productivity Gap | $Gap \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Complexity}}{\text{Designer Productivity}}$ | Moore's gap widens $2\times$ every 18 months | — |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

EDA tools are not academic curiosities — they enable every commercial chip on the market:

- **Smartphones:** Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon — designed using Synopsys + Cadence full-flow EDA.
- **Automotive:** Bosch, NXP, Infineon automotive MCUs — verified using Mentor Calibre DRC/LVS.
- **FPGA Prototyping:** AMD Xilinx Versal, Intel Agilex — designed using Vivado and Quartus.
- **IoT & Embedded:** STM32 firmware co-designed using STM32CubeIDE (hardware-aware IDE).
- **Open-Source Hardware:** RISC-V cores compiled using **Yosys + nextpnr + OpenROAD** — a complete free EDA stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Insight (KTU Expectation):** The **EDA market** is dominated by exactly three vendors — **Cadence, Synopsys, Siemens EDA (formerly Mentor Graphics)** — forming the "EDA Big Three." They collectively command **>75% of the global EDA market** and their tools are practically mandatory for any serious VLSI/embedded design house.

## 2.4 Hardware/Software Co-Design in Embedded EDA

Modern embedded systems are *heterogeneous* — they integrate hardware (RTL/FPGA/ASIC) with software (firmware on CPU cores). EDA tools increasingly support **co-design**:

- **Xilinx Vitis + Vivado:** Unified C/C++/OpenCL + RTL flow for Zynq SoCs.
- **Intel HLS Compiler:** Translates C to Verilog for FPGA acceleration.
- **ARM DS-5 / Keil MDK:** Hardware-aware debuggers that correlate JTAG traces with software breakpoints.
- **Model-Based Design:** MATLAB/Simulink generates both HDL code and embedded C from a single block diagram.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"How do EDA tools help embedded system design?"* — always mention the **co-design** angle. It shows awareness that modern embedded systems are not pure hardware, and the examiner expects this nuance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Operating Frequency from Synthesis Reports

**KTU Board Problem Type:** Given gate delays from an EDA synthesis report, compute the maximum clock frequency supported by the critical path.

**Problem Setup:**
After synthesis using Synopsys Design Compiler, a digital block reports:
- Clock-to-Q delay of flip-flop: $t_{cq} = 0.15 \, \text{ns}$
- Combinational logic delay on critical path: $t_{comb} = 1.25 \, \text{ns}$
- Setup time requirement of destination flip-flop: $t_{setup} = 0.10 \, \text{ns}$
- Clock skew: $t_{skew} = 0.05 \, \text{ns}$ (assume positive skew makes timing tighter)
- Hold time requirement: $t_{hold} = 0.08 \, \text{ns}$

### Step 1 — Write the Setup Time Constraint Equation

For a synchronous digital circuit, the **setup time constraint** states that data launched by one flip-flop on a rising clock edge must arrive at the next flip-flop **before the next rising clock edge minus the setup time requirement**.

$$T_{clk} \;\geq\; t_{cq} \;+\; t_{comb} \;+\; t_{setup} \;+\; t_{skew}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Minimum Clock Period

Rearrange the inequality to isolate $T_{clk}$:

$$T_{clk(min)} \;=\; t_{cq} \;+\; t_{comb} \;+\; t_{setup} \;+\; t_{skew}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$T_{clk(min)} \;=\; 0.15 \;+\; 1.25 \;+\; 0.10 \;+\; 0.05$$

Add the first two terms:

$$0.15 \;+\; 1.25 \;=\; 1.40$$

Add the next term:

$$1.40 \;+\; 0.10 \;=\; 1.50$$

Add the skew:

$$1.50 \;+\; 0.05 \;=\; 1.55 \, \text{ns}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Maximum Frequency

$$f_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{clk(min)}}$$

$$f_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1.55 \times 10^{-9}} \, \text{Hz}$$

$$f_{max} \;=\; 6.4516 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Hz} \;\approx\; 645.16 \, \text{MHz}$$

### Step 5 — Verify Hold Time Constraint

The hold time check must also pass:

$$t_{hold} \;\leq\; t_{cq} \;+\; t_{comb(min)}$$

Assuming minimum combinational delay $t_{comb(min)} = 0.40 \, \text{ns}$:

$$0.08 \;\leq\; 0.15 \;+\; 0.40$$

$$0.08 \;\leq\; 0.55 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Hold constraint satisfied)}$$

### Final Answer Summary

$$\boxed{T_{clk(min)} \;=\; 1.55 \, \text{ns} \quad , \quad f_{max} \;\approx\; 645.16 \, \text{MHz}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The EDA synthesis report is essentially a *numerical certificate of correctness* — it quantifies whether the design is fabricatable. A negative slack would have flagged this as a **timing violation**, requiring either pipeline retiming, logic restructuring, or cell up-sizing.

## 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Power Optimization Using EDA-Driven Voltage Scaling

**Problem Setup:** A synthesized SoC block consumes $P_{dyn,1} = 250 \, \text{mW}$ at $V_{DD,1} = 1.20 \, \text{V}$ and $f_1 = 500 \, \text{MHz}$. Using EDA-driven voltage scaling, the voltage is reduced to $V_{DD,2} = 0.85 \, \text{V}$ at the same frequency. Compute the new dynamic power (assume activity factor $\alpha$ and load capacitance $C$ remain constant).

### Step 1 — Recall the Dynamic Power Equation

$$P_{dyn} \;=\; \alpha \cdot C_{load} \cdot V_{DD}^{\,2} \cdot f$$

### Step 2 — Form the Power Ratio

Because $\alpha$, $C$, and $f$ are unchanged, take the ratio:

$$\dfrac{P_{dyn,2}}{P_{dyn,1}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{DD,2}^{\,2}}{V_{DD,1}^{\,2}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$\dfrac{P_{dyn,2}}{250} \;=\; \dfrac{(0.85)^{2}}{(1.20)^{2}}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Squares

$$(0.85)^{2} \;=\; 0.7225$$

$$(1.20)^{2} \;=\; 1.44$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Ratio

$$\dfrac{0.7225}{1.44} \;=\; 0.5017$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the New Power

$$P_{dyn,2} \;=\; 250 \times 0.5017$$

$$P_{dyn,2} \;\approx\; 125.43 \, \text{mW}$$

### Step 7 — Compute Power Savings

$$\text{Savings} \;=\; 250 - 125.43 \;=\; 124.57 \, \text{mW}$$

$$\text{Savings \%} \;=\; \dfrac{124.57}{250} \times 100 \;\approx\; 49.83\%$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{P_{dyn,2} \;\approx\; 125.43 \, \text{mW} \quad \text{(49.83\% power reduction)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why EDA Tools Matter Here:** This voltage-frequency island partitioning, multi-voltage domain insertion, and power-gating transistor insertion are **all automated by EDA tools** like Synopsys PrimePower and Cadence Voltus. Manually optimizing power across millions of gates is infeasible.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — EDA Tool Mapping by Design Stage

| **Design Stage** | **Input Artifact** | **Output Artifact** | **Primary EDA Tool** | **Open-Source Alternative** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specification | English + UML | Behavioral model | MATLAB/Simulink | — |
| RTL Design | — | `.v` / `.vhd` | Vivado IDE, Vim + Verilog-mode | Emacs + verilog-mode |
| Functional Simulation | HDL + Testbench | Waveforms / Reports | ModelSim, Questa | Icarus Verilog, GHDL |
| Logic Synthesis | HDL | Gate-level netlist | Synopsys DC, Genus | Yosys |
| Equivalence Check | RTL + Netlist | Proof certificate | Formality, JasperGold | SymbiYosys |
| Floorplanning | Netlist + Constraints | Die floorplan | Innovus, ICC2 | OpenROAD |
| Placement | Netlist + Floorplan | Placed cells | Innovus, ICC2 | OpenROAD |
| Clock Tree Synthesis | Placed netlist | Routed clock tree | CCOpt, Innovus | OpenROAD |
| Routing | Placed netlist | Routed design | Innovus, ICC2 | OpenROAD |
| DRC | GDSII | DRC report | Calibre, IC Validator | Magic VLSI |
| LVS | GDSII + Netlist | LVS report | Calibre | Netgen |
| STA | Netlist + SPEF | Slack report | PrimeTime, Tempus | OpenSTA |
| FPGA Bitstream | Synthesized netlist | `.bit` file | Vivado, Quartus | nextpnr + icestorm |
| PCB Layout | Schematic + Netlist | Gerber files | Altium, KiCad | KiCad (free) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete EDA Tool Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specification and System Modeling] --> B[RTL Design Entry Verilog or VHDL]
    B --> C[Functional Simulation ModelSim or Vivado Sim]
    C --> D{Simulation Pass?}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Logic Synthesis Synopsys DC or Yosys]
    E --> F[Formal Equivalence Check Formality or SymbiYosys]
    F --> G[Physical Design Floorplan Place Route]
    G --> H[Clock Tree Synthesis]
    H --> I[Static Timing Analysis PrimeTime or OpenSTA]
    I --> J{Timing Met?}
    J -- No --> E
    J -- Yes --> K[Physical Verification DRC and LVS Calibre]
    K --> L{DRC LVS Clean?}
    L -- No --> G
    L -- Yes --> M[Tapeout Fabrication or FPGA Bitstream]
    M --> N[Silicon Chip or Programmed FPGA]
    N --> O[Embedded System Integration PCB Layout]
    O --> P[Final Product]
```

## 4.2 EDA Tool Taxonomy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EDA[Electronic Design Automation Tools] --> F[Frontend Tools]
    EDA --> G[Backend Tools]
    EDA --> H[Verification Tools]
    EDA --> I[Embedded PCB Tools]
    F --> F1[HDL Editors]
    F --> F2[Logic Simulators]
    F --> F3[Logic Synthesizers]
    F --> F4[HLS Tools]
    G --> G1[Floorplanners]
    G --> G2[Place and Route]
    G --> G3[CTS Engines]
    G --> G4[Layout Editors]
    H --> H1[Static Timing]
    H --> H2[Formal Verification]
    H --> H3[DRC and LVS]
    H --> H4[Power Analysis]
    I --> I1[Schematic Capture]
    I --> I2[PCB Layout]
    I --> I3[Signal Integrity]
    I --> I4[Gerber Generation]
```

## 4.3 Hardware Software Co-Design Flow for Embedded SoCs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Embedded System Specification] --> B[Partition HW vs SW]
    B --> C[HW Path]
    B --> D[SW Path]
    C --> E[RTL Design in Verilog or VHDL]
    E --> F[Synthesis and FPGA Implementation]
    F --> G[Bitstream Generation]
    D --> H[C and C plus plus Firmware Development]
    H --> I[Cross Compilation with GCC]
    I --> J[Linking and ELF Generation]
    G --> K[FPGA or SoC Platform]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Joint Debug via JTAG]
    L --> M{Functional Correct?}
    M -- No --> A
    M -- Yes --> N[Production Deployment]
```

## 4.4 Open Source vs Commercial EDA Stack Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Commercial[Commercial EDA Stack]
        C1[Synopsys Design Compiler]
        C2[Cadence Innovus]
        C3[Siemens Calibre]
        C4[Synopsys PrimeTime]
        C5[ModelSim Questa]
    end
    subgraph OpenSource[Open Source EDA Stack]
        O1[Yosys Synthesis]
        O2[OpenROAD PnR]
        O3[Magic VLSI and Netgen]
        O4[OpenSTA Timing]
        O5[Icarus Verilog GHDL]
    end
    Commercial -->|Industry Standard| Target[Silicon Tapeout]
    OpenSource -->|Research and Education| Target
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
