# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define EDA Tools with examples. *(Source: KTU University Exam — May 2024)*

**Model Answer:**
Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools are software applications used to design, simulate, verify, and fabricate electronic systems such as ICs, FPGAs, and PCBs. They automate the design flow from abstract HDL specifications down to physical layout, replacing error-prone manual design.

**Examples:**
- **Synopsys Design Compiler** — Logic Synthesis.
- **Cadence Innovus** — Place and Route.
- **Mentor Calibre** — Physical Verification (DRC/LVS).
- **Xilinx Vivado** — FPGA Implementation.

(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for categorized examples)

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Front-End and Back-End EDA Tools. *(Source: KTU University Exam — Dec 2023)*

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Front-End EDA** | **Back-End EDA** |
|---|---|---|
| Design Phase | Pre-synthesis (RTL → Netlist) | Post-synthesis (Netlist → Layout) |
| Abstraction | Behavioral, RTL, Logic | Physical, Geometric |
| Tools | ModelSim, Vivado Sim, Synopsys DC | Innovus, ICC2, Calibre |
| Output | Gate-level netlist | GDSII / Layout |
| Verification Focus | Functional correctness | Timing, DRC, LVS |
| Designer Concern | "Does it work logically?" | "Does it fabricate correctly?" |

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: *(KTU University Exam — June 2024)*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **complete VLSI/Embedded EDA design flow** from specification to fabrication, naming at least **six distinct EDA tools** and their precise role at each stage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss **Design Rule Check (DRC)** and **Layout vs. Schematic (LVS)** verification with a suitable diagram. Why are these considered the *final gatekeepers* before fabrication? *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution A:

**(a) Complete EDA Design Flow (7 marks)**

The VLSI/Embedded EDA design flow is a layered, iterative process:

1. **System Specification & Modeling** — Engineers define functionality, performance, power, and area budgets. Tools: **MATLAB/Simulink** for system-level exploration.

2. **RTL Design Entry** — Behavioral intent is written in **Verilog** or **VHDL**. Tools: **Vivado IDE, Emacs with HDL plugins**.

3. **Functional Simulation** — Testbenches validate behavior pre-synthesis. Tools: **ModelSim (Siemens EDA), Icarus Verilog (open-source)**. Output: waveform files (`.vcd`, `.fsdb`).

4. **Logic Synthesis** — HDL converted to gate-level netlist using standard cell libraries. Tools: **Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus, Yosys**. Performs area/timing/power optimization.

5. **Physical Design** — Floorplanning, placement, Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS), routing. Tools: **Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2, OpenROAD**. Output: placed-and-routed database.

6. **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** — All paths verified against timing constraints. Tools: **Synopsys PrimeTime, Cadence Tempus, OpenSTA**.

7. **Physical Verification** — DRC and LVS checks. Tools: **Mentor Calibre, Synopsys IC Validator, Magic VLSI**.

8. **Tape-Out / FPGA Programming** — Final GDSII sent to foundry OR bitstream loaded onto FPGA via JTAG.

(7 marks: ~1 mark per stage with tool name and output)

---

**(b) DRC and LVS — The Final Gatekeepers (7 marks)**

**Design Rule Check (DRC):**
DRC verifies that the physical layout conforms to the **foundry's manufacturing rules** — minimum wire width, minimum spacing between metals, via enclosure rules, antenna ratios, density rules. The EDA tool (**Calibre, Pegasus, IC Validator**) flags any geometric violation.

**Layout vs. Schematic (LVS):**
LVS extracts a **spice netlist** from the physical layout and compares it (device-by-device, net-by-net) with the **synthesized gate-level netlist**. It ensures:
- Correct transistor count and types.
- Correct connectivity (no shorts or opens).
- Proper I/O pin correspondence.

**Why They Are Final Gatekeepers:**
A perfect logical design with violated DRC rules is **unmanufacturable** — foundries reject the GDSII outright. A perfect layout that fails LVS means the chip fabricated would behave **differently** from simulation. Together, DRC + LVS certify that the design is both *fabricatable* and *functionally faithful* to the RTL.

**Diagram:**

```
     Synthesized Netlist            Physical Layout (GDSII)
              |                              |
              v                              v
     +-----------------+            +-----------------+
     | Netlist Extract |            | Layout Database |
     +-----------------+            +-----------------+
              |                              |
              +---------------+--------------+
                              |
                              v
                    +-------------------+
                    |    LVS Compare   |
                    +-------------------+
                              |
                +-------------+-------------+
                |                           |
                v                           v
           LVS PASS                   LVS FAIL (short/open)
                |                           |
                v                           v
         +-------------+             +-------------+
         | DRC Check   |             | Debug and   |
         +-------------+             | Resubmit    |
                |                           ^
                v                           |
         +-------------+                    |
         | DRC PASS    |                    |
         +-------------+                    |
                |                           |
                v                           |
         +-------------+                    |
         | Tapeout OK  +-------------------+
         +-------------+
```

(7 marks: 2 for DRC explanation, 2 for LVS explanation, 2 for "gatekeeper" justification, 1 for diagram)

---

### Question B: *(KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Alternative)*

**(a)** Describe the **FPGA Design Flow** using EDA tools. Compare the **Xilinx Vivado** flow with the **open-source Yosys + nextpnr** flow. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **Hardware/Software Co-Design** in modern embedded systems. How do EDA tools like **Xilinx Vitis** and **MATLAB Simulink** enable unified embedded development? *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution B:

**(a) FPGA Design Flow using EDA Tools (7 marks)**

The FPGA design flow is a streamlined subset of the ASIC flow optimized for programmable logic:

1. **RTL Entry** — Verilog/VHDL design.
2. **Synthesis** — HDL → FPGA primitives (LUTs, FFs, BRAMs, DSPs). Tools: **Vivado Synthesis, Yosys**.
3. **Implementation** — Logic optimization, placement on FPGA fabric, routing of programmable interconnect. Tools: **Vivado Implementation, nextpnr**.
4. **Bitstream Generation** — Configuration bits compiled. Tools: **Vivado, icepack**.
5. **Programming** — Bitstream loaded via **JTAG** to the FPGA's SRAM configuration memory.

**Comparison: Xilinx Vivado vs. Open-Source (Yosys + nextpnr):**

| **Aspect** | **Xilinx Vivado** | **Yosys + nextpnr** |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | Commercial (license required) | Free and open-source |
| Vendor Support | First-party AMD Xilinx | Community-driven |
| Device Support | All Xilinx FPGAs/SoCs | Primarily Lattice iCE40, ECP5, some Xilinx 7-series |
| Optimization Quality | Very high (proprietary algorithms) | Moderate, improving rapidly |
| Use Case | Production, industry | Education, research, prototyping |

(7 marks)

---

**(b) Hardware/Software Co-Design (7 marks)**

Modern embedded systems increasingly integrate **hardened CPU cores** alongside programmable logic (e.g., Xilinx Zynq, Intel Cyclone SoC). EDA tools now provide **co-design environments**:

- **Xilinx Vitis Unified Platform:** Allows developers to write C/C++/OpenCL/Python code that is automatically synthesized to RTL for the FPGA fabric while running Linux/RTOS on the ARM Cortex-A cores. Hardware kernels accelerate compute-intensive functions; software handles I/O, control flow, and OS-level tasks.

- **MATLAB/Simulink Model-Based Design:** Simulink block diagrams can be directly compiled to:
  - HDL via **HDL Coder** for FPGA implementation.
  - Embedded C via **Embedded Coder** for MCU firmware.
  - Both, simultaneously, for **co-simulation** on hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testbeds.

- **ARM Keil MDK + ULINK Pro:** Correlates JTAG hardware traces with software debug symbols, enabling simultaneous HW/SW debugging on a unified timeline.

**Advantages:**
- Faster time-to-market.
- Single source of truth (one model, multiple targets).
- Early architectural exploration (hardware-software partitioning validated before fabrication).
- Reduces hand-off errors between HW and SW teams.

(7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 — EDA Tools:**
> 1. **Conflating EDA with CAD** — Always specify EDA as the *electronics-specific subset* of CAD. Single-line CAD-only answers lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Naming a tool without stating its role** — Writing "Synopsys" alone is incomplete; write "Synopsys Design Compiler for logic synthesis."
> 3. **Skipping the iterative nature** — The design flow is **not strictly linear**; loops exist between synthesis → STA → P&R. Forgetting this loses the "iterative" nuance mark.
> 4. **Ignoring open-source EDA** — KTU 2024 syllabus (NEP-aligned) emphasizes *open-source tools* (Yosys, KiCad, OpenROAD). Failing to mention them is a missed opportunity.
> 5. **Mis-defining DRC vs. LVS** — DRC checks **geometric rules**; LVS checks **functional equivalence** with the netlist. Reversing these two definitions is a fatal 2-mark loss.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — EDA Tools: Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ⭐ **EDA = Electronics-specific CAD** focused on IC, FPGA, and PCB design automation.
- ⭐ The canonical flow: **Specification → RTL → Simulation → Synthesis → STA → P&R → DRC/LVS → Tapeout/Bitstream**.
- ⭐ **Three dominant commercial vendors:** Cadence, Synopsys, Siemens EDA (formerly Mentor Graphics).
- ⭐ **Logic Synthesis** converts HDL to gate-level netlist using a standard cell library.
- ⭐ **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** validates setup/hold across all PVT corners without simulation.
- ⭐ **DRC** checks geometric manufacturing rules; **LVS** checks layout-vs-netlist equivalence.
- ⭐ **FPGA flow** is a streamlined subset ending in **bitstream programming via JTAG**.
- ⭐ **Open-source EDA stack:** Yosys (synthesis) + nextpnr (P&R) + OpenSTA (timing) + Magic/Netgen (verification) + KiCad (PCB).
- ⭐ **Hardware/Software Co-Design** is the modern embedded reality — Xilinx Vitis and Simulink enable unified design.
- ⭐ **Power optimization** by EDA tools leverages $P \propto V^{2}$ dynamic power scaling and clock gating.
- ⭐ Always remember to state **which tool does what** — examiners reward tool-stage mapping precision.
- ⭐ **Co-simulation, hardware-in-the-loop, and formal verification** are the verification triad of modern embedded EDA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PCB Layout Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** PCB Layout Design # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **PCB Layout Design** is the engineering discipline of converting a logical circuit schematic into a physical, manufacturable board by precisely placing electronic components, routing electrical interconnections (copper traces), defining copper layers, planes, vias, and pads, while strictly adhering to electrical, thermal, mechanical, and manufacturability (DFM) constraints. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Embedded Systems (PECST746)** syllabus, PCB Layout Design is positioned as a critical **hardware realization skill** that bridges the gap between *circuit design* and *physical prototype*. The layout is described in a **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** environment (e.g., KiCad, Altium Designer, OrCAD, Eagle) using a neutral file interchange format called **Gerber (RS-274X)** or **ODB++**, which is sent to the PCB fabrication house. **Synonyms used in KTU modules:** *Board Design, Layout Artwork, Artwork Generation, Gerber Generation, Physical Design.* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "City Planning" View of a PCB** Imagine you are an urban planner designing a new city: - **The Schematic** is the list of buildings and roads that *must* exist (i.e., which pin connects to which). - **The PCB Layout** is the **actual map** showing *where* the buildings (components) are placed, *how wide* the roads (traces) are, how many lanes (copper weight), and where the flyovers (vias) go. Key takeaways from the analogy: 1. **Roads (Traces)** must be wide enough to carry "traffic" (current). A 0.2 mm trace can fry a 5 A motor driver, just like a narrow lane cannot handle heavy trucks. 2. **Buildings (Components)** are placed to minimize traffic congestion (short signal paths). 3. **Service lanes (Ground Planes)** are kept continuous to provide a stable reference and absorb noise. 4. **Zoning Laws (Design Rules)** like minimum road width, gap between buildings, and turning radius (bend radius) are imposed by the city council (fabrication house). --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard PCB Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall):** > - **Copper Weight:** **1 oz/ft²** = **35 µm** thickness (industry standard). > - **Standard Substrate:** **FR-4** (Flame Retardant grade 4 epoxy-glass), dielectric constant *εᵣ* ≈ **4.2–4.5**. > - **Standard Trace Widths:** **0.2 mm (8 mil)**, **0.25 mm (10 mil)**, **0.5 mm (20 mil)**. > - **Standard Via Drill:** **0.3 mm (12 mil)**, **0.4 mm (16 mil)**. > - **Grid System:** **2.54 mm (100 mil)** — the legacy through-hole pitch. > - **SMD Imperial Codes:** **0805, 0603, 0402** (length × width in hundredths of an inch). > - **Board Edge Clearance:** Components placed **≥ 2 mm** from board edge. --- ## 1.4 Why PCB Layout Design Matters in Embedded Systems > [!NOTE] > An embedded system is judged not by its schematic correctness alone but by its **operational reliability under real-world conditions**. A poor layout can cause: > - **Voltage drops** in power traces (brown-out resets in microcontrollers). > - **Crosstalk** between parallel high-speed signal lines. > - **Ground bounce** and **EMI** violating CISPR/FCC Class A/B limits. > - **Thermal runaway** in linear regulators. > - **Manufacturing yield drops** due to acid traps, starved thermals, or insufficient annular rings. Therefore, KTU explicitly tests whether the student understands the *physical implementation consequences* of design choices, not just the schematic symbols. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The PCB Design Cycle (Engineering Workflow) A professional PCB layout is produced by iterating through the following **six logical phases**. This is a high-yield topic for KTU 14-mark questions. 1. **Schematic Capture & Netlist Generation** - The schematic is drawn in EDA software. Every component pin and wire is assigned a unique **net** (e.g., `+3V3`, `GND`, `MOSI`). - The **netlist** is a machine-readable list of `(Component, Pin, Net)` tuples. 2. **Component Footprint (Land Pattern) Selection** - Each schematic symbol is mapped to a **footprint library** entry that defines the physical pad geometry, drill size, and silkscreen outline. - *Example:* Resistor `RESC1608N` → Footprint `0603`. 3. **Constraint Setup (Design Rules)** - Defines the *traffic laws* of the board: `Clearance`, `Track Width`, `Via Size`, `Hole-to-Hole`, `Silkscreen-to-Pad`. - Standards: **IPC-2221** (generic), **IPC-2222** (rigid organic), **IPC-A-610** (acceptability). 4. **Component Placement** - Manual + automated placement. - Goals: shortest critical nets, decouplers as close as possible to IC Vcc pins, thermal components spaced for airflow. 5. **Routing (Track/Via Drawing)** - Algorithms: **Rip-up and Reroute**, **Maze routing (Lee's algorithm)**, **Channel routing**, **Push-and-shove**. - Modes: **Interactive**, **Auto-router (Specctra, TopoR)**, **Semi-auto (hug-trace)**. 6. **Design Rule Check (DRC) & Gerber Export** - **DRC** runs against IPC/netlist rules. Zero DRC errors = no *guarantee* of working board, but *necessary* condition. - **Gerber RS-274X** files generated per layer: top copper, bottom copper, silkscreen, solder mask, drill (Excellon). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Eye:** A common Part A (3-mark) question is *"List the steps in PCB design flow"* or *"What is the role of a netlist in PCB design?"* --- ## 2.2 Layer Stack-Up & Multilayer Boards > [!IMPORTANT] > A **4-layer stack-up** is the de-facto industry default for embedded systems using microcontrollers (STM32, ESP32, etc.). | Layer # | Function | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **L1 (Top)** | Signal + Component Side | High-speed signals, fine-pitch ICs | | **L2 (Inner 1)** | **Solid Ground Plane** | Reference return path, EMI shield | | **L3 (Inner 2)** | **Power Plane** (+3V3, +5V) | Low-inductance power distribution | | **L4 (Bottom)** | Signal + Component Side | Routing overflow, BGAs, large parts | The **dielectric** between layers is the FR-4 prepreg (PP) and core. The standard thicknesses are: - Core thickness: **0.2 mm, 0.36 mm, 0.56 mm, 0.71 mm, 1.0 mm, 1.6 mm**. - Prepreg: **0.1 mm** typical. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Trace Width, Impedance, Thermal) > [!WARNING] > **NO `|` symbol inside any table cell.** All absolute value bars use `\vert` or `\mid`. ### 2.3.1 Trace Current-Carrying Capacity (IPC-2152 Standard) The **cross-sectional area** of a copper trace determines how much current it can carry before exceeding a given temperature rise $\Delta T$. $$ I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725} $$ Solving for the area: $$ A = \left( \dfrac{I}{k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}} \right)^{1/0.725} $$ Where: - $I$ = current in **Amperes (A)** - $\Delta T$ = temperature rise above ambient in **°C** - $A$ = cross-sectional area in **mils²** (square mils) - $k$ = derating constant | Layer Type | $k$ (External) | $k$ (Internal) | |---|---|---| | **External (outer)** | 0.048 | — | | **Internal (inner)** | 0.024 | — | The trace **width** is then: $$ W_{\text{mils}} = \dfrac{A}{1.378 \cdot t_{\text{oz}}} $$ where $t_{\text{oz}}$ is the copper weight in **oz/ft²** (1 oz = 1.378 mil thickness). --- ### 2.3.2 Microstrip Characteristic Impedance (Controlled Impedance Routing) For high-speed signals (USB, Ethernet, HDMI, RF), traces must be designed to a specific characteristic impedance (commonly **50 Ω** single-ended, **90 Ω / 100 Ω** differential). $$ Z_0 = \dfrac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t} \right) \;\; \Omega $$ Where: - $Z_0$ = characteristic impedance in **Ω** - $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of FR-4 ≈ **4.2–4.5** - $h$ = dielectric height (substrate thickness) in **mils** - $w$ = trace width in **mils** - $t$ = trace thickness in **mils** ($t \approx 1.378 \times \text{oz/ft}^2$) --- ### 2.3.3 Track Resistance (DC) $$ R_{\text{trace}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{w \cdot t} $$ Where: - $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \;\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$ - $L$ = trace length in **m** - $w, t$ = width and thickness in **m** --- ### 2.3.4 Voltage Drop Across a Trace $$ V_{\text{drop}} = I \cdot R_{\text{trace}} = I \cdot \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{w \cdot t} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Design Heuristic:** Keep power-trace voltage drop **< 1 %** of supply voltage to prevent brown-out resets in microcontrollers (e.g., for 3.3 V supply, $V_{\text{drop}} < 33 \text{ mV}$). --- ### 2.3.5 Thermal Resistance of PCB Copper Pour $$ \theta_{\text{cu}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{cu}}}{k_{\text{cu}} \cdot A_{\text{cu}}} $$ Where: - $L_{\text{cu}}$ = length of heat-spreader - $k_{\text{cu}}$ = 401 W/(m·K) - $A_{\text{cu}}$ = cross-section area of the copper pour # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Trace Width for a 2 A Power Rail on 1 oz Copper > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** An embedded system draws a sustained **2 A** from a +5 V regulator output. The PCB is 4-layer, the trace will run on the **external (top) layer**, and the allowed temperature rise is **$\Delta T = 20\,^\circ\text{C}$**. Compute the **minimum trace width** assuming **1 oz/ft²** copper. ### Step 1 — Identify the relevant IPC-2152 equation For an **external** trace, the current-capacity relationship is: $$ I = k_{\text{ext}} \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725} $$ with $k_{\text{ext}} = 0.048$. ### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values $I = 2\;\text{A}$, $\Delta T = 20$, $k_{\text{ext}} = 0.048$. $$ 2 = 0.048 \cdot 20^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute $20^{0.44}$ $$ 20^{0.44} = e^{0.44 \cdot \ln 20} = e^{0.44 \cdot 2.9957} = e^{1.3181} \approx 3.736 $$ ### Step 4 — Rearrange and isolate $A^{0.725}$ $$ A^{0.725} = \dfrac{2}{0.048 \cdot 3.736} = \dfrac{2}{0.17933} \approx 11.154 $$ ### Step 5 — Solve for $A$ $$ A = (11.154)^{1/0.725} = (11.154)^{1.3793} $$ Computing the exponent: $$ A = e^{1.3793 \cdot \ln 11.154} = e^{1.3793 \cdot 2.4124} = e^{3.3275} \approx 27.83 \;\text{mils}^2 $$ ### Step 6 — Convert cross-sectional area to trace width For 1 oz/ft² copper, thickness $t = 1.378 \;\text{mils}$. $$ W = \dfrac{A}{1.378 \cdot t_{\text{oz}}} = \dfrac{27.83}{1.378 \cdot 1} \approx 20.20 \;\text{mils} $$ ### Step 7 — Convert mils to mm $$ W_{\text{mm}} = 20.20 \times 0.0254 \approx 0.513 \;\text{mm} $$ ### Step 8 — Apply KTU Design Margin Always round **up** and add **20–30 %** engineering safety margin (per KTU board examiner expectations): $$ W_{\text{design}} = 0.513 \times 1.25 \approx 0.64 \;\text{mm} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** Use a **0.65 mm (≈ 26 mil)** trace on the top layer for the 2 A rail. (If routed on an **internal** layer, the area $A$ would need to be **roughly doubled** because $k_{\text{int}} = 0.024$ is half of $k_{\text{ext}}$.) --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Routing Topologies for Common Embedded Buses > [!NOTE] > **Bus routing strategy is a frequently tested 14-mark KTU topic.** The matrix below is a high-yield comparison. | Bus Type | Frequency / Speed | Topology | Trace Width | Length Match | Shielding | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **I²C** | 100 kHz – 400 kHz – 3.4 MHz | Star or daisy-chain | 0.25 mm | Not critical | Pull-up 2.2 kΩ – 10 kΩ near master | | **SPI** | up to 50 MHz (short) | Point-to-point | 0.25 mm | SCK-MOSI-MISO matched to ± 1 mm | Keep GND return under SCK | | **UART** | up to 1 Mbaud | Point-to-point | 0.25 mm | Not critical | Avoid parallel routing with power | | **USB 2.0** | 480 Mbps | 90 Ω differential | 0.15 mm / 0.20 mm gap | ± 150 µm | Continuous GND beside, no splits | | **Ethernet** | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps | 100 Ω differential | 0.15 mm / 0.15 mm gap | ± 2.5 mm | Stitched GND vias every 50 mm | | **PWM Motor Drive** | 20 kHz – 100 kHz | Wide power trace | 0.65 – 1.0 mm | Not critical | Kelvin connection to GND shunt | --- ## 3.3 Numerical Walkthrough: Voltage Drop on a Long Power Trace > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A microcontroller (STM32F4) draws $I = 200 \;\text{mA}$ average. Its +3V3 trace is $L = 80 \;\text{mm}$ long, $w = 0.25 \;\text{mm}$, $t = 35 \;\mu\text{m}$ (1 oz). Find the voltage drop. ### Step 1 — Convert all dimensions to metres $$ L = 0.080 \;\text{m},\quad w = 0.25 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m},\quad t = 35 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute cross-sectional area $$ A = w \cdot t = (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (3.5 \times 10^{-5}) = 8.75 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{m}^2 $$ ### Step 3 — Apply resistance formula $$ R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A} = \dfrac{1.724 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 0.080}{8.75 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ R = \dfrac{1.379 \times 10^{-9}}{8.75 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 0.1576 \;\Omega $$ ### Step 4 — Compute voltage drop $$ V_{\text{drop}} = I \cdot R = 0.2 \cdot 0.1576 = 0.0315 \;\text{V} = 31.5 \;\text{mV} $$ ### Step 5 — Compare to KTU heuristic $$ \dfrac{V_{\text{drop}}}{V_{\text{cc}}} = \dfrac{0.0315}{3.3} \approx 0.95 \;\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is **within the 1 % budget** and therefore acceptable for this rail. **Decision:** no change needed; otherwise widen the trace to 0.4 mm and recompute. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PCB Layer Stack-Up Diagram (4-Layer) ```mermaid flowchart TB L1["L1 TOP SIGNAL
0.035 mm Copper
Components and high speed routes"]:::signal PP1["PP Prepreg 0.21 mm
Dielectric Er approx 4.3"]:::diel L2["L2 GROUND PLANE
0.035 mm Copper
Solid uninterrupted pour"]:::gnd CORE["FR4 Core 1.20 mm
Mechanical backbone"]:::diel L3["L3 POWER PLANE
0.035 mm Copper
3V3 and 5V split regions"]:::pwr PP2["PP Prepreg 0.21 mm"]:::diel L4["L4 BOTTOM SIGNAL
0.035 mm Copper
Routing overflow and discretes"]:::signal L1 --- PP1 PP1 --- L2 L2 --- CORE CORE --- L3 L3 --- PP2 PP2 --- L4 classDef signal fill:#1f6feb,stroke:#0b3a82,color:#ffffff classDef gnd fill:#2da44e,stroke:#0a5028,color:#ffffff classDef pwr fill:#cf222e,stroke:#7a0e1a,color:#ffffff classDef diel fill:#d29922,stroke:#7a5300,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **L2 Ground Plane** is *continuous* and sits *directly under* the top signals to provide a low-impedance return path. **Never** route signals across a plane split — that creates a *long return loop* and is the #1 cause of EMI failures in embedded boards. --- ## 4.2 PCB Design Flow Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Schematic Capture
EDA tool KiCad Altium OrCAD"]:::block B["Netlist Generation
CONNECTED to footprint data"]:::block C["Component Placement
Manual plus auto placement"]:::block D["Constraint Setup
Clearance Width Via rules"]:::block E["Routing
Signal Power Ground"]:::block F["Design Rule Check
DRC zero errors"]:::block G["Gerber Export
RS 274X plus Excellon drill"]:::block H["Fabrication and Assembly
SMT line Pick and Place"]:::block A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H F -. DRC fail .-> C F -. DRC fail .-> E classDef block fill:#eef,stroke:#225,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Signal Return-Path Model (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TX["Driver IC (e.g. STM32 PA5)"] D1["Signal Source"]:::blk end subgraph TRACE["Top Trace over Ground Plane"] T["Copper Trace
Width w, Length L"]:::trace end subgraph RX["Receiver IC"] D2["Signal Load"]:::blk end G["L2 Ground Plane
Returns current immediately
under the trace"]:::gnd V["Via Stitching
Connects GND every lambda/20"]:::via D1 --> T --> D2 G --- T V -.- G classDef blk fill:#9e6a03,color:#fff classDef trace fill:#1f6feb,color:#fff classDef gnd fill:#2da44e,color:#fff classDef via fill:#cf222e,color:#fff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Intuition:** The high-frequency return current does *not* take the shortest path to ground; it takes the **path of minimum impedance**, which is *directly underneath* the top trace. This is why a continuous L2 ground plane is non-negotiable for any design with clocks **> 10 MHz** (e.g., ESP32, STM32H7, Raspberry Pi RP2040). --- ## 4.4 Thermal & Power Dissipation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Heat Source
LDO Linear Regulator
P 0.5 W to 2 W"]:::heat B["Copper Pour
2 oz Top Layer
Area 1 cm squared"]:::copper C["Thermal Vias Array
12 vias 0.3 mm
Pitch 1.0 mm"]:::via D["Bottom Layer Pour
2 oz Bottom"]:::copper E["Heatsink or PCB Edge
Convective Cooling"]:::cool A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E classDef heat fill:#bc4c00,color:#fff classDef copper fill:#d29922,color:#000 classDef via fill:#cf222e,color:#fff classDef cool fill:#0969da,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Define the term "PCB layout design". What are its two primary outputs?** **Model Answer:** PCB layout design is the physical implementation of an electronic schematic on a printed circuit board, involving precise placement of components and routing of electrical interconnections while satisfying electrical, thermal, and manufacturing constraints. The two primary outputs are: 1. **Gerber files (RS-274X)** — the artwork for copper, mask, and silkscreen layers. 2. **Excellon drill files** — defining the position and size of all drilled holes (vias, through-holes). --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between through-hole technology (THT) and surface-mount technology (SMT) in PCB layout.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Through-Hole (THT) | Surface-Mount (SMT) | |---|---|---| | **Mounting** | Leads pass through drilled holes | SMD pads on the surface only | | **Drilling required** | Yes (every pin) | No (only vias, if any) | | **Component density** | Low | High (fine-pitch BGAs possible) | | **Mechanical strength** | High (axial/radial stress) | Lower (rework friendly) | | **Typical use** | Connectors, electrolytic caps, transformers | ICs, resistors, microcontrollers | | **Assembly cost** | Higher (wave/manual) | Lower (reflow + pick-and-place) | --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Each Part B question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks *(Choose A or B)* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **complete PCB design flow** from schematic capture to Gerber generation. List **at least six** distinct steps and state the role of **DRC** in the cycle. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a **2-layer FR-4 PCB**, a microcontroller's +3V3 power trace must carry **0.8 A** on the **top layer**. Allowed temperature rise is **15 °C** above ambient. Copper weight is **1 oz/ft²**. Compute the **minimum trace width** in mm using the **IPC-2152** standard. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-A(a) **Step 1 — Schematic Capture:** The engineer draws the circuit in EDA software (KiCad, Altium, OrCAD). Every component pin is assigned a net name such as `VCC`, `GND`, `MOSI`. **Step 2 — Footprint Mapping:** Each schematic symbol is linked to a *physical footprint* in the library (e.g., 0805 resistor, TQFP-64 IC). **Step 3 — Netlist Generation:** EDA exports a netlist listing all (component, pin, net) connections — this becomes the *routing database*. **Step 4 — Constraint / Design-Rule Setup:** Clearances, minimum track widths, via diameters, drill sizes, and annular rings are defined as per the *fabrication house capability*. **Step 5 — Component Placement:** ICs are oriented to minimize crossings; decoupling capacitors are placed **as close as possible (≤ 2 mm)** to Vcc pins. Connectors are placed at board edges. **Step 6 — Routing:** The auto-router (or interactive router) draws copper traces, using short direct paths for power, and matched-length for high-speed buses (USB, DDR, Ethernet). **Step 7 — Design Rule Check (DRC):** A *zero-error DRC* confirms the layout does not violate clearance, width, or connectivity rules. The *role of DRC* is to **catch manufacturing violations before fabrication**, ensuring DFM compliance and high yield. **Step 8 — Gerber Export:** The final layout is exported as **Gerber RS-274X** files (one per layer) plus **Excellon drill** files for fabrication. --- #### Model Solution to Q-A(b) We use the IPC-2152 external-trace equation: $$ I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725} $$ Substituting $I = 0.8$, $\Delta T = 15$, $k_{\text{ext}} = 0.048$: $$ 0.8 = 0.048 \cdot 15^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725} $$ $$ 15^{0.44} = e^{0.44 \cdot \ln 15} = e^{0.44 \cdot 2.7081} = e^{1.1915} \approx 3.293 $$ $$ A^{0.725} = \dfrac{0.8}{0.048 \cdot 3.293} = \dfrac{0.8}{0.1580} \approx 5.063 $$ $$ A = (5.063)^{1/0.725} = (5.063)^{1.3793} $$ $$ A = e^{1.3793 \cdot \ln 5.063} = e^{1.3793 \cdot 1.6224} = e^{2.2378} \approx 9.37 \;\text{mils}^2 $$ Trace width for 1 oz copper ($t = 1.378$ mils): $$ W = \dfrac{9.37}{1.378} \approx 6.80 \;\text{mils} \approx 0.173 \;\text{mm} $$ Apply **25 % engineering safety margin**: $$ W_{\text{design}} = 0.173 \times 1.25 \approx 0.216 \;\text{mm} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** Use a **0.25 mm (10 mil)** trace width (rounded to nearest standard EDA grid value). This satisfies the 0.8 A / 15 °C requirement on the top layer with safe margin. --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks *(Alternative Choice)* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the **4-layer PCB stack-up** used in modern embedded systems. Justify why **Layer 2 is a solid ground plane** and **Layer 3 is a power plane**. Mention **dielectric thickness** considerations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **+5 V** motor-driver trace is **120 mm long**, **0.5 mm wide**, **35 µm thick** (1 oz copper). It carries a continuous **1.5 A**. Compute: - (i) the **DC resistance** of the trace, - (ii) the **voltage drop**, - (iii) whether the **1 % KTU heuristic** is satisfied. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-B(a) A standard **4-layer PCB** stack-up from top to bottom: - **L1 (Top Signal):** Holds most components; high-speed and short routes. - **L2 (Ground Plane):** A *continuous* copper pour. Provides a low-inductance return path for **all** L1 and L4 signals. - **L3 (Power Plane):** Carries +3V3, +5V, or split analog/digital power regions. - **L4 (Bottom Signal):** Routes signals that cannot be placed on L1, plus through-hole or large components. **Why L2 is Ground:** 1. **Return-path integrity:** High-frequency return currents follow the path of *minimum loop area*, which is *directly under* the top trace. A solid L2 ground guarantees this loop is small → reduced EMI and inductance. 2. **Reference for controlled impedance:** L2 is the *reference plane* for microstrip traces on L1 (and L4). A continuous plane enables accurate $Z_0$ calculation. 3. **EMI shielding:** It acts as a *Faraday shield* between L1 and L3. **Why L3 is Power:** 1. **Low-inductance power distribution:** A plane has much lower inductance than a track, so decoupling is more effective. 2. **Bulk capacitance effect:** The plane pair (L2-L3) forms a *distributed decoupling capacitor* of ~ 1 nF over the whole board. 3. **Reduced IR drop** for high-current rails. **Dielectric Thickness:** Typical thicknesses: - **PP (prepreg) L1–L2 ≈ 0.21 mm**, **Core L2–L3 ≈ 1.20 mm**, **PP L3–L4 ≈ 0.21 mm**. - *Trade-off:* **Thinner** L1–L2 dielectric → **higher** distributed capacitance and **tighter** controlled impedance (good for USB) but **worse** PCB fab yield. - *Standard 1.6 mm total board thickness* is the most cost-effective choice for embedded systems. --- #### Model Solution to Q-B(b) **Given:** $I = 1.5$ A, $L = 0.12$ m, $w = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $t = 35 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\;\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$. **(i) Cross-section and Resistance:** $$ A = w \cdot t = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (3.5 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.75 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{m}^2 $$ $$ R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A} = \dfrac{1.724 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 0.12}{1.75 \times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{2.069 \times 10^{-9}}{1.75 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 0.118\;\Omega $$ **(ii) Voltage Drop:** $$ V_{\text{drop}} = I \cdot R = 1.5 \cdot 0.118 = 0.177 \;\text{V} = 177 \;\text{mV} $$ **(iii) 1 % Heuristic Check:** $$ \dfrac{V_{\text{drop}}}{V_{\text{cc}}} = \dfrac{0.177}{5.0} = 3.54 \;\% $$ > [!WARNING] > **3.54 % > 1 % heuristic** — the trace **fails** the KTU design budget. **Recommendation:** Widen the trace to **1.0 mm** (which halves the resistance) → new $V_{\text{drop}} \approx 88$ mV → 1.77 % (still high) → widen further to **1.5 mm** → $V_{\text{drop}} \approx 59$ mV → 1.18 % → close, but **for a motor driver, it is better to use a power plane polygon instead of a track** to get below 1 %. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in PCB Layout Design questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to specify the layer** (external vs internal) — the constant $k$ is **halved** for internal layers. > 2. **Mixing units** (mils vs mm vs cm). 1 mil = 0.0254 mm; 1 oz/ft² copper = 1.378 mils = 35 µm. > 3. **Ignoring the engineering safety margin** — board examiners expect a 20–30 % margin over the calculated minimum width. > 4. **Omitting dielectric thickness** in microstrip impedance answers — $h$ must be stated. > 5. **Confusing schematic capture with PCB layout** — they are *distinct* stages. A schematic-only answer on a PCB question gets **0 marks**. > 6. **Not stating Gerbers are RS-274X format** — saying "Gerber file" alone is incomplete. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **PCB Layout Design — Important Things to Remember** - **Definition:** PCB layout = physical realization of a schematic, output as **Gerber RS-274X + Excellon drill files**. - **Design Flow:** Schematic → Netlist → Footprint Map → Constraints → Placement → Routing → **DRC** → Gerber → Fabrication. - **IPC-2152 Trace Equation:** $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$, with $k_{\text{ext}} = 0.048$ and $k_{\text{int}} = 0.024$. - **1 oz copper** = 1.378 mils = 35 µm thick. - **Standard FR-4** $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.2$–$4.5$. - **4-Layer Stack-up:** L1 signal → L2 ground → L3 power → L4 signal. - **Microstrip Impedance:** $Z_0 = \dfrac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\!\left( \dfrac{5.98 h}{0.8 w + t} \right)$. - **High-Frequency Rule:** *Never route across a plane split* — return current must flow directly under the trace. - **Decoupling caps:** Place within **2 mm** of every IC Vcc pin, via-to-ground-plane. - **Trace Voltage Drop Heuristic:** Keep **< 1 %** of $V_{\text{cc}}$. - **Standard Trace Widths:** 0.2 mm, 0.25 mm, 0.5 mm, 0.65 mm, 1.0 mm. - **Standard Via Drill:** 0.3 mm, 0.4 mm; annular ring ≥ 0.15 mm. - **Component Clearance from Board Edge:** ≥ 2 mm (or per fab house spec). - **Solder Mask:** Liquid Photo-Imageable (LPI) green lacquer; defines **non-solderable** areas. - **Silkscreen:** White epoxy ink for reference designators, polarity marks, logos. - **EMC practices:** Stitching vias every $\lambda/20$, guard rings around antenna traces, 4-layer always preferred over 2-layer for clocks > 10 MHz. - **Thermal:** Use **copper pours + thermal via arrays** (12 vias, 0.3 mm, 1 mm pitch) under hot components. - **DFM Rule:** Always run a **zero-error DRC** before exporting Gerbers; a DRC-clean board is a *necessary but not sufficient* condition for a working prototype.

topics.title: Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Fabrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Fabrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Fabrication** refers to the entire industrial process of manufacturing a flat, laminated, non-conductive substrate (typically fiberglass-reinforced epoxy resin) with conductive copper pathways etched or deposited onto it, onto which electronic components are soldered to form a functional electronic assembly. In the context of KTU's Embedded Systems curriculum, PCB fabrication is treated as the foundational hardware realization stage that converts a logical circuit schematic (netlist + component placement) into a physical interconnect platform capable of mechanically supporting and electrically interconnecting ICs, passives, and connectors.

> [!NOTE]
> **PCB Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** A Printed Circuit Board is a self-contained, multi-layered assembly of dielectric substrates and patterned conductive copper sheets, manufactured by **subtractive etching**, **additive plating**, or a combination of both, used to provide both **mechanical rigidity** and **electrical connectivity** for surface-mounted and through-hole electronic components in an embedded system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Expectation:**
> The examiner expects students to know the **physical layer manufacturing process** of a PCB, the materials involved, the role of each chemical/mechanical step, the difference between **subtractive** and **additive** processes, and the key engineering trade-offs (cost vs. layer count, trace impedance vs. width, thermal dissipation vs. copper weight).

---

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — "The City Map of Electronics"

Imagine you are designing a **city** where every house (electronic component) needs a road (copper trace) connecting it to other houses, power stations, and water reservoirs. A PCB is exactly that — a meticulously planned **map of a city** etched onto a rigid sheet. The **copper traces** are the roads, the **vias** are the interchanges connecting one layer to another, the **solder mask** is the green paint that protects unwanted grass (short circuits) from growing between roads, and the **silkscreen** is the labeling layer that names every house for the technician (assembly engineer) to identify it quickly.

Before PCBs, engineers used **point-to-point wiring** on bakelite sheets — a "spaghetti junction" of tangled wires that was unreliable, noisy, and impossible to mass-produce. The PCB is the **industrial revolution** of the electronics city: standardized, repeatable, testable, and scalable.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of a PCB

| Pillar | Engineering Function | Physical Realization |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Support** | Holds components rigidly | FR-4 fiberglass-epoxy substrate (dielectric) |
| **Electrical Interconnection** | Routes signals & power | Patterned copper (Cu) layers — outer and inner |
| **Solderability & Protection** | Joins and shields components | Solder mask, silkscreen, surface finish (HASL, ENIG) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **standard copper thickness** used universally in KTU laboratory and commercial PCBs is **1 oz/ft² (≈ 35 µm)**. High-power PCBs use **2 oz/ft² (≈ 70 µm)** copper. This constant is the foundation of every trace resistance, current capacity, and impedance calculation.

---

## 1.4 Classification of PCBs

PCBs are classified based on the number of conductive layers and the rigidity of the substrate. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to identify at least these four categories:

1. **Single-Sided PCB** — One copper layer on one side of the substrate. Used in low-cost consumer electronics (calculators, FM radios, power supplies).
2. **Double-Sided PCB** — Copper layers on both sides, connected through **plated through-holes (PTH)**. Used in embedded controllers, motor drivers, and most laboratory projects.
3. **Multilayer PCB** — Three or more copper layers sandwiched between prepreg and core laminates, connected by **vias**. Used in smartphones, motherboards, FPGA boards (4 to 24 layers).
4. **Rigid-Flex PCB** — A combination of rigid FR-4 and flexible polyimide sections. Used in compact wearables and aerospace systems.

---

## 1.5 Why PCB Fabrication Matters in Embedded Systems

In an embedded system, the PCB is **not just a passive carrier** — it actively shapes:

- **Signal Integrity**: Trace geometry, layer stackup, and dielectric constant control impedance and crosstalk.
- **Thermal Management**: Copper pour areas act as heat spreaders for power devices.
- **EMI/EMC Compliance**: Ground planes and proper stackup reduce electromagnetic emissions.
- **Manufacturing Yield**: Fabrication tolerances (drill accuracy, etch factor) determine defect rate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Manufacturing Philosophies — Subtractive vs. Additive

PCB fabrication is fundamentally a **patterning** problem: how do we place copper **only** where we want it?

### A. Subtractive Process (Dominant in Industry)

The subtractive process starts with a **fully copper-clad laminate** and **chemically removes** the unwanted copper using an etchant (Ferric Chloride, Cupric Chloride, or Ammonium Persulfate). The desired copper pattern is **protected** by a photo-resist or screen-printed etch-resist ink. This is the **most widely used process** for FR-4 commercial and laboratory PCBs.

**Subtractive Process — Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. Start with copper-clad laminate (e.g., FR-4 with 35 µm copper on both sides).
2. Clean and **de-oxidize** the copper surface (micro-etch, pumice scrub).
3. Laminate **dry-film photoresist** (25–50 µm) using hot rollers (110–120 °C).
4. Expose the photoresist to **UV light** through a phototool (Gerber film) — the exposed (positive resist) or unexposed (negative resist) areas polymerize/crosslink.
5. **Develop** the resist in sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) solution — unpolymerized resist washes away, exposing copper to be etched.
6. **Etch** the exposed copper in Ferric Chloride — the protected copper pattern remains.
7. **Strip** the remaining photoresist using NaOH.
8. Proceed to **drilling, plating, solder mask, and surface finish**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ferric Chloride (FeCl₃)?** FeCl₃ is the most popular etchant for laboratory and small-batch PCB fabrication because it is **forgiving**, **economical**, and can be regenerated. The reaction: 2 FeCl₃ + Cu → 2 FeCl₂ + CuCl₂. The cupric chloride by-product can be re-oxidized back to ferric chloride by bubbling air/oxygen through the solution.

### B. Additive Process (Used in Advanced Multilayer & IC Substrates)

The additive process **deposits** copper only where needed on a bare (un-clad) substrate using **electroless plating** or **electrolytic plating**. It is more expensive but yields **sharper trace edges**, finer pitch, and is used for **High-Density Interconnect (HDI)** boards and IC substrates in BGA packages.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations for PCB Fabrication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY READ:** The following formulas are tested in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the formula, the **variable meanings**, the **units**, and the **typical numerical values** for FR-4 copper.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variable Definitions | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Trace DC Resistance** | $R = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{w \cdot t}$ | $\rho$ = resistivity of copper; $L$ = trace length; $w$ = trace width; $t$ = trace thickness | $\rho_{Cu} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$ at 20 °C; $t = 35\ \mu\text{m}$ for 1 oz Cu |
| 2 | **Trace Resistance per Unit Length (mil/Ω)** | $R_{\text{per in}} = \dfrac{\rho}{w \cdot t \cdot (\text{inch to m})}$ | Useful for quick design checks | $R \approx 0.5\ \text{m}\Omega/\text{in}$ for 10 mil wide, 1 oz trace |
| 3 | **Etch Factor (EF)** | $EF = \dfrac{2T}{W_b - W_t}$ | $T$ = copper thickness; $W_b$ = trace width at bottom; $W_t$ = trace width at top | Typical EF = 2 to 4 for chemical etching |
| 4 | **Etch Time** | $t_{\text{etch}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{Cu}}}{R_{\text{etch}}}$ | $T_{\text{Cu}}$ = copper thickness to remove; $R_{\text{etch}}$ = etch rate of etchant | FeCl₃ ≈ 25–40 µm/min; alkaline ≈ 30 µm/min |
| 5 | **Current Carrying Capacity (Internal Trace, IPC-2152 simplified)** | $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ | $\Delta T$ = allowable temperature rise (°C); $A$ = cross-section area (mil²); $k$ = empirical constant | Internal trace $k \approx 0.024$; $\Delta T \leq 10\text{ °C}$ for conservative design |
| 6 | **Characteristic Impedance (Microstrip)** | $Z_0 = \dfrac{87}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r + 1.41}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5.98h}{0.8w + t}\right)$ | $\varepsilon_r$ = dielectric constant of substrate; $h$ = dielectric height; $w$ = trace width; $t$ = trace thickness | FR-4: $\varepsilon_r = 4.2$ to $4.6$; for 50 $\Omega$ microstrip, $w/h \approx 1.8$ |
| 7 | **Capacitance per Unit Area (Parallel Plate)** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; $A$ = plate area; $d$ = dielectric thickness | Used for power plane decoupling estimation |
| 8 | **Dielectric Loss Tangent** | $\alpha_d = \dfrac{\pi \cdot f \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c} \cdot \tan \delta$ | $f$ = frequency; $c$ = speed of light; $\tan \delta$ = loss tangent | FR-4: $\tan \delta = 0.02$; useful up to ~1 GHz only |
| 9 | **Thermal Resistance of Copper Pour** | $R_{\theta,\text{Cu}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{k_{\text{Cu}} \cdot A_{\text{cu}}}$ | $L_{\text{eff}}$ = effective heat path length; $k_{\text{Cu}} = 401\ \text{W/(m·K)}$; $A_{\text{cu}}$ = copper cross-section area | Used in thermal pad design for power ICs |
| 10 | **Via DC Resistance (PTH)** | $R_{\text{via}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot h_{\text{via}}}{\pi \cdot r_{\text{via}}^2}$ | $h_{\text{via}}$ = plated barrel height; $r_{\text{via}}$ = finished via radius | Typical 0.3 mm via: 10–30 m$\Omega$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall — Do NOT use the vertical pipe symbol "$\vert$" or "$\mid$" inside the table cells above. The vertical pipe is the markdown table separator and will break the rendering. The equations above use the $\vert$ character strictly as a mathematical "divides" or "such that" symbol only outside table contexts, or are written without absolute value bars.**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic exercises — they are used daily in:

- **Embedded Hardware Companies** (e.g., Intel, Texas Instruments, Bosch) for designing high-speed digital boards (DDR4, USB 3.0, PCIe traces).
- **IoT Product Companies** for cost-optimized 2-layer Arduino-class designs.
- **Aerospace & Automotive** (ISO/TS 16949, IPC-6012 Class 3) for high-reliability multilayer PCBs.
- **Student Lab Projects** (KTU PSoC, ARM Cortex-M, FPGA boards) where trace widths must be calculated to safely carry LED or motor currents.

The etch factor formula, for example, is the **single most important parameter** that determines the minimum achievable trace width and spacing — the cornerstone of **Design for Manufacturing (DFM)**. A poor etch factor (EF = 1) means the sidewalls of traces are almost vertical, allowing fine-pitch QFN packages. A high EF (4) means sloped sidewalls, which limits line density.

---

## 2.4 The Materials Science Behind the PCB

The **dielectric substrate** is the heart of a PCB. The most common KTU-relevant materials are:

| Material | Composition | Dielectric Constant ($\varepsilon_r$) | Max Operating Temp | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FR-2** | Phenolic resin + paper | 4.5 | 105 °C | Toy electronics, calculators (low-cost) |
| **FR-4** | Epoxy resin + woven glass | 4.2 – 4.6 | 130–140 °C | Universal — 90% of all PCBs |
| **CEM-1** | Epoxy + paper + glass | 4.2 | 110 °C | Low-end consumer |
| **CEM-3** | Epoxy + glass | 4.5 | 120 °C | Mid-range consumer |
| **Polyimide** | Polyimide film | 3.5 | 260 °C | Flex PCBs, aerospace, military |
| **Rogers (RO4003C)** | Ceramic-filled PTFE | 3.38 | 280 °C | RF, microwave, 5G |
| **BT Resin** | Bismaleimide-Triazine | 3.9 | 180 °C | IC substrates (BGA, CSP) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** "Why is FR-4 called FR-4?" — The **"FR"** stands for **"Flame Retardant"** and the **"4"** designates the NEMA (National Electrical Manufacturers Association) grade. Grade 4 is a **woven glass-reinforced epoxy laminate** with superior mechanical and electrical properties — it self-extinguishes when the flame is removed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Trace DC Resistance from First Principles

### Problem Statement
A **10 mm long**, **0.2 mm wide** copper trace of **35 µm (1 oz)** thickness carries a DC signal on a FR-4 PCB. Calculate its DC resistance at room temperature (20 °C) and at 100 °C. Given: $\rho_{Cu,20} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$; temperature coefficient $\alpha = 3.93 \times 10^{-3}\ /\text{°C}$ (referenced to 20 °C).

### Step 1 — Identify the geometric cross-section area

The cross-section of the trace is a rectangle. The current flows along the length ($L$) and the cross-section normal to the current is $w \times t$.

$$A = w \times t = 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 35 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$A = 7.0 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Resistivity Formula

The fundamental relationship between resistance, resistivity, length, and area is:

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

Substituting the known values:

$$R_{20} = \frac{1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{7.0 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$R_{20} = \frac{1.724 \times 10^{-10}}{7.0 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$R_{20} = 0.0246\ \Omega \approx 24.6\ \text{m}\Omega$$

### Step 3 — Correct for Temperature (Linear Approximation)

The resistivity of copper increases linearly with temperature:

$$\rho(T) = \rho_{20}\bigl[1 + \alpha(T - 20)\bigr]$$

At $T = 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$:

$$\rho_{100} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times \bigl[1 + 3.93 \times 10^{-3} \times (100 - 20)\bigr]$$

$$\rho_{100} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times \bigl[1 + 0.3144\bigr]$$

$$\rho_{100} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.3144 = 2.266 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Resistance at 100 °C

$$R_{100} = \frac{2.266 \times 10^{-8} \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{7.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.0324\ \Omega \approx 32.4\ \text{m}\Omega$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

The trace resistance **increased by 31.4%** from 20 °C to 100 °C. For a low-power logic signal this is negligible, but for **high-current power traces** (e.g., 1 A flowing through a 32 mΩ trace dissipates 32 mW → 32 mV drop), this is critical and motivates **thick copper (2 oz, 70 µm)** or **wide traces / copper pours**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Verification (Sanity Check):** The rule of thumb for a 1 oz copper trace is **~0.5 mΩ per inch per 10 mil width**. Here, length = 10 mm = 0.394 inch, width = 0.2 mm = 7.87 mil ≈ 10 mil. Expected $R \approx 0.5 \times 0.394 = 0.197\ \text{m}\Omega$ **per square** — this is **sheet resistance**, not total. Multiplying by length/width ratio (0.394/0.0787) gives **~1 mΩ**, which is a factor off because the rule of thumb uses 1 oz/sqft sheet resistance of 0.5 mΩ/sq. Our calculated 24.6 mΩ is realistic for a long, narrow power trace. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Trace Width for a Given Current

### Problem Statement
A 2-layer FR-4 PCB carries a steady current of $I = 2\ \text{A}$ through a power trace. The maximum allowable temperature rise is $\Delta T = 10\ ^\circ\text{C}$ above ambient. Using the **IPC-2152 empirical formula** for an **external (outer layer)** trace with 1 oz copper, find the minimum trace width $w_{\text{min}}$.

### Governing Equation (External Trace, Conservative)

$$I = k_{\text{ext}} \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$$

where $A$ is the cross-section in **mil²**, $\Delta T$ in °C, $I$ in Amps, and $k_{\text{ext}} = 0.048$ (IPC-2152 standard, external trace, 1 oz Cu).

### Step 1 — Solve for $A$

Rearranging:

$$A^{0.725} = \frac{I}{k_{\text{ext}} \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}}$$

$$A = \left(\frac{I}{k_{\text{ext}} \cdot \Delta T^{0.44}}\right)^{\frac{1}{0.725}}$$

### Step 2 — Plug in Numbers

$$A = \left(\frac{2}{0.048 \times 10^{0.44}}\right)^{1.379}$$

Compute $10^{0.44}$:

$$10^{0.44} = 2.754$$

$$A = \left(\frac{2}{0.048 \times 2.754}\right)^{1.379} = \left(\frac{2}{0.1322}\right)^{1.379} = (15.13)^{1.379}$$

$$A = 15.13^{1.379} \approx 41.8\ \text{mil}^2$$

### Step 3 — Compute Trace Width

For 1 oz copper, $t = 1.4\ \text{mil}$ (35 µm ≈ 1.378 mil). The cross-section is $A = w \times t$, so:

$$w_{\text{min}} = \frac{A}{t} = \frac{41.8}{1.378} \approx 30.3\ \text{mil}$$

Convert to mm:

$$w_{\text{min}} = 30.3 \times 0.0254 = 0.77\ \text{mm}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Decision

A 2 A trace on outer layer with 10 °C rise requires a trace **at least 0.8 mm wide** (using standard design margin of 25%). The designer should further add a **solder mask opening** and **thermal relief** if connecting to a copper pour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Always use a **safety factor of 1.5× to 2×** on the calculated minimum trace width to account for manufacturing tolerance (±10% on trace width from etching) and transient current spikes (inrush, motor stall).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Etch Time Calculation

### Problem Statement
A laboratory fabricator uses **Sodium Persulfate (Na₂S₂O₈)** etchant with a measured etch rate of $R_{\text{etch}} = 25\ \mu\text{m/min}$ at 50 °C. The copper foil is 35 µm (1 oz). If the etchant is applied for $t = 2\ \text{min}$, what is the **remaining copper thickness** at the protected regions? (Hint: the etchant attacks copper isotropically.)

### Step 1 — Lateral Etch Distance

In a real chemical etch, the etchant removes copper both **vertically** (downward) and **laterally** (under the resist edge). The lateral etch distance after time $t$ is:

$$d_{\text{lateral}} = R_{\text{etch}} \times t$$

$$d_{\text{lateral}} = 25 \times 2 = 50\ \mu\text{m}$$

This means each edge of the trace recedes by 50 µm.

### Step 2 — Net Trace Width Reduction

If the design photomask specified a width $W_{\text{mask}}$, the **final trace width** on the PCB is:

$$W_{\text{final}} = W_{\text{mask}} - 2 \cdot d_{\text{lateral}}$$

This is the **undercut effect** of isotropic etching.

### Step 3 — Compute Etch Factor

The etch factor relates the lateral undercut to the vertical copper thickness removed:

$$EF = \frac{2T_{\text{Cu}}}{W_{\text{mask}} - W_{\text{final}}} = \frac{2 \times 35}{2 \times 50} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.7$$

A **low etch factor (< 1)** indicates **significant lateral undercut** — undesirable for fine-pitch traces. Industrial spray etching achieves **EF = 2 to 4** by using **directional spray nozzles**, **higher temperature (50–55 °C)**, and **agitation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Compensation:** When designing a PCB, the photomask must compensate for the lateral etch. If the desired final trace width is $W_{\text{final}}$ and the etch rate is known, the photomask width is enlarged to: $W_{\text{mask}} = W_{\text{final}} + 2 d_{\text{lateral}}$. This is a classic **DFM (Design for Manufacturing)** adjustment in KTU lab PCB design.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Dissipation in a Trace

### Problem Statement
A 1 A current flows through the 24.6 mΩ trace calculated in Section 3.1. Calculate the **power dissipated** as heat in the trace, and the **temperature rise** using the thermal resistance of the copper pour (assume $R_{\theta,\text{Cu}} = 50\ ^\circ\text{C/W}$ for the trace-to-ambient path on a 2-layer FR-4 board).

### Step 1 — Power Dissipation

$$P = I^2 \cdot R = 1^2 \times 0.0246 = 0.0246\ \text{W} = 24.6\ \text{mW}$$

### Step 2 — Temperature Rise

$$\Delta T = P \times R_{\theta,\text{Cu}} = 0.0246 \times 50 = 1.23\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

This is well within the **10 °C conservative limit**, confirming the trace is safe for 1 A. However, at 5 A:

$$P = 5^2 \times 0.0246 = 0.615\ \text{W}$$

$$\Delta T = 0.615 \times 50 = 30.75\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

This **violates** the 10 °C limit — a clear case for using **2 oz copper** (halving resistance, halving dissipation) or **widening the trace**.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — End-to-End PCB Fabrication Process Flow

This flowchart maps the complete manufacturing pipeline of a double-sided FR-4 PCB, from CAD input to final electrical test. It serves as the **block-level functional architecture** of the entire fabrication chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD Schematic Entry] --> A2[Netlist Generation]
    A2 --> A3[Component Placement]
    A3 --> A4[Routing and Gerber File Export]
    A4 --> B1[Phototool Film Printing]
    B1 --> C1[Copper Clad Laminate Preparation]
    C1 --> C2[Mechanical Scrub and Micro-etch Cleaning]
    C2 --> C3[Dry Film Photoresist Lamination]
    C3 --> C4[UV Exposure Through Phototool]
    C4 --> C5[Resist Development in Na2CO3]
    C5 --> C6[Chemical Etching in FeCl3]
    C6 --> C7[Photoresist Stripping in NaOH]
    C7 --> D1[Optical Inspection AOI]
    D1 --> D2[NC Drilling of Holes and Vias]
    D2 --> D3[Electroless Copper Plating for PTH]
    D3 --> D4[Electroplate Copper to Build Barrel]
    D4 --> D5[Apply Tin-Lead or ENIG Surface Finish]
    D5 --> E1[Solder Mask Lamination and Imaging]
    E1 --> E2[Silkscreen Legend Printing]
    E2 --> E3[Final Bake and Curing]
    E3 --> F1[Routing and V-Cut Profiling]
    F1 --> F2[Electrical Continuity Test FCT]
    F2 --> F3[Final Visual Inspection and Packaging]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The pipeline is split into six logical **islands** — Design (A), Imaging (B), Imaging-Realization (C), Metallization (D), Finishing (E), and Singulation-Test (F). The arrows are **strictly sequential** for the subtractive process; modifications to the flow (e.g., additive semi-additive process) occur at the metallization stage D.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Layer Stackup of a 4-Layer PCB

This diagram presents the **physical cross-section** of a typical 4-layer FR-4 PCB used in embedded controllers, FPGAs, and high-speed digital designs.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Component Side - Top Silkscreen Legend]
    L1[L1 - Top Copper Layer 35 um Signal Traces]
    L2[Prepreg 2125 microns Dielectric FR4 eps_r 4.3]
    L3[L2 - Inner Plane 1 Ground GND Pour]
    L4[Core 800 microns Dielectric FR4]
    L5[L3 - Inner Plane 2 Power VCC Pour]
    L6[Prepreg 2125 microns Dielectric FR4]
    L7[L4 - Bottom Copper Layer 35 um Signal Traces]
    L8[Solder Side - Bottom Solder Mask Green]
    V1((PTH Via 0.3 mm)) -. connects .-> L1
    V1 -. passes through .-> L4
    V1 -. terminates at .-> L7
    V2((Blind Via)) -. L1 to L3 .-> L1
    V2 -. to .-> L3
    V3((Buried Via)) -. L3 to L4 .-> L3
    V3 -. to .-> L4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **two inner planes (L2, L3)** are continuous copper pours that act as low-impedance **ground** and **power** references. They:
> (a) Provide excellent **power distribution** (decoupling capacitance $\approx 1\ \text{pF per cm}^2$ of plane area at 0.2 mm dielectric thickness);
> (b) Create a **controlled-impedance environment** for high-speed signals on L1 and L4;
> (c) Reduce **EMI radiation** by shielding inner signals from the outside world.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Decision Tree for PCB Process Selection

This decision tree helps the KTU 2024 student identify which **fabrication process variant** is appropriate for a given application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0{Is the design a 1 layer or 2 layer board?}
    S0 -->|Yes 1-2 layers| S1{Is the feature size greater than 200 um?}
    S0 -->|No 4 layers| S2{Is the design high speed greater than 100 MHz?}
    S1 -->|Yes feature greater than 200 um| S3[Use Conventional Subtractive Process]
    S1 -->|No feature less than 200 um| S4{Is the volume greater than 1000 units?}
    S4 -->|Yes high volume| S5[Use Subtractive with ENIG Finish]
    S4 -->|No low volume or prototype| S6[Use Modified Semi Additive Process mSAP]
    S2 -->|Yes high speed| S7{Is the layer count greater than 8?}
    S2 -->|No low speed 4 layer| S8[Use Standard 4-Layer Subtractive FR4]
    S7 -->|Yes greater than 8 layers| S9[Use Sequential Lamination with HDI]
    S7 -->|No 4-8 layers| S10[Use Subtractively Patterned with Impedance Control]
    S3 --> DONE[End - Conventional Subtractive]
    S5 --> DONE
    S6 --> DONE
    S8 --> DONE
    S9 --> DONE
    S10 --> DONE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Decision Tree:** The KTU laboratory PCB prototyping typically falls into the **S3 branch** (conventional subtractive, 1–2 layer, > 200 µm feature), which is the path students will physically execute in the workshop using FeCl₃ etching and a UV exposure box. The **S6 and S9 branches** are industrial processes (HDI, mSAP) found in smartphone and IC substrate manufacturing.
