- **SPI:** Loopback test — connect MOSI to MISO externally, transmit and compare.

### Phase 7 – Bootloader & OS Hand-off
- Program a minimal bootloader (e.g., **U-Boot SPL**, **TF-A BL2**, or vendor's **BootROM** secondary stage) into QSPI/eMMC.
- Verify autoboot from the correct boot source (configured via boot pins / eFuses).
- Observe the serial console for the bootloader banner — confirms that DDR, UART, Flash, and clock tree are *all* working in concert.

## 2.2 The Power Sequencing Equation

While board bring-up is largely procedural, the **power-up sequence timing** can be expressed quantitatively. The standard rule for multi-rail SoCs is:

$$T_{\text{seq},i} = T_{\text{ramp},i} + T_{\text{settle},i}$$

where:
- $T_{\text{seq},i}$ is the total time for rail $i$ to become valid after the previous rail reaches 90 % of its target.
- $T_{\text{ramp},i}$ is the monotonic rise time (typically **1 ms – 5 ms** for PMIC-controlled rails).
- $T_{\text{settle},i}$ is the time to settle within the **±3 %** regulation band (typically **< 1 ms** for a well-compensated buck regulator).

The SoC datasheet specifies a **maximum inter-rail delay**, $T_{\text{max-delay}}$, and a **maximum total bring-up time**, $T_{\text{max-total}}$, which must not be violated:

$$T_{\text{max-total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{\text{seq},i} \leq T_{\text{max-total, datasheet}}$$

For a typical 4-rail SoC, $T_{\text{max-total}} \approx$ **20 ms – 100 ms**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|--------------------|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Power-rail tolerance | $\Delta V / V$ | **±3 % to ±5 %** | Allowable deviation from nominal |
| 2 | Cold-start in-rush limit | $I_{\text{inrush,max}}$ | 1.5× to 2× of $I_{\text{steady}}$ | Decays in 20–100 ms |
| 3 | Crystal start-up time | $T_{\text{start}}$ | **1 ms – 10 ms** | Depends on ESR, load caps |
| 4 | Crystal duty cycle | $D$ | **45 % – 55 %** | Symmetric for digital logic |
| 5 | Reset pulse width (min) | $T_{\text{RST,min}}$ | **> 1 µs** | SoC-datasheet dependent |
| 6 | JTAG TCK frequency (initial scan) | $f_{\text{TCK}}$ | **1 MHz** | Per IEEE 1149.1 |
| 7 | Total power sequencing window | $T_{\text{max-total}}$ | **20 ms – 100 ms** | Across all N rails |
| 8 | UART boot console baud rate | $f_{\text{baud}}$ | **115200 bps** | 8-N-1, no flow control (typical) |
| 9 | DDR calibration test duration | $T_{\text{cal}}$ | **≥ 24 hours** | Stress test for intermittent faults |
| 10 | Logic analyzer sample rate | $f_{\text{sample}}$ | **≥ 4× bus frequency** | Nyquist for digital capture |
| 11 | Probe input impedance | $Z_{\text{in}}$ | **1 MΩ // 10 pF** | Standard 10× passive |
| 12 | Power sequencing inter-rail delay | $T_{\text{delay},i}$ | **0 ms – 10 ms** | Per rail $i$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Board bring-up is the **single highest-leverage activity** in the entire embedded product development cycle because:

1. **It de-risks the project** before hundreds of engineer-hours are spent on driver development.
2. **It catches schematic errors early** — e.g., swapped I²C SDA/SCL, wrong boot-pin strapping, missing pull-ups.
3. **It establishes the BSP foundation** — without verified DDR and clock tree, the OS will not boot.
4. **It provides the regression baseline** — every future firmware update is validated against the *known-good* bring-up firmware.
5. **It is the only phase where PCB re-spin cost is still acceptable** — fixing a hardware bug during EVT costs **₹2–5 lakhs** and **2–4 weeks**; fixing it post-PVT can cost **₹20+ lakhs** in tooling and **3+ months** in market delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry context:** Major OEMs (e.g., **Bosch, Continental, Harman, Qualcomm, Intel**) have dedicated "Hardware Bring-up Teams" of 3–6 engineers whose *only* job for the first 4 weeks of a new SoC platform is to perform this process. It is considered a **senior-level skill** in the embedded industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** Board bring-up is a *procedural engineering discipline*, not a closed-form physics derivation. Therefore, the "derivations" in this section are **step-by-step diagnostic procedures** — the exact methodology a KTU examiner expects a student to write out for full marks.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The "First-Light" Power-Up Procedure

**Problem Statement (KTU Part B style):** *"You have just received a fresh PCBA for a 4-rail SoC (1.8 V, 3.3 V, 1.2 V, 1.0 V). The PMIC has an ENABLE pin. Describe, step-by-step, the procedure you will follow to safely bring the board to its first powered state without damaging any component. Justify each step."*

**Step-by-step solution (exhaustive):**

**Step 1 – Confirm lab setup and safety.**
- Bench supply current limit: $I_{\text{lim}} = 0.5 \times I_{\text{expected}}$.
- OVP threshold: $V_{\text{OVP}} = 1.10 \times V_{\text{input,nominal}}$.
- Connect a **10× oscilloscope probe** across the input bulk capacitor.
- Connect a **DMM in series (4-wire mode preferred)** for current measurement.
- Grounding wrist strap worn; ESD-safe mat connected to earth.

**Step 2 – Visual inspection (Phase 0).**
- Magnifying lamp: scan for solder bridges, missing passives, rotated ICs.
- Verify PCB silkscreen revision matches the schematic baseline.
- Confirm JTAG connector pin-1 orientation.

**Step 3 – Power supply connection.**
- Set supply to **0 V**, current limit engaged.
- Power on the supply; verify output is **0 V ± 10 mV**.

**Step 4 – Voltage ramp-up (the "First Light" moment).**
- Slowly increase $V_{\text{IN}}$ in **0.5 V increments**, pausing **2 seconds** at each step.
- At each step, observe: (a) input current, (b) any warm components (touch with finger cautiously).
- For a typical 5 V input system, full voltage should be reached within **10 seconds** of the start of the ramp.

**Step 5 – Initial rail check.**
- Probe the 1.8 V rail first (usually the first regulator upstream of the SoC's I/O).
- Verify $V_{1.8}$ is within **±3 %** of **1.8 V**, i.e., **1.746 V – 1.854 V**.
- Verify 3.3 V, 1.2 V, 1.0 V in sequence.
- Measure total input current. If $I_{\text{in}} > 1.5 \times I_{\text{expected,quiescent}}$, immediately power down and re-inspect.

**Step 6 – Power-rail sequencing check.**
- Use a **4-channel oscilloscope** with probes on each rail.
- Trigger on the 1.8 V rising edge.
- Verify the **inter-rail delay** for each subsequent rail:

$$T_{\text{delay},i} = T_{\text{rise}(90\%),i} - T_{\text{rise}(90\%),i-1}$$

- Compare with the SoC datasheet's $T_{\text{max-delay}}$. For a typical NXP i.MX8M:

$$T_{\text{max-delay}} = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(per rail-to-rail transition)}$$

- If any rail violates the sequence, the PMIC's **SEQ_CONFIG** registers must be reprogrammed or external delay circuits (RC networks) must be added.

**Step 7 – Steady-state power quality check.**
- AC-couple the oscilloscope and measure **peak-to-peak ripple** on each rail:

$$V_{\text{ripple,pp}} \leq 1\% \text{ of } V_{\text{rail}}$$

- For 1.0 V core: $V_{\text{ripple,pp}} \leq$ **10 mV**.
- Excessive ripple usually indicates inadequate decoupling, a faulty capacitor, or a saturated inductor.

**Step 8 – Power-down and proceed to clock verification.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The JTAG IDCODE Verification Walk-Through

**Problem Statement:** *"You have just powered up a custom board featuring an ARM Cortex-A53 SoC. How will you use the JTAG interface to verify that the SoC is alive, and what specific register read will constitute the proof of life?"*

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 – Physical connection.**
- Connect J-Link debugger to the board's 20-pin JTAG header (or 10-pin Cortex Debug for SWD).
- Connect USB to host PC.

**Step 2 – Host-side software launch.**
- Open a terminal and run `JLinkExe -device <SoC-PN> -if JTAG -speed 1000`.
- The `-speed 1000` sets $f_{\text{TCK}} =$ **1 MHz** (safe initial speed).

**Step 3 – Establish TAP state machine in Reset.**
- The debugger automatically drives TMS = 1 for 5 TCK cycles to enter **Test-Logic-Reset** state.

**Step 4 – Shift the IDCODE instruction.**
- Move to **Shift-IR** state, shift in the 4-bit IR value for IDCODE: **`0001`** (for most ARM cores, this is `0b0001` = 0x1).
- Move to **Update-IR**, then **Shift-DR**.

**Step 5 – Clock out the 32-bit IDCODE.**
- The SoC shifts out 32 bits: a 1-bit start (always 1), 11 bits of manufacturer ID, 16 bits of part number, and 4 bits of version.
- Example IDCODE for **NXP i.MX8M Quad:** `0x5BA00477`.

**Step 6 – Interpret the result.**
- If the read IDCODE matches the expected `0x5BA00477` ± the 4-bit version field, the SoC is **alive and electrically responding**.
- If the IDCODE reads as all-ones (`0xFFFFFFFF`) or all-zeros (`0x00000000`), it indicates a fault in: (a) power, (b) clock, (c) reset, (d) JTAG wiring, (e) damaged SoC.

**Step 7 – Iterate to higher TCK.**
- Once IDCODE is verified, gradually increase TCK to **10 MHz, 25 MHz, 50 MHz** to verify signal integrity on the JTAG bus.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Phase-to-Failure-Cause Mapping

This matrix is **critical for KTU Part B answers** because examiners love to ask *"If [symptom] occurs during [phase], what is the most likely root cause?"*

| Phase | Symptom Observed | Most Likely Root Cause | Verification Action |
|-------|------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 0 (Visual) | Solder bridge under BGA | PCB fab defect or reflow profile error | X-ray inspection, re-reflow |
| 1 (Power) | Input current = 0 (no consumption) | Open input fuse, wrong input polarity | Check fuse, DMM continuity |
| 1 (Power) | Input current = short (supply current limits) | Capacitor solder bridge, reverse-polarity cap | Power off, visual + LCR check |
| 1 (Power) | Rail is present but noisy ($V_{\text{ripple,pp}} > 1\%$) | Missing/broken decoupling cap | Check caps, scope FFT |
| 1 (Power) | Rail out-of-spec (e.g., 1.65 V instead of 1.8 V) | PMIC feedback resistor wrong value, FB pin open | Cross-check schematic vs. BOM |
| 2 (Clock) | No clock output at crystal pins | Crystal damaged, load caps missing, XO enable low | Substitute known-good crystal |
| 2 (Clock) | Clock present but jitter > datasheet | Poor PCB layout, noisy supply to XO | Improve grounding, check decoupling |
| 3 (Reset) | SoC stuck in reset (RESETn held low) | Reset supervisor not releasing, push-button shorted | Probe RESETn, check supervisor |
| 4 (JTAG) | IDCODE reads `0xFFFFFFFF` | No power, no clock, TMS/TDO shorted, TDO floating | Re-verify Phases 1–3 first |
| 4 (JTAG) | IDCODE reads wrong value | Cross-talk on JTAG bus, incorrect IR length | Lower TCK speed, check layout |
| 5 (DDR) | DDR calibration fails | Wrong ZQ calibration resistor, signal-integrity issue | Run vendor stress tester, check SI |
| 5 (DDR) | Random data corruption in DDR | Marginal timing, insufficient decoupling | Tune DDR timing, add decoupling |
| 6 (UART) | Garbage on serial console | Wrong baud rate, wrong voltage level (RS-232 vs TTL) | Verify UART mode, level shifter |
| 6 (I²C) | No ACK from device | Wrong slave address, missing pull-up, SDA/SCL swap | Check pull-ups, scan full 7-bit range |
| 7 (Boot) | Bootloader banner not seen | Wrong boot source, corrupted image, bad eMMC | Verify boot pins, reflash via JTAG |

## 3.4 Quantitative Example: Power Budget Verification

**Problem Statement:** *"A 4-rail SoC system has the following expected steady-state currents: 1.8 V @ 250 mA, 3.3 V @ 400 mA, 1.2 V @ 800 mA, 1.0 V @ 1200 mA. The input supply is 5.0 V. The total PMIC efficiency is 88 %. During bring-up, you measure an input current of 2.8 A at 5.0 V. Is this within the expected range?"*

**Step 1 – Calculate total output power.**

$$P_{\text{out}} = (1.8)(0.25) + (3.3)(0.40) + (1.2)(0.80) + (1.0)(1.20)$$

$$\quad\quad\quad = 0.45 + 1.32 + 0.96 + 1.20 = 3.93 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 – Calculate required input power at 88 % efficiency.**

$$P_{\text{in,expected}} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta} = \frac{3.93}{0.88} = 4.466 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 – Calculate expected input current at 5.0 V.**

$$I_{\text{in,expected}} = \frac{P_{\text{in,expected}}}{V_{\text{in}}} = \frac{4.466}{5.0} = 0.893 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 – Compare with measured value.**

$$I_{\text{in,measured}} = 2.8 \text{ A} \quad \text{vs} \quad I_{\text{in,expected}} = 0.893 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 – Conclusion.**

$$I_{\text{in,measured}} \approx 3.13 \times I_{\text{in,expected}}$$

This is **>3× higher** than expected, indicating a **fault condition**. Likely root causes:
- A short circuit on one of the rails (most probable).
- An LDO in dropout drawing excessive current.
- An incorrectly seated SoC with internal substrate shorts.

**Action:** Power off immediately. Re-verify Phase 0 (visual) and use a **thermal camera** or **fingertip touch** to locate the hot component, then isolate the fault by removing power rails one at a time (using a current-limited supply per rail).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1: Top-Level Board Bring-up Workflow (Phased State Machine)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the strict ordering of bring-up phases and the failure-handling loop that dominates real-world bring-up work.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 0: Visual and Mechanical Inspection] --> B[Phase 1: PDN Verification]
    B --> C{All rails within 3 percent and sequence correct?}
    C -- No --> B1[Failure Loop: Identify short, open, or sequencing violation]
    B1 --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Phase 2: Clock Tree Validation]
    D --> E{Clock stable, correct frequency, duty 45 to 55 percent?}
    E -- No --> E1[Failure Loop: Check crystal, load caps, XO enable]
    E1 --> D
    E -- Yes --> F[Phase 3: Reset Architecture Verification]
    F --> G{Reset deasserts cleanly after rails stable?}
    G -- No --> G1[Failure Loop: Check reset supervisor, button circuit]
    G1 --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Phase 4: SoC Sanity via JTAG IDCODE]
    H --> I{IDCODE matches expected manufacturer plus part number?}
    I -- No --> I1[Failure Loop: Recheck power, clock, reset, JTAG wiring]
    I1 --> H
    I -- Yes --> J[Phase 5: Memory Subsystem Validation]
    J --> K{SRAM pass, DDR calibration passes 24 hour stress, Flash ID correct?}
    K -- No --> K1[Failure Loop: Tune DDR timing, replace Flash, reball SoC]
    K1 --> J
    K -- Yes --> L[Phase 6: Basic Peripheral Bring-up]
    L --> M{GPIO LED blinks, UART prints banner, I2C and SPI respond?}
    M -- No --> M1[Failure Loop: Check pin mux, level shifters, pull-ups]
    M1 --> L
    M -- Yes --> N[Phase 7: Bootloader and OS Hand-off]
    N --> O{Bootloader banner on serial console, autoboot from correct source?}
    O -- No --> O1[Failure Loop: Verify boot pins, reflash, check eMMC or QSPI]
    O1 --> N
    O -- Yes --> P[BRING-UP COMPLETE: BSP Development Begins]
```

## 4.2 Figure 2: Power Delivery Network (PDN) Block Diagram for a 4-Rail SoC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN[DC Input: 5V to 12V Bench Supply with OCP] --> PMIC[PMIC: Multi-Rail Buck and LDO Controller]
    PMIC --> R1[1.8V Rail: SoC I/O and Analog]
    PMIC --> R2[3.3V Rail: Peripheral and External Devices]
    PMIC --> R3[1.2V Rail: SoC LPDDR4 Interface and PHY]
    PMIC --> R4[1.0V Rail: SoC Core and GPU VDD]
    R1 --> DEC1[Decoupling Network: 100nF plus 10uF plus Bulk]
    R2 --> DEC2[Decoupling Network: 100nF plus 10uF plus Bulk]
    R3 --> DEC3[Decoupling Network: 100nF plus 10uF plus Bulk]
    R4 --> DEC4[Decoupling Network: 100nF plus 10uF plus Bulk]
    DEC1 --> SOC[SoC: Application Processor]
    DEC2 --> SOC
    DEC3 --> SOC
    DEC4 --> SOC
    SOC --> XO[External Crystal Oscillator or XO with Enable]
    XO --> SOC
    SOC --> RST[Reset Supervisor and Push Button]
    RST --> SOC
    SOC --> JTAG[JTAG or SWD Debug Header]
    SOC --> DDR[LPDDR4 or DDR3L Memory]
    SOC --> FLASH[QSPI NOR or eMMC Flash]
    SOC --> UART[UART to USB Serial Console]
```

## 4.3 Figure 3: JTAG TAP Controller State Machine (IEEE 1149.1)

This diagram shows the canonical **16-state TAP controller** through which the debugger communicates with the SoC during the IDCODE scan.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TestLogicReset
    TestLogicReset --> RunTestIdle: TMS = 0
    RunTestIdle --> SelectDRScan: TMS = 1
    SelectDRScan --> SelectIRScan: TMS = 1
    SelectDRScan --> CaptureDR: TMS = 0
    CaptureDR --> ShiftDR: TMS = 0
    ShiftDR --> ShiftDR: TMS = 0
    ShiftDR --> Exit1DR: TMS = 1
    Exit1DR --> PauseDR: TMS = 0
    Exit1DR --> UpdateDR: TMS = 1
    UpdateDR --> RunTestIdle: TMS = 0
    UpdateDR --> SelectDRScan: TMS = 1
    PauseDR --> Exit2DR: TMS = 1
    Exit2DR --> ShiftDR: TMS = 0
    Exit2DR --> UpdateDR: TMS = 1
    SelectIRScan --> CaptureIR: TMS = 0
    CaptureIR --> ShiftIR: TMS = 0
    ShiftIR --> ShiftIR: TMS = 0
    ShiftIR --> Exit1IR: TMS = 1
    Exit1IR --> PauseIR: TMS = 0
    Exit1IR --> UpdateIR: TMS = 1
    UpdateIR --> RunTestIdle: TMS = 0
    UpdateIR --> SelectDRScan: TMS = 1
    PauseIR --> Exit2IR: TMS = 1
    Exit2IR --> ShiftIR: TMS = 0
    Exit2IR --> UpdateIR: TMS = 1
    TestLogicReset --> TestLogicReset: TMS = 1
```

## 4.4 Figure 4: Layered Bring-up Stack — Hardware-to-Software Hand-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Layer 0: Bare Silicon and PCB - No Code] --> L1[Layer 1: First Light - Power Rails Verified]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2: Clocks and Reset Stable]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: JTAG Debugger Can Halt and Inspect Core]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Internal SRAM Read-Write Verified]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: External DDR Calibrated and Stress Tested]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6: GPIO and UART Functional - LED Blink and Serial Console]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7: Bootloader Loaded and Executing from Flash]
    L7 --> L8[Layer 8: Board Support Package - BSP - Complete]
    L8 --> L9[Layer 9: OS Ported - FreeRTOS or Linux Kernel Boots]
    L9 --> L10[Layer 10: Application Development Begins]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Define the term 'Board Bring-up' in the context of embedded product development. List any FOUR distinct phases involved in the process."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Board bring-up is the systematic, phased engineering process of verifying the functionality of a newly fabricated embedded hardware board, beginning from the moment the first supply voltage is applied and culminating in the successful execution of a minimal "Hello World" firmware (typically a GPIO LED blink) on the target hardware.

**Four Phases (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Phase 0:** Visual and mechanical inspection (solder bridges, missing components).
2. **Phase 1:** Power Delivery Network (PDN) verification — all rails within ±3 % tolerance.
3. **Phase 4:** SoC sanity check via JTAG IDCODE scan.
4. **Phase 6:** Basic peripheral bring-up — GPIO blink, UART console output.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"What is the significance of reading the JTAG IDCODE register during board bring-up? What does an IDCODE of `0xFFFFFFFF` typically indicate?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Significance (1.5 marks):** The IDCODE register is a 32-bit value defined by the IEEE 1149.1 standard and contains the manufacturer ID, part number, and version of the device. A successful read proves that:
- The SoC is receiving clean power on all required rails.
- The clock tree is functional.
- The reset signal has been de-asserted.
- The JTAG signals (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) are correctly wired and signal-integrity is acceptable.

**`0xFFFFFFFF` interpretation (1.5 marks):** An IDCODE of all-ones indicates that the TDO line is stuck high, typically due to one of: (a) no power to the SoC, (b) no clock, (c) SoC held in reset, (d) TDO not connected / floating, or (e) damaged SoC. The engineer must revisit Phases 1–3 to isolate the fault.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *"Explain, with a suitable block diagram, the complete phased procedure of board bring-up for a multi-rail ARM-based SoC system. Clearly justify why each phase must be performed in the prescribed order."*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *"During the power-up verification phase of a 4-rail SoC system, the following steady-state currents are expected: 1.8 V @ 200 mA, 3.3 V @ 350 mA, 1.2 V @ 700 mA, 1.0 V @ 1100 mA. The input is 5.0 V, and the PMIC efficiency is 90 %. The measured input current is 4.5 A. (i) Calculate the expected input current. (ii) Comment on the measured value. (iii) List FOUR possible root causes and the corrective action for each."*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The phased procedure of board bring-up, with justification for ordering, is as follows:

**Phase 0 — Visual Inspection (1 mark):**
Performed before any power is applied. Checks for solder bridges, missing/wrong components, PCB revision correctness, and connector orientation. *Justification:* Applying power to a board with a hidden short can permanently destroy the SoC; visual inspection is a near-zero-cost method of preventing catastrophic damage.

**Phase 1 — Power Delivery Network (1.5 marks):**
Verify all supply rails (1.8 V, 3.3 V, 1.2 V, 1.0 V) are within **±3 %** of nominal, monotonic, sequenced correctly, and ripple is <1 % of rail voltage. *Justification:* No digital logic can operate without clean power; all subsequent debug work is meaningless if the rails are unstable.

**Phase 2 — Clock Tree Validation (1 mark):**
Probe each clock source with an oscilloscope; verify frequency, duty cycle (45–55 %), and stable start-up. *Justification:* The processor cannot fetch a single instruction without a stable reference clock.

**Phase 3 — Reset Architecture (0.5 mark):**
Verify RESETn de-asserts cleanly after rails stabilize and the minimum reset pulse width is met. *Justification:* A stuck-in-reset SoC mimics a dead board and wastes debugging effort.

**Phase 4 — SoC Sanity via JTAG IDCODE (1 mark):**
Read the 32-bit IDCODE via JTAG/SWD. A correct IDCODE proves power, clock, reset, and debug interface are all functional. *Justification:* This is the first unambiguous bidirectional handshake between hardware and software.

**Phase 5 — Memory Subsystem (1 mark):**
Validate internal SRAM, then run DDR calibration stress test for **≥ 24 hours**, verify Flash JEDEC ID. *Justification:* Firmware cannot run reliably without verified memory; intermittent DDR issues are the #1 source of mysterious OS crashes.

**Phase 6 — Basic Peripherals (1 mark):**
GPIO LED blink, UART console print, I²C device scan, SPI loopback. *Justification:* These prove the most-used peripheral buses work and provide a baseline for driver development.

**Ordering Justification Summary:** The phases are ordered by *dependency*. A higher phase cannot be meaningfully debugged if a lower phase has not been verified. Debugging a JTAG issue when power is bad wastes hours.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Calculate expected input current (2.5 marks):**

$$P_{\text{out}} = (1.8)(0.200) + (3.3)(0.350) + (1.2)(0.700) + (1.0)(1.100)$$

$$= 0.36 + 1.155 + 0.84 + 1.10 = 3.455 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{in,expected}} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta} = \frac{3.455}{0.90} = 3.839 \text{ W}$$

$$I_{\text{in,expected}} = \frac{P_{\text{in,expected}}}{V_{\text{in}}} = \frac{3.839}{5.0} = 0.768 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Comment on measured value (1.5 marks):**

Measured $I_{\text{in}} = 4.5 \text{ A}$ versus expected $I_{\text{in}} = 0.768 \text{ A}$:

$$\text{Excess ratio} = \frac{4.5}{0.768} \approx 5.86$$

The measured current is nearly **6× higher** than expected, indicating a **severe fault condition** such as a short circuit, latch-up, or component failure. The board must be powered off immediately to prevent further damage.

**(iii) Four possible root causes and corrective actions (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Solder bridge under a BGA/QFN package** — Action: visual + X-ray inspection, re-work with hot air, or board re-spin if defect is severe.
2. **Reverse-polarity or failed input capacitor** — Action: remove suspicious capacitor, test with LCR meter, replace.
3. **Damaged SoC (latch-up from EOS/ESD)** — Action: substitute a known-good SoC if available, or re-ball the package.
4. **Incorrect feedback resistor on a PMIC rail, causing over-voltage condition** — Action: cross-check BOM with schematic, replace feedback divider.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** *"Describe the role of the JTAG interface in board bring-up. With a clear flow diagram, explain how the TAP controller state machine is used to read the IDCODE register of an SoC. What is the minimum set of JTAG signals required?"*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *"Compare Cold-Boot and Warm-Boot sequences in an embedded system. Which sequence is more critical to verify during board bring-up, and why? List THREE failure symptoms that may arise if the cold-boot path is broken."*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Role of JTAG (2 marks):**
The JTAG (Joint Test Action Group) interface, defined by the **IEEE 1149.1** standard, is the universal low-level debug and boundary-scan interface used during board bring-up to:
- Verify SoC "aliveness" by reading the IDCODE register.
- Halt the processor core and inspect/modify registers and memory.
- Program non-volatile memory (NOR Flash, eMMC).
- Perform boundary-scan chain tests to detect solder opens/shorts on the PCB.

**Minimum JTAG signals (1.5 marks):**
- **TCK** — Test Clock (driven by the debugger).
- **TMS** — Test Mode Select (driven by the debugger; sets state transitions).
- **TDI** — Test Data In (data into the SoC).
- **TDO** — Test Data Out (data out of the SoC).
- **TRST** — Test Reset (optional; asynchronous TAP controller reset).
- **GND** — common ground reference.

**IDCODE read flow (3.5 marks):**
1. Power on the board. All rails stable.
2. Connect debugger, set TCK = 1 MHz.
3. Drive TMS = 1 for 5 TCK cycles → TAP enters **Test-Logic-Reset**.
4. TMS sequence: 0,1,1,1,1,0 → TAP enters **Shift-IR**.
5. Shift in the 4-bit IDCODE instruction (`0001`) via TDI, with TMS = 0 for 4 clocks.
6. TMS = 1 → **Exit1-IR**, then 1 → **Update-IR** (IR is now latched).
7. TMS = 1,0,0 → **Shift-DR**.
8. Clock 32 times with TMS = 0, reading TDO on each falling edge.
9. TMS = 1 → **Exit1-DR**, then 1 → **Update-DR** (IDCODE is now latched for reading).
10. Interpret the 32-bit value: MSB is always 1, next 11 bits are manufacturer ID, next 16 bits are part number, last 4 bits are version.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Cold-Boot vs Warm-Boot (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Cold-Boot (Power-On Reset) | Warm-Boot (Software Reset) |
|--------|----------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Trigger | $V_{\text{in}}$ applied from 0 V, or RESETn asserted | Watchdog timeout, software `reboot()` call, or reset register write |
| Power state | All rails ramp from 0 V | All rails remain stable; only the SoC's internal state is reset |
| PMIC state | PMIC re-initializes; sequencing re-executed | PMIC stays in regulation |
| PLL state | PLLs unlock, must re-lock | PLLs may or may not be reset, depending on SoC design |
| DRAM state | DDR must be re-trained (calibration) | DDR contents may be preserved (self-refresh) |
| Boot path | Full BootROM → SPL → U-Boot → OS | May skip BootROM; jump to reset vector of running firmware |
| Verification priority | **CRITICAL** during bring-up | Lower priority; validated in DVT/PVT |

**Which is more critical to verify? (1 mark):** The **cold-boot path** is more critical because it exercises the full power-on chain: PMIC sequencing, clock start-up, reset release, BootROM execution, and bootloader fetch from non-volatile storage. A broken cold-boot path means the product cannot be shipped to end users.

**Three failure symptoms of a broken cold-boot (2 marks):**
1. **No serial console output** — the BootROM never reaches the UART initialization stage, often due to bad clock or failed DDR.
2. **System resets in a loop** — the bootloader cannot find a valid image and re-enters BootROM, repeating the cycle.
3. **Current draw higher than expected** — the SoC may be partially booted, hanging in a state where peripherals are misconfigured and drawing excess current.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in Board Bring-up Questions:**
> 1. **Skipping Phase 0 (Visual Inspection):** Examiners award 1 mark for mentioning visual inspection *before* powering. Omitting it = -1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting power sequencing:** Just saying "verify 1.8 V and 3.3 V" is incomplete. You must state that the **order and inter-rail delay** must match the SoC datasheet. Missing this = -1 to -2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing JTAG and SWD:** SWD (Serial Wire Debug) uses only 2 signals (SWDIO, SWCLK) and is ARM-specific; JTAG uses 4/5 signals and is IEEE 1149.1 standard. Mixing them up = conceptual error.
> 4. **Stating "IDCODE confirms SoC works":** IDCODE only confirms the SoC is *electrically responding*, not that it is fully functional. Be precise in wording.
> 5. **Not mentioning watchdog disable during bring-up:** A common real-world pitfall that loses marks.
> 6. **Wrong units in power calculations:** Mixing mW and W, or A and mA, is a guaranteed 0.5-mark deduction.
> 7. **Omitting the 24-hour DDR stress test:** The "≥ 24 hours" criterion is a high-yield fact; missing it = -1 mark in Phase 5.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Board Bring-up** is the phased verification of a new PCBA from first power to first firmware execution.
- [ ] **Strict order:** Visual → Power → Clock → Reset → JTAG IDCODE → Memory → Peripherals → Bootloader.
- [ ] **Power rails** must be within **±3 % to ±5 %** of nominal; ripple < 1 % of $V_{\text{rail}}$.
- [ ] **Power sequencing** must respect the SoC datasheet's $T_{\text{max-delay}}$ and $T_{\text{max-total}}$ (typically **20–100 ms** total).
- [ ] **Current-limited bench supply** with OVP/OCP is mandatory for the first power-up.
- [ ] **JTAG IDCODE** is the canonical "proof of life" — read via 32-bit DR scan with IR = `0001`.
- [ ] **IDCODE structure:** 1 (start) + 11 (mfr) + 16 (part) + 4 (version) = 32 bits.
- [ ] **`0xFFFFFFFF` IDCODE** = TDO stuck high → recheck power, clock, reset, JTAG wiring.
- [ ] **JTAG signals (minimum):** TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO + GND. Optional: TRST.
- [ ] **Initial JTAG TCK = 1 MHz** per IEEE 1149.1 safe-scan practice.
- [ ] **DDR stress test duration:** ≥ **24 hours** to catch intermittent faults.
- [ ] **GPIO LED blink** is the canonical "Hello World" of bring-up.
- [ ] **UART console** typically runs at **115200 baud, 8-N-1** for first debug output.
- [ ] **Disable watchdog timer** during bring-up to prevent spurious resets.
- [ ] **Bring-up precedes BSP**, which precedes OS porting, which precedes application development.
- [ ] **Industry nomenclature:** EVT (Engineering Validation Test) = bring-up; DVT (Design Validation) follows.
- [ ] **Standard tools:** DMM, oscilloscope (≥ 100 MHz, 4-channel), logic analyzer, JTAG debugger, current-limited supply, serial console.
- [ ] **The seven phases of bring-up** are a high-yield exam fact — memorize the order, not just the names.
- [ ] **Power budget calculation:** $I_{\text{in,expected}} = \dfrac{\sum V_i I_i}{\eta V_{\text{in}}}$.
- [ ] **A measured input current > 1.5× expected = fault condition** → power off immediately and isolate.
- [ ] **TAP controller states (16 total):** Test-Logic-Reset, Run-Test/Idle, Select-DR-Scan, Capture-DR, Shift-DR, Exit1-DR, Pause-DR, Exit2-DR, Update-DR, Select-IR-Scan, Capture-IR, Shift-IR, Exit1-IR, Pause-IR, Exit2-IR, Update-IR.
- [ ] **Bring-up is an EVT-stage activity** in the NPI (New Product Introduction) flow.
- [ ] **A successful bring-up ends** when the bootloader banner appears on the serial console and the system is ready for BSP/OS porting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Embedded System Development Environment - The Integrated Development Environment (IDE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Embedded System Development Environment - The Integrated Development Environment (IDE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrated Development Environment (IDE):** An *Integrated Development Environment* is a unified software application suite that consolidates all the essential tools required for embedded firmware development — including a **source code editor**, **cross-compiler/cross-assembler**, **linker/locator**, **debugger**, **simulator/emulator interface**, **flash programmer**, and **project manager** — into a single coherent graphical framework to streamline the **edit → build → debug → deploy** lifecycle of embedded software targeting a specific microcontroller or microprocessor.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the IDE is treated as the **central workstation-side tool** that bridges the **Host Machine** (developer PC running Windows/Linux) with the **Target Machine** (the embedded hardware under development).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Carpenter's Workshop"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are building a precision wooden chair. You do not buy each tool from a different shop and assemble them yourself each morning. Instead, you walk into a **single workshop** where the workbench, saw, measuring scale, polishing machine, finishing room, and quality-check inspector are **all pre-arranged in one continuous workflow line**.

An IDE is exactly that workshop for an embedded developer:

| Workshop Item | IDE Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Workbench with clamps | Source Code Editor (with syntax highlighting) |
| Saw & Chisel | Compiler / Assembler |
| Wood Glue & Joiner | Linker / Locator |
| Blueprint cabinet | Project Manager |
| Trial assembly room | Simulator / Emulator |
| Quality inspection booth | Debugger (JTAG/SWD) |
| Paint & finishing spray | Flash Programmer / Loader |

### 1.3 Why an IDE is Non-Negotiable in Embedded Systems

Unlike desktop software development, embedded development requires:
- **Cross-compilation** (compile on x86 host, run on ARM/AVR/PIC target)
- **Memory-mapped debugging** via hardware probes
- **Register-level visibility** of peripherals (GPIO, ADC, UART, Timers)
- **Binary generation** in specific formats (ELF, HEX, BIN, SREC)

All of these specialized needs are integrated inside a single IDE shell — making it the **operational nerve centre** of any embedded design.

### 1.4 Popular IDEs in the Embedded Industry

- **Keil µVision** — Industry standard for ARM Cortex-M (STM32, NXP, LPC)
- **IAR Embedded Workbench** — Premium commercial IDE (Renesas, MSP430, ARM)
- **MPLAB X** — Microchip's official IDE for PIC & dsPIC microcontrollers
- **STM32CubeIDE** — STMicroelectronics' free Eclipse-based IDE
- **Arduino IDE** — Beginner-friendly, abstracts low-level complexity
- **Eclipse + GCC (ARM GNU Toolchain)** — Open-source workflow
- **Code Composer Studio (CCS)** — Texas Instruments (MSP430, Tiva-C, C2000)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The PECST746 syllabus treats the IDE as a *black-box orchestrator*. Students must understand **what each internal component does**, not necessarily how to operate a specific commercial IDE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of an Embedded IDE — Component-Wise Breakdown

An embedded IDE is not a single monolithic program. It is a **federation of cooperating tools**, orchestrated by a graphical shell. Let us dissect each sub-component:

#### 2.1.1 Source Code Editor
- Provides **syntax highlighting**, **auto-indentation**, **bracket matching**, **code completion (IntelliSense)**.
- Supports embedded-friendly languages: **C, C++, Embedded C, Assembly**.
- Plug-in architecture (especially in Eclipse-based IDEs) allows adding MISRA-C checkers, static analyzers.

#### 2.1.2 Cross-Compiler / Cross-Assembler
- **Cross-Compiler:** Converts C/C++ source into target processor machine code.
  * Example: `arm-none-eabi-gcc` compiles C on x86 host to ARM Cortex-M binary.
- **Cross-Assembler:** Converts assembly mnemonics (`.s` files) into object code (`.o` files).
- Output: **Relocatable Object Files** in ELF (Executable and Linkable Format).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The compiler runs on the **Host (Development PC)** but produces machine code for the **Target (Embedded Board)**. This is what makes it a *cross* compiler.

#### 2.1.3 Linker and Locator
- **Linker:** Merges multiple `.o` object files and libraries (`.a`/`.lib`) into a single executable image.
- **Locator (Scatter Loader):** Assigns absolute physical addresses to code and data sections per the **target memory map** (Flash, SRAM, EEPROM).
- Produces final binary in formats: **ELF, Intel HEX, Motorola SREC, raw BIN**.

#### 2.1.4 Debugger
- Two-tier architecture:
  * **GDB (GNU Debugger)** or vendor-specific debugger (e.g., µVision Debugger) running on host.
  * **Debug Probe (Hardware)** connecting host ↔ target via **JTAG** or **SWD (Serial Wire Debug)**.
- Supports **breakpoints**, **watchpoints**, **step-over**, **step-into**, **register inspection**, **memory dump**, **live variable tracking**.

#### 2.1.5 Simulator
- A **purely software-based** execution of the target binary on the host.
- No real hardware needed — useful for **early-stage logic validation**.
- Trade-off: Cannot model real-time peripheral behaviour accurately.

#### 2.1.6 Emulator
- Historically a hardware device that **physically replaces** the target microcontroller to provide real-time visibility.
- Today largely superseded by **in-circuit debuggers (ICD)** and **on-chip debug (OCD)** modules.

#### 2.1.7 Flash Programmer / Loader
- Pushes the final HEX/BIN file into the target's **non-volatile memory (Flash/ROM)**.
- Tools: **OpenOCD, ST-Link Utility, J-Flash, PICKit**.

#### 2.1.8 Project Manager / Build System
- Tracks dependencies between files.
- Maintains **Makefiles** (Eclipse/CDT) or **project descriptor files** (Keil `.uvprojx`).
- Orchestrates the **edit → compile → assemble → link** sequence via a *build button*.

### 2.2 IDE Workflow — The "Embedded Development Lifecycle"

Every embedded project in an IDE follows this canonical pipeline:

1. **Create/Select Project** → Choose target MCU, toolchain, board support package (BSP).
2. **Author Source Code** → Write `.c`, `.h`, `.s` files in the editor.
3. **Configure Build** → Set optimization flags (`-O0`, `-O2`, `-Os`), define macros, include paths.
4. **Compile & Assemble** → Cross-compiler produces `.o` files.
5. **Link & Locate** → Linker produces final ELF/HEX image.
6. **Static Analysis** → Optional MISRA-C / lint checks.
7. **Simulate or Debug** → Run on simulator or flash to target.
8. **Flash & Verify** → Program memory and verify checksum.
9. **Iterate** → Modify code based on debug observations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Component | Input | Output | Tool Example | Run Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Editor | Keystrokes | `.c / .h / .s` files | Built-in | Host |
| Preprocessor | `.c` | Expanded `.i` | `arm-none-eabi-cpp` | Host |
| Cross-Compiler | `.c` / `.i` | `.s` (assembly) | `arm-none-eabi-gcc` | Host |
| Cross-Assembler | `.s` | `.o` (relocatable object) | `arm-none-eabi-as` | Host |
| Linker | `.o` + `.a` | `.elf` | `arm-none-eabi-ld` | Host |
| Locator | `.elf` | `.hex` / `.bin` | `arm-none-eabi-objcopy` | Host |
| Flash Programmer | `.hex` / `.bin` | Programmed Flash | OpenOCD / ST-Link | Host → Target |
| Debugger | Live target data | Variable traces | GDB + Probe | Host ↔ Target |
| Simulator | `.elf` | Console trace | QEMU / Vendor sim | Host only |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Favourite:** A common question frames the build process as **"What is the role of the linker-locator?"** Always answer: *merging object files AND assigning physical addresses per the target memory map*.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (Bosch, Continental):** Use **Vector DaVinci + Tasking VX-toolset IDE** for AUTOSAR-compliant development.
- **IoT Devices (Espressif ESP32):** Use **ESP-IDF (Eclipse-based)** for Wi-Fi/BLE firmware.
- **Medical Implants:** Use **IAR Embedded Workbench** under **IEC 62304** certification.
- **Aerospace (NASA, ISRO):** Use **LDRA / Coverity** static analysis plugged into custom Eclipse IDEs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — IDE Build Process Derivation

### 3.1 Exhaustive Build Pipeline Walkthrough

Let us trace a single C source file `main.c` containing a GPIO toggle function through the entire IDE build chain.

**Step 1 — Source Authoring**

The developer writes `main.c` in the IDE editor. The IDE tags it under the **Sources** virtual folder and records it in the project descriptor.

**Step 2 — Preprocessing**

The IDE invokes the cross-compiler in preprocessor mode. All `#include`, `#define`, and `#ifdef` directives are expanded.

$$
\text{Input: } main.c \xrightarrow{\text{cpp}} main.i
$$

**Step 3 — Compilation to Assembly**

The compiler performs lexical analysis → parsing → semantic analysis → optimization → code generation. Output is target-specific assembly.

$$
\text{Input: } main.i \xrightarrow{\text{cc1}} main.s
$$

**Step 4 — Assembly to Object**

The cross-assembler converts assembly mnemonics into machine code, producing a **relocatable** object file (addresses are still symbolic).

$$
\text{Input: } main.s \xrightarrow{\text{as}} main.o
$$

**Step 5 — Linking**

If the project has `main.c`, `uart.c`, `timer.c`, the linker combines all `.o` files plus precompiled libraries (`libc.a`, `libm.a`) into one ELF.

$$
\text{Input: } main.o + uart.o + timer.o + libc.a \xrightarrow{\text{ld}} firmware.elf
$$

**Step 6 — Locating (Address Assignment)**

The locator reads the **scatter file / linker script** which defines the target memory map, e.g., for STM32F407:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FLASH (0x08000000, size 1 MB)} &: \text{.isr_vector, .text, .rodata} \\
\text{SRAM  (0x20000000, size 192 KB)} &: \text{.data, .bss, .heap, .stack}
\end{aligned}
$$

Output: a fully addressed executable image, then converted to HEX format.

$$
firmware.elf \xrightarrow{\text{objcopy -O ihex}} firmware.hex
$$

**Step 7 — Flash Programming**

The IDE calls `OpenOCD` or vendor programmer to push `firmware.hex` into the target's Flash at address `0x08000000`.

$$
\text{Host USB} \xrightarrow{\text{ST-Link/J-Link probe}} \text{Target Flash @ 0x08000000}
$$

**Step 8 — Verification**

The IDE reads back the programmed memory and compares CRC/checksum against the original HEX to confirm a clean burn.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — IDE Tools vs Functions

| Stage | IDE Tool | Engineering Purpose | Failure Symptom if Skipped |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Editor | Human-readable code authoring | None (manual editing possible) |
| 2 | Preprocessor | Macro expansion | Compilation errors on raw macros |
| 3 | Compiler | C → Assembly translation | No binary output |
| 4 | Assembler | Assembly → Machine code | Unresolved instruction errors |
| 5 | Linker | Symbol resolution across files | "Undefined reference" errors |
| 6 | Locator | Physical address binding | Program runs at wrong memory region |
| 7 | Programmer | Flash memory write | Code never executes on target |
| 8 | Debugger | Runtime introspection | No visibility into firmware bugs |

### 3.3 Cross-Development Equation (KTU High-Yield)

The IDE enables a **Host-Target Architecture** that can be expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Host Binary Output} &= f_{\text{cross-compile}}(\text{Source Code}, \text{Target ISA}) \\
\text{Target Execution} &= g_{\text{flash}}(\text{Binary}, \text{Probe}) \\
\text{Developer Insight} &= h_{\text{debug}}(\text{Target State}, \text{Probe})
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $f_{\text{cross-compile}}$ runs on **Host CPU (e.g., x86_64)** but emits **ARM/AVR/PIC machine code**.
- $g_{\text{flash}}$ uses **JTAG/SWD protocol** for memory write.
- $h_{\text{debug}}$ is a **bidirectional** channel: probe reads target registers → debugger GUI displays them.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Build Command Chain (GNU ARM Toolchain)

A typical IDE-invoked build sequence under the hood is:

```
arm-none-eabi-gcc -c -mcpu=cortex-m4 -mthumb -O2 main.c -o main.o
arm-none-eabi-gcc -c -mcpu=cortex-m4 -mthumb -O2 uart.c -o uart.o
arm-none-eabi-gcc -T stm32f407vg.ld main.o uart.o -o firmware.elf
arm-none-eabi-objcopy -O ihex firmware.elf firmware.hex
st-flash write firmware.hex 0x08000000
```

Each line above corresponds to one **IDE build stage** mapped 1:1 — the IDE simply hides these commands behind the **Build** and **Download** buttons.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of an Embedded IDE

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UI["Graphical Shell IDE Frontend"]
    ED["Source Code Editor"]
    PM["Project Manager Makefiles"]
    PP["Cross Preprocessor cpp"]
    CC["Cross Compiler gcc"]
    AS["Cross Assembler as"]
    LD["Linker and Locator ld"]
    OC["Objcopy HEX BIN"]
    SIM["Simulator Engine"]
    DBG["GDB Debugger"]
    PRB["JTAG SWD Probe"]
    TGT["Target Board MCU"]
    FL["Flash Programmer"]

    UI --> ED
    UI --> PM
    PM --> PP
    PP --> CC
    CC --> AS
    AS --> LD
    LD --> OC
    OC --> FL
    FL --> PRB
    PRB --> TGT
    UI --> SIM
    UI --> DBG
    DBG --> PRB
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Complete IDE Build and Debug Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1 Author Source Code"] --> B["Step 2 Configure Toolchain"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Compile and Assemble"]
    C --> D{"Step 4 Build Successful"}
    D -- No --> E["Step 5 Fix Errors"]
    E --> A
    D -- Yes --> F["Step 6 Link and Locate"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Generate HEX BIN"]
    G --> H["Step 8 Flash to Target"]
    H --> I["Step 9 Run and Observe"]
    I --> J{"Step 10 Behaviour Correct"}
    J -- No --> K["Step 11 Attach Debugger"]
    K --> L["Step 12 Step Trace Breakpoints"]
    L --> E
    J -- Yes --> M["Step 13 Validate and Ship"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Host vs Target Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOST["HOST MACHINE - Developer PC"]
        H1["IDE Application"]
        H2["Cross Toolchain gcc ld objcopy"]
        H3["Debugger GDB Server"]
        H4["Driver ST-Link J-Link"]
    end

    subgraph PROBE["DEBUG PROBE - Hardware Interface"]
        P1["JTAG or SWD Pins"]
    end

    subgraph TARGET["TARGET MACHINE - Embedded Board"]
        T1["MCU Cortex-M4"]
        T2["On-Chip Debug Module"]
        T3["Internal Flash and RAM"]
        T4["Peripherals GPIO ADC UART"]
    end

    H1 --> H2
    H1 --> H3
    H3 --> H4
    H4 --> P1
    P1 --> T2
    T2 --> T1
    T1 --> T3
    T1 --> T4
    T3 -.readback.-> P1
```

### 4.4 IDE Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Tool Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1["Need Real Hardware Behaviour"]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2["Have Target Board"]
    Q1 -- No --> S1["Use Simulator"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> P1["Use Hardware Debugger via JTAG SWD"]
    Q2 -- No --> S1
    P1 --> B1["Set Breakpoints and Watch Variables"]
    S1 --> B2["Inspect Register Dumps and Trace"]
    B1 --> V1["Validate Firmware Behaviour"]
    B2 --> V1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Integrated Development Environment" in the context of embedded systems. List any four major components of an IDE.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** is a unified software application that consolidates all tools required for embedded firmware development — editing, compiling, assembling, linking, debugging, and flashing — into a single graphical framework running on the host machine, while targeting an embedded processor on the target hardware.

Four major components (1/2 mark each):

1. **Source Code Editor** — with syntax highlighting and auto-completion.
2. **Cross-Compiler/Cross-Assembler** — translates source to target machine code.
3. **Linker and Locator** — merges object files and assigns physical addresses.
4. **Debugger** — provides runtime visibility via JTAG/SWD probes.

*(Alt acceptable components: Simulator, Emulator, Flash Programmer, Project Manager)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a cross-compiler and a native compiler with an embedded systems example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Native Compiler | Cross-Compiler |
|---|---|---|
| Host = Target? | Yes | No |
| Runs on | Same machine as target | Host PC (x86) |
| Produces code for | Host CPU | Target MCU (ARM/AVR/PIC) |
| Example | `gcc` on Linux PC compiling a Linux program | `arm-none-eabi-gcc` on PC producing ARM Cortex-M binary |

(2 marks for distinction table, 1 mark for embedded example)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of an embedded IDE. List and briefly describe any **six** major components.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["IDE Shell"] --> B["Editor"]
    A --> C["Compiler"]
    A --> D["Assembler"]
    A --> E["Linker Locator"]
    A --> F["Debugger"]
    A --> G["Simulator"]
    A --> H["Flash Programmer"]
```

**(ii) Six Major Components (4 Marks — 2/3 mark each, write any 6):**

1. **Editor:** Source code authoring with syntax highlighting.
2. **Cross-Compiler:** Translates C/C++ into target assembly.
3. **Cross-Assembler:** Converts assembly to relocatable object code.
4. **Linker-Locator:** Merges object files and assigns physical memory addresses using a scatter file.
5. **Debugger:** Real-time runtime introspection via JTAG/SWD probes.
6. **Simulator:** Software-based execution model of the target.
7. **Flash Programmer:** Writes the final HEX/BIN image into target Flash.
8. **Project Manager:** Tracks file dependencies and orchestrates the build.

---

**(b)** *Explain the **complete IDE build pipeline** starting from a C source file to a working binary on an ARM Cortex-M target. Mention the role of the **linker-locator** and the **scatter file** in detail.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step-by-step pipeline (5 marks):**

1. **Preprocessing** — `#include`, `#define` expansion via `cpp`.
2. **Compilation** — C source → target assembly via `gcc`.
3. **Assembly** — Assembly mnemonics → relocatable `.o` object files.
4. **Linking** — Multiple `.o` files + libraries combined into single `.elf`.
5. **Locating** — Physical addresses assigned per scatter file.
6. **Format conversion** — ELF → Intel HEX via `objcopy`.
7. **Flash programming** — HEX written to target via JTAG/SWD.

**Linker-Locator and Scatter File (2 marks):**

The **linker** resolves cross-file symbols and merges sections. The **locator** uses the **scatter file** (e.g., `stm32f407.ld`) to map logical sections (`.text`, `.data`, `.bss`) onto physical memory regions. For example:

```
MEMORY {
  FLASH (rx) : ORIGIN = 0x08000000, LENGTH = 1024K
  RAM   (rwx): ORIGIN = 0x20000000, LENGTH = 192K
}
SECTIONS {
  .text : { *(.isr_vector) *(.text*) *(.rodata*) } > FLASH
  .data : { *(.data*) } > RAM AT > FLASH
  .bss  : { *(.bss*) *(COMMON) } > RAM
}
```

This ensures the interrupt vector table lands at `0x08000000` and stack pointer initializes correctly at MCU reset.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** *Explain the **Host–Target Development Model** used in embedded systems. Describe how the IDE coordinates between them using JTAG/SWD.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Host-Target Model (4 marks):**

- **Host:** Developer workstation (PC/laptop) running IDE, cross-toolchain, debugger server.
- **Target:** Embedded board containing MCU, Flash, RAM, peripherals.
- The IDE compiles on the host but executes/debugs on the target.

**Coordination via JTAG/SWD (3 marks):**

- **JTAG** (Joint Test Action Group) — 4-wire standard (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) plus optional TRST.
- **SWD** (Serial Wire Debug) — 2-wire ARM alternative (SWDIO, SWCLK), saves GPIO pins.
- The probe (e.g., **ST-Link V2**, **J-Link**) bridges USB on host to JTAG/SWD pins on target.
- IDE debugger (e.g., GDB) sends commands through the probe to:
  * Halt the CPU at breakpoints.
  * Read/write CPU registers.
  * Read/write memory and Flash.
  * Single-step instructions.

This bidirectional channel enables **live firmware debugging** without removing the MCU from the board.

---

**(b)** *Compare **Simulator-based debugging** and **Emulator/On-Chip Debug-based debugging** in embedded IDEs. State two advantages and one limitation of each.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Simulator | Emulator / OCD (JTAG/SWD) |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware required | None | Target board + probe |
| Real-time accuracy | Low (no real I/O) | High |
| Peripheral behaviour | Modelled only | Actual hardware |
| Cost | Free | Probe cost |
| Use case | Early-stage logic tests | Production-level validation |

**Simulator advantages (2 marks):**
1. No hardware dependency — works in early design phase.
2. Fast and safe for algorithm logic validation.

**Simulator limitation (1 mark):**
1. Cannot model real-time peripheral timing, electrical noise, or sensor inputs accurately.

**Emulator/OCD advantages (2 marks):**
1. Full real-time visibility into actual hardware behaviour.
2. Supports on-the-fly register/memory modification.

**Emulator limitation (1 mark):**
1. Requires target hardware availability and a working probe connection; JTAG pins must not be disabled in firmware.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing linker with locator** — Linker merges symbols; Locator assigns physical addresses. Writing only "combines files" loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting "cross" prefix** — Always say *cross-compiler*, not just compiler, when discussing embedded toolchains.
> 3. **Missing JTAG pin count** — Mentioning JTAG without TCK/TMS/TDI/TDO loses credit on protocol questions.
> 4. **Confusing simulator vs emulator** — Simulator is *software-only*; emulator traditionally *replaces* the MCU. Modern usage favours OCD.
> 5. **Not mentioning the scatter file** — A linker-locator question without scatter file mention is incomplete for full marks.
> 6. **Skipping Host–Target distinction** — Embedded development is fundamentally *cross-development*. Always emphasize this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Board Exam:**

- **IDE = Editor + Cross-Compiler + Cross-Assembler + Linker + Locator + Debugger + Flash Programmer + Project Manager**.
- **Cross-compilation:** Compilation on host, execution on target — defines embedded toolchain.
- **Linker-Locator dual role:** Symbol resolution (linker) + physical address binding (locator via scatter file).
- **Output formats:** ELF (debug) → HEX/BIN (production flash).
- **JTAG = 4-wire** (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO); **SWD = 2-wire** (SWDIO, SWCLK) — ARM-specific.
- **Simulator** is software-only; **Emulator/OCD** requires target hardware + probe.
- **Host = Development PC**, **Target = Embedded board** — never confuse the two.
- **Build pipeline order:** Preprocess → Compile → Assemble → Link → Locate → Objcopy → Flash.
- **Project Manager** handles dependencies and Makefile generation automatically.
- **Popular IDEs to name-drop:** Keil µVision, IAR, MPLAB X, STM32CubeIDE, Arduino IDE, Eclipse + GCC.
- **Static analysis tools** (MISRA-C, Lint) plug into modern IDEs for safety-critical compliance.
- **Debug probe examples:** ST-Link, J-Link, PICKit, XDS110 — know at least two by name.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Files Generated on Cross-Compilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Files Generated on Cross-Compilation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Types of Files Generated on Cross-Compilation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Cross-Compilation** is the process of generating executable code for a *target platform* (e.g., ARM Cortex-M microcontroller) on a *host platform* (e.g., x86 PC running Linux/Windows) using a toolchain whose architecture differs from the target. The files produced at every intermediate stage and at the final output stage are categorized as **Cross-Compilation Generated Files**.

According to the KTU 2024 PECST746 syllabus (Module 4), the cross-compilation workflow (typically driven by `arm-none-eabi-gcc` for ARM targets) traverses four canonical stages — **Preprocessing → Compilation → Assembly → Linking** — and each stage leaves behind a specific class of artifacts. The *types* of these files are dictated by the **GNU Binutils** and **GCC** toolchain internals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to **identify, classify, and describe** the purpose of every file extension (`.c`, `.i`, `.s`, `.o`, `.elf`, `.bin`, `.hex`, `.map`, `.lst`, `.ld`) that appears in an embedded project directory. Examiner focus is on the *mapping* between each stage and the *file produced*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bakery Production Line"

Imagine a bakery where:
- **Raw ingredients** (`.c` source files) are mixed and sifted by a **preprocessor** to yield refined batter (`.i`).
- The batter is poured into a shaping machine (**compiler**) that produces dough shapes (`.s` assembly).
- These shapes are baked individually into semi-finished biscuits (`.o` object files).
- Finally, a packaging line (**linker**) bundles biscuits into a finished box (`.elf`/`.bin`/`.hex`) with a **shipping label** (`.map`).

Every artifact on this line is a **cross-compilation generated file** — and just like in a bakery, *you cannot skip a stage without losing correctness*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Host vs Target):** The *tool* (compiler binary) runs on the **host** (e.g., `gcc` compiled for x86), but the *output machine code* targets the **target CPU** (e.g., ARM Thumb-2). The files generated are stored on the host filesystem and are *not directly executable* on the host.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Section Alignment** | **0x1000 = 4096 bytes (4 KB)** | Page-aligned ELF sections |
| **ARM Vector Table Size (Cortex-M)** | **0x130 = 304 bytes** | `.vector_table` size in `.elf` |
| **GCC Optimization Levels** | `-O0`, `-O1`, `-O2`, `-O3`, `-Os`, `-Ofast` | Affects generated `.s` and `.o` size |
| **Standard Cross Compiler Prefix** | `arm-none-eabi-` | GNU ARM Embedded Toolchain |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Stages and Their Outputs

The cross-compilation pipeline is formally expressed as:

$$
\text{Source } (.c) \xrightarrow{\text{Preprocessor}} \text{Preprocessed } (.i) \xrightarrow{\text{Compiler}} \text{Assembly } (.s) \xrightarrow{\text{Assembler}} \text{Object } (.o) \xrightarrow{\text{Linker}} \text{Executable } (.elf / .bin / .hex)
$$

### Stage 1: Preprocessing (`.c` → `.i`)

**Why it exists:** The C preprocessor (CPP) implements macro expansion, file inclusion, and conditional compilation. It must run *before* the compiler proper so the compiler only sees *pure* C tokens.

**How it works:**
- Trigraph replacement (legacy)
- Line splicing (backslash-newline continuation)
- Tokenization
- Macro substitution (`#define` → literal replacement)
- File inclusion (`#include` → textual paste)
- Conditional skipping (`#if`, `#ifdef`, `#else`, `#elif`, `#endif`)

**Output:** A single `.i` file containing the *fully expanded* C source, with all includes inlined and all macros resolved.

```bash
arm-none-eabi-gcc -E main.c -o main.i
```

### Stage 2: Compilation (`.i` → `.s`)

**Why it exists:** The compiler performs **lexical analysis → syntax analysis → semantic analysis → intermediate representation (IR) → target-specific code generation**.

**How it works:**
- Parses `.i` into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
- Performs type checking, constant folding, and dead-code elimination.
- Lowers to GCC's GIMPLE and then to **Register Transfer Language (RTL)**.
- Selects target instructions and emits **assembly language** text.

**Output:** A `.s` file containing *human-readable* assembly mnemonics (`MOV`, `ADD`, `BL`, `LDR`, `STR`) with directives (`.text`, `.data`, `.bss`, `.global`, `.align`).

```bash
arm-none-eabi-gcc -S main.i -o main.s
```

### Stage 3: Assembly (`.s` → `.o`)

**Why it exists:** Assembly mnemonics must be converted into **machine code opcodes** and packaged with relocation information.

**How it works:**
- The **assembler** (`arm-none-eabi-as`) reads the `.s` file.
- Each instruction is translated to its binary opcode.
- A **symbol table** is built (labels → addresses).
- **Relocation entries** are emitted (addresses that need fixing by the linker).
- Section headers are created.

**Output:** A `.o` (object) file in **ELF (Executable and Linkable Format)** — *not yet executable*, contains unresolved symbols.

```bash
arm-none-eabi-as -mcpu=cortex-m4 main.s -o main.o
```

### Stage 4: Linking (`.o` + libraries → `.elf` / `.bin` / `.hex`)

**Why it exists:** Multiple object files must be combined, unresolved symbols resolved against libraries, and the final memory map laid out.

**How it works:**
- The **linker** (`arm-none-eabi-ld`) reads all `.o` files and libraries (`.a`).
- Symbol resolution: matches undefined symbols against definitions in object files and libraries.
- **Section merging** (all `.text` sections joined, all `.data`, all `.bss`).
- **Address assignment** based on the **linker script** (`.ld` file) — defines memory regions (`FLASH (rx) : ORIGIN = 0x08000000, LENGTH = 256K` and `RAM (rwx) : ORIGIN = 0x20000000, LENGTH = 64K`).
- **Relocation fixup** is performed.
- A `.map` file is produced detailing the final memory layout.

**Output:** A final `.elf` file, which is then converted via `objcopy` to raw `.bin` (binary) and/or `.hex` (Intel HEX) for flashing.

```bash
arm-none-eabi-ld -T stm32f4.ld main.o startup.o -o firmware.elf
arm-none-eabi-objcopy -O binary firmware.elf firmware.bin
arm-none-eabi-objcopy -O ihex   firmware.elf firmware.hex
```

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield File Classification Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permanent Reference — Memorize for Module 4.** The following table categorizes **every file type** that can appear in an embedded cross-compilation project. The *right column* (Generation Stage) is the most frequently asked mapping in KTU exams.

| File Extension | Full Name | Category | Generation Stage | Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **`.c`** | C Source | Input | Before compile | Editor | Raw human-written source code |
| **`.h`** | C Header | Input | Before compile | Editor | Function prototypes, macros, typedefs |
| **`.i`** | Preprocessed C | Intermediate | After preprocessor | `gcc -E` | Fully expanded C source |
| **`.s`** | Assembly Source | Intermediate | After compiler | `gcc -S` | Target-specific assembly mnemonics |
| **`.S`** | Assembly w/ CPP | Intermediate | After compiler | `gcc -S` | Assembly that needs preprocessing |
| **`.o`** | Object File | Intermediate | After assembler | `as` | Machine code + unresolved symbols (ELF) |
| **`.a`** | Static Library | Library | Archive of `.o` | `ar` | Collection of object files, linked at link-time |
| **`.so`** | Shared Object | Library | Position-independent | `gcc -shared` | Dynamically linked (rare in bare-metal MCUs) |
| **`.ko`** | Kernel Object | Library | Linux kernel build | `kbuild` | Loadable Linux kernel module |
| **`.ld`** | Linker Script | Configuration | Before link | Editor | Defines memory map and section placement |
| **`.elf`** | Executable and Linkable Format | Output | After link | `ld` | Final executable with symbols and debug info |
| **`.bin`** | Binary | Output | Post-`elf` | `objcopy` | Raw machine code, no metadata |
| **`.hex`** | Intel HEX | Output | Post-`elf` | `objcopy -O ihex` | ASCII hex records, used by programmers |
| **`.map`** | Memory Map | Diagnostic | After link | `ld` | Lists symbols, sections, addresses, sizes |
| **`.lst`** | Listing File | Diagnostic | After assembly | `as -a` | Assembly listing with addresses and opcodes |
| **`.sym`** | Symbol Dump | Diagnostic | Post-`elf` | `nm` / `objdump` | Symbol table extraction |
| **`.dump`** / **`.dis`** | Disassembly | Diagnostic | Post-`elf` | `objdump -d` | Human-readable disassembly of `.elf` |
| **`Makefile`** | Build Script | Configuration | N/A | Editor | Automates the entire build pipeline |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **automotive ECUs**, the `.elf` file is used for **debugging** (semihosting, JTAG/SWD), while the `.hex` file is the **production artifact** flashed to the ECU's flash memory during manufacturing. The `.map` file is a **legal deliverable** for ISO 26262 certification — auditors verify that the compiled code fits within the declared memory budget. A **size mismatch** between the `.elf` and the target's flash size is detected by reading the `.map` file's `*(.text*)` section size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both `.bin` AND `.hex` exist:** `.bin` is a *raw memory image* (compact, fast to flash) but has no address information. `.hex` includes addresses and checksums in ASCII — slower to flash, but **self-describing** and **error-detectable**. Bootloaders prefer `.hex`; production line programmers often prefer `.bin`.

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions & Size Formulas

The size of the final image, as reported in the `.map` file, follows:

$$
\text{Image Size} = \sum_{s \in \text{sections}} \text{Size}(s)
$$

where the critical sections for an embedded image are:

$$
\text{FLASH Occupied} = \text{Size}(.text) + \text{Size}(.rodata) + \text{Size}(.data_{\text{init}}) + \text{Size}(.isr\_vector)
$$

$$
\text{RAM Occupied} = \text{Size}(.data) + \text{Size}(.bss) + \text{Size}(.heap) + \text{Size}(.stack)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **`.data` vs `.bss` (Frequently Confused in Exams):**
> - `.data` is **initialized** global/static variables → occupies both **FLASH** (initial values) and **RAM** (runtime copy).
> - `.bss` is **uninitialized** or **zero-initialized** global/static variables → occupies only **RAM** (no flash storage; the C runtime zero-fills it at startup).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From `.c` to `.elf` — A Worked Example

**Given** the following minimal embedded C source `main.c`:

```c
#include "led.h"
static int counter = 0;          // .data (initialized)
static int uninit_var;           // .bss  (uninitialized)
const int lookup[4] = {1,2,3,4}; // .rodata

int main(void) {
    led_init();
    counter++;
    return 0;
}
```

**Goal:** Trace *every file* generated and the *section* each variable/function lands in within the final `.elf`.

### Step 1: Preprocessing → `main.i`

The preprocessor performs the textual inclusion of `led.h` and macro expansion.

```bash
arm-none-eabi-gcc -E -mcpu=cortex-m4 main.c -o main.i
```

The resulting `main.i` contains the *fully expanded* source — every `#include "led.h"` is replaced by the literal contents of `led.h`, and every `#define` macro is substituted.

**Generated file:** `main.i` (typically **5×–10× larger** than the original `.c`).

**Section assignment at this stage:** None yet — the preprocessor is *language-agnostic* and does not know about CPU sections.

### Step 2: Compilation → `main.s`

```bash
arm-none-eabi-gcc -S -mcpu=cortex-m4 -mthumb main.i -o main.s
```

The compiler emits target-specific ARM Thumb-2 assembly. A representative excerpt of `main.s` (simplified for clarity):

```asm
    .syntax unified
    .cpu cortex-m4
    .thumb
    .section .text.main,"ax",%progbits
    .global main
    .type   main, %function
main:
    push    {r7, lr}
    sub     sp, #8
    bl      led_init
    ldr     r3, .L3
    ldr     r3, [r3]
    adds    r3, r3, #1
    ldr     r2, .L3
    str     r3, [r2]
    movs    r0, #0
    ...
    .section .data
    .global counter
counter:
    .word   0
    .section .bss
    .global uninit_var
    .align  2
uninit_var:
    .skip   4
    .section .rodata
    .align  2
lookup:
    .word   1
    .word   2
    .word   3
    .word   4
.L3:
    .word   counter
```

**Generated file:** `main.s` — this is the *bridge* between the high-level language and the machine code.

**Section directive analysis:**

$$
\text{Section Assignment} = \begin{cases}
.section\ .text.main & \text{for the function \texttt{main} (executable code)} \\
.section\ .data & \text{for the initialized global \texttt{counter}} \\
.section\ .bss & \text{for the uninitialized global \texttt{uninit\_var}} \\
.section\ .rodata & \text{for the constant array \texttt{lookup}}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: Assembly → `main.o`

```bash
arm-none-eabi-as -mcpu=cortex-m4 -mthumb main.s -o main.o
```

The assembler converts each assembly instruction to a **4-byte (or 2-byte) Thumb opcode** and builds an **ELF relocatable object file**.

The `.o` file structure is:

$$
\text{ELF Object File} = \begin{cases}
\text{ELF Header} & \text{(identifies file as ELF, target machine, entry point)} \\
\text{Section Headers} & \text{(describes \texttt{.text}, \texttt{.data}, \texttt{.bss}, \texttt{.symtab}, \texttt{.strtab}, \texttt{.rel.text})} \\
\text{Sections} & \text{(actual content of each section)} \\
\text{Symbol Table} & \text{(labels like \texttt{main}, \texttt{counter}, \texttt{led\_init})} \\
\text{Relocation Entries} & \text{(places where addresses need patching)}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** At this stage, `main.o` contains *unresolved* symbols such as `led_init` (defined in `led.o`) and `counter` (whose address is not yet known). The `.rel.text` relocation table tells the linker: *"When you place this section, fix up the LDR instruction at offset X with the address of `counter`."*

### Step 4: Linking → `firmware.elf`

```bash
arm-none-eabi-ld -T stm32f407vg.ld main.o led.o startup_stm32f407.s.o -o firmware.elf -Map=firmware.map
```

The linker script `stm32f407vg.ld` defines the memory map:

```ld
MEMORY
{
    FLASH (rx)  : ORIGIN = 0x08000000, LENGTH = 1024K
    RAM   (rwx) : ORIGIN = 0x20000000, LENGTH = 128K
}
SECTIONS
{
    .isr_vector : { KEEP(*(.isr_vector)) } > FLASH
    .text       : { *(.text*) *(.rodata*) } > FLASH
    .data       : AT(LOADADDR(.text) + SIZEOF(.text))
                   { *(.data*) } > RAM
    .bss        : { *(.bss*) *(COMMON) } > RAM
}
```

**Linker actions:**

1. **Section Collection:** Gathers `.text.main` from `main.o`, `.text.led_init` from `led.o`, and `.text.startup` from the startup file.
2. **Memory Layout:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address}_{\texttt{.isr\_vector}} &= \text{0x08000000} \\
\text{Address}_{\texttt{.text}} &= \text{0x08000000} + \text{Size}(\texttt{.isr\_vector}) \\
\text{Address}_{\texttt{.data (RAM)}} &= \text{0x20000000} \\
\text{Address}_{\texttt{.data (LMA)}} &= \text{Address}_{\texttt{.text}} + \text{Size}(\texttt{.text}) \\
\text{Address}_{\texttt{.bss}} &= \text{0x20000000} + \text{Size}(\texttt{.data})
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Symbol Resolution:** `led_init` is matched with its definition in `led.o`. The relocation entry for `counter`'s LDR instruction is patched with `0x20000000` (the RAM address assigned to `counter`).
4. **ELF Generation:** Produces `firmware.elf` with a fully resolved symbol table, debug info (if `-g` was used), and section headers.

**Generated files:** `firmware.elf` (executable), `firmware.map` (memory report).

### Step 5: Post-Processing → `.bin` and `.hex`

```bash
arm-none-eabi-objcopy -O binary firmware.elf firmware.bin
arm-none-eabi-objcopy -O ihex   firmware.elf firmware.hex
arm-none-eabi-objdump -d        firmware.elf > firmware.dis
arm-none-eabi-nm                 firmware.elf > firmware.sym
```

**Generated files and their properties:**

| File | Size Property | Content Property | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| `firmware.elf` | Largest | Full debug + symbols | Debugging (GDB, OpenOCD) |
| `firmware.bin` | Smallest | Raw bytes | Bootloader, direct flash |
| `firmware.hex` | Medium | ASCII Intel HEX | Production programmers (ST-Link) |
| `firmware.map` | Text | Memory map report | Size auditing, certification |
| `firmware.dis` | Text | Disassembly | Reverse engineering, code review |
| `firmware.sym` | Text | Symbol addresses | Stack analysis, debugging |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: File-to-Tool Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for KTU Exam — "Which tool generates which file?"** This is a **high-frequency** question (asked as a 3-mark or 7-mark question).

| Tool | Binary Name | Input File | Output File | Stage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Preprocessor** | `arm-none-eabi-cpp` (or `gcc -E`) | `.c` / `.h` | `.i` | Preprocessing |
| **Compiler** | `arm-none-eabi-gcc -S` | `.i` (or `.c`) | `.s` | Compilation |
| **Assembler** | `arm-none-eabi-as` | `.s` | `.o` | Assembly |
| **Linker** | `arm-none-eabi-ld` | `.o` + `.a` + `.ld` | `.elf` | Linking |
| **Archiver** | `arm-none-eabi-ar` | `.o` files | `.a` | Library creation |
| **Object Copy** | `arm-none-eabi-objcopy` | `.elf` | `.bin` / `.hex` | Format conversion |
| **Object Dump** | `arm-none-eabi-objdump` | `.elf` | `.dis` / `.dump` | Disassembly / info |
| **Symbol List** | `arm-none-eabi-nm` | `.elf` / `.o` | `.sym` | Symbol extraction |
| **Size Analyzer** | `arm-none-eabi-size` | `.elf` | stdout text | Section size report |
| **Strip** | `arm-none-eabi-strip` | `.elf` | stripped `.elf` | Remove debug info |

## 3.3 Size Calculation Derivation (from the `.map` file)

Suppose the `.map` file reports the following:

```
.text        0x08000000    12340 bytes
.rodata      0x08003034      512 bytes
.data (LMA)  0x08003234      256 bytes
.data (VMA)  0x20000000      256 bytes
.bss         0x20000100      128 bytes
```

Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total FLASH used} &= \text{Size}(\texttt{.text}) + \text{Size}(\texttt{.rodata}) + \text{Size}(\texttt{.data}_{\text{LMA}}) \\
&= 12340 + 512 + 256 \\
&= 13108 \text{ bytes} \approx 12.80 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total RAM used} &= \text{Size}(\texttt{.data}_{\text{VMA}}) + \text{Size}(\texttt{.bss}) \\
&= 256 + 128 \\
&= 384 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **VMA vs LMA (Virtual vs Load Memory Address):** `.data` has *two* addresses — the **LMA** (where it is stored in flash, so the initial values are preserved) and the **VMA** (where it resides in RAM at runtime). The startup code copies `.data` from LMA to VMA during `__main`. This is why `.data` contributes to *both* flash and RAM budgets.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: ELF File Internal Structure

The final `.elf` file is not a flat blob — it is a **structured container** with headers, sections, and metadata. This diagram shows how the linker organizes the output.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ELF["firmware.elf - Executable and Linkable Format"]
        H1["ELF Header 52 bytes on 32 bit ARM"]:::hdr
        H2["Program Headers PHDR - tells loader how to flash this"]:::hdr
        S1[".isr_vector - Vector table at 0x08000000"]:::sec
        S2[".text - Executable code 12340 bytes"]:::sec
        S3[".rodata - Read only data 512 bytes"]:::sec
        S4[".data init - Initial values 256 bytes"]:::sec
        S5[".bss - Zero init uninitialized 128 bytes"]:::sec
        H3["Section Headers SHDR - for objdump and nm"]:::hdr
        S6[".symtab - Symbol table debug only"]:::diag2
        S7[".strtab - String table"]:::diag2
        S8[".debug_info - DWARF debug data"]:::diag2
    end

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Memory Map at Runtime vs Flash

This diagram illustrates **why `.data` and `.bss` behave differently** — a KTU-favorite diagram question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FLASH["FLASH Memory 0x08000000 - 1 MB"]
        F1["0x08000000: .isr_vector"]:::flash
        F2["0x08000130: .text code"]:::flash
        F3["0x08003300: .rodata constants"]:::flash
        F4["0x08003500: .data initial values"]:::flash
    end

    subgraph RAM["RAM Memory 0x20000000 - 128 KB"]
        R1["0x20000000: .data runtime copy"]:::ram
        R2["0x20000100: .bss zero filled at startup"]:::ram
        R3["0x20000200: .heap malloc region grows up"]:::ram
        R4["0x2001E000: .stack grows down to .heap"]:::ram
    end

    F4 -. "Copy at startup __main" .-> R1
    R3 <--> R4

    classDef flash fill:#FFECB3,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If asked to draw a memory map, always show the **two columns** (FLASH on the left, RAM on the right) and explicitly mark the **LMA → VMA copy** for `.data` with a dashed arrow. This shows the examiner you understand the *runtime initialization* phase.
