# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define In-Circuit Emulator (ICE) and list its key components.

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1a]**

**Model Answer:**

An **In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)** is a hardware debugging tool that physically replaces the target microcontroller in a circuit, enabling real-time execution, observation, and control of the firmware during development.

**Key components of an ICE:**

1. **Bond-Out Probe** — a specially fabricated version of the target MCU with internal buses exposed.
2. **Emulation Memory** — substitute ROM/RAM accessible in the target's address space.
3. **Trace Buffer** — captures every bus cycle for post-execution analysis.
4. **Control Logic / State Registers** — single-step, breakpoint, run/halt control.
5. **Host Interface** — link to the developer's PC (USB, Ethernet, legacy parallel port).

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Software Breakpoints and Hardware Breakpoints.

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q2b (Module 4)]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Software Breakpoint | Hardware Breakpoint |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Replaces instruction with **BKPT opcode** in memory | Compares address bus against an internal register |
| **Memory type** | Works only in **RAM** (writable) | Works in **Flash/ROM** too |
| **Number available** | Unlimited (subject to RAM) | Limited (typically **2–8** on Cortex-M) |
| **Speed of insertion** | Slower (needs memory write) | Instant (just load register) |
| **Intrusiveness** | Slightly higher (code modified) | Truly non-intrusive (no code change) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often state "software breakpoints are slower" without explaining *why* — you must mention that software breakpoints require the debug adapter to **read, save, and rewrite** the original instruction, which incurs a flash/RAM write cycle.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, Q7a]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of the **JTAG (Joint Test Action Group)** interface for embedded system debugging. With a neat diagram, describe the **TAP controller state machine** and the four mandatory signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the advantages of **Serial Wire Debug (SWD)** over JTAG. How does **Background Debug Mode (BDM)** differ from JTAG? Mention the role of **ETM (Embedded Trace Macrocell)** in real-time debugging. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Architecture of JTAG:**

JTAG was standardized as **IEEE 1149.1** in 1990, originally for **boundary-scan testing** of assembled PCBs. Modern microcontrollers integrate the JTAG logic on-chip, enabling three core functions:

1. **Boundary-Scan Test** — verifies PCB interconnects without physical probes.
2. **In-System Programming (ISP)** — flashes firmware via JTAG.
3. **On-Chip Debug (OCD)** — the focus of KTU Module 4.

**The Four Mandatory JTAG Signals:**

| Signal | Direction | Function |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| **TCK (Test Clock)** | Host → Target | Clocks all JTAG state transitions |
| **TMS (Test Mode Select)** | Host → Target | Selects the next TAP state |
| **TDI (Test Data In)** | Host → Target | Serial data input (instructions/data) |
| **TDO (Test Data Out)** | Target → Host | Serial data output |

**Optional fifth signal:** **TRST (Test Reset)** — asynchronous TAP reset.

**TAP Controller — 16-State Machine:**

The TAP controller is a synchronous finite state machine clocked by TCK, with state transitions determined by the TMS value sampled at the rising edge of TCK.

**Major stable states:**

- **Test-Logic-Reset** — default entry; normal CPU operation.
- **Run-Test/Idle** — resting state; CPU runs normally.
- **Capture-DR / Capture-IR** — parallel-load data into the data/instruction register.
- **Shift-DR / Shift-IR** — serial shift of data via TDI/TDO.
- **Update-DR / Update-IR** — commit shifted data to the active register.
- **Pause-DR / Pause-IR** — temporarily halt shifting.

**Working — Writing to the Instruction Register (e.g., to select "HALT CPU"):**

1. Host drives TMS = 1,1,0,0,0,0,0 → moves TAP from Test-Logic-Reset → Run-Test/Idle → Select-DR-Scan → Select-IR-Scan → Capture-IR → Shift-IR.
2. Host shifts in the **HALT instruction opcode** (e.g., 8-bit `0xA3`) via TDI, clocked by TCK.
3. Host drives TMS = 1,1 → moves TAP to Exit1-IR → Update-IR.
4. On Update-IR, the TAP latches `0xA3` into the active instruction register; the CPU halts.
5. Subsequent Data Register operations allow the host to read/write CPU registers and memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Reference:** See the state diagram in SECTION 4.3 of these notes.

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Advantages of SWD over JTAG:**

| Aspect | JTAG | SWD |
|--------|------|-----|
| **Pin count** | 4 (+ optional TRST) | **2** |
| **Speed** | Comparable | Comparable or faster |
| **Overhead** | Higher | Lower |
| **Reset** | TRST optional | Standard ARM debug reset |
| **Use case** | General-purpose ARM, RISC-V, DSP | **Pin-constrained Cortex-M** |

**SWD-specific advantages:**

1. **Pin savings** — critical for small packages (QFN-32, WLCSP).
2. **Bidirectional single data line** — SWDIO handles both read and write.
3. **Robust against noise** — SWD uses a packet-based protocol with parity.
4. **Native to ARM Cortex-M** — coexists with JTAG on the same pins (multiplexed via the JTAG-DP / SW-DP switch inside the MCU).

**BDM (Background Debug Mode) — Key Differences from JTAG:**

| Parameter | JTAG | BDM |
|-----------|------|-----|
| **Origin** | Industry standard (IEEE 1149.1) | **Motorola/NXP proprietary** |
| **MCUs** | ARM, RISC-V, MIPS, PowerPC | NXP S08, S12, ColdFire, HC11 |
| **Pin count** | 4 | 1 bidirectional (BKGD) + reset |
| **CPU state** | Halt via debug logic | **Background mode** (special CPU state) |
| **Speed** | High | Moderate |

BDM's "background" mode is fundamentally different — the CPU executes a **separate instruction stream** from a special ROM-resident debugging micro-kernel, allowing register inspection without halting the foreground program in some implementations.

**Role of ETM (Embedded Trace Macrocell):**

ETM is a non-intrusive **real-time instruction trace** module on ARM Cortex-A/R/M cores. Its functions include:

1. **Capturing every executed instruction** into a circular trace buffer.
2. **Timestamping** each instruction for post-mortem timing analysis.
3. **Filtering** — trace only specific code regions or interrupts.
4. **Streaming** — high-speed serial trace (HSSTP, parallel TracePort) to off-chip hardware.

ETM is essential for debugging **hard real-time bugs** (e.g., race conditions, interrupt latency violations) that cannot tolerate the intrusive halt of JTAG breakpoints.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Retest, Q8b]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the **Host-Target Debug Architecture** used in modern embedded development. Describe the roles of **OpenOCD, GDB, and the debug adapter (J-Link/ST-Link)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **In-Circuit Emulators (ICE)**, **ROM Monitor debugging**, and **On-Chip Debugging (OCD)** with respect to intrusiveness, cost, real-time fidelity, and modern applicability. When would you choose a **logic analyzer** instead of a software debugger? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Host-Target Debug Architecture:**

The modern embedded debug chain is a **four-layer architecture** connecting the developer's PC to the silicon of the target MCU:

$$
\text{IDE} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{GDB Client} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Debug Adapter} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Target MCU Debug Logic}
$$

**Layer 1 — IDE (Integrated Development Environment):**

- Examples: **Eclipse CDT, Keil µVision, IAR Embedded Workbench, VS Code + Cortex-Debug**.
- Provides the source-level GUI: setting breakpoints, viewing variables, stepping.
- Forwards user actions to a **GDB client** running as a child process.

**Layer 2 — GDB Client (e.g., `arm-none-eabi-gdb`):**

- GNU Debugger compiled for the target architecture (ARM, RISC-V, AVR).
- Speaks the **GDB Remote Serial Protocol (RSP)** over a TCP socket or serial line.
- Issues textual commands such as `Z0,0x08001234,4` (set software breakpoint at address `0x08001234`, size 4 bytes).

**Layer 3 — Debug Adapter (Hardware + Firmware):**

- Examples: **SEGGER J-Link, ST-LINK/V2, CMSIS-DAP, Black Magic Probe**.
- Translates GDB RSP packets into **JTAG/SWD TAP controller state sequences**.
- Handles the **physical layer** (voltage levels, clock generation, signal integrity).

**Layer 4 — Target MCU Debug Logic:**

- The **TAP controller**, **Debug Access Port (DAP)**, and **AHB-AP / APB-AP bridges** inside the MCU.
- Provides **halt, step, register read/write, memory read/write** primitives to the adapter.

**Role of OpenOCD (Open On-Chip Debugger):**

OpenOCD is an **open-source bridge** that sits between GDB and a wide range of JTAG/SWD adapters. It:

1. Drives the adapter hardware (J-Link, ST-LINK, FTDI-based cables).
2. Implements the **TAP state machine** in software.
3. Speaks RSP to GDB.
4. Supports a vast array of MCUs and boards via configuration scripts.

**Workflow Example — Setting a Breakpoint in Eclipse:**

1. Developer double-clicks the IDE gutter to set a breakpoint at `main.c:42`.
2. Eclipse calls GDB with the `break main.c:42` command.
3. GDB translates this to `Z0,0x08001234,4` and sends it to OpenOCD.
4. OpenOCD instructs the J-Link to perform a 4-wire JTAG write to flash the **BKPT opcode** at `0x08001234`.
5. The original instruction is saved in OpenOCD's breakpoint cache.
6. On execution, the CPU halts; OpenOCD reports the halt to GDB, which notifies Eclipse, which highlights line 42 in red.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Reference:** See the modern debug architecture in SECTION 4.1.

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Comparison Table — ICE vs. ROM Monitor vs. OCD:**

| Parameter | **ICE (In-Circuit Emulator)** | **ROM Monitor** | **OCD (On-Chip Debug)** |
|-----------|------------------------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| **Hardware needed** | Bond-out probe, special HW | UART + 1 KB ROM | JTAG/SWD pins on MCU |
| **Cost** | **Very High** ($5K–$50K) | **Near-zero** (software only) | **Low** ($20–$200 adapter) |
| **Real-time fidelity** | Excellent (true real-time) | Good (UART overhead) | Near (halts CPU at breakpoints) |
| **Intrusiveness** | Low (CPU substituted) | Low–Medium (UART traffic) | Medium (breakpoint inserts BKPT) |
| **Modern applicability** | **Obsolete** (QFP/BGA, no bond-out) | Still used (bootloader) | **Standard today** |
| **Trace capability** | Full bus trace | None (typically) | ETM / SWO trace |
| **Setup time** | Hours | Minutes | Minutes |
| **MCUs supported** | Few legacy (8051, 68HC11) | All with UART | All modern ARM/RISC-V |

**When to use a Logic Analyzer instead of a Software Debugger:**

A **logic analyzer** is preferred when:

1. **Bus-level protocol debugging** — capturing SPI, I²C, UART, CAN, USB packet sequences between multiple devices.
2. **Timing violations** — setup/hold violations, race conditions across signal edges.
3. **Non-intrusive observation** — when even SWD's halt would alter the bug's behavior.
4. **Multi-channel correlation** — observing 16, 32, or 64 signals simultaneously (e.g., address + data bus + control signals).
5. **Decoding proprietary protocols** — modern logic analyzers (Saleae, Keysight) include protocol decoders.
6. **Glitch detection** — capturing rare, sub-microsecond anomalies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pro Tip — When NOT to use a logic analyzer:** A logic analyzer cannot view **internal CPU registers** or **single-step through C code**. For firmware-level bugs, JTAG/SWD + GDB is always the first choice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Module 4 — Emulators and Debugging:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ICE and OCD:** Students often describe ICE when the question asks for modern JTAG debugging. Always read the question's context (legacy 8051 → ICE; ARM Cortex → JTAG/SWD).
>
> 2. **Omitting the TAP state machine:** For a 7-mark JTAG question, merely listing TCK/TMS/TDI/TDO is worth only **2–3 marks**. The TAP controller state diagram and the IR/DR shift process are essential.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the SWD pin count:** A frequent one-mark loss: stating "SWD uses 4 wires" — it uses **2 wires** (SWDIO + SWCLK).
>
> 4. **Missing the "why" behind breakpoints:** Don't just say "software breakpoint replaces instruction." Explain **what happens when the instruction is in flash** (the flash is read-only, hence hardware breakpoints).
>
> 5. **Neglecting ETM in real-time questions:** For hard real-time systems, OCD's halt-based debugging is **intrusive**. ETM is the correct answer for non-intrusive trace.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Emulators and Debugging**

- 🔹 **ICE (In-Circuit Emulator)** = physical CPU replacement via bond-out probe; largely obsolete today.
- 🔹 **JTAG (IEEE 1149.1)** = 4-wire (TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO) on-chip debug standard; uses a 16-state TAP controller.
- 🔹 **SWD (Serial Wire Debug)** = 2-wire (SWDIO, SWCLK) ARM-specific alternative; pin-saving.
- 🔹 **BDM (Background Debug Mode)** = NXP-proprietary, single-wire, used in S12 / ColdFire MCUs.
- 🔹 **Software breakpoint** = BKPT opcode in RAM; works only in writable memory.
- 🔹 **Hardware breakpoint** = address comparator; works in flash; limited in number (2–8 typical).
- 🔹 **Watchpoint (data breakpoint)** = triggers on memory read/write, not on instruction fetch.
- 🔹 **ETM (Embedded Trace Macrocell)** = non-intrusive instruction trace for hard real-time debugging.
- 🔹 **SWO (Serial Wire Output)** = one-way trace streaming from target to host via SWD.
- 🔹 **ROM Monitor** = small debug stub in protected ROM, communicates via UART, used by GDB stubs.
- 🔹 **OpenOCD** = open-source bridge between GDB and JTAG/SWD adapters.
- 🔹 **Logic Analyzer** = passive multi-channel digital capture; used for bus-level protocol and timing analysis.
- 🔹 **Oscilloscope** = analog signal integrity debugging; 2–4 channels; for voltage/frequency analysis.
- 🔹 **JTAG for boundary scan** = PCB interconnect test, NOT debugging (original IEEE 1149.1 purpose).
- 🔹 **TAP Controller** = 16 states; key states: Test-Logic-Reset, Run-Test/Idle, Shift-DR, Shift-IR, Update-DR, Update-IR.
- 🔹 **Debug adapter cost range** = $20 (CMSIS-DAP) to $1000+ (J-Link Pro / Trace).
- 🔹 **Modern debug stack** = IDE → GDB client → OpenOCD/J-Link → SWD/JTAG → MCU DAP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on this module, always draw a **diagram** (debug architecture or TAP state machine) and **label** the data flow. Diagrams fetch at least **2–3 marks** even if your text explanation is incomplete.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Target Hardware Debugging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Target Hardware Debugging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Target Hardware Debugging** is the systematic process of identifying, isolating, and correcting software and hardware defects in an embedded system by executing the application code directly on the **production target hardware** (the actual microcontroller/SoC board) under controlled observation, rather than relying solely on host-based simulation or functional models.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST746 – Embedded Systems)**, this topic sits at the intersection of three engineering sub-disciplines:

- **Software Engineering:** Tracing execution flow, inspecting memory, and validating logic on real silicon.
- **Digital Electronics:** Using boundary-scan protocols (IEEE 1149.1) and dedicated debug pins to gain non-intrusive observability.
- **System Architecture:** Exploiting on-chip debug (OCD) logic, debug exceptions (e.g., `BKPT`, `SWI`), and trace macrocells.

The *target* in "Target Hardware Debugging" specifically refers to the **final deployment platform** — the exact processor, memory map, clock tree, peripherals, and PCB that the product will ship with. Debugging done anywhere else (simulators, QEMU, instruction-set simulators) is a *proxy* and cannot capture real-world timing, signal-integrity, EMI, peripheral-driver, and boot-loader anomalies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Mechanic's Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A car mechanic tuning an engine in the showroom vs. on the highway.**
>
> Imagine a car manufacturer who wants to test a new fuel-injection map. They can:
> 1. **Simulate** it on a computer (host-based simulation — works for math, misses real wind, fuel, heat).
> 2. **Run the engine on a bench** (in-circuit emulator — good, but the engine is not in the chassis).
> 3. **Drive the actual car on the highway with sensors plugged in** (target hardware debugging — the *real* deal).
>
> In approach (3), the mechanic connects an **OBD-II scanner** to the car's diagnostic port, reads live sensor data, sets breakpoints in the ECU's firmware, and steps through the injection control loop in real time. The car drives, the firmware runs, and the mechanic *observes reality*.

This is precisely Target Hardware Debugging:
- The **car** = your target hardware (the microcontroller board).
- The **OBD-II port** = the JTAG/SWD/cJTAG debug interface.
- The **mechanic's diagnostic software** = the host debugger (GDB, IAR, Keil µVision, OpenOCD).
- The **sensor stream** = the trace output (ITM, ETM, SWO, printf-style UART).

## 1.3 Why Target Hardware Debugging is Non-Negotiable

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Timing reality** | Simulation often mis-models cache, wait-states, DMA, and interrupt latency. Only the silicon tells the truth. |
| **Peripheral behavior** | SPI glitches, I²C clock-stretching, ADC settling errors are visible only on real pins. |
| **Boot/reset behaviour** | ROM boot-loaders, watchdog resets, brown-out detection — these never appear in a host simulator. |
| **Compiler-target mismatch** | The generated machine code may interact with hardware in ways the simulator cannot predict. |
| **Real-time constraints** | Hard real-time deadlines (µs-level) can only be validated by running on the actual CPU clock. |
| **Signal integrity** | Voltage levels, noise, and ground bounce are pure hardware phenomena requiring a logic analyzer or oscilloscope. |

## 1.4 Categorization at a Glance

Target hardware debugging techniques split into two broad philosophies:

1. **Intrusive (stops/alters execution):** JTAG/SWD debug, BDM, ROM monitors, hardware breakpoints.
2. **Non-intrusive (observes without halting):** Trace macrocells (ETM, ITM, PTM), logic analyzers, oscilloscopes, printf over UART.

A senior KTU examiner will expect you to **distinguish clearly between intrusive and non-intrusive methods**, and to know that the golden standard combines both: use JTAG for control, and trace for high-bandwidth observation.

## 1.5 Syllabus Mapping (KTU 2024 — PECST746)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic maps to the following KTU 2024 Course Outcomes (illustrative, per the official syllabus descriptors):
> - **CO4:** *Design and debug embedded applications on target hardware using appropriate tools.*
> - **CO5:** *Apply on-chip debug and trace infrastructure to validate real-time behaviour.*

Topics expected: JTAG (IEEE 1149.1), SWD, cJTAG, OCD, BDM, hardware vs. software breakpoints, ROM monitors, in-circuit emulators, logic analyzers, and on-chip trace (ETM/ITM).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Target Debug Architecture — Layered View

Target hardware debugging is best understood as a **four-layer stack**. Every KTU question on this topic will probe at least one of these layers.

### Layer 1 — Host Side (Developer Workstation)
The **Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** runs the high-level debugger (e.g., GDB, IAR Embedded Workbench, Keil µVision, Lauterbach TRACE32). The user sets breakpoints, watches variables, and steers execution. The host encodes these requests in a **debug protocol** (RDI, GDB RSP, proprietary) and ships them over a transport (USB, Ethernet, parallel).

### Layer 2 — Probe / Adapter
A hardware pod translates the host protocol into the **chip-level debug protocol**. Examples:
- **J-Link** (SEGGER) → JTAG/SWD
- **PEMicro Multilink** → BDM/ColdFire/JTAG
- **Lauterbach PowerTrace** → high-bandwidth parallel trace
- **ST-LINK** (on-board for STM32) → SWD/JTAG

### Layer 3 — On-Chip Debug Logic (the silicon's "OBD-II port")
Built into the microcontroller. The two industry-standard physical interfaces are:
- **JTAG (Joint Test Action Group)** — IEEE 1149.1, 4 (or 5) wires: `TCK`, `TMS`, `TDI`, `TDO`, optional `TRST`.
- **SWD (Serial Wire Debug)** — ARM's 2-wire alternative: `SWDIO`, `SWCLK`.
- **cJTAG (compact JTAG, IEEE 1149.7)** — 2-wire JTAG for high-density designs.
- **BDM (Background Debug Mode)** — Freescale/NXP ColdFire and HCS08, single-wire asynchronous.

This layer implements:
- The **TAP (Test Access Port)** state machine (16 states in JTAG).
- A **Debug Control Block** that can halt the CPU, single-step, read/write registers, read/write memory.
- **Breakpoint comparators** (hardware watchpoints/breakpoints).
- Optional **trace macrocells** (ETM, ITM, SWO, PTM).

### Layer 4 — Target CPU and Peripherals
The actual Cortex-M, RISC-V, AVR, MSP430, PIC, or proprietary core. The debug logic interfaces with the core via:
- A **Debug Exception and Monitor Control** register block.
- **CoreSight** infrastructure (on ARM).
- **DMI (Debug Memory Interface)** for memory-mapped register access.

## 2.2 JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) — The Cornerstone Standard

### 2.2.1 The Five JTAG Signals

| Signal | Full Name | Direction | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TCK** | Test Clock | Input to target | Synchronous clock for TAP state machine |
| **TMS** | Test Mode Select | Input to target | Sampled on TCK rising edge; navigates TAP FSM |
| **TDI** | Test Data In | Input to target | Serial data shifted into target registers |
| **TDO** | Test Data Out | Output from target | Serial data shifted out of target |
| **TRST** | Test Reset (optional) | Input to target | Asynchronous reset of TAP FSM |

> [!NOTE]
> The **TAP controller** is a 16-state finite state machine. It must be navigated with TMS to perform operations. The most important state is `Update-DR`, where data latched in the shift register is committed to the actual instruction or data register.

### 2.2.2 The Two Register Classes

- **Instruction Register (IR):** Selects which data register to access (e.g., BYPASS, IDCODE, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, EXTEST, debug instructions).
- **Data Registers (DR):** The actual scan chains, boundary-scan cells, or debug registers.

### 2.2.3 The Boundary-Scan Chain

A chain of **boundary-scan cells** is inserted between each functional pin and the core logic. This allows the debugger to:
- Drive known values on output pins (EXTEST).
- Sample the logic level on input pins (SAMPLE).
- Test inter-pin connectivity (interconnect test, manufacturing).
- Hijack pins for debug access.

## 2.3 ARM CoreSight — The De-Facto Modern Architecture

ARM's **CoreSight** is the canonical on-chip debug and trace infrastructure for modern Cortex-M/R/A cores. It consists of:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **SWD (Serial Wire Debug)** | 2-wire debug access (SWDIO + SWCLK). |
| **SWO (Serial Wire Output)** | 1-wire trace output for ITM messages and instrumentation. |
| **ETM (Embedded Trace Macrocell)** | Compressed, high-bandwidth execution trace. |
| **ITM (Instrumentation Trace Macrocell)** | 32-channel software-driven printf-style trace. |
| **DWT (Data Watchpoint and Trace)** | Hardware breakpoints, watchpoints, PC sampling, event generation. |
| **TPIU (Trace Port Interface Unit)** | Formats trace for off-chip capture (parallel or SWO). |
| **FPB (Flash Patch and Breakpoint)** | Implements up to 8 hardware breakpoints in flash. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical KTU question will test whether you know that **CoreSight uses a memory-mapped debug bus** (the **Debug Access Port, DAP**) and that the SWD protocol operates on the **ARM Debug Interface v5 (ADI v5)** specification.

## 2.4 Breakpoints — Hardware vs. Software

### 2.4.1 Software Breakpoints
- A `BKPT #imm` or `BRK` instruction is patched into the code stream at the breakpoint address.
- Requires writable memory (RAM or flash with patch support).
- The original instruction is saved in a debugger-side table; on hit, the original instruction is restored, single-stepped, and re-patched.
- **Cons:** Cannot be placed in ROM; modifies code (intrusive to instruction cache); can disrupt pipelines.

### 2.4.2 Hardware Breakpoints (Watchpoint Comparators)
- The on-chip **FPB** or **DWT** comparator watches the **Program Counter** (PC) bus.
- When PC == breakpoint address, the debug logic halts the core.
- **Pros:** Works in ROM; does not modify code; can also be data watchpoints (break on memory read/write of an address).
- **Cons:** Limited in number (e.g., 8 on Cortex-M4 FPB); consumes silicon real estate.

### 2.4.3 The Trade-off Matrix

| Criterion | Software BP | Hardware BP |
|---|---|---|
| Number available | Unlimited (in RAM) | Limited by comparators |
| Works in ROM/Flash | Only if flash-patch supported | Always |
| Code modification | Yes (patch instruction) | No |
| Data watchpoint | No | Yes (DWT) |
| Pipeline visibility | Limited | Full |

## 2.5 ROM Monitor (ROM-monitor / Debug Monitor)

A small **debug firmware** is pre-baked into the target's ROM or boot flash. It receives commands over a UART, USB, or CAN and:
- Sets/clears software breakpoints.
- Reads/writes memory and CPU registers.
- Single-steps the core.
- Resumes execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pros:** No JTAG hardware required; works on any chip with a UART.
> **Cons:** Uses a hardware interrupt (e.g., SWI on ARM) for breakpoints; steals an interrupt vector; intrusive to interrupt latency; cannot debug the monitor itself.

## 2.6 In-Circuit Emulator (ICE)

The original, pre-1980s approach. A **bond-out chip** (a special version of the target CPU with extra debug pins exposed) is plugged into a pod that emulates the CPU electrically while a host debugger controls execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern "ICE" marketing (e.g., Atmel AVR ICE, MPLAB ICE) is misleading — these are **on-chip debuggers**, not true bond-out emulators. The bond-out era is essentially over for new designs.

## 2.7 Logic Analyzers and Oscilloscopes

These are **non-intrusive observation tools** that do not interfere with code execution.

### 2.7.1 Logic Analyzer
- Many digital channels (16, 32, 64, 136, …).
- Samples a bus (e.g., address, data, control lines) at hundreds of MHz.
- Decodes protocols (I²C, SPI, UART, CAN, USB).
- **Use case:** Capture a corrupted SPI transaction between the MCU and an external ADC.

### 2.7.2 Oscilloscope
- Few channels (2–4), analog bandwidth up to GHz.
- Used for **analog** signals: rise time, ringing, power-rail noise, ADC waveform.
- **Use case:** Diagnose brown-out during a Wi-Fi transmit burst.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "formulas" below are **engineering performance metrics** for a debug system. They are exactly the kind of quantitative question KTU may ask in Part A or Part B.

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **JTAG TCK max frequency** | $f_{TCK}^{max} = \frac{1}{t_{setup} + t_{hold}}$ | Upper bound for reliable scan-clock. |
| **Trace bandwidth (parallel)** | $BW = N_{pins} \times f_{TCLK}$ | Throughput of a parallel trace port. |
| **Trace compression ratio** | $\eta = \frac{BW_{uncompressed}}{BW_{compressed}}$ | ETM/PTM efficiency metric. |
| **SWD bit time** | $T_{bit} = \frac{1}{f_{SWCLK}}$ | Period of one SWD clock. |
| **Breakpoint comparator count** | $N_{BP} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{comparator}_i$ | Total HW breakpoints available. |
| **DAP access latency** | $t_{DAP} = t_{AHB-AP} + t_{MEM-AP}$ | Read/write round-trip to memory. |
| **JTAG instruction-register length** | $L_{IR} = \lceil \log_2(N_{instr}) \rceil$ | Bits needed to encode all JTAG IR values. |
| **Scan-chain shift time** | $t_{shift} = (L_{chain} \times T_{TCK})$ | Time to clock one full bitstream. |
| **Oscilloscope sample rate (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{signal}^{max}$ | Minimum to avoid aliasing. |
| **Logic-analyzer memory depth** | $M = t_{capture} \times f_{s}$ | Samples storable. |

> **Boundary conditions to remember:**
> - $f_{TCK}$ is capped by the target's pin driver strength and PCB capacitance.
> - $\eta \geq 1$ always; $\eta = 1$ means no compression.
> - $L_{IR} \geq 2$ for the mandatory `BYPASS` and `IDCODE` instructions.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In industry, target hardware debugging is the **rate-limiting step** in embedded firmware development. Aerospace (DO-178C), automotive (ISO 26262), and medical (IEC 62304) standards mandate rigorous on-target testing with trace evidence. A graduate who masters this topic saves their company months of certification time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — JTAG TAP State Machine Transition Count

**Problem (KTU-style):** How many TCK cycles are required to navigate from `Test-Logic-Reset` to the `Shift-DR` state and clock out a 32-bit IDCODE register?

**Solution — Step by Step:**

The canonical JTAG state machine (IEEE 1149.1) has **16 states**. The two main paths from `Test-Logic-Reset` are:

- `Test-Logic-Reset` → `Run-Test/Idle` → `Select-DR-Scan` → `Select-IR-Scan` → `Capture-IR` → `Shift-IR` → …
- `Test-Logic-Reset` → `Run-Test/Idle` → `Select-DR-Scan` → `Capture-DR` → `Shift-DR` → …

We want to reach `Shift-DR` from `Test-Logic-Reset`. The minimum TCK count is the number of state transitions.

Let us enumerate the **TMS bit pattern** (LSB first, sampled on each rising TCK). The state diagram is binary-encoded; reading the IEEE 1149.1 figure, the shortest path to `Shift-DR` is:

$$\text{Path} = \text{Test-Logic-Reset} \rightarrow \text{Run-Test/Idle} \rightarrow \text{Select-DR-Scan} \rightarrow \text{Capture-DR} \rightarrow \text{Shift-DR}$$

This requires **3 transitions** after leaving `Run-Test/Idle` (you must traverse Select-DR-Scan, then Capture-DR, then Shift-DR), plus 1 transition from Test-Logic-Reset to Run-Test/Idle.

Total navigation TCK cycles:
$$N_{nav} = 4 \text{ cycles}$$

For the 32-bit IDCODE shift, the standard also requires holding the state in `Shift-DR` for $L$ cycles, where $L$ is the register length. After the last bit is clocked, you transition to `Exit1-DR` (1 cycle), then `Update-DR` (1 cycle).

$$\text{Shift cycles} = L = 32$$

Total cycles to read IDCODE:
$$N_{total} = N_{nav} + L + 2 = 4 + 32 + 2 = 38 \text{ TCK cycles}$$

**Final answer:**
$$N_{total} = 38 \text{ TCK cycles}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SWD vs. JTAG Bandwidth Comparison

**Problem:** Compare the theoretical throughput of a 4-wire JTAG and a 2-wire SWD link, both clocked at $f_{clk} = 10 \text{ MHz}$, for a 32-bit register read.

**Step 1 — JTAG throughput.**
JTAG is **serial**: one bit is shifted per TCK in TDI and one bit per TCK out TDO. To transfer 32 bits:
$$t_{JTAG} = \frac{32}{f_{clk}} = \frac{32}{10 \times 10^6} = 3.2 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2 — SWD throughput.**
SWD is also **serial** but uses a more efficient packet protocol. The 32-bit data payload is wrapped in a packet (start, parity, stop, park bits) of length typically **8-bit header + 32-bit data + parity**. We approximate **42 SCLK cycles** for a full read transaction.

$$t_{SWD} = \frac{42}{10 \times 10^6} = 4.2 \, \mu s$$

**Step 3 — Normalized comparison.**
Even though SWD has *more* overhead per packet, in **multi-register burst** reads the turnaround overhead is amortized:

$$\text{Throughput}_{SWD}^{burst} \approx \frac{32 \text{ bits}}{34 \text{ cycles}} \times 10 \text{ MHz} \approx 9.41 \text{ Mbps}$$

versus JTAG:
$$\text{Throughput}_{JTAG}^{burst} \approx \frac{32 \text{ bits}}{33 \text{ cycles}} \times 10 \text{ MHz} \approx 9.70 \text{ Mbps}$$

> **Final insight:** JTAG and SWD have **comparable bandwidth** in steady state. SWD's *real* win is **pin count** (2 vs. 4/5), not raw throughput.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Trace Port Sizing for an ETM-Equipped Cortex-M4

**Problem:** A Cortex-M4F running at $f_{CPU} = 168 \text{ MHz}$ has an ETM trace stream. The trace must capture every executed branch. If each branch instruction encodes to an average of 8 bits, and the trace port is 4-bit parallel, what is the minimum $TCLK$ such that the trace can keep up?

**Step 1 — Branch rate estimate.**
Assume an average instruction rate of one instruction per cycle at peak, with **1 branch every 5 instructions** (typical for embedded C code):

$$f_{branch} = \frac{f_{CPU}}{5} = \frac{168 \times 10^6}{5} = 33.6 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Required raw bit rate.**
Each branch packet is 8 bits on average:
$$R_{raw} = f_{branch} \times 8 = 33.6 \times 10^6 \times 8 = 268.8 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 3 — Throughput of a 4-bit parallel port.**
With $N = 4$ data pins:
$$BW = N \times f_{TCLK}$$

To meet $BW \geq R_{raw}$:
$$f_{TCLK} \geq \frac{R_{raw}}{N} = \frac{268.8 \times 10^6}{4} = 67.2 \text{ MHz}$$

**Final answer:**
$$f_{TCLK}^{min} \approx 67.2 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A practical designer would round this to $f_{TCLK} = 75 \text{ MHz}$ or $100 \text{ MHz}$ to leave margin. The TPIU (Trace Port Interface Unit) buffers trace packets, so the average can be lower than the peak; this calculation uses a peak assumption.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Debugging Tool Selection by Use Case

| Symptom | Best Primary Tool | Best Supporting Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Code never reaches a function | Hardware BP at function entry | DWT PC sampling | Confirm execution path on silicon. |
| Variable has wrong value intermittently | DWT data watchpoint | Logic analyzer on data bus | Catch the precise cycle the corruption occurs. |
| SPI peripheral gives garbled bytes | Logic analyzer (SPI decoded) | Oscilloscope on SCK/MOSI | Look for clock-stretching, ringing. |
| Hard-fault on boot | JTAG halt at Reset_Handler | ROM-monitor `info regs` | Inspect reset cause register. |
| Interrupt fires late | ITM timestamps + GPIO toggle | Oscilloscope on the GPIO | Correlate code path with real time. |
| Power consumption spikes | Joulescope / INA219 | ETM trace + DWT event counters | Tie current spikes to code regions. |
| Brown-out reset during Wi-Fi TX | Oscilloscope on VCC | JTAG halt + reset-cause register | Capture supply sag. |
| Flash programming fails | JTAG flash loader | Boundary-scan interconnect test | Diagnose pin or flash-driver issue. |
| Watchdog bites | JTAG halt with `WDG` reason | Logic analyzer on WDI pin | Verify kick cadence. |
| DMA transfer corruption | DWT watch on DMA source/dest | Logic analyzer on DMA request lines | Catch bus contention. |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Debug Protocols

| Property | JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) | SWD (ARM ADI v5) | cJTAG (IEEE 1149.7) | BDM (Freescale) | ROM Monitor |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pin count** | 4 or 5 | 2 | 2 | 1 (bidir) | 1 (UART) |
| **Pin sharing** | Often multiplexed | Often multiplexed | Yes | N/A | N/A |
| **Bandwidth** | High | Comparable | Lower | Low | Low |
| **Boundary scan** | Yes (primary purpose) | No (uses JTAG under the hood) | Yes | No | No |
| **Multi-drop (chain)** | Yes (daisy-chain) | No | Yes (star) | No | No |
| **Trace support** | Yes (TDI/TDO) | Yes (SWO pin) | Yes | No | No |
| **Royalty** | None (open IEEE) | ARM-proprietary | None (IEEE) | Freescale-proprietary | None |
| **CPU halt** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (via SWI) |
| **Used in** | Almost all MCUs | ARM Cortex-M/R | Modern space-constrained | Freescale HCS08/ColdFire | Legacy/bootloader |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Target Hardware Debugging Stack

- No reserved words (end, subgraph, style) used as node IDs.

- Plain ASCII arrows.
-->

```mermaid
graph TD

    host1 -- "DEBUG PROTOCOL<br>RDI OR GDB RSP" --> probe2
    probe2 -- "JTAG OR SWD SIGNALS<br>TCK TMS TDI TDO" --> chip3
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — JTAG Debug Session Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["START DEBUG SESSION<br>HOST SENDS RESET COMMAND"]
    s2["PROBE DRIVES TCK<br>NAVIGATE TAP TO TEST LOGIC RESET"]

    s10["RESUME EXECUTION<br>NAVIGATE TO RUN TEST IDLE"]
    s11["CPU HITS BREAKPOINT<br>TAP DRIVES DEBUG EXCEPTION"]
    s12["HOST READS MEMORY<br>AND VARIABLES"]
    s13{"DEBUG SESSION<br>ENDS"}
    s14["END<br>RELEASE PROBE"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7
    s7 -- "STEP" --> s8 --> s6
    s7 -- "BREAKPOINT" --> s9 --> s10
    s10 -- "HIT" --> s11 --> s12 --> s7
    s7 -- "QUIT" --> s13 --> s14
```

```mermaid
    cpu1 --> dwt2
    cpu1 --> fpb3
    cpu1 --> itm4
    cpu1 --> etm5
    dwt2 --> tpiu6
    itm4 --> tpiu6
    etm5 --> tpiu6

    tpiu6 -- "PARALLEL MODE" --> pad8

    pad8 --> ext9
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question below is followed by the exact model answer that the KTU board examiner expects.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between intrusive and non-intrusive target hardware debugging techniques. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Intrusive debugging** modifies or halts the normal execution of the target CPU to gain visibility. Examples:
- JTAG/SWD hardware breakpoints (halt CPU on PC match).
- ROM-monitor `BKPT`-style software breakpoints.
- Single-stepping the CPU.

**Non-intrusive debugging** observes the running system without altering its behaviour. Examples:
- Logic-analyzer capture of external pins.
- ETM/ITM on-chip trace streaming.
- Oscilloscope measurement of analog signals.

The key contrast: intrusive methods **change timing** (and can mask race conditions), while non-intrusive methods **do not** but often provide less semantic context.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**List the five JTAG signals defined in IEEE 1149.1 and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Signal | Function |
|---|---|
| **TCK** | Test Clock — drives the TAP state machine; all TMS/TDI sampling is synchronous to TCK rising edge. |
| **TMS** | Test Mode Select — sampled on TCK rising edge; controls transitions of the 16-state TAP FSM. |
| **TDI** | Test Data In — serial data shifted into the selected JTAG register. |
| **TDO** | Test Data Out — serial data shifted out of the selected JTAG register. |
| **TRST** | Test Reset (optional) — asynchronous reset of the TAP FSM to Test-Logic-Reset. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, every Part B question is an **ESE-style 14-mark question with internal choice**. We provide TWO fully worked-out alternative questions. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the JTAG TAP controller state machine with reference to the IEEE 1149.1 standard. List the mandatory and optional JTAG instructions, and describe the role of the `BYPASS` and `IDCODE` instructions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Cortex-M3 target runs at $f_{CPU} = 72 \text{ MHz}$. An SWD debugger is connected at $f_{SWCLK} = 4 \text{ MHz}$. The debugger needs to read a 1 KB block of memory over SWD. Each 32-bit SWD read transaction requires 42 SWCLK cycles (header + payload + turnaround). Calculate the **total time** to read the entire 1 KB block. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**JTAG TAP State Machine (IEEE 1149.1):**

The Test Access Port (TAP) is a 16-state finite state machine clocked by `TCK` and steered by `TMS`. The states are grouped as:

- **Steady states:** `Test-Logic-Reset`, `Run-Test/Idle`.
- **Instruction path:** `Select-IR-Scan`, `Capture-IR`, `Shift-IR`, `Exit1-IR`, `Pause-IR`, `Exit2-IR`, `Update-IR`.
- **Data path:** `Select-DR-Scan`, `Capture-DR`, `Shift-DR`, `Exit1-DR`, `Pause-DR`, `Exit2-DR`, `Update-DR`.

**Operation:** The host drives `TMS` high or low on each `TCK` rising edge to move the FSM. In `Shift-IR` and `Shift-DR`, the chosen register shifts one bit per `TCK` on `TDI`/`TDO`. On entering `Update-IR` or `Update-DR`, the shifted data is committed to the active register.

**Mandatory JTAG Instructions:**

| Instruction | Opcode (typical) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `BYPASS` | All 1s | Routes TDI to TDO through a 1-bit register; used to skip a device in a multi-drop chain. |
| `IDCODE` | `0b0001` (some vendors) | Selects the 32-bit Device Identification Register; reads manufacturer, part number, version. |
| `SAMPLE/PRELOAD` | `0b0011` | Captures pin state into boundary scan; preloads values for upcoming EXTEST. |

**Optional but common:**

- `EXTEST` — drives boundary-scan cells for interconnect test.
- `INTEST` — applies test vectors to internal core logic.
- `HIGHZ` — places all outputs in high-impedance.
- `CLAMP` — drives boundary-scan cells to a fixed state while bypassing the core.
- Vendor-specific debug instructions (e.g., `ARM's DEBUGEN`, `HALT`).

**Role of `BYPASS`:**
Used in daisy-chained JTAG topologies where multiple devices share `TDI`/`TDO`. A device not under test is placed in `BYPASS` so that its contribution to the scan chain is exactly 1 bit, minimizing scan time.

**Role of `IDCODE`:**
Read in `Shift-DR` after selecting `IDCODE` in the IR. Returns 32 bits: `<mfr[11]><partno[16]><version[4]><1>`. The LSB is always 1. This is how the host verifies the chain is intact and identifies the silicon.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Memory size in 32-bit words.**
$$N_{words} = \frac{1024 \text{ bytes}}{4 \text{ bytes/word}} = 256 \text{ words}$$

**Step 2 — SWCLK cycles per 32-bit read.**
$$N_{cycles/word} = 42$$

**Step 3 — Total SWCLK cycles for the block.**
$$N_{total} = N_{words} \times N_{cycles/word} = 256 \times 42 = 10{,}752 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 4 — Time per SWCLK cycle.**
$$T_{SWCLK} = \frac{1}{f_{SWCLK}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^6} = 0.25 \, \mu s$$

**Step 5 — Total transfer time.**
$$t_{total} = N_{total} \times T_{SWCLK} = 10{,}752 \times 0.25 \, \mu s = 2{,}688 \, \mu s$$

**Final answer:**
$$t_{total} \approx 2.69 \text{ ms}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** forgetting to convert **bytes to 32-bit words**. A 1 KB read needs 256 SWD transactions, not 1024. Examiners will deduct 2 marks if this is wrong.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Q. (a)** Compare **hardware breakpoints** and **software breakpoints** in the context of on-chip debug. Mention the units on a typical ARM Cortex-M microcontroller that implement each, and the key advantages/limitations. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **ARM CoreSight** debug architecture. What is the role of the **DAP, FPB, DWT, ITM, ETM, and TPIU** in this architecture? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Hardware Breakpoints:**
- Implemented by on-chip **comparator** circuits (e.g., **FPB** on Cortex-M).
- The comparator monitors the **Program Counter** (PC) bus. When PC equals the configured address, the debug logic asserts a debug halt request.
- **Typical count:** Cortex-M3/M4 has 8 FPB comparators; Cortex-M0+ has 4.
- **Advantages:**
  - Works in **ROM and Flash** without modification.
  - Does **not modify code**, so instruction-cache and pipeline are not perturbed.
  - Can also be **data watchpoints** (break on memory read or write of a specific address).
- **Limitations:**
  - Limited number (a few comparators per chip).
  - Consumes silicon area.

**Software Breakpoints:**
- Implemented by patching a **`BKPT` instruction** at the desired address.
- The original instruction byte is saved by the host debugger; on hit, the debug exception handler restores the original instruction, single-steps, and re-patches.
- **Typical instruction on ARM:** `BKPT #imm` (Thumb-2 encoding).
- **Advantages:**
  - **Unlimited** in number (limited only by memory).
  - Simple to set from the host.
- **Limitations:**
  - Requires **writable memory** — cannot be placed in ROM.
  - **Modifies code stream**, which can perturb cache and timing.
  - Cannot easily be a data watchpoint.

**Summary Table (write this in the exam):**

| Property | Hardware BP | Software BP |
|---|---|---|
| Number | Limited (e.g., 8) | Unlimited |
| Works in ROM/Flash | Yes | Only if flash patch supported |
| Code modification | No | Yes |
| Data watchpoint | Yes (DWT) | No |
| Pipeline perturbation | None | Possible |
| Cost | Silicon area | Memory write cycles |

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**ARM CoreSight Architecture (Block Diagram described textually since the question expects one):**

The CoreSight architecture is ARM's standardized on-chip debug and trace framework. The block diagram has the following hierarchy:

- **Top-level host interface:** **Debug Access Port (DAP)** — implements the **JTAG** (4-wire) or **SWD** (2-wire) physical layer. The DAP exposes an **AMBA AXI/APB bus** internally, allowing the host to read/write target memory and CPU registers.
- **DAP child ports:**
  - **AHB-AP (AHB Access Port):** Used for memory and register access.
  - **APB-AP (APB Access Port):** Used for debug-component configuration.
- **Core-side debug logic:**
  - **FPB (Flash Patch and Breakpoint):** Implements up to 8 hardware breakpoints, plus flash-patching (re-mapping).
  - **DWT (Data Watchpoint and Trace):** Implements data watchpoints, PC sampling, exception/interrupt tracing, performance counters, and clock-cycle measurement.
  - **ITM (Instrumentation Trace Macrocell):** 32 software-driven trace channels; the `printf` of embedded — write to stimulus registers, host reads on SWO.
  - **ETM (Embedded Trace Macrocell):** Compressed, cycle-accurate execution trace; captures branches, exceptions, and data traces.
- **Trace funnel and output:**
  - **TPIU (Trace Port Interface Unit):** Aggregates trace from DWT, ITM, ETM via a **Trace Funnel**, and outputs it either via **SWO** (1-wire) or a **parallel trace port** (4/8/16 bits).

**Function-by-function summary:**

| Unit | Role |
|---|---|
| **DAP** | Host ↔ silicon bridge; converts JTAG/SWD transactions to internal bus accesses. |
| **FPB** | Hardware breakpoints and flash remapping. |
| **DWT** | Data watchpoints, PC sampling, event counters, exception trace. |
| **ITM** | 32-channel software-instrumentation trace. |
| **ETM** | High-fidelity execution trace with branch history. |
| **TPIU** | Multiplexes and formats trace for off-chip capture. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common examiner complaint:** students often confuse **ITM** (software-instrumentation) with **ETM** (full execution trace). Remember — **ITM is what `printf` becomes; ETM is what a logic analyzer would record if it could see the CPU pipeline.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for the KTU exam hall:**

- ☐ **Target hardware debugging** = debugging on the actual production silicon, not a simulator.
- ☐ **JTAG** = IEEE 1149.1, 4 mandatory signals `TCK`, `TMS`, `TDI`, `TDO` + optional `TRST`.
- ☐ **SWD** = ARM 2-wire alternative (`SWDIO`, `SWCLK`), part of CoreSight.
- ☐ **TAP controller** = 16-state FSM steered by `TMS`, clocked by `TCK`.
- ☐ **`BYPASS` instruction** = 1-bit pass-through for daisy-chained devices.
- ☐ **`IDCODE` instruction** = 32-bit device identification read.
- ☐ **Mandatory JTAG instructions**: `BYPASS`, `IDCODE`, `SAMPLE/PRELOAD`.
- ☐ **Boundary-scan cells** sit between functional pins and core logic; enable `EXTEST`, `SAMPLE`.
- ☐ **CoreSight components** = DAP, FPB, DWT, ITM, ETM, TPIU, SWO.
- ☐ **FPB** = hardware breakpoints (up to 8 on Cortex-M4) and flash patching.
- ☐ **DWT** = data watchpoints, PC sampling, performance counters, exception trace.
- ☐ **ITM** = 32-channel software-driven `printf`-style trace.
- ☐ **ETM** = compressed, high-fidelity execution trace.
- ☐ **TPIU** = formats and exports trace via SWO or parallel port.
- ☐ **Hardware breakpoints** work in ROM, do not modify code, are limited in number.
- ☐ **Software breakpoints** (`BKPT` instruction) are unlimited but require writable memory.
- ☐ **ROM monitor** = debug firmware in ROM, uses UART + SWI; intrusive to interrupts.
- ☐ **In-circuit emulator (ICE)** = historical bond-out chip pod; modern "ICE" marketing is misleading.
- ☐ **Logic analyzer** = multi-channel digital capture, decodes protocols (I²C/SPI/UART/CAN).
- ☐ **Oscilloscope** = analog bandwidth, used for signal integrity, power rails, ADC waveforms.
- ☐ **Intrusive debugging** halts/modifies execution; **non-intrusive** observes.
- ☐ **Nyquist rule** for oscilloscope: $f_s \geq 2 f_{signal}^{max}$.
- ☐ **JTAG TCK max** limited by setup/hold times and PCB capacitance.
- ☐ **Trace bandwidth** for parallel port: $BW = N_{pins} \times f_{TCLK}$.
- ☐ **JTAG vs SWD**: comparable bandwidth; SWD wins on pin count (2 vs 4/5).
- ☐ **cJTAG (IEEE 1149.7)** = 2-wire JTAG variant for high-density designs.
- ☐ **BDM** = Freescale/NXP 1-wire background debug for HCS08/ColdFire.
- ☐ **Reset cause register** = first place to look after an unexpected reset.
- ☐ **DAP access latency** is the time to read/write memory via the debug bus.

> **Closing note for students:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, questions on this topic are increasingly **scenario-based** ("a sensor reading is wrong intermittently — propose a debugging strategy"). Memorize the analytical matrix in §3.4 and the tool-comparison matrix in §3.5 — they are the single highest-yield memory aids for the 14-mark Part B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Scan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST746 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Boundary Scan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Boundary Scan** is a structured design-for-test (DFT) methodology formalized under the **IEEE 1149.1 Standard** (commonly known as **JTAG — Joint Test Action Group**) that embeds dedicated test logic (boundary scan cells) around the periphery of an integrated circuit (IC) to allow interconnections between chips on a PCB, internal chip logic, and cluster conditions to be tested without physical probe access.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Boundary Scan solves the "**Physical Access Problem**" that arose due to Ball Grid Array (BGA), Chip-on-Board (COB), and multi-layer PCB manufacturing, where traditional bed-of-nails in-circuit testers could no longer physically contact every node.

## 1.2 The Five Mandatory Boundary Scan Signals (TAP Port)

Every JTAG-compliant device exposes a **4-wire (or 5-wire) Test Access Port (TAP)**:

| Signal | Full Name | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TCK** | Test Clock | Input | Provides the synchronous clock for the boundary scan logic |
| **TMS** | Test Mode Select | Input | Controls the state transitions of the TAP controller FSM |
| **TDI** | Test Data In | Input | Serial input of data/instructions into the JTAG register chain |
| **TDO** | Test Data Out | Output | Serial output of data from the JTAG register chain |
| **TRST\*** | Test Reset (Optional) | Input | Asynchronous reset of the TAP controller (active LOW) |

> [!NOTE]
> A series daisy-chain of JTAG devices is often called a **scan chain** or **JTAG chain**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Doctor's Stethoscope for PCBs"

Imagine a complex, sealed black box (your populated PCB) where you cannot see the joints, traces, or internal wires. In the old days (pre-1990), a technician would use sharp metallic probes (a "bed of nails") to touch every solder joint to verify continuity. With BGA packages, the joints are **hidden under the chip** — totally inaccessible.

**Boundary Scan** acts like a **miniature set of sentries stationed at every chip's I/O pin**. Each sentry can:
1. **Capture** the logic value arriving at the pin.
2. **Override** the pin's normal value and inject a test pattern onto the PCB trace.
3. **Read back** the test pattern at the neighboring chip's pin to verify the trace is intact.

The sentries are coordinated by a **traffic controller (TAP Controller)** that uses only **4 wires** running in series across all chips — just as a doctor uses a single stethoscope tube to listen to the heart, lungs, and intestines in sequence.

## 1.4 Why It Was Created — The Driving Problems

- **Miniaturization:** Surface-mount and BGA packages removed physical access for probes.
- **Density:** Modern PCBs may have **6,000+ nets**, making fixtures impossibly expensive.
- **Speed:** Bed-of-nails testers cap at ~**100 MHz**; JTAG can shift at TCK rates of **10–100 MHz**+.
- **Cost:** A JTAG test costs a fraction of a custom fixture (which can exceed **$50,000 USD** for a dense board).

## 1.5 Core Architecture at a Glance

Every JTAG-compliant IC contains:
- **Boundary Scan Cells (BSCs)** — one per functional I/O pin.
- **TAP Controller** — a 16-state finite state machine.
- **Instruction Register (IR)** — holds the current test command.
- **Data Registers** — Bypass, Boundary Scan, Device ID, and optionally USERCODE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Boundary Scan Cell — The Atomic Unit

A **Boundary Scan Cell (BSC)** is a 1-bit storage element inserted between the core logic of the IC and its physical pin. Every functional I/O pin has **two** boundary scan cells (one for input, one for output direction) plus cells for control signals (enable, high-impedance).

### Operational Modes of a BSC

1. **Normal Mode** — The cell is transparent; data flows directly between the core logic and the pin. JTAG logic is invisible to the application.
2. **Capture Mode** — The cell samples the current value on the pin or the core-side signal.
3. **Shift Mode** — The cell becomes part of a serial scan chain. Data shifts from **TDI** to **TDO** through every cell.
4. **Update Mode** — The value in the parallel output latch is updated, potentially overriding the pin.

## 2.2 The TAP Controller — 16-State FSM

The **Test Access Port (TAP) Controller** is a synchronous finite state machine clocked by **TCK** and steered by **TMS**. It dictates whether the IR or a selected Data Register (DR) is connected between TDI and TDO.

### State Categories
- **Reset & Idle:** Test-Logic-Reset, Run-Test/Idle
- **DR Path:** Select-DR-Scan, Capture-DR, Shift-DR, Exit1-DR, Pause-DR, Exit2-DR, Update-DR
- **IR Path:** Select-IR-Scan, Capture-IR, Shift-IR, Exit1-IR, Pause-IR, Exit2-IR, Update-IR

> [!NOTE]
> **State Transition Rule:** On each rising edge of TCK, the controller samples TMS and transitions to the next state. Holding **TMS = 1** for **5 consecutive TCK cycles** guarantees entry to Test-Logic-Reset from any state.

## 2.3 Standard IEEE 1149.1 Instructions

| Instruction | Opcode (Typical) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **BYPASS** | `1111...` | Connects a 1-bit bypass register between TDI and TDO, shortening the chain |
| **EXTEST** | `0000...` | Tests **off-chip** interconnects by driving pins from BSCs and sampling them on neighbor ICs |
| **SAMPLE/PRELOAD** | `0011...` | Snapshots pin values into BSRs without affecting system operation; also preloads data for next EXTEST |
| **INTEST** | `0100...` | Tests **on-chip** logic by applying stimuli from BSCs to the core |
| **IDCODE** | `0111...` | Selects the 32-bit Device Identification Register; used for board-level inventory |
| **USERCODE** | `1110...` | Selects user-programmable identification for FPGAs/configurable devices |
| **HIGHZ** | `1001...` | Forces all outputs to high-impedance |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Equation / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **TCK Period** | $T_{TCK} = \frac{1}{f_{TCK}}$ | Seconds; typical $f_{TCK}$ = 10–100 MHz |
| **Total Shift Time** | $T_{shift} = (N_{IR} + N_{DR} + 1) \cdot n_{cycles} \cdot T_{TCK}$ | n = number of devices in chain |
| **Scan Chain Length** | $L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$ | $L_i$ = length of DR/IR in device *i* |
| **Stuck-at Fault Coverage** | $C_{SA} = \frac{N_{detected}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| **Bypass Latency** | $T_{bypass} = 1 \cdot T_{TCK}$ | Always 1 TCK per bypassed device |
| **EXTEST Coverage** | $C_{EXTEST} = \frac{N_{nets\_tested}}{N_{nets\_total}} \times 100\%$ | Excludes non-JTAG nets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In markdown tables, the absolute-value symbol $\vert x \vert$ is rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` — never use a raw vertical bar `|`, as it breaks the table column delimiter.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real-World Systems

- **Automotive ECU Manufacturing** (Bosch, Continental) — Every ECU leaving the production line is JTAG-tested for solder defects.
- **FPGA & CPLD Programming** — All major vendors (Xilinx, Altera/Intel, Lattice) use JTAG as the primary configuration port.
- **In-System Programming (ISP)** — Flash microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, MSP430) program firmware over JTAG/SWD.
- **Debug Interfaces** — ARM's **CoreSight** debug logic piggybacks on JTAG to provide real-time trace and breakpoints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Total JTAG Test Time

A complete boundary scan test consists of three phases: **Shift**, **Update**, and **Capture**. The dominant time component is shifting the test vector through the chain.

### Given
- $n$ = number of JTAG devices in the chain
- $L_{IR}^{(i)}$ = length of the Instruction Register in device *i* (in bits)
- $L_{DR}^{(i)}$ = length of the Data Register selected by the instruction in device *i*
- $f_{TCK}$ = TCK frequency in Hz
- $k$ = number of test vectors to apply

### Step 1 — Compute the chain length for the IR path
The effective IR chain length across all devices in the chain is:

$$
L_{IR,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{IR}^{(i)}
$$

*Textual explanation:* The IR bits of all devices form a single shift register during instruction loading; hence, summation across all *n* devices gives the total number of bits to shift.

### Step 2 — Compute the chain length for the DR path
For a particular test instruction (e.g., EXTEST), each device's data path length is summed:

$$
L_{DR,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{DR}^{(i)}
$$

*Textual explanation:* If the selected DR for device *i* is its Boundary Scan Register, then $L_{DR}^{(i)}$ equals the number of BSCs in that device. BYPASS instruction substitutes $L_{DR}^{(i)} = 1$.

### Step 3 — Compute cycles per vector
Loading an instruction requires $L_{IR,total}$ shift cycles plus 2 cycles for Select-IR-Scan and Update-IR (effectively, 1 extra cycle for Capture-IR). Applying a test vector requires $L_{DR,total}$ shift cycles plus 1 update cycle. Therefore, the cycle count per vector is:

$$
N_{cycles} = L_{IR,total} + L_{DR,total} + 2
$$

*Textual explanation:* The "+2" accounts for the two state transitions into Update-IR and Update-DR (each costing 1 TCK edge beyond the shift itself).

### Step 4 — Compute total test time
For *k* test vectors, the total shift time is:

$$
T_{total} = k \cdot N_{cycles} \cdot T_{TCK}
$$

Substituting $T_{TCK} = 1 / f_{TCK}$:

$$
T_{total} = \frac{k \cdot N_{cycles}}{f_{TCK}}
$$

*Textual explanation:* Each cycle consumes one TCK period; multiplying by the number of cycles and the number of vectors gives the wall-clock test duration.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example
Consider a PCB with **n = 3** JTAG devices:
- Device A: $L_{IR} = 4$, $L_{DR}$ (BSR) = 200
- Device B: $L_{IR} = 8$, $L_{DR}$ (BYPASS) = 1
- Device C: $L_{IR} = 4$, $L_{DR}$ (BSR) = 150

Compute the total chain lengths:

$$
L_{IR,total} = 4 + 8 + 4 = 16 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
L_{DR,total} = 200 + 1 + 150 = 351 \text{ bits}
$$

Compute cycles per vector:

$$
N_{cycles} = 16 + 351 + 2 = 369 \text{ cycles/vector}
$$

For $k = 100$ test vectors at $f_{TCK} = 10 \text{ MHz}$:

$$
T_{total} = \frac{100 \cdot 369}{10 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{36{,}900}{10^{7}} = 3.69 \text{ ms}
$$

*Textual interpretation:* A modest 100-vector test on a 351-bit chain completes in under **4 milliseconds** — orders of magnitude faster than a bed-of-nails ICT.

## 3.2 Derivation of Fault Coverage for Stuck-At Defects

### Step 1 — Define testable net count
A net is "JTAG-testable" if **both** endpoints are pins of JTAG-compliant ICs. A traditional BGA-internal trace is not directly testable.

$$
N_{testable} = N_{nets} - N_{non\_jtag\_nets}
$$

### Step 2 — Define detected faults
For each testable net, EXTEST can detect:
- **Stuck-at-0 (SA0)**
- **Stuck-at-1 (SA1)**
- **Open circuits**
- **Bridging faults (between adjacent nets)** — with appropriate vector pairs

Let $N_{detected}$ be the count of faults actually detected by the test vector set.

### Step 3 — Compute coverage

$$
C_{SA} = \frac{N_{detected}}{2 \cdot N_{testable}} \times 100\%
$$

*Textual note:* The denominator is $2 \cdot N_{testable}$ because each net has both an SA0 and an SA1 fault model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** In exam derivations, always show the units and explicitly state the assumptions (e.g., "Assuming single-fault model and complete controllability/observability of all JTAG pins").

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — JTAG vs. Traditional ICT

| Parameter | Boundary Scan (JTAG) | Bed-of-Nails ICT |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Access** | None (4-wire serial) | Required for every node |
| **Test Vector Generation** | Automatic (BSDL files) | Manual fixture design |
| **Fixture Cost** | Negligible | $10K – $100K+ |
| **BGA Compatibility** | Full | Poor / Impossible |
| **Test Time (typical board)** | **3–10 ms** | **10–60 s** |
| **Fault Coverage** | 70–95% of nets | Up to 98% (but limited access) |
| **Debug Capability** | Excellent (real-time) | Limited |
| **Programming Support** | Yes (ISP via JTAG) | No |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — JTAG Architecture Inside an IC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TCK[TCK Pin] --> TCLK[TAP Clock]
    TMS[TMS Pin] --> TCTRL[TAP Controller FSM]
    TDI[TDI Pin] --> MUX1{MUX}
    TDO[TDO Pin] --> MUXOUT
    TRST[TRST Pin] --> TCTRL

    TCTRL -->|Controls| MUX1
    TCTRL -->|Controls| MUXOUT

    IR[Instruction Register] --> MUX1
    DR1[Bypass Register 1-bit] --> MUX1
    DR2[Boundary Scan Register] --> MUX1
    DR3[Device ID Register] --> MUX1

    MUX1 --> MUXOUT
    MUXOUT --> TDO

    IR --> DECODER[Instruction Decoder]
    DECODER -->|Selects Active DR| MUX1

    DR2 --> BSCells[Boundary Scan Cells around I/O Pins]
    BSCells --> IOPins[Functional I/O Pins to PCB]
```

*Description:* The TAP Controller arbitrates which register is connected between TDI and TDO. The Instruction Decoder reads the IR's opcode and enables the corresponding data path.

## 4.2 TAP Controller State Diagram (16 States)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TestLogicReset

    TestLogicReset --> RunTestIdle: TMS=0
    TestLogicReset --> TestLogicReset: TMS=1

    RunTestIdle --> SelectDRScan: TMS=1
    SelectDRScan --> SelectIRScan: TMS=1
    SelectDRScan --> CaptureDR: TMS=0

    CaptureDR --> ShiftDR: TMS=0
    CaptureDR --> Exit1DR: TMS=1

    ShiftDR --> ShiftDR: TMS=0
    ShiftDR --> Exit1DR: TMS=1

    Exit1DR --> PauseDR: TMS=0
    Exit1DR --> UpdateDR: TMS=1
    Exit1DR --> SelectDRScan: TMS=1

    PauseDR --> PauseDR: TMS=0
    PauseDR --> Exit2DR: TMS=1

    Exit2DR --> ShiftDR: TMS=0
    Exit2DR --> UpdateDR: TMS=1
    Exit2DR --> SelectDRScan: TMS=1

    UpdateDR --> SelectDRScan: TMS=1
    UpdateDR --> RunTestIdle: TMS=0

    SelectIRScan --> CaptureIR: TMS=0
    SelectIRScan --> TestLogicReset: TMS=1

    CaptureIR --> ShiftIR: TMS=0
    CaptureIR --> Exit1IR: TMS=1

    ShiftIR --> ShiftIR: TMS=0
    ShiftIR --> Exit1IR: TMS=1

    Exit1IR --> PauseIR: TMS=0
    Exit1IR --> UpdateIR: TMS=1
    Exit1IR --> SelectIRScan: TMS=1

    PauseIR --> PauseIR: TMS=0
    PauseIR --> Exit2IR: TMS=1

    Exit2IR --> ShiftIR: TMS=0
    Exit2IR --> UpdateIR: TMS=1
    Exit2IR --> SelectIRScan: TMS=1

    UpdateIR --> SelectDRScan: TMS=1
    UpdateIR --> RunTestIdle: TMS=0
```

## 4.3 Daisy-Chain Configuration Across Multiple Devices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tester / Controller] -->|TDI| DevA[Device A TAP]
    DevA -->|TDO| DevB[Device B TAP]
    DevB -->|TDO| DevC[Device C TAP]
    DevC -->|TDO| BackToA[Back to Tester]

    TCKBus[TCK Bus] -.-> DevA
    TCKBus -.-> DevB
    TCKBus -.-> DevC

    TMSBus[TMS Bus] -.-> DevA
    TMSBus -.-> DevB
    TMSBus -.-> DevC
```

*Explanation:* All TCK and TMS signals are tied in parallel (broadcast), while TDI → TDO form a serial daisy chain through all devices.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Boundary Scan and list the four mandatory signals of the TAP port.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Boundary Scan is a DFT technique standardized under **IEEE 1149.1** that embeds test cells around an IC's pins to enable interconnect testing without physical probes.

The four mandatory TAP signals are:
1. **TCK** – Test Clock
2. **TMS** – Test Mode Select
3. **TDI** – Test Data In
4. **TDO** – Test Data Out

(Optional: **TRST\*** for asynchronous reset.)

---

### Question 2: What is the function of the BYPASS instruction in JTAG?
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
The **BYPASS** instruction selects a 1-bit bypass register between TDI and TDO for a particular device in the chain. This effectively **removes the device from the active scan path** during a test, reducing the chain length and accelerating test execution. Only one TCK cycle is required for data to pass through a bypassed device.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Weight Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Module Internal Choice Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an IEEE 1149.1 boundary scan testable IC. Discuss the role of the **TAP Controller**, **Instruction Register**, and **Boundary Scan Register**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **EXTEST** and **SAMPLE/PRELOAD** instructions in detail. How does EXTEST detect interconnect faults between two ICs on a PCB? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

The IEEE 1149.1 architecture consists of four primary blocks (see Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **TAP Controller** — A 16-state FSM clocked by TCK, controlled by TMS. It manages transitions between data and instruction scan operations.
2. **Instruction Register (IR)** — Stores the current test command (e.g., EXTEST, BYPASS). Its output feeds the Instruction Decoder, which enables the corresponding Data Register.
3. **Data Registers** — Includes the Bypass Register (1-bit), Boundary Scan Register (BSR), and Device Identification Register.
4. **Boundary Scan Cells** — Placed between core logic and each I/O pin; they capture, shift, or update pin data.

**Role summary:**
- The **TAP Controller** acts as a sequencer.
- The **IR** defines *what* test is being run.
- The **BSR** defines *which pins* are exercised and *what pattern* is applied/sampled.

#### Model Solution (b):

**EXTEST (External Test):**
- Used to test **off-chip** interconnections (PCB traces, solder joints).
- The BSR is connected between TDI and TDO.
- Values shifted into the BSR are driven onto the output pins of the IC, *overriding* normal functional behavior.
- The neighboring IC (also in EXTEST mode) samples its input pins via its own BSRs.
- Mismatches between the driven pattern and the captured pattern indicate **stuck-at**, **open**, or **bridging** faults.

**SAMPLE/PRELOAD:**
- **Sample mode:** BSCs capture the current pin values into the shift register *without disturbing* normal operation. Used for **observability** during functional mode.
- **Preload mode:** Loads a known data pattern into the BSR's parallel latches in preparation for the next EXTEST cycle, speeding up the test.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Module Internal Choice Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **TAP Controller state diagram**. What is the role of the **Test-Logic-Reset** state, and how is it reliably entered? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PCB contains **4 JTAG devices** in a daisy chain. Device IR lengths are 4, 8, 5, 4 bits respectively. The selected EXTEST data register lengths are 250, 1 (BYPASS), 180, 120 bits. The TCK frequency is **25 MHz**, and the test applies **200 vectors**. Compute the total test time. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

The TAP Controller is a 16-state synchronous FSM. (Refer to the state diagram in Section 4.2.)

**Major State Groups:**
- **DR Path States** (Capture-DR → Shift-DR → Exit1-DR → Pause-DR → Exit2-DR → Update-DR)
- **IR Path States** (Capture-IR → Shift-IR → Exit1-IR → Pause-IR → Exit2-IR → Update-IR)
- **Control States** (Test-Logic-Reset, Run-Test/Idle, Select-DR-Scan, Select-IR-Scan)

**Role of Test-Logic-Reset:**
- All test logic is disabled; the IC operates in normal functional mode.
- The Instruction Register is reset to IDCODE or BYPASS.
- Data registers are cleared.

**Reliable Entry:**
- Power-on reset (TRST\* if present) OR
- Holding **TMS = 1** for **5 consecutive rising TCK edges** from any state.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1 — Compute IR total:**

$$
L_{IR,total} = 4 + 8 + 5 + 4 = 21 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DR total:**

$$
L_{DR,total} = 250 + 1 + 180 + 120 = 551 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute cycles per vector:**

$$
N_{cycles} = 21 + 551 + 2 = 574 \text{ cycles/vector}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total cycles:**

$$
N_{total} = 200 \times 574 = 114{,}800 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute total time:**

$$
T_{total} = \frac{N_{total}}{f_{TCK}} = \frac{114{,}800}{25 \times 10^{6}} = 4.592 \text{ ms}
$$

*Final Answer:* **Total test time ≈ 4.59 ms.**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+2 cycles"** in the total cycle count. The transitions into Update-IR and Update-DR each cost 1 extra TCK edge beyond the shift itself. Omitting this loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing the IR and DR lengths.** The IR length is always small (typically 4–8 bits) and is shifted *once* per vector; the DR length is large and shifted *per vector*.
> 3. **Mixing up units.** $T_{TCK}$ is in **seconds**, $f_{TCK}$ in **Hz**. A common slip is writing $T_{total} = N_{cycles} \cdot f_{TCK}$ instead of dividing.
> 4. **Drawing the TAP FSM with 15 or 17 states.** It has exactly **16 states** — drawing the wrong count is an immediate 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention TRST\*** is *optional*. Examiners deduct marks if you claim it is mandatory.
> 6. **Calling JTAG a "protocol"** — it is a **standard (IEEE 1149.1)** and a **methodology**, not a communication protocol like SPI or I²C.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Boundary Scan**

- ✅ Full name: **IEEE 1149.1 / JTAG** (Joint Test Action Group).
- ✅ Purpose: Test interconnects **without physical probe access**.
- ✅ TAP Port: **TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO** (TRST\* optional).
- ✅ TAP Controller has **exactly 16 states** — all transitions occur on rising TCK.
- ✅ Holding **TMS = 1 for 5 cycles** forces **Test-Logic-Reset** from any state.
- ✅ **Bypass Register** = 1-bit shortcut to speed up chain shifting.
- ✅ **EXTEST** = tests PCB traces (off-chip).
- ✅ **INTEST** = tests core logic (on-chip).
- ✅ **SAMPLE/PRELOAD** = observability + preloading for next test.
- ✅ **IDCODE** = 32-bit device identification.
- ✅ Chain is **daisy-chained** (TDI → TDO), with TCK and TMS broadcast in parallel.
- ✅ Test time formula:

$$
T_{total} = \frac{k \cdot (L_{IR,total} + L_{DR,total} + 2)}{f_{TCK}}
$$

- ✅ BSDL (Boundary Scan Description Language) files describe each device's JTAG behavior.
- ✅ Used in **FPGA configuration, MCU flash programming, and SoC debug (ARM CoreSight)**.
- ✅ Draw the **block diagram** (Section 4.1) and **state diagram** (Section 4.2) cleanly — these are guaranteed 7-mark questions.
